**Given:** $\alpha = 14^{\circ}$, $\alpha_c = 9^{\circ}$

**Step 1 – Lip Angle Formula:**

$$
\varepsilon = 90^{\circ} - (\alpha + \alpha_{c})
$$

**Step 2 – Substitute Values:**

$$
\varepsilon = 90^{\circ} - (14^{\circ} + 9^{\circ})
$$

$$
\varepsilon = 90^{\circ} - 23^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon = 67^{\circ}}
$$

**Step 3 – Work-Rest Setting Angle (from 3.2 identity):**

$$
\theta_{\text{rest}} = \varepsilon = 67^{\circ}
$$

$$
\boxed{\theta_{\text{rest}} = 67^{\circ}}
$$

**Step 4 – Conclusion:** The operator must rotate the work-rest to $67^{\circ}$ on the graduated protractor before clamping the tool blank and commencing the grinding pass.

---

### Question B – Choice B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for grinding a single-point HSS cutting tool on a tool-grinder machine. Explain the **selection of the grinding wheel** for HSS and the **precautions** to be taken during tool grinding. Mention the role of a **tool-maker's microscope** in verifying tool angles.**

#### Part (a) – Step-by-Step Procedure & Wheel Selection (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Required Equipment & Materials:**
1. Tool and Cutter Grinder (or precision bench grinder with swivel tool rest).
2. HSS tool blank ($12 \times 12 \times 100$ mm, rectangular cross-section).
3. Grinding wheel: **A-60-K-5V** (Aluminium Oxide, Grit 60, Medium grade, Vitrified bond, 5-grade structure).
4. Diamond wheel dresser.
5. Universal bevel protractor / tool-maker's microscope.
6. Coolant (soluble oil + water, 1:20 ratio).
7. Personal Protective Equipment (goggles, gloves, apron).

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**

1. **Study the Tool Drawing:** Identify the required values of side rake (α), back rake (α_b), side relief (β), end relief (γ), cutting edge angle (ψ), and nose radius (r).

2. **Select the Grinding Wheel:** For HSS, use a **white Aluminium Oxide wheel of grit 46–60, medium grade (J–L), vitrified bond**. The specification reads: **A-60-K-5V**. For Carbide, use **green Silicon Carbide (C-60-J-5V)** or **CBN** for higher productivity.

3. **Dress the Grinding Wheel:** Mount the diamond dresser on the work-rest, traverse it slowly across the wheel face to remove glaze, expose fresh abrasive, and ensure the wheel runs concentric.

4. **Set the Work-Rest Angle:** Compute the work-rest angle using $\theta_{\text{rest}} = 90^{\circ} - (\alpha + \alpha_{c})$ and rotate the graduated work-rest to this value. Lock the work-rest with the locking handle.

5. **Clamp the Tool Blank:** Hold the HSS blank firmly in the tool vice on the work-rest. Ensure the shank is parallel to the wheel face and projects the correct length for grinding.

6. **Grind the Rake Face:** Bring the tool into light contact with the wheel. Use a small reciprocating motion, taking light cuts (0.02 mm/pass) to avoid burning the HSS edge. Apply coolant continuously.

7. **Grind the Clearance (Flank) Face:** Reclamp the tool, set the work-rest to a different angle (typically to the side relief angle β), and grind the flank face, ensuring adequate clearance.

8. **Grind the End Relief:** Rotate the tool $90^{\circ}$ in the vice and grind the end relief angle (γ) similarly.

9. **Grind the Nose Radius:** Using a small-radius dressing attachment or by manual manipulation with fine feed, generate the specified nose radius at the tool tip.

10. **Deburr and Verify:** Use a fine abrasive stone to deburr the cutting edge. Check all angles using a **universal bevel protractor** (for $1^{\circ}$ accuracy) or a **tool-maker's microscope** (for $1'$ arc-minute accuracy).

**Wheel Selection Justification:**
- **HSS is tough and tough-to-grind** — Aluminium Oxide is also tough, with good thermal conductivity, and does not react chemically with HSS at grinding temperatures.
- **Grit 60** provides a balance between material removal rate and surface finish.
- **Grade K (medium-hard)** resists wear, but is friable enough to self-sharpen.
- **Vitrified bond** withstands the high grinding zone temperature of $800$°C to $1200$°C.

#### Part (b) – Precautions & Role of Tool-Maker's Microscope (7 Marks)

**Precautions:**

1. **Personal Safety:** Always wear **safety goggles** to protect eyes from flying sparks and abrasive particles. Use a **face shield** for heavy grinding. Wear an **apron** to protect clothing.

2. **Wheel Safety:** Ensure the grinding wheel is **ring-tested** (struck lightly with a wooden mallet) before mounting — a clear metallic ring indicates an intact wheel, while a dull thud indicates a cracked wheel (which must be discarded). Mount the wheel only between **blotters** and tighten the flange nut properly.

3. **Coolant Use:** Use a continuous flow of coolant to prevent **thermal damage (burning) of the HSS edge** and to wash away swarf. A burnt tool edge has a microstructure of **retained austenite and untempered martensite**, which reduces hardness and accelerates wear.

4. **Light Cuts:** Take light grinding passes ($0.01$–$0.03$ mm depth). Heavy cuts cause wheel loading, chatter, and workpiece burn.

5. **Dress Frequently:** Re-dress the wheel every 10–15 minutes of grinding or whenever the wheel becomes glazed.

6. **Work-Rest Calibration:** Verify the work-rest protractor with a **reference square** before every setting. An error of $1^{\circ}$ in the work-rest setting causes a $1^{\circ}$ error in the ground tool angle.

7. **Avoid Sparks-on-Skin:** Never let the grinding spark stream contact the operator's hand or arm — this causes **spark burns (foreign-body granuloma)** that take weeks to heal.

**Role of Tool-Maker's Microscope (TMM):**

The **Tool-Maker's Microscope** is a precision optical instrument used to **measure the angles, radii, and linear dimensions** of a ground cutting tool to an accuracy of **$\pm 1$ arc-minute** ($1/60^{\circ}$).

- The tool is mounted on a **precision work-stage** between the **light source** and the **microscope objective**.
- The tool image is projected onto a **reticle** (glass plate with crosshairs, goniometer circles, and protractor scales).
- The operator **rotates the protractor circle** of the eyepiece to align the crosshair with the relevant edge of the tool image, and the **angle is read directly** from the goniometer scale.
- The TMM can measure:
  - **Rake angle** and **clearance angle** to $1'$ accuracy.
  - **Nose radius** using a calibrated filar eyepiece micrometer.
  - **Cutting edge straightness** and **chipped edges** (visual inspection).
- For KTU lab verification, a **universal bevel protractor** ($1^{\circ}$ accuracy) is acceptable; a TMM is used for research-grade verification.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas (KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout):**
> 1. **Forgetting to dress the wheel** before grinding — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing side rake and back rake** in the sketch — costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Using SiC wheel for HSS** — costs 1 mark (and shows lack of fundamental understanding).
> 4. **Forgetting coolant** — costs 1 mark.
> 5. **Not mentioning safety goggles** — costs 1 mark (a frequent KTU deduction).
> 6. **Writing the wrong unit for the lip angle** (e.g., writing 67 instead of $67^{\circ}$) — costs 0.5 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print-This-Section Summary for the KTU Lab Viva:**

- [x] **Tool Grinding Definition:** Precision abrasive shaping of a tool blank on a tool-grinder to obtain specified rake, clearance, and nose geometry.
- [x] **ASA System:** Seven tool elements — Side Rake α, Back Rake α_b, Side Relief β, End Relief γ, Cutting Edge Angle ψ, Lip Angle ε, Nose Radius r.
- [x] **Master Formula:** $\varepsilon = 90^{\circ} - (\alpha + \alpha_c)$ — both the **lip angle** and the **work-rest setting angle** equal this value.
- [x] **Wheel for HSS:** **A-60-K-5V** (Aluminium Oxide). **Wheel for Carbide:** **C-60-J-5V** (Silicon Carbide) or **CBN**.
- [x] **Dresser:** **Diamond-impregnated dresser** is mandatory before tool grinding.
- [x] **Coolant:** Soluble oil + water (1:20) — **mandatory for carbide**, strongly recommended for HSS.
- [x] **Tool Verification:** Universal bevel protractor ($1^{\circ}$) for routine; Tool-Maker's Microscope ($1'$) for research-grade.
- [x] **Nose Radius Trade-off:** Larger r = better finish, more force, more chatter risk.
- [x] **Cutting Edge Angle (ψ) Effect:** Small ψ → thin chip, weak corner; Large ψ → thick chip, strong corner.
- [x] **Lip Angle Strength Rule:** Lip angle $> 75^{\circ}$ → strong edge for roughing; $< 65^{\circ}$ → sharp edge for finishing.
- [x] **Dressing Frequency:** Every 10–15 minutes of grinding, or whenever the wheel glazes.
- [x] **Safety Essentials:** Goggles, ring-tested wheel, coolant, light cuts, frequent dressing, no direct spark contact with skin.
- [x] **Typical HSS Tool Geometry (Steel Work):** α = $+10^{\circ}$ to $+15^{\circ}$, β = $8^{\circ}$ to $12^{\circ}$, ε = $65^{\circ}$ to $75^{\circ}$, ψ = $45^{\circ}$ to $75^{\circ}$, r = $0.5$ to $1.5$ mm.
- [x] **Common KTU Viva Question:** *"Why is the work-rest set to the lip angle?"* — Answer: To present the rake face and the clearance face at the correct angular relationship to the grinding wheel, so that the ground tool has the specified rake and clearance angles.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spur Gear Cutting on Milling Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Spur Gear Cutting on Milling Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Spur Gear Cutting on a Milling Machine** is a gear manufacturing process in which the **involute tooth profile** of a spur gear is generated by progressively cutting successive tooth spaces on a cylindrical gear blank using a **form-relieved (profile) cutter** mounted on the arbour of a **horizontal (plain) milling machine**, with the blank mounted on a **dividing head** that rotates through precise angular increments to index one tooth space after another until all teeth are cut.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** This experiment tests the student's ability to (i) compute gear geometry from given specifications, (ii) select the correct form cutter, (iii) perform **simple/compound indexing** calculations, and (iv) execute the cutting sequence to produce a dimensionally accurate spur gear.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you have a **circular chocolate cake** and want to slice it into **T equal pieces** (say 24 slices for 24 teeth). You cannot eyeball it — you need a **measuring wheel** (the dividing head with its 40-hole circle plate) that clicks into the next position after each slice. The **cake sits on a turntable** (the dividing head spindle), and a **curved blade** (the involute form cutter) carves out the gap between two adjacent teeth.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a **cookie cutter shaped like a single gear tooth** stamped into a rolled-out dough circle. We stamp once, rotate the dough a precise small angle (one tooth-pitch), stamp again, and repeat T times. The dividing head does the precise "rotate-a-small-angle" job, and the form cutter does the "stamp-a-tooth-gap" job.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Module (m)** — the fundamental unit of gear size, in **mm**.
- **Pressure angle (φ)** — standard values are **20°** (preferred, ISO/DIN) and **14.5°** (older AGMA).
- **Number of teeth (T or z)** — integer, dimensionless.
- **Addendum coefficient** = **1.0 m**
- **Dedendum coefficient** = **1.25 m**
- **Tooth clearance** = **0.25 m**
- **Dividing head constant** = **40** (number of holes on the standard indexing plate).
- **Circular pitch (p_c)** = **π m**
- **Tooth thickness on pitch circle** = **1.5708 m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 40?** The standard dividing head has a worm gear ratio of **40:1**, meaning **40 turns of the index crank = 1 full revolution of the workpiece spindle**. This number "40" is the *cornerstone* of every indexing calculation in the KTU lab exam.

### 1.4 Equipment & Tooling Overview

| S.No | Item | Role in the Experiment |
|------|------|------------------------|
| 1 | Horizontal milling machine | Provides spindle rotation to drive the cutter |
| 2 | Dividing head (BS-0 type) | Holds and rotates the gear blank in precise angular steps |
| 3 | Tailstock | Supports the free end of the mandrel |
| 4 | Indexing plates (with hole circles: 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37) | Used in compound indexing |
| 5 | Spur gear form cutter (set of 8) | Cuts the involute profile of a tooth space |
| 6 | Cutter arbour | Holds the form cutter on the machine spindle |
| 7 | Mandrel (between centers) | Mounts the gear blank between dividing head & tailstock |
| 8 | Gear blank (mild steel / aluminium) | The workpiece to be cut |
| 9 | Vernier caliper / Gear tooth vernier | For measurement and verification |

### 1.5 Cutter Selection (Form Cutter Set)

Form-relieved cutters are supplied in **sets of 8**, each cutter being sized for a *range* of teeth. The selection is based on the **number of teeth (T)** to be cut:

| Cutter No. | Range of Teeth |
|------------|----------------|
| 1 | 135 – ∞ (rack) |
| 2 | 55 – 134 |
| 3 | 35 – 54 |
| 4 | 26 – 34 |
| 5 | 21 – 25 |
| 6 | 17 – 20 |
| 7 | 14 – 16 |
| 8 | 12 – 13 |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory tip:** A *smaller* cutter number cuts a gear with a *larger* number of teeth. The cutter profile matches the *conjugate rack* profile, and the involute curvature flattens as T increases.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why the Form Cutter Works

A spur gear tooth has an **involute curve** as its flank. A standard spur gear has the property that any two conjugate gears mesh correctly if they share the **same module (m)** and **same pressure angle (φ)**. The form cutter is manufactured with a profile that matches the **conjugate rack tooth profile** (a straight-flanked trapezoidal shape). When this rack is "rolled" around the pitch circle of the gear blank, it generates the involute flanks.

Since milling a true generating motion is impractical on a plain mill, we use an **approximation**: a cutter whose profile matches the **average tooth shape for a given tooth range**. Eight cutters in the set cover the entire practical range (T = 12 to rack).

### 2.2 Gear Geometry — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Define the Module (m).**
The module is the *fundamental* design parameter chosen first. It is essentially the *size* of the tooth:
$$m = \frac{D}{T}$$
where D = Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD) and T = Number of Teeth.

**Step 2 — Compute Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD).**
$$D = m \times T$$

**Step 3 — Addendum (a) and Dedendum (b).**
- Addendum = the radial height of the tooth *above* the pitch circle.
- Dedendum = the radial depth of the tooth *below* the pitch circle (into the root).
$$a = 1.0 \, m \qquad b = 1.25 \, m$$
The extra 0.25 m in dedendum is the **clearance** (radial gap at the root).

**Step 4 — Outside (Addendum) Diameter and Root Diameter.**
$$D_o = D + 2a = m(T + 2)$$
$$D_r = D - 2b = m(T - 2.5)$$

**Step 5 — Circular Pitch (p_c) and Tooth Thickness.**
$$p_c = \pi \, m$$
$$\text{Tooth thickness at PCD} = \frac{p_c}{2} = 1.5708 \, m$$

**Step 6 — Whole Depth and Working Depth.**
$$h_w = 2.25 \, m \quad \text{(whole depth)}$$
$$h_{wk} = 2.0 \, m \quad \text{(working depth)}$$

### 2.3 Why We Use a Dividing Head — The Indexing Principle

The milling cutter can only cut **one tooth space at a time**. After each cut, the blank must rotate by exactly:

$$\text{Angular advance per tooth} = \frac{360°}{T} = \frac{1}{T} \text{ revolution}$$

The dividing head converts this fractional revolution into **whole turns + a fraction of a turn** of the index crank, using the standard ratio **40:1**:

$$\text{Crank rotation} = \frac{40}{T} \text{ revolutions}$$

If this gives a whole number → **Simple Indexing** is possible.
If not → **Compound indexing** with two change gears is required (very rare for T = 12 to 60).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `|` is replaced by `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbol | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|--------|------|
| 1 | Module | $m = D \div T$ | m | mm |
| 2 | Pitch Circle Diameter | $D = m \times T$ | D | mm |
| 3 | Circular Pitch | $p_c = \pi \times m$ | $p_c$ | mm |
| 4 | Diametral Pitch (English) | $P = T \div D$ | P | teeth/inch |
| 5 | Addendum | $a = 1.0 \times m$ | a | mm |
| 6 | Dedendum | $b = 1.25 \times m$ | b | mm |
| 7 | Tooth thickness (at PCD) | $t = 1.5708 \times m$ | t | mm |
| 8 | Outside diameter | $D_o = m(T + 2)$ | $D_o$ | mm |
| 9 | Root diameter | $D_r = m(T - 2.5)$ | $D_r$ | mm |
| 10 | Whole depth | $h_w = 2.25 \times m$ | $h_w$ | mm |
| 11 | Working depth | $h_{wk} = 2.0 \times m$ | $h_{wk}$ | mm |
| 12 | Clearance | $c = 0.25 \times m$ | c | mm |
| 13 | Center distance (2 mating gears) | $C = m(T_1 + T_2) \div 2$ | C | mm |
| 14 | Index crank rotation | $N = 40 \div T$ | N | rev |
| 15 | Simple indexing hole count | $H = 40 \div T$ holes / turn | H | — |
| 16 | Tooth space width | $= p_c \div 2$ | — | mm |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spur gear cutting on a milling machine is widely used in:

- **Tool room production** of replacement gears.
- **Maintenance workshops** for one-off or low-volume gears.
- **Prototype development** before mass-production via hobbing or shaping.
- **Educational labs** (this very experiment) for teaching gear geometry, indexing, and milling operations.
- **Custom gears** in jigs, fixtures, conveyors, and small machine tools.

> [!NOTE]
> In industry, for volume production, **gear hobbing** is preferred (continuous generating motion). Form-cutting on a mill is **only suitable for low-volume / prototype / tool-room work**, and the tooth profile is an *approximation* (not a true involute) because the cutter is shaped for a *range* of teeth.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Module, PCD, and Tooth Count

### Given
- A spur gear with **T teeth** of **module m**.

### To Derive
- The expression for the Pitch Circle Diameter D.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** The module is *defined* as the ratio of the PCD to the number of teeth.

$$
m \;=\; \frac{D}{T}
$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by T to isolate D.

$$
D \;=\; m \times T
$$

**Step 3 (Sanity check with units):** Since m is in mm, D comes out in mm, and T is dimensionless — units are consistent.

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** If m = 2 mm and T = 30, then

$$
D \;=\; 2 \times 30 \;=\; 60 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Outside Diameter from Addendum Geometry

### Given
- The addendum extends *above* the pitch circle by a distance **a = 1.0 m**.

### To Derive
- The outside (tip) diameter $D_o$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** The radius of the outside circle is the PCD radius **plus** the addendum.

$$
R_o \;=\; \frac{D}{2} + a
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $a = 1.0 \, m$ and $D = m \, T$.

$$
R_o \;=\; \frac{m \, T}{2} + m \;=\; m \left( \frac{T}{2} + 1 \right) \;=\; m \left( \frac{T + 2}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 2 to get the outside diameter.

$$
D_o \;=\; 2 R_o \;=\; m \, (T + 2)
$$

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** For m = 2 mm, T = 30,

$$
D_o \;=\; 2 \times (30 + 2) \;=\; 64 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Root Diameter from Dedendum Geometry

### Given
- The dedendum extends *below* the pitch circle by **b = 1.25 m**.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** Root radius = PCD radius − Dedendum.

$$
R_r \;=\; \frac{D}{2} - b
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $b = 1.25 \, m$ and $D = m \, T$.

$$
R_r \;=\; \frac{m \, T}{2} - 1.25 \, m \;=\; m \left( \frac{T}{2} - 1.25 \right) \;=\; m \left( \frac{T - 2.5}{2} \right)
$$

**Step 3:** Multiply by 2.

$$
D_r \;=\; m \, (T - 2.5)
$$

**Step 4 (Numerical Example):** For m = 2 mm, T = 30,

$$
D_r \;=\; 2 \times (30 - 2.5) \;=\; 55 \text{ mm}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Indexing Calculation (Simple Indexing)

### Given
- A gear with **T = 60** teeth to be cut.
- Dividing head worm ratio = **40:1**.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1:** The blank must rotate by **1/T** of a revolution per tooth.

$$
\text{Workpiece rotation per index} \;=\; \frac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 2:** The index crank must rotate **40 times faster** than the workpiece (because of the 40:1 worm).

$$
N \;=\; 40 \times \frac{1}{T} \;=\; \frac{40}{T}
$$

**Step 3:** For T = 60,

$$
N \;=\; \frac{40}{60} \;=\; \frac{2}{3} \text{ revolution}
$$

**Step 4:** This means **2 full turns + 2/3 of the next turn**. On a 40-hole plate, 2/3 of a turn corresponds to:

$$
\frac{2}{3} \times 40 \;=\; 26.67 \text{ holes}
$$

Since 26.67 is **not an integer**, simple indexing fails for T = 60. We must then use **compound indexing** (not required in this experiment since the KTU typical values are T = 20, 25, 30, 40, 50).

**Step 5 (For T = 30, a typical KTU value):**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{40}{30} \;=\; \frac{4}{3} \;=\; 1 \tfrac{1}{3}
$$

This is **1 full turn + 1/3 of the next turn**. On a 40-hole plate:

$$
\frac{1}{3} \times 40 \;=\; 13.33 \text{ holes} \quad \text{(also not an integer)}
$$

Hmm — let us reconsider. For T = 30, simple indexing is *not* directly possible on the 40-hole plate. A **27-hole circle** with 9 holes per turn is often used:

$$
\frac{1}{3} \times 27 \;=\; 9 \text{ holes} \quad \checkmark
$$

So we use the **27-hole plate, 9 holes per index, with 1 full turn + 9 holes on the 27 circle**. This is a standard KTU lab indexing case.

**Step 6 (For T = 40, a clean case):**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{40}{40} \;=\; 1 \text{ turn, no fraction}
$$

So for T = 40, the index crank rotates **1 full turn between every two teeth** — no hole plate movement needed (only a full turn is made).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Problem (Full KTU-Style)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A spur gear is to be cut on a milling machine with the following specifications: Number of teeth T = 25, module m = 3 mm, pressure angle φ = 20°. Calculate (a) Pitch Circle Diameter, (b) Addendum, (c) Dedendum, (d) Outside Diameter, (e) Root Diameter, (f) Circular Pitch, (g) Tooth Thickness, (h) Whole Depth, and (i) the indexing movement using simple indexing on a 40-hole plate.

### Solution

**(a) Pitch Circle Diameter**

$$
D \;=\; m \times T \;=\; 3 \times 25 \;=\; 75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(b) Addendum**

$$
a \;=\; 1.0 \times m \;=\; 1.0 \times 3 \;=\; 3 \text{ mm}
$$

**(c) Dedendum**

$$
b \;=\; 1.25 \times m \;=\; 1.25 \times 3 \;=\; 3.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(d) Outside Diameter**

$$
D_o \;=\; m \, (T + 2) \;=\; 3 \times (25 + 2) \;=\; 81 \text{ mm}
$$

**(e) Root Diameter**

$$
D_r \;=\; m \, (T - 2.5) \;=\; 3 \times (25 - 2.5) \;=\; 67.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(f) Circular Pitch**

$$
p_c \;=\; \pi \times m \;=\; \pi \times 3 \;\approx\; 9.4248 \text{ mm}
$$

**(g) Tooth Thickness (at PCD)**

$$
t \;=\; 1.5708 \times m \;=\; 1.5708 \times 3 \;\approx\; 4.7124 \text{ mm}
$$

**(h) Whole Depth**

$$
h_w \;=\; 2.25 \times m \;=\; 2.25 \times 3 \;=\; 6.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(i) Simple Indexing**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{40}{T} \;=\; \frac{40}{25} \;=\; 1.6 \text{ revolutions}
$$

This is **1 full turn + 0.6 of a turn**. On the 40-hole plate, 0.6 turn = 0.6 × 40 = 24 holes. But 24 must be *equally divisible* on the 40-hole circle (it is, since 24/40 simplifies to 3/5). So the standard KTU lab procedure would use the 40-hole plate and 24 holes per index, with 1 full turn + 24 holes.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's note on T = 25:** The fraction 40/25 = 8/5, meaning 1 full turn + 3/5 turn = 24 holes on the 40-hole plate. This is a clean simple-indexing case, very commonly asked in KTU exams.

## 3.6 Setting the Cutter Height (Centre to Pitch Circle)

The cutter is set with its **pitch line (mid-height of tooth space)** exactly at the **height of the pitch circle** of the blank. The depth to which the cutter is fed in the Z direction is:

$$
\text{Cutter depth setting} \;=\; \text{whole depth} \;=\; h_w \;=\; 2.25 \, m
$$

The blank is positioned vertically so that the cutter tip aligns with the **root circle** and the cutter body rides at the pitch circle level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU marking tip:** Many students forget to subtract the small **swivel clearance** when the blank is on a vertical mandrel. The blank's centre must be set such that the cutter centre is exactly at the **pitch-circle radius height** of the blank, not the *root* circle. The depth dial is then set to give **whole depth (2.25 m)** feed.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Spur-Gear Cutting Setup

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional interaction** between the milling machine, the dividing head, the workpiece, the cutter, and the indexing mechanism.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Milling Machine Spindle] -- drives --> B[Gear Form Cutter]
    B -- cuts tooth space --> C[Spur Gear Blank]
    C -- mounted on --> D[Workpiece Mandrel]
    D -- held between --> E[Dividing Head Spindle]
    D -- supported at far end --> F[Tailstock Centre]
    E -- rotated by --> G[Worm Wheel 40 teeth]
    G -- driven by --> H[Worm 1 start]
    H -- turned by --> I[Index Crank]
    I -- indexed on --> J[Index Plate 40 holes]
    J -- holds crank at --> K[Index Pin]
    K -- releases and re-engages --> I
    L[Vertical Slide] -- raises blank into --> B
    M[Table Feed] -- controls depth of cut --> L
    C -- measured by --> N[Gear Tooth Vernier]
    C -- inspected by --> O[Profile Projector]
```

**Interpretation of the block diagram:**
- The **spindle** rotates the **form cutter** (B).
- The cutter is fed into the **gear blank** (C) which is mounted on a **mandrel** (D).
- The mandrel is held between the **dividing head spindle** (E) and the **tailstock** (F).
- After each cut, the **index crank** (I) is rotated by the calculated number of turns/holes, which rotates the worm (H) and the 40-tooth worm wheel (G), turning the dividing head spindle (E) and hence the blank (C) by exactly 1/T of a revolution.
- The **index pin** (K) is withdrawn, the crank rotated, and re-engaged in the next hole of the **index plate** (J) before the next cut.
- The **vertical slide** (L) raises the blank into the rotating cutter to give the correct depth of cut (2.25 m).

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Start: Read Gear Specification] --> P1[Calculate Gear Geometry]
    P1 --> P2[Select Correct Cutter No from Set of 8]
    P2 --> P3[Mount Cutter on Arbor and Set Centre Height]
    P3 --> P4[Mount Blank on Mandrel Between Centers]
    P4 --> P5[Calculate Indexing Movement 40 over T]
    P5 --> P6[Choose Correct Hole Circle on Index Plate]
    P6 --> P7[Set Cutter to Pitch Circle Height of Blank]
    P7 --> P8[Engage Half-Nuts and Take Trial Cut to Full Depth]
    P8 --> P9[Index to Next Tooth Space]
    P9 --> P10{All Teeth Cut?}
    P10 -- No --> P9
    P10 -- Yes --> P11[Retract Cutter and Remove Blank]
    P11 --> P12[Measure Tooth Thickness and Profile]
    P12 --> P13[Verify Addendum Diameter and Root Diameter]
    P13 --> P14[Record Observations and Tabulate Results]
    P14 --> P15[End]
```

**Key decision nodes in the process flow:**
- **P5 → P6:** The choice of *which* hole circle to use depends on whether 40/T yields a fraction that simplifies to an integer on any of the standard hole circles (15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 40).
- **P10:** After each cut, the operator decides whether to continue or finish — the loop is the **indexing cycle**.

## 4.3 Cutter Profile Mapping

The 8-cutter set maps to the following selection logic:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Number of Teeth T] --> B{T range}
    B -- 12 to 13 --> C1[Cutter No 8]
    B -- 14 to 16 --> C2[Cutter No 7]
    B -- 17 to 20 --> C3[Cutter No 6]
    B -- 21 to 25 --> C4[Cutter No 5]
    B -- 26 to 34 --> C5[Cutter No 4]
    B -- 35 to 54 --> C6[Cutter No 3]
    B -- 55 to 134 --> C7[Cutter No 2]
    B -- 135 to infinity --> C8[Cutter No 1 Rack]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical artefact reminder:** A *physical* gear-tooth profile diagram (involute curve, base circle, addendum circle, dedendum circle, pressure line) cannot be accurately drawn in Mermaid. For the lab record, students must hand-draw this on graph paper with the involute curve constructed using the standard *string-and-pencil* method, marking the **base circle, pitch circle, addendum circle, and dedendum circle**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Module" of a spur gear. How is it different from circular pitch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **module (m)** of a spur gear is defined as the ratio of the pitch circle diameter (D) to the number of teeth (T), i.e. **m = D/T**. It is the fundamental unit of gear size and is measured in **mm**. Two gears will mesh only if they have the *same module*.

The **circular pitch (p_c)** is the *arc length* measured along the pitch circle between corresponding points on adjacent teeth, i.e. **p_c = π m**. It is measured in mm of arc length.

**Key difference:** Module is a *linear* measure of tooth size; circular pitch is the *arc* measure. They are related by **p_c = π m**.

*(Marking scheme: 1 mark definition of module, 1 mark definition of circular pitch, 1 mark for the relation p_c = π m and statement of difference.)*

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why is a "set of 8 form cutters" used for cutting spur gears, and how is the cutter selected for a given gear?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *single* involute profile would change continuously with the number of teeth (because the curvature of the involute depends on T). To avoid making a different cutter for every possible tooth count, manufacturers supply a **set of 8 form cutters**, each cutter being a compromise profile that is accurate for a *range* of tooth counts. This drastically reduces cost and inventory while keeping the profile error negligible.

**Selection is based on the number of teeth** to be cut: e.g. cutter No. 5 is used for T = 21 to 25, cutter No. 6 for T = 17 to 20, etc. The chosen cutter is mounted on the milling machine arbour.

*(Marking scheme: 2 marks for the rationale (profile variation with T + compromise to reduce inventory), 1 mark for the selection rule.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** Most students lose marks in this experiment for **three common reasons:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dividing head constant of 40** — they index by 1/T instead of 40/T. Result: every indexing answer is wrong by a factor of 40.
> 2. **Confusing addendum and dedendum** — addendum is *above* the pitch circle (1 m), dedendum is *below* (1.25 m). Students often swap the coefficients.
> 3. **Not justifying the choice of hole circle** — they state the hole count but forget to mention *which* hole plate (15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37) is being used. Examiners *will* deduct a mark for this.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A spur gear is to be manufactured on a horizontal milling machine with the following specifications:
- Number of teeth T = 30
- Module m = 2.5 mm
- Pressure angle φ = 20°

**(a)** Calculate all the standard gear tooth dimensions: pitch circle diameter, addendum, dedendum, outside diameter, root diameter, circular pitch, tooth thickness at the pitch circle, and whole depth. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the indexing movement required using a 40-hole index plate, and explain the experimental procedure for cutting the gear. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

##### Part (a) — Gear Geometry Calculations (7 marks)

**(i) Pitch Circle Diameter (PCD)**

$$
D \;=\; m \times T \;=\; 2.5 \times 30 \;=\; 75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ii) Addendum**

$$
a \;=\; 1.0 \times m \;=\; 1.0 \times 2.5 \;=\; 2.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Dedendum**

$$
b \;=\; 1.25 \times m \;=\; 1.25 \times 2.5 \;=\; 3.125 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iv) Outside Diameter**

$$
D_o \;=\; m \, (T + 2) \;=\; 2.5 \times (30 + 2) \;=\; 2.5 \times 32 \;=\; 80 \text{ mm}
$$

**(v) Root Diameter**

$$
D_r \;=\; m \, (T - 2.5) \;=\; 2.5 \times (30 - 2.5) \;=\; 2.5 \times 27.5 \;=\; 68.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(vi) Circular Pitch**

$$
p_c \;=\; \pi \times m \;=\; \pi \times 2.5 \;\approx\; 7.854 \text{ mm}
$$

**(vii) Tooth Thickness at PCD**

$$
t \;=\; 1.5708 \times m \;=\; 1.5708 \times 2.5 \;\approx\; 3.927 \text{ mm}
$$

**(viii) Whole Depth**

$$
h_w \;=\; 2.25 \times m \;=\; 2.25 \times 2.5 \;=\; 5.625 \text{ mm}
$$

##### Part (b) — Indexing and Procedure (7 marks)

**Indexing Calculation:**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{40}{T} \;=\; \frac{40}{30} \;=\; \frac{4}{3} \;=\; 1 \tfrac{1}{3} \text{ revolutions}
$$

This means **1 full turn + 1/3 of the next turn** of the index crank. On the 27-hole plate:

$$
\frac{1}{3} \times 27 \;=\; 9 \text{ holes}
$$

So the indexing is: **1 full turn + 9 holes on the 27-hole circle** (3 marks for the calculation and plate choice).

**Cutter Selection:** T = 30 falls in the range 26–34 → **Cutter No. 4** from the set of 8 (1 mark).

**Experimental Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Mount the **form cutter No. 4** on the milling machine arbour and set the cutter centre at the correct height (i.e. at the pitch-circle radius of the blank).
2. Mount the **gear blank** on the mandrel between the dividing head spindle and the tailstock centre.
3. Calculate the indexing movement and identify the correct **hole circle** (27-hole plate, 9 holes).
4. Bring the blank close to the cutter, then using the **table vertical feed**, raise the blank into the rotating cutter to a depth of **whole depth = 5.625 mm** (set on the depth dial).
5. Withdraw the cutter, **withdraw the index pin**, rotate the crank through 1 full turn + 9 holes, re-engage the pin, and bring the blank into the cutter for the next tooth space.
6. Repeat the cycle until all 30 teeth are cut.
7. Remove the gear, measure **outside diameter, root diameter, tooth thickness** with a gear-tooth vernier, and tabulate the results.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Alternative Choice

A spur gear is to be cut on a horizontal milling machine with the following specifications:
- Pitch Circle Diameter D = 120 mm
- Module m = 3 mm
- Pressure angle φ = 20°

**(a)** Calculate the number of teeth and all standard tooth dimensions (addendum, dedendum, outside diameter, root diameter, circular pitch, tooth thickness, whole depth). Also state which form cutter from the 8-cutter set should be used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the indexing movement using a 40-hole plate and briefly explain the difference between **simple indexing** and **compound indexing**. When is each used? **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

##### Part (a) — Gear Geometry (7 marks)

**(i) Number of Teeth**

$$
T \;=\; \frac{D}{m} \;=\; \frac{120}{3} \;=\; 40 \text{ teeth}
$$

**(ii) Addendum**

$$
a \;=\; 1.0 \times 3 \;=\; 3 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Dedendum**

$$
b \;=\; 1.25 \times 3 \;=\; 3.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iv) Outside Diameter**

$$
D_o \;=\; m \, (T + 2) \;=\; 3 \times 42 \;=\; 126 \text{ mm}
$$

**(v) Root Diameter**

$$
D_r \;=\; m \, (T - 2.5) \;=\; 3 \times 37.5 \;=\; 112.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**(vi) Circular Pitch**

$$
p_c \;=\; \pi \times 3 \;\approx\; 9.4248 \text{ mm}
$$

**(vii) Tooth Thickness at PCD**

$$
t \;=\; 1.5708 \times 3 \;\approx\; 4.7124 \text{ mm}
$$

**(viii) Whole Depth**

$$
h_w \;=\; 2.25 \times 3 \;=\; 6.75 \text{ mm}
$$

**(ix) Cutter Selection:** T = 40 falls in the range 35–54 → **Cutter No. 3** from the 8-cutter set.

##### Part (b) — Indexing (7 marks)

**Indexing Calculation:**

$$
N \;=\; \frac{40}{T} \;=\; \frac{40}{40} \;=\; 1 \text{ revolution exactly}
$$

So the index crank is rotated by **exactly 1 full turn** between successive teeth. **No hole-circle movement is required** — the crank is simply rotated through one full revolution (3 marks for the calculation).

**Simple vs Compound Indexing (4 marks):**

| Feature | Simple Indexing | Compound Indexing |
|---------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Crank rotation per index | $40 \div T$ | Two-stage gear ratio |
| Hole plate required | Yes (if 40/T is not an integer) | Two plates simultaneously |
| Used when | $40 \div T$ gives an integer (after simplifying to a hole plate) | $40 \div T$ is prime or non-divisible |
| Accuracy | High | Slightly less (compound gear train backlash) |
| Setup time | Quick | Slow |
| Example | T = 20 → 2 turns; T = 40 → 1 turn | T = 60, 73, 91, 127, etc. |

**Simple indexing** uses a *single* hole plate and a *single* crank rotation per index step. It works when 40/T, when reduced to lowest terms, has a denominator that matches the hole count on one of the standard index plates (15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 40).

**Compound indexing** uses *two* change gears between the crank and the worm, allowing rotation through a *fractional* amount that cannot be obtained by any single hole plate. It is used for prime-numbered divisions like 73, 91, 127, etc. (1 mark each for the four key points of difference.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — Memorize these 12 facts:**

- ☐ **Module m = D / T** — the *fundamental* gear size parameter, in **mm**.
- ☐ **Pitch Circle Diameter D = m × T** — the imaginary circle on which two gears effectively roll.
- ☐ **Addendum a = 1.0 m** (above PCD); **Dedendum b = 1.25 m** (below PCD).
- ☐ **Outside diameter D_o = m(T + 2)**; **Root diameter D_r = m(T − 2.5)**.
- ☐ **Circular pitch p_c = π m**; **Tooth thickness = 1.5708 m** (= p_c/2).
- ☐ **Whole depth h_w = 2.25 m** — the *depth of cut* set on the milling machine.
- ☐ **Dividing head worm ratio is 40:1** — therefore **index crank turns = 40 / T**.
- ☐ **Cutter selection from the 8-cutter set** is based on the *number of teeth* (T).
- ☐ **Cutter No. 4** is used for **T = 26 to 34**; **Cutter No. 3** for T = 35 to 54; etc.
- ☐ **Pressure angle standard is 20°** (ISO) — affects the cutter profile but not the indexing math.
- ☐ **Simple indexing** when 40/T simplifies to a hole count on a standard index plate; **compound indexing** otherwise.
- ☐ **Always end the calculation with verification** — check that tooth thickness × 2 ≈ circular pitch (1.5708 × 2 = 3.1416 ≈ π) and that D_o − D_r ≈ 4.5 m (= 2a + 2b).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lab Viva Quick-Fire Q&A:**
> - *Q: Why is a horizontal (plain) milling machine used, not a vertical one?*
> - A: The form cutter has a relatively large diameter and the gear blank is large; a horizontal spindle with an arbour gives better rigidity and clearance for the cutter.
> - *Q: What is the function of the worm gear in the dividing head?*
> - A: It steps down the crank's rotation by 40:1, providing precise small angular movements of the workpiece.
> - *Q: Why 8 cutters and not 1?*
> - A: Because the involute profile changes with tooth count; 8 cutters cover the practical range economically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machining a Block on Shaper Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Machining a Block on Shaper Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Aim of the Experiment
To study the constructional features and working principle of a **Shaper Machine**, and to perform a **flat surface machining operation** on a mild steel / cast iron block by varying the depth of cut, feed, and cutting speed, followed by evaluation of **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** and **machining time**.

## 1.2 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shaper Machine:** A shaper is a reciprocating-type machine tool that removes metal from a workpiece by generating a **linear relative motion** between a *single-point cutting tool* held in the *ram* and the *workpiece* mounted on the *table*. The cutting action occurs only during the **forward (cutting) stroke** of the ram, while the return stroke is idle (non-cutting).

Unlike a lathe (rotary) or a milling machine (rotary + linear), a shaper produces **straight, flat, or contoured surfaces** using purely linear, reciprocating kinematics governed by a **Quick-Return Mechanism (QRM)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Wood Sawsaw"

Imagine a hand saw cutting a wooden log:
- The carpenter pushes the saw **forward** — wood is cut ✂️
- The carpenter pulls the saw **backward** — saw returns idle 🚫
- The log (workpiece) advances slightly sideways after every stroke so a fresh strip of wood is presented to the blade.

A shaper is essentially the **industrial, mechanized cousin** of this hand-saw concept — except that here the *tool moves* (not the workpiece), and the kinematic linkage ensures the **return stroke is faster** than the cutting stroke (typically 2 : 1 ratio) to save time.

## 1.4 Standard Specifications & Physical Parameters

| Parameter | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Maximum Stroke Length** | 250 – 900 | mm |
| **Number of Strokes per Minute (N)** | 8 – 150 | strokes/min |
| **Cross-Slide Travel** | 200 – 400 | mm |
| **Table Size (L × W)** | 350 × 250 to 1200 × 900 | mm |
| **Motor Power** | 1.5 – 7.5 | kW |
| **Feed per Stroke (f)** | 0.05 – 3.0 | mm/stroke |
| **Depth of Cut (d)** | 0.5 – 12 | mm |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)** is defined as:
> 
> $$\text{QRR} = \frac{T_{return}}{T_{cutting}} = \frac{\text{Return Stroke Time}}{\text{Cutting Stroke Time}}$$
> 
> Typical QRR lies between **1.5 : 1 and 2 : 1**, meaning the return stroke is 1.5 to 2 times faster than the cutting stroke.

## 1.5 Applications of Shaper
- Machining **flat surfaces, horizontal, vertical, and inclined planes**
- Producing **slots, keyways, dovetails, and T-slots**
- Cutting **external and internal gears** (rare, in gear shapers)
- Surface contouring using **form tools**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Features — Main Parts

A shaper machine is broadly divided into **5 functional zones**:

### (a) Drive Zone
- **Electric Motor** → primary rotary power source
- **Gear Box** → stepped/cone pulleys to vary spindle speed (N)
- **Bull Gear** → large gear on the crank disc shaft

### (b) Quick-Return Mechanism (QRM)
The most commonly used is the **Crank and Slotted Lever** mechanism:
- **Crank Disc (Bull Wheel)** with an eccentric crank pin
- **Slotted Lever (Whitworth Lever)** pivoted at the column
- The crank pin slides inside the slot, converting rotation → oscillation

### (c) Ram Assembly
- **Ram** — heavy slide carrying the tool head; reciprocates linearly
- **Tool Head** — swivel type, can tilt up to ±60° for angled cuts
- **Clapper Box** — hinged block that lifts the tool on the return stroke to prevent rubbing

### (d) Table Assembly
- **Cross Rail** — horizontal support mounted on the column
- **Saddle** — vertical slide for raising/lowering the table
- **Table** — top platform with T-slots to clamp the workpiece
- **Table Feed Mechanism** — automatically advances the table by feed (f) per stroke via a ratchet-and-pawl or hydraulic system

### (e) Bed & Base
- **Base** — heavy cast-iron foundation
- **Column** — vertical pillar housing the QRM and supporting the ram slideways

## 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Crank and Slotted Lever Quick-Return Mechanism** converts uniform rotary motion of the motor into **non-uniform reciprocating motion** of the ram.

**Working Cycle (per revolution of crank):**

1. Crank rotates through angle **α** (cutting stroke) → ram moves forward with **slow, uniform velocity**
2. Crank rotates through angle **β** (return stroke) → ram moves backward with **fast velocity**
3. Note: **α + β = 360°**, and **α > β** → hence the return stroke is **faster** but covers the **same distance**
4. As the ram completes a forward stroke, the **clapper box** lifts the tool slightly, and the table feeds sideways by amount **f**
5. Fresh material is presented for the next forward stroke

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these four equations** — every KTU numerical question on this experiment is built on them.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Cutting Velocity:} \quad V_c = \frac{N \cdot (L + A)}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(2) Material Removal Rate:} \quad \text{MRR} = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N \;\; \text{mm}^3\text{/min} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(3) Number of Strokes Required:} \quad n_{req} = \frac{W}{f} \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(4) Machining Time:} \quad T = \frac{W}{f \cdot N} \;\; \text{minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Where:**
- $N$ = number of strokes per minute (strokes/min)
- $L$ = length of cut or stroke (mm)
- $A$ = approach + over-run clearance (mm), typically **10–25% of L**
- $f$ = feed per stroke (mm/stroke)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $W$ = total width of material to be removed (mm)

### Boundary Conditions & Unit Conversions
- $L$ and $A$ must be in **mm**, $V_c$ is in **m/min**
- MRR is in **mm³/min** — convert to **cm³/min** by dividing by **1000**
- $N$ is read directly from the **machine's stroke-counter dial** or calculated as:

$$
N = \frac{V_c \cdot 1000}{(L + A)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility of MRR formula:** MRR is the universal productivity metric in machining. It is used to compare the efficiency of shaper vs. planer vs. milling for the same job, and to estimate **power requirement** of the cutting process.

## 2.4 Power and Specific Energy Concept

$$
P = \frac{\text{MRR} \times K_c}{60 \times 10^6} \;\; \text{kW}
$$

Where $K_c$ is the **specific cutting force** in N/mm² (≈ 700–2000 N/mm² for mild steel).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)

Consider the **crank and slotted lever** mechanism shown in the functional block architecture (Section 4).

Let:
- $R$ = crank radius (eccentricity of crank pin from center of bull gear)
- $L_S$ = length of slotted lever from pivot $O$ to crank center $C$
- $\alpha$ = angle of crank rotation during **cutting stroke** (forward)
- $\beta$ = angle of crank rotation during **return stroke** (backward)
- $\alpha + \beta = 360°$

**Step 1:** During the cutting stroke, the crank pin travels from the **extreme top** position to the **extreme bottom** position. The angular sweep is:

$$
\alpha = 180° + 2\theta
$$

where $\theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{R}{L_S}\right)$ is the angular offset of the slotted lever from the vertical.

**Step 2:** The corresponding return stroke angle:

$$
\beta = 180° - 2\theta
$$

**Step 3:** Since the crank rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$, the **cutting time** $T_c$ and **return time** $T_r$ are proportional to $\alpha$ and $\beta$:

$$
T_c = \frac{\alpha}{\omega}, \quad T_r = \frac{\beta}{\omega}
$$

**Step 4:** The **Quick-Return Ratio** is:

$$
\boxed{\text{QRR} = \frac{T_c}{T_r} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} = \frac{180° + 2\theta}{180° - 2\theta}}
$$

## 3.2 Detailed Worked-Out Numerical (Solved Example)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical KTU problem type** for this experiment. Practice it thoroughly.

**Problem Statement (KTU 2018 / 2023 Type):**
> A mild steel block of dimensions 150 mm × 80 mm × 30 mm is machined on a shaper. The length of stroke is 200 mm, the approach and over-run together equal 20 mm, the feed is 0.8 mm/stroke, and the depth of cut is 2 mm. If the cutting speed is 12 m/min, calculate:
> 1. Number of strokes per minute
> 2. Material Removal Rate (MRR)
> 3. Time required to machine the 80 mm width

**Given Data:**

$$
L = 200\;\text{mm}, \quad A = 20\;\text{mm}, \quad f = 0.8\;\text{mm/stroke}, \quad d = 2\;\text{mm}, \quad V_c = 12\;\text{m/min}, \quad W = 80\;\text{mm}
$$

### Part (i): Number of Strokes per Minute

Using Formula (1):

$$
V_c = \frac{N \cdot (L + A)}{1000}
$$

$$
12 = \frac{N \cdot (200 + 20)}{1000}
$$

$$
N = \frac{12 \times 1000}{220} = \frac{12000}{220}
$$

$$
\boxed{N = 54.55 \;\text{strokes/min} \approx 55 \;\text{strokes/min}}
$$

### Part (ii): Material Removal Rate

Using Formula (2):

$$
\text{MRR} = L \cdot f \cdot d \cdot N
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 200 \times 0.8 \times 2 \times 54.55
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 320 \times 54.55
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = 17{,}456 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min} \approx 17.46 \;\text{cm}^3\text{/min}}
$$

### Part (iii): Machining Time

Using Formula (4):

$$
T = \frac{W}{f \cdot N}
$$

$$
T = \frac{80}{0.8 \times 54.55}
$$

$$
T = \frac{80}{43.64}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = 1.833 \;\text{minutes} \approx 1\;\text{min}\;50\;\text{sec}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Parameter vs. Effect Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Effect on MRR | Effect on Time | Effect on Surface Finish |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Length of stroke | $L$ | Increases MRR ∝ $L$ | No direct effect | No effect |
| Feed per stroke | $f$ | Increases MRR ∝ $f$ | **Decreases** $T$ ∝ $1/f$ | **Worsens** (coarser) finish |
| Depth of cut | $d$ | Increases MRR ∝ $d$ | No direct effect | No effect |
| Strokes per minute | $N$ | Increases MRR ∝ $N$ | **Decreases** $T$ ∝ $1/N$ | Worsens (rougher) |
| Approach/Over-run | $A$ | No effect on MRR | No effect | No effect |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** To **increase MRR** (productivity), increase $d$ and $N$. To **improve surface finish**, decrease $f$ and $N$ — but at the cost of lower MRR. This is the **classic productivity vs. quality trade-off** in machining.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Shaper Machine (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional interaction** of all major subassemblies of a shaper machine, the direction of power flow, and the kinematic chain from motor to tool.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DRIVE[ZONE A: MAIN DRIVE]
        MOTOR[Electric Motor]
        GEARBOX[Stepped Gearbox]
        BULL[Bull Gear Shaft]
    end

    subgraph QRM[ZONE B: QUICK RETURN MECHANISM]
        CRANK[Crank Disc with Pin]
        SLOTTED[Slotted Lever Whitworth]
        PIVOT[Pivot Point on Column]
    end

    subgraph RAMZONE[ZONE C: RAM ASSEMBLY]
        RAM[Reciprocating Ram]
        TOOLHEAD[Swivel Tool Head]
        CLAPPER[Clapper Box]
        TOOL[Single Point Cutting Tool]
    end

    subgraph TABLEZONE[ZONE D: TABLE ASSEMBLY]
        CROSS[Cross Rail]
        SADDLE[Vertical Saddle]
        TABLE[Work Table with T Slots]
        WORKPIECE[Workpiece Block]
        FEED[Automatic Feed Ratchet]
    end

    subgraph BASEZONE[ZONE E: BED AND COLUMN]
        BASE[Cast Iron Base]
        COLUMN[Vertical Column]
    end

    MOTOR --> GEARBOX --> BULL
    BULL --> CRANK
    CRANK --> SLOTTED
    SLOTTED -. pivots at .-> PIVOT
    SLOTTED --> RAM
    RAM --> TOOLHEAD --> CLAPPER --> TOOL
    BASE --> COLUMN
    COLUMN --> CROSS --> SADDLE --> TABLE --> WORKPIECE
    CROSS --> FEED
    FEED -. feeds .-> TABLE
    TOOL -. cuts forward stroke .-> WORKPIECE
```

## 4.2 Kinematic Chain — Power Flow Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: 3 phase AC Supply] --> B[Electric Motor 1.5 to 7.5 kW]
    B --> C[Gearbox Speed Selection]
    C --> D[Bull Gear Constant Rotation]
    D --> E[Crank Pin Eccentric Motion]
    E --> F[Slotted Lever Oscillation]
    F --> G[Ram Linear Reciprocation]
    G --> H[Tool Forward Stroke Cutting]
    G --> I[Tool Return Stroke Clapper Lifts]
    H --> J[Table Feeds by f mm]
    I --> J
    J --> K{More Width to Cut?}
    K -->|Yes| G
    K -->|No| L[Job Complete]
```

## 4.3 Experiment Setup Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SETUP[LABORATORY EXPERIMENT SETUP]
        direction TB
        SHAPER[Shaper Machine]
        BLOCK[Workpiece Block MS or CI]
        TOOLHOLDER[Tool Holder with HSS Tool]
        DIALGAUGE[Dial Indicator for Alignment]
        VERNIER[Vernier Caliper 0.02 mm]
        RULER[Steel Rule 300 mm]
        STOPWATCH[Digital Stopwatch]
    end

    SHAPER -. holds .-> BLOCK
    SHAPER -. holds .-> TOOLHOLDER
    VERNIER -. measures .-> BLOCK
    RULER -. checks stroke .-> SHAPER
    STOPWATCH -. times .-> SHAPER
    DIALGAUGE -. aligns .-> BLOCK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Physical Drawing:** The schematic above represents the *functional block architecture* of the shaper. A full orthographic / pictorial drawing (front view, top view, side view with section) is expected in the **KTU Lab Record Manual** — students are required to draw the machine diagram with all 12 main parts labelled in the *Lab Record Book*.

## 4.4 Tabular Form — Experimental Data Recording (to be filled in Lab Record)

| Sl. No. | Length of Stroke $L$ (mm) | Feed $f$ (mm/stroke) | Depth $d$ (mm) | Strokes/min $N$ | $V_c$ (m/min) | MRR (cm³/min) | Time $T$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 200 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 55 | 12.1 | 5.5 | 2.91 |
| 2 | 200 | 0.8 | 1.5 | 55 | 12.1 | 13.2 | 1.82 |
| 3 | 200 | 1.0 | 2.0 | 55 | 12.1 | 22.0 | 1.45 |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**State the principle of working of a shaper machine. Why is a quick-return mechanism used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A shaper machine operates on the principle of **reciprocating linear motion** between a single-point cutting tool and a stationary/linearly-fed workpiece, where **cutting occurs only during the forward stroke** of the ram and the return stroke is non-cutting (1 mark).
- A **Quick-Return Mechanism (QRM)** is used to make the return stroke **faster** than the cutting stroke, thus reducing idle time and improving productivity (1 mark).
- The QRR is typically **2 : 1**, and the mechanism is the **crank and slotted lever (Whitworth quick-return)** in crank-type shapers (1 mark).

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any six main parts of a shaper with one-line functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per correct part-function pair):**
1. **Base** — supports the entire machine and absorbs vibrations.
2. **Column** — vertical pillar housing the QRM and ram slideways.
3. **Ram** — heavy reciprocating slide that carries the tool head.
4. **Tool Head** — swivel-type holder that holds the single-point tool and can tilt.
5. **Clapper Box** — hinged block that lifts the tool on the return stroke to prevent rubbing.
6. **Table** — top platform with T-slots for clamping the workpiece; provides cross-feed.
7. **Cross Rail** — horizontal beam supporting the table, allows vertical adjustment.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **working of a crank and slotted lever quick-return mechanism** used in a shaper machine. Derive the expression for the **Quick-Return Ratio (QRR)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cast iron block of 200 mm × 100 mm × 40 mm is machined on a shaper using a cutting speed of 15 m/min, length of stroke 250 mm, approach and over-run together 25 mm, feed 1.0 mm/stroke, and depth of cut 2.5 mm. Calculate:
- (i) Number of strokes per minute
- (ii) Material Removal Rate in cm³/min
- (iii) Machining time for the 100 mm width **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw crank disc, slotted lever, pivot O, crank pin position at extreme top and bottom positions, with $R$ as crank radius and $L_S$ as slotted-lever length.

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- Motor rotates the bull gear at constant angular velocity
- Crank pin $P$ on the disc describes a circle of radius $R$
- Pin $P$ slides in the slot of the slotted lever, which is pivoted at $O$ on the column
- As the disc rotates, the lever oscillates back and forth
- This oscillation is transmitted to the ram, producing reciprocation

**Derivation (2 marks):**

$$
\alpha = 180° + 2\theta, \quad \beta = 180° - 2\theta, \quad \theta = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{R}{L_S}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{QRR} = \frac{\alpha}{\beta} = \frac{180° + 2\theta}{180° - 2\theta}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $L = 250$ mm, $A = 25$ mm, $f = 1.0$ mm/stroke, $d = 2.5$ mm, $V_c = 15$ m/min, $W = 100$ mm

**Part (i) — Number of strokes per minute (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{V_c \times 1000}{L + A} = \frac{15 \times 1000}{275} = 54.55 \;\text{strokes/min} \approx 55
$$

**Part (ii) — MRR in cm³/min (3 marks):**

$$
\text{MRR} = L \times f \times d \times N = 250 \times 1.0 \times 2.5 \times 54.55 = 34{,}094 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = 34.09 \;\text{cm}^3\text{/min}}
$$

**Part (iii) — Machining time (2 marks):**

$$
T = \frac{W}{f \times N} = \frac{100}{1.0 \times 54.55} = 1.833 \;\text{min} \approx 1\;\text{min}\;50\;\text{sec}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[Alternative to Question A]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **different types of single-point cutting tools** used in a shaper, and state the effect of **rake angle** on cutting performance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **constructional features of a shaper machine** and explain the function of the **clapper box mechanism**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

**Tool Types (5 marks):**
1. **Roughing Tool** — large nose radius, zero or negative rake, high depth of cut
2. **Finishing Tool** — small nose radius, positive rake, shallow cut for fine finish
3. **Parting-Off Tool** — narrow, used for slotting
4. **Form Tool** — shaped profile, used for contouring
5. **Side-Face Tool** — cuts on both side and face

**Effect of Rake Angle (2 marks):**
- **Positive Rake** → reduces cutting force, better chip flow, improves surface finish; used for soft materials
- **Negative Rake** → stronger cutting edge, used for hard materials and interrupted cuts
- **Zero Rake** → intermediate behaviour

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (4 marks):** See Section 4.1 of these notes. Clearly label the 5 zones — Drive, QRM, Ram, Table, Bed-Column.

**Clapper Box Explanation (3 marks):**
- The clapper box is a **hinged, spring-loaded block** mounted on the tool head
- During the **forward (cutting) stroke**, the clapper is held down by spring force, allowing the tool to cut
- During the **return (idle) stroke**, the hinged clapper **swings upward and lifts the tool** away from the machined surface
- This prevents the tool flank from rubbing against the freshly cut surface, thus improving **surface finish** and reducing **tool wear**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add $A$ (approach + over-run)** in the cutting speed formula. Most students write $V_c = LN/1000$ instead of $V_c = N(L+A)/1000$. **This costs 1 full mark.**
> 2. **Unit mismatch in MRR** — writing MRR in mm³/min when the question asks for cm³/min (must divide by 1000).
> 3. **Confusing MRR with material removed per stroke** — MRR is per minute, not per stroke.
> 4. **Drawing diagrams without arrowheads or direction labels** — even a perfect block diagram without power-flow arrows loses 1–2 marks in Part B.
> 5. **Not stating the clapper box function explicitly** in Q.B(b) — examiners look for the phrase *"lifts the tool on return stroke"*.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [x] **Shaper** = reciprocating linear cutting tool vs. workpiece, single-point tool
- [x] **Cutting** happens on forward stroke only; return is non-cutting
- [x] **Quick-Return Mechanism (QRM)** = crank and slotted lever (Whitworth); QRR typically 2 : 1
- [x] **QRR formula:** $\text{QRR} = (180° + 2\theta)/(180° - 2\theta)$ where $\theta = \sin^{-1}(R/L_S)$
- [x] **Five zones** of shaper: Drive, QRM, Ram, Table, Bed-Column
- [x] **Clapper box** = lifts tool on return stroke to prevent rubbing and improve finish
- [x] **Cutting speed:** $V_c = N(L+A)/1000$ m/min — **include $A$ always!**
- [x] **MRR:** $L \times f \times d \times N$ mm³/min (convert to cm³/min by /1000)
- [x] **Machining time:** $T = W/(f \times N)$ minutes
- [x] **Tool head** is swivel-type, can tilt up to ±60° for inclined surfaces
- [x] **Table feed** is given as mm per stroke, not per minute
- [x] **Higher $d$ and $N$** → higher MRR (productivity ↑), but surface finish ↓
- [x] **Lower $f$ and $N$** → better surface finish, but MRR ↓ (trade-off)
- [x] **Lab Viva Questions to Prepare:**
  - Difference between shaper and planer?
  - Why is return stroke faster than cutting stroke?
  - What is the function of the clapper box?
  - How is stroke length adjusted on a crank-type shaper?
  - What type of tool material is used (HSS, Carbide)?
  - What is the role of coolant in shaping?

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Final Tip:** For the **Lab End-Semester Practical Exam**, the external examiner will typically (i) ask you to *identify and name* 6–8 parts of the shaper, (ii) ask the *QRM derivation*, and (iii) give you a *numerical on MRR/Machining Time* similar to Q.A(b) above. **Master Section 3.2 of these notes verbatim** — it is the single most-likely numerical that will appear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finishing of a Surface on Surface-Grinding Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Finishing of a Surface on Surface-Grinding Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Surface Grinding** is a precision material removal process in which a rotating abrasive grinding wheel is used as the cutting tool to produce a smooth, flat, and accurate surface on a workpiece. The workpiece is mounted on a worktable that reciprocates linearly (or rotates) beneath the wheel, and material is removed in the form of micro-chips by the abrasive grits on the periphery or face of the wheel.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PCAUL407 – Production Engineering Lab**, the experiment on "Finishing of a Surface on Surface-Grinding Machine" trains students in:
- Identifying the components and types of surface grinders
- Selecting appropriate **grinding wheels** (abrasive type, grit size, grade, structure, bond)
- Setting process parameters (**wheel speed, table feed, depth of cut**)
- Performing the grinding operation safely
- Measuring the resulting **surface roughness (Ra)** and **dimensional accuracy**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Recall Question Expectation):**
> *Surface grinding is an abrasive machining process in which a grinding wheel, rotating at high speed, removes a thin layer of material from the surface of a workpiece to obtain a high degree of surface finish, dimensional accuracy, and flatness, typically within a tolerance of ±0.01 mm and a surface roughness (Ra) of 0.2–1.6 µm.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **smooth a rough wooden plank** using sandpaper. The sandpaper has tiny, hard particles stuck to its surface — these are the *abrasive grits*. As you move the sandpaper back and forth over the plank, the grits scrape off minuscule wood fibers, leaving the surface smoother and flatter.

A **surface grinder** is essentially a **motorized, super-precise industrial version of this sanding action**:
- The **sandpaper** = the **grinding wheel** (wheel of bonded abrasive grains)
- Your **hand motion** = the **worktable reciprocation** (machine-driven feed)
- The **pressure of your hand** = the **depth of cut (down-feed)**
- The **wood shavings** = the **micro-chips** (often glowing as sparks!)
- The **finer the sandpaper** = the **finer the grit size** of the wheel

In short, surface grinding is like "industrial sandpapering on a precision machine" — done with cooling fluid, high wheel speeds, and sub-millimetre control.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities & Standard Metrics (Highlighted in **Bold**)

| Quantity | Typical Value (Steel) |
|---|---|
| **Wheel peripheral speed (V)** | **1800–2500 m/min** (often **2000 m/min**) |
| **Table traverse speed (V_w)** | **5–25 m/min** |
| **Cross-feed per stroke** | **1–5 mm/stroke** |
| **Vertical down-feed (depth of cut)** | **0.005–0.05 mm/pass** |
| **Surface roughness achieved (Ra)** | **0.2–1.6 µm** |
| **Dimensional tolerance** | **±0.01 to ±0.025 mm** |
| **Coolant flow rate** | **10–20 L/min** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (ISO 4287:1997):** Surface roughness in KTU labs is universally reported as **Ra (Arithmetic Mean Roughness)** in **micrometres (µm)** — not RMS. Always use µm in your records.

## 1.4 Classification of Surface Grinding Machines (KTU 2024 Highlight)

Surface grinders are classified based on **two design choices**:

1. **Orientation of the grinding wheel spindle** → Horizontal or Vertical
2. **Motion of the worktable** → Reciprocating (linear) or Rotary

This gives **four canonical types** (the **2×2 matrix** the KTU board loves to ask):

| Type | Spindle | Table | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Horizontal Spindle, Reciprocating Table** | Horizontal | Back-and-forth linear | **Most common** — flat surfaces, dies, gauges |
| **2. Horizontal Spindle, Rotary Table** | Horizontal | Rotating round | Cylindrical/annular surfaces, large lots |
| **3. Vertical Spindle, Reciprocating Table** | Vertical (cup wheel) | Back-and-forth | Heavy stock removal, roughing |
| **4. Vertical Spindle, Rotary Table** | Vertical (cup wheel) | Rotating round | Flat surfaces, large workpieces |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Favourite:** *"Name and sketch the four types of surface grinding machines."* — Be ready to draw Type-1 (horizontal spindle, reciprocating table) in your lab record.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Grinding Process — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The surface grinding process is governed by **three simultaneous motions**, which the student must internalize before doing any calculation:

### Step 1: The Primary Motion — Wheel Rotation
- The **grinding wheel rotates at high speed** about its own axis.
- This is called the **cutting speed (V)**.
- Each abrasive grit on the wheel surface acts as a tiny cutting tool.
- At operating speeds, a grit contacts the workpiece for only **microseconds**, generating extreme **localized heat (~1500°C)**.

### Step 2: The Secondary Motion — Worktable Feed
- The **workpiece is moved** so that fresh material is exposed to the wheel.
- In a **horizontal-spindle reciprocating grinder**, the table moves **linearly back-and-forth** under the wheel.
- The **table traverse speed (V_w)** is typically **5 to 25 m/min**.

### Step 3: The Tertiary Motion — In-Feed (Depth of Cut)
- The **wheel is lowered vertically by a small amount** (the "down-feed" or "in-feed").
- This is the **depth of cut per pass**, usually **0.005 to 0.05 mm**.
- After each table stroke, the wheel is indexed sideways (**cross-feed**) by 1–5 mm, then the next pass begins.

### The "Why" Behind the Three Motions
> Think of it like mowing a lawn: the **blade spins** (cutting), the **mower moves forward** (feed), and you **push it down slightly into the grass** (depth). The grinder does exactly this, but with micrometre-level precision and abrasive grains instead of a blade.

## 2.2 The Grinding Wheel — Anatomy (KTU Mandatory Topic)

A grinding wheel is a **self-sharpening multi-tooth cutting tool** made of abrasive grains held together by a **bond**. It is specified using a **standard marking system** (e.g., **ASA / ISO 525**):

| Code Letter | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Abrasive type** | A = Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃), C = Silicon carbide (SiC), S = Sol gel Al₂O₃, D = Diamond, B = CBN | **A** |
| **B — Grit size** | Mesh number (coarse = low number; fine = high number) | **60** |
| **C — Grade** | Hardness of bond: A (soft) → Z (hard) | **K** (medium) |
| **D — Structure** | Spacing between grains: 1 (dense) → 16 (open) | **5** |
| **E — Bond** | V = Vitrified, B = Resin, R = Rubber, M = Metal | **V** |
| **F — Manufacturer's mark** | Free text | — |

**Example marking:** `A 60 K 5 V` → Aluminium oxide, grit 60 (medium), grade K (medium), structure 5, vitrified bond.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wheel Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU Favourite):**
> - **Hard workpiece (e.g., HSS, tool steel)** → **Aluminium oxide (A)**
> - **Soft, ductile, low-density material (e.g., aluminium, brass, carbide)** → **Silicon carbide (C)**
> - **Fine finish** → **smaller grit number wait — no!** → **Larger grit size number** (e.g., 80, 100)
> - **Roughing** → **coarse grit** (e.g., 36, 46)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the **only ones** the KTU board has historically asked from this experiment. Memorize them as a **single block**.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wheel peripheral speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ = wheel diameter (mm), $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Wheel rpm | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Rearrangement of (1) |
| 3 | Table traverse speed (reciprocating) | $V_w = 2 \cdot L \cdot n_s$ | m/min | $L$ = stroke length (m), $n_s$ = strokes/min |
| 4 | Cross-feed per pass | $f_c$ | mm/stroke | Usually 0.5–0.85 × wheel width |
| 5 | Material Removal Rate (MRR) | $\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d$ | mm³/min | $d$ = down-feed (mm); $V_w$ in **mm/min** here |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate (per pass) | $\text{MRR}_p = L \cdot f_c \cdot d$ | mm³/pass | $L$ in mm, $f_c$ in mm, $d$ in mm |
| 7 | Number of passes | $n = \dfrac{W}{f_c}$ | passes | $W$ = width to be ground (mm) |
| 8 | Total grinding time | $T = \dfrac{n \cdot L}{V_w / 60}$ | minutes | (or $T = n / n_s$ directly) |
| 9 | Power required | $P = \text{MRR} \cdot U_s$ | Watts | $U_s$ = specific energy (J/mm³) |
| 10 | Specific grinding energy | $U_s = \dfrac{P}{\text{MRR}}$ | J/mm³ | Steel ≈ 20–50 J/mm³ |
| 11 | Spark-out time | $T_{so} = \dfrac{L}{V_w}$ | min | One extra no-feed pass to improve finish |
| 12 | Equivalent chip thickness | $h_{eq} = \dfrac{V_w \cdot d}{V}$ | mm | Determines surface finish |
| 13 | Surface roughness (theoretical) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{h_{eq}}{4}$ | mm | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Consistency Trap:** When computing MRR using $V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d$, the table speed $V_w$ **must be in mm/min** (convert from m/min by ×1000). Most students lose marks by mixing m/min and mm/min. KTU examiners explicitly test this.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Surface grinding is **the workhorse of tool rooms and precision manufacturing**:
- **Tool & die making** — finishing die cavities, punches
- **Machine tool beds** — reference flat surfaces (scraping alternative)
- **Gauge manufacture** — surface plates, slip gauges (final finishing)
- **Automotive** — flat surfaces on connecting rods, brake plates
- **Aerospace** — pre-finish for bearing seats
- **Medical implants** — titanium knee/hip joint flats

> Without surface grinding, **sub-1 µm flatness tolerances** required in metrology and semiconductor equipment would be impossible to achieve economically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wheel Peripheral Speed (V)

The peripheral speed of the grinding wheel is the **linear distance traveled by a point on the wheel rim per unit time**.

**Step 1 — Circumference of the wheel:**
The distance covered in one full revolution is the circumference:

$$C = \pi \cdot D$$

where $D$ is the wheel diameter (in **mm**, for our convention).

**Step 2 — Distance per minute:**
If the wheel rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute (rpm), the total linear distance traveled per minute is:

$$\text{Distance/min} = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$$

This has units of mm/min, since $D$ is in mm and $N$ is in 1/min.

**Step 3 — Convert to m/min (standard KTU unit):**
Divide by 1000:

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of units:**

$$\frac{[\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{rev/min}]}{1000} = \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{min}} \cdot \frac{1}{1000} = \text{m/min} \;\; \checkmark$$

> **Engineering insight:** Wheel speed is set by the machine's spindle and is **kept constant** (most surface grinders use a fixed rpm, e.g., 1400 or 1750 rpm). The **wheel diameter decreases** as the wheel wears, so peripheral speed *V* slowly drops with use.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

MRR is the **volume of material removed per unit time**, a fundamental productivity metric.

**Step 1 — Geometry of one pass:**
In a single table stroke of length $L$ (mm) with a cross-feed of $f_c$ (mm) and down-feed $d$ (mm), the volume swept by the wheel is:

$$V_{\text{pass}} = L \cdot f_c \cdot d \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{]}$$

This is a **rectangular slab** of workpiece material removed per pass.

**Step 2 — Time per pass:**
The table moves at $V_w$ (in mm/min). One stroke covers length $L$ (and returns, but only one direction is cutting). The time for **one cutting stroke** is:

$$t_{\text{pass}} = \frac{L}{V_w} \quad \text{[min]}$$

**Step 3 — MRR in mm³/min:**

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{V_{\text{pass}}}{t_{\text{pass}}} = \frac{L \cdot f_c \cdot d}{L / V_w} = V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d$$

$$\boxed{\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/min]}}$$

where $V_w$ is **in mm/min**.

**Step 4 — Convert $V_w$ from m/min (lab convention) to mm/min:**

$$V_w\,[\text{mm/min}] = V_w\,[\text{m/min}] \times 1000$$

So if you are given $V_w = 12$ m/min, use $V_w = 12\,000$ mm/min in the formula.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Number of Passes & Total Grinding Time

**Step 1 — Number of passes:**
Each pass grinds a strip of width $f_c$ (the cross-feed). To cover a total width $W$ of the workpiece surface:

$$n = \frac{W}{f_c} \quad \text{[passes]}$$

(round up to the next integer — you cannot do a fraction of a pass).

**Step 2 — Time per pass (one full reciprocating cycle):**
For a horizontal-spindle reciprocating grinder, **one pass = one forward stroke + one return stroke**. Only the forward stroke cuts. If the strokes per minute are $n_s$:

$$T = \frac{n}{n_s} \quad \text{[minutes]}$$

**Step 3 — Alternative form using $V_w$ and $L$:**

$$T = \frac{n \cdot 2L}{V_w / 60 \times 1000} \quad \text{[minutes]}$$

(The factor 2 accounts for forward + return; $L$ in m, $V_w$ in m/min.)

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Power Required

**Step 1 — Definition of specific energy:**
Specific energy $U_s$ is the energy required to remove a unit volume of material. It is **material-dependent** and represents the resistance to grinding:

$$U_s = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Volume}} = \frac{P}{\text{MRR}} \quad \text{[J/mm}^3\text{]}$$

**Step 2 — Power from MRR and $U_s$:**

$$P = U_s \cdot \text{MRR} \quad \text{[Watts]}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to kW (motor rating convention):**

$$P_{kW} = \frac{P_W}{1000} = \frac{U_s \cdot \text{MRR}}{1000}$$

> **Typical specific energy values (recall-ready):**
> - Mild steel grinding → $U_s \approx 20$–$30$ J/mm³
> - Hardened tool steel → $U_s \approx 35$–$50$ J/mm³
> - Cast iron → $U_s \approx 15$–$25$ J/mm³
> - Aluminium → $U_s \approx 5$–$10$ J/mm³

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "What to Do If They Give You..."

| Given in Question | Goal | Steps |
|---|---|---|
| Wheel diameter $D$, rpm $N$ | Wheel speed $V$ | Apply $V = \pi D N / 1000$ |
| Wheel speed $V$, required $V$ | Required rpm $N$ | $N = 1000V / (\pi D)$ |
| Table traverse $V_w$, cross-feed $f_c$, depth $d$ | MRR | MRR = $V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d$ (convert units!) |
| $W$, $f_c$ | Number of passes | $n = W / f_c$, round up |
| $W$, $f_c$, $L$, $V_w$ | Total time $T$ | $n = W/f_c$, then $T = 2nL / V_w$ (in min) |
| MRR, $U_s$ | Power $P$ | $P = U_s \cdot$ MRR, convert to kW |
| $V_w$, $d$, $V$ | Equivalent chip thickness | $h_{eq} = V_w \cdot d / V$ |
| $h_{eq}$ | Theoretical $R_a$ | $R_a \approx h_{eq} / 4$ |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (Full Step-by-Step — KTU Style)

> **Problem [Model-style KTU 2024]:**
> A horizontal-spindle surface grinder has a wheel of **diameter 250 mm** rotating at **1750 rpm**. The table traverse is set to **12 m/min**, the cross-feed is **2.5 mm/stroke**, and the vertical down-feed is **0.02 mm**. The workpiece is **120 mm × 80 mm**.
> Given specific energy for steel = **30 J/mm³**, calculate:
> (i) Wheel peripheral speed
> (ii) MRR
> (iii) Number of passes to grind the 80 mm width
> (iv) Power required

**Solution:**

**(i) Wheel speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 250 \times 1750}{1000}$$

$$V = \frac{1\,374\,447}{1000} \approx 1374 \;\; \text{m/min}$$

**(ii) MRR:**
Convert table speed to mm/min:

$$V_w = 12 \;\text{m/min} = 12\,000 \;\text{mm/min}$$

$$\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d = 12\,000 \times 2.5 \times 0.02$$

$$\text{MRR} = 600 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iii) Number of passes:**

$$n = \frac{W}{f_c} = \frac{80}{2.5} = 32 \;\text{passes}$$

**(iv) Power required:**

$$P = U_s \cdot \text{MRR} = 30 \times 600 = 18\,000 \;\text{W} = 18 \;\text{kW}$$

> **Final Answers (in KTU answer-book format):**
> (i) $V = 1374$ m/min     (ii) MRR = 600 mm³/min     (iii) $n = 32$ passes     (iv) $P = 18$ kW
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Surface Grinding Process Flow

This diagram maps the **inputs, machine subsystems, process parameters, and outputs** of a surface-grinding operation. (Adapted per the "Mermaid Compilation Safeguard" rule — node IDs are alphanumeric; labels are plain text.)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputWorkpiece[Raw Workpiece Blank] --> mounting[Work Holding Magnetic Chuck or Vise]
    wheelSpec[Wheel Selection Abrasive Grit Grade Bond] --> wheelInstall[Wheel Mounting on Spindle]
    mounting --> machineReady[Set Process Parameters]
    wheelInstall --> machineReady
    paramInputs[Inputs Wheel Speed Table Feed Depth of Cut Cross Feed] --> machineReady
    machineReady --> operation[Grinding Operation Begins]
    operation --> sparkOut[Spark Out Pass No Feed]
    sparkOut --> measurement[Surface Roughness Ra Measurement]
    measurement --> decision{Dimension and Finish OK}
    decision -->|Yes| finish[Finished Workpiece Output]
    decision -->|No| operation
    operation --> coolant[Coolant Flow 10 to 20 L per min]
    coolant --> operation
    operation --> chips[Micro Chips and Heat]
    chips --> swarf[Swarf Collection]
    wheelInstall --> dressOp[Wheel Dressing Truing]
    dressOp --> wheelInstall
```

**How to read this chart:**
- The **top row** = *preparatory inputs* (workpiece, wheel, parameters)
