- The **middle row** = *the actual grinding cycle* (including spark-out, which is the no-feed final pass that improves surface finish)
- The **feedback loop** from `decision` back to `operation` represents **re-grinding if tolerance/finish is not yet met**
- **Coolant** and **swarf** are environmental/auxiliary flows shown as side streams

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Subsystems of a Horizontal-Surface Grinder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DriveSystem[Power and Drive Subsystem]
        motor[Electric Motor 1.5 to 5 kW] --> spindle[Spindle Shaft]
        spindle --> wheel[Grinding Wheel 250 mm]
    end
    subgraph TableSystem[Worktable Subsystem]
        hydraulic[Hydraulic Cylinder] --> table[Reciprocating Table]
        table --> chuck[Magnetic Chuck]
        chuck --> workpiece[Workpiece]
    end
    subgraph FeedSystem[Feed Subsystem]
        handwheel[Cross Feed Handwheel] --> saddle[Saddle Carriage]
        saddle --> column[Column Slide]
        column --> wheelhead[Wheel Head Down Feed]
    end
    subgraph CoolantSystem[Coolant Subsystem]
        tank[Coolant Tank] --> pump[Coolant Pump]
        pump --> nozzle[Nozzle over Grinding Zone]
    end
    tableSystem --> DriveSystem
    FeedSystem --> DriveSystem
    CoolantSystem --> DriveSystem
```

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab viva, the most-asked question is: *"Name the components of a horizontal-spindle reciprocating-table surface grinder."* You should be able to name at least: **base, column, wheel head, spindle, grinding wheel, worktable, magnetic chuck, cross-feed handwheel, vertical down-feed handwheel, hydraulic power pack, coolant system.**

## 4.3 Grinding Wheel Anatomy — Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    core[Central Bore Arbor Hole] --> hub[Hub]
    hub --> body[Abrasive Body Grains and Bond]
    body --> periphery[Periphery Active Cutting Surface]
    body --> face[Side Face Used in Face Grinding]
    periphery --> grits[Abrasive Grits Cutting Edges]
    face --> grits
    body --> pores[Pores Chip Storage and Coolant Channels]
```

**Engineering significance:**
- **Periphery** = used in **horizontal-spindle surface grinding** (Type 1 & 2)
- **Face** = used in **vertical-spindle grinding** with cup wheels (Type 3 & 4)
- **Pores** = critical — they carry away chips and allow coolant to reach the cutting zone, preventing wheel glazing
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> The most common **mark-losing mistakes** in this experiment (as seen in 2022–2024 answer scripts):
> 1. **Unit mix-up in MRR formula** — using $V_w$ in m/min directly. KTU deducts 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mm³/min to cm³/min** if the question asks for the latter.
> 3. **Confusing "cross-feed" and "depth of cut"** — cross-feed is sideways (mm/stroke); depth is vertical (mm/pass). KTU explicitly distinguishes these.
> 4. **Writing "rpm" instead of "rev/min" or "1/min"** in the wheel-speed formula. SI units preferred.
> 5. **No mention of coolant** in the procedure → lose 1 mark on full-mark questions.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define surface grinding. List any four process parameters of a horizontal-spindle surface grinder.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~50 words):**

Surface grinding is an **abrasive machining process** in which a rotating grinding wheel removes a thin layer of material from a flat workpiece surface to achieve high dimensional accuracy and fine surface finish.

**Process parameters:**
1. **Wheel peripheral speed (V)** — typically 1800–2500 m/min
2. **Table traverse speed (V_w)** — 5–25 m/min
3. **Cross-feed per stroke (f_c)** — 1–5 mm
4. **Vertical down-feed / depth of cut (d)** — 0.005–0.05 mm
5. *(bonus)* Spark-out passes and coolant flow rate

*(1 mark for definition, 0.5 each for the four parameters. Total = 3)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the function of a magnetic chuck? Why is coolant used in surface grinding?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Magnetic chuck (1.5 marks):**
A magnetic chuck is a **work-holding device** that uses the force of an electromagnet (or permanent magnet) to clamp ferromagnetic workpieces flat on the machine table. It provides:
- Uniform holding pressure over the entire contact area (no distortion of thin parts)
- Quick loading/unloading → high productivity
- Allows grinding on **multiple sides** without re-clamping

**Coolant in surface grinding (1.5 marks):**
Coolant (usually a water-based emulsion) is used to:
1. **Reduce grinding zone temperature** (otherwise >1000°C, causing thermal damage to workpiece and wheel)
2. **Wash away chips** from the wheel-workpiece interface
3. **Lubricate** the grit-workpiece contact → reduces grit wear
4. **Improve surface finish** by preventing re-welding of chips
5. **Extend wheel life** by preventing glazing

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**A(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **main parts and working principle of a horizontal-spindle reciprocating-table surface grinder**. Mention the function of the magnetic chuck, spindle, and hydraulic table drive. **(7 marks)**

**A(b)** A surface-grinding operation uses a wheel of diameter **300 mm** running at **1440 rpm**. The table traverse is **10 m/min**, cross-feed is **2 mm/stroke**, depth of cut is **0.025 mm/pass**. The workpiece width to be ground is **100 mm**. Calculate:
  (i) Wheel peripheral speed
  (ii) Material removal rate (MRR)
  (iii) Number of passes required
  (iv) Total grinding time (one forward + one return = 2L per pass; assume stroke length $L$ = 150 mm) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution A(a)

**Main parts (block diagram follows):**

| Part | Function |
|---|---|
| **Base** | Heavy cast-iron bed that supports all other components; absorbs vibration |
| **Column** | Vertical pillar carrying the wheel head; provides rigidity |
| **Wheel head** | Houses the spindle, motor, and grinding wheel; can be moved up/down |
| **Spindle** | Rotates the grinding wheel at high rpm (driven by electric motor) |
| **Grinding wheel** | The cutting tool — bonded abrasive grains that remove material |
| **Worktable** | Reciprocates linearly under the wheel; carries the magnetic chuck |
| **Magnetic chuck** | Electromagnetic work-holding device — clamps the workpiece uniformly |
| **Saddle & cross-feed handwheel** | Provides lateral (cross) movement of the table to expose fresh material |
| **Wheel-head down-feed handwheel** | Controls the vertical depth of cut (in-feed) |
| **Hydraulic power pack** | Drives the table reciprocation at controlled, adjustable speeds |
| **Coolant system** | Tank, pump, nozzle — supplies cutting fluid to the grinding zone |

**Working principle (2 marks):**
- The electric motor drives the grinding wheel through the spindle at high rpm.
- The workpiece is clamped on the magnetic chuck mounted on the worktable.
- The hydraulic system moves the table back and forth linearly, passing the workpiece under the rotating wheel.
- At the end of each stroke, the cross-feed mechanism advances the table sideways by a small amount ($f_c$).
- The wheel head is gradually lowered by the vertical feed handwheel to remove material layer-by-layer until the desired dimension and finish are achieved.
- Coolant is sprayed continuously to dissipate heat and wash away chips.

*(Mark split: 2 parts + 2 function + 2 working = 6, plus diagram = 1. Total 7.)*

#### Model Solution A(b)

**Given:**
- $D = 300$ mm, $N = 1440$ rpm
- $V_w = 10$ m/min $= 10\,000$ mm/min
- $f_c = 2$ mm/stroke, $d = 0.025$ mm/pass
- $W = 100$ mm, $L = 150$ mm

**(i) Wheel peripheral speed:**

$$V = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \times 300 \times 1440}{1000} = \frac{1\,357\,168}{1000} \approx 1357 \;\text{m/min}$$

**(ii) MRR:**

$$\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d = 10\,000 \times 2 \times 0.025 = 500 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}$$

**(iii) Number of passes:**

$$n = \frac{W}{f_c} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \;\text{passes}$$

**(iv) Total grinding time:**
Per pass, the table travels a forward stroke (L) and a return stroke (L) = 2L = 300 mm at 10 m/min = 10 000 mm/min.

$$T_{\text{per pass}} = \frac{2L}{V_w} = \frac{300}{10\,000} = 0.03 \;\text{min/pass}$$

$$T_{\text{total}} = 0.03 \times 50 = 1.5 \;\text{minutes}$$

**Final Answers:**
(i) $V = 1357$ m/min     (ii) MRR = 500 mm³/min     (iii) $n = 50$ passes     (iv) $T = 1.5$ min

*(Mark split: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 7)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**B(a)** Explain the **ISO/ASA grinding wheel marking system** with a suitable example. Discuss the criteria for **selection of abrasive type and grit size** for grinding (i) hardened tool steel and (ii) cast iron. **(7 marks)**

**B(b)** In a surface grinding operation on mild steel, the specific energy is **25 J/mm³**. The wheel speed is **1800 m/min**, table speed is **15 m/min**, cross-feed is **3 mm**, and down-feed is **0.02 mm**. Calculate:
  (i) MRR in mm³/s
  (ii) Power consumed (in kW)
  (iii) Equivalent chip thickness $h_{eq}$
  (iv) Theoretical surface roughness $R_a$ **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution B(a)

**Grinding wheel marking (ISO 525 / ASA system):**
A standard grinding wheel is specified by a **six-character code** — *A B C D E F*:

| Position | Meaning | Common values |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Type of abrasive | A = Aluminium oxide, C = Silicon carbide, S = Sol-gel alumina, B = CBN, D = Diamond |
| **B** | Grit size (mesh) | 36 (coarse), 60 (medium), 80/100 (fine) |
| **C** | Grade (hardness of bond) | A (very soft) → Z (very hard); typical: H, J, K (medium) |
| **D** | Structure (grain spacing) | 1 (dense) → 16 (open) |
| **E** | Type of bond | V = Vitrified, B = Resin, R = Rubber, M = Metal |
| **F** | Manufacturer's mark | Optional |

**Example:** `A 60 K 5 V 21` means:
- Abrasive: Aluminium oxide
- Grit size: 60 (medium)
- Grade: K (medium hardness)
- Structure: 5
- Bond: Vitrified

**Selection criteria (4 marks):**

| Workpiece | Abrasive | Grit size | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hardened tool steel (HSS, die steel)** | **Aluminium oxide (A)** — preferably white/single-crystal (WA) or Sol-gel (SG) | Fine (60–80) | Tough abrasive; resists dulling on hard, tough steel; sharp cutting edges |
| **Cast iron** | **Aluminium oxide (A)** OR Silicon carbide (C) | Medium (46–60) | Cast iron produces discontinuous chips; SiC is friable and self-sharpens; grit size depends on finish requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** Use **C (silicon carbide) for non-ferrous soft materials (Al, brass, carbide) and low-tensile-strength ferrous** (cast iron, soft bronze). Use **A (alumina) for high-tensile ferrous** (steel, HSS, die steel).

*(Mark split: 2 marking + 1 example + 4 selection = 7)*

#### Model Solution B(b)

**Given:**
- $U_s = 25$ J/mm³
- $V = 1800$ m/min
- $V_w = 15$ m/min $= 15\,000$ mm/min
- $f_c = 3$ mm, $d = 0.02$ mm

**(i) MRR in mm³/s:**

$$\text{MRR} = V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d = 15\,000 \times 3 \times 0.02 = 900 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/min}$$

Convert to mm³/s:

$$\text{MRR} = \frac{900}{60} = 15 \;\text{mm}^3\text{/s}$$

**(ii) Power consumed:**

$$P = U_s \cdot \text{MRR (in mm}^3\text{/s)} = 25 \times 15 = 375 \;\text{W} = 0.375 \;\text{kW}$$

> Note: If MRR is in **mm³/min**, the result will be in W directly (no /60 needed). Many students make this conversion error.

**(iii) Equivalent chip thickness:**

$$h_{eq} = \frac{V_w \cdot d}{V} = \frac{15\,000 \times 0.02}{1800 \times 1000} = \frac{300}{1\,800\,000} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{mm}$$

$$h_{eq} = 0.1667 \;\mu\text{m}$$

(Here $V$ must be in mm/min = 1800 × 1000 = 1 800 000 mm/min.)

**(iv) Theoretical surface roughness:**

$$R_a \approx \frac{h_{eq}}{4} = \frac{0.1667}{4} = 0.0417 \;\mu\text{m} \approx 0.04 \;\mu\text{m}$$

(Actual measured $R_a$ is typically 2–4× this theoretical value due to grit-protrusion randomness, vibration, and chatter.)

**Final Answers:**
(i) MRR = 900 mm³/min = **15 mm³/s**
(ii) $P = 375$ W = **0.375 kW**
(iii) $h_{eq} = 0.167$ µm
(iv) Theoretical $R_a \approx 0.04$ µm

*(Mark split: 1.5 + 2 + 2 + 1.5 = 7)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print and keep this checklist beside your lab record.**

- ☐ Surface grinding = **abrasive material removal** using a rotating bonded wheel on a flat workpiece.
- ☐ Four machine types: **(1) H-spindle reciprocating table** *(most common)*, **(2) H-spindle rotary table**, **(3) V-spindle reciprocating**, **(4) V-spindle rotary table**.
- ☐ Three motions: **Wheel rotation (V)** + **Table feed (V_w)** + **Down-feed (d)**.
- ☐ Standard wheel speed $V$ in **m/min** = $\pi D N / 1000$ — never forget the /1000.
- ☐ MRR = $V_w \cdot f_c \cdot d$ — always with **$V_w$ in mm/min**.
- ☐ $n$ (passes) = $W / f_c$, **round up**.
- ☐ Wheel marking = **Abrasive + Grit + Grade + Structure + Bond** (e.g., `A 60 K 5 V`).
- ☐ **A**brasive **O**xide for steel; **C**arborundum (SiC) for cast iron, Al, brass.
- ☐ **Vitrified bond (V)** = most common; **Resin (B)** for cut-off wheels; **Rubber (R)** for regulating wheels.
- ☐ **Magnetic chuck** = work-holding for ferromagnetic parts.
- ☐ **Coolant** does 5 jobs: **cool, lubricate, wash chips, prevent re-weld, extend wheel life**.
- ☐ **Spark-out** = final no-feed pass → improves $R_a$.
- ☐ Typical achievable $R_a$ in surface grinding: **0.2–1.6 µm**.
- ☐ Tolerance: **±0.01 to ±0.025 mm**.
- ☐ Specific energy: steel ≈ 25–35 J/mm³, cast iron ≈ 15–25 J/mm³, Al ≈ 5–10 J/mm³.
- ☐ **Safety**: always wear safety goggles, ensure wheel is **rung-tested** before mounting, **never** exceed the maximum safe rpm marked on the wheel, use a **wheel guard**, never grind on the **side** of a straight wheel.
- ☐ **Procedure steps for the lab record**: (1) Study machine & identify parts → (2) Mount & true the wheel → (3) Set magnetic chuck → (4) Mount & align workpiece → (5) Set table speed, cross-feed, down-feed → (6) Start coolant flow → (7) Grind using roughing pass then finishing pass → (8) Spark-out → (9) Measure $R_a$ with a **surftest (profilometer)** and dimensions with a **micrometer** → (10) Record observations & calculate MRR, $V$, $P$.

> **One-liner to remember forever:** *"In surface grinding, the **wheel spins**, the **table slides**, and the **head dives down by a few microns** — repeat this for hours, and you have a mirror-flat surface."* ✨
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drilling Holes on Drilling Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Drilling Holes on Drilling Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Drilling** is a fundamental material-removal machining process in which a **multi-point cutting tool** (commonly a *twist drill*) rotates against a stationary workpiece, generating a **cylindrical hole** of circular cross-section. The process is performed on a dedicated machine tool called a **Drilling Machine** (also known as a *Drill Press*).

According to the KTU 2024 Production Engineering syllabus, drilling is classified as a **generative (or material-additive removal) process** that produces internal cylindrical surfaces by the shearing action of the drill bit's cutting lips, with chips evacuated through helical flutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCAUL407 – Module 2):**
> The experiment aims to familiarize the student with the **types of drilling machines**, the **nomenclature of twist drills**, **work-holding devices**, **selection of cutting parameters (spindle speed and feed)**, **cutting fluid application**, and the **safety procedures** to be followed while producing accurate holes on metallic workpieces.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to dig a perfectly circular hole in the ground using a hand auger. You push down while rotating the handle, and the spiral blades lift the soil out continuously. A drilling machine performs **exactly the same action** on metal — except:

- The **rotation** is provided by an **electric motor** (not your hands).
- The **downward thrust (feed)** is mechanically controlled by a **lever or automatic quill feed**.
- The **chip evacuation** is built into the tool itself through **helical flutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Screw and the Cork":**
> Think of a wood-screw being driven into a piece of soft wood. The screw's threads carve the wood, the body pushes the chips outward, and as it penetrates, a clean cylindrical hole remains. A twist drill is essentially a *very hard, precisely ground metal screw* that cuts instead of displaces. The two **cutting lips** at the tip are like the leading edge of the screw thread, and the **flutes** act like a chip conveyor.

## 1.3 Classification of Drilling Machines (KTU Standard Nomenclature)

| Machine Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Bench Drilling Machine** | Small, mounted on a workbench; capacity up to ~12 mm | Light workshop / lab use |
| **Pillar / Column Drill** | Floor-standing with rigid column; capacity up to ~25 mm | General engineering |
| **Radial Arm Drill** | Head rides on a radial arm; can drill large workpieces | Heavy fabrication, large jobs |
| **Gang Drill** | Multiple spindles on a single column | Production drilling of identical parts |
| **Sensitive / Drill Press** | Hand-feed only, no power feed | Precision small-diameter holes |
| **Multi-Spindle Drill** | Several spindles drill simultaneously | Mass production |

> [!NOTE]
> For the **PCAUL407 lab**, the most commonly used machine is the **Pillar / Bench Drilling Machine** with a maximum drilling capacity ranging from **12 mm to 25 mm** in mild steel.

## 1.4 Anatomy of a Drilling Machine (Key Components)

A radial/pillar drilling machine has the following primary components:

- **Base** — Provides rigidity and houses the motor and coolant pump.
- **Column (Pillar)** — Vertical pillar supporting the head and table.
- **Table** — Adjustable work-holding platform that moves vertically along the column.
- **Head** — Houses the spindle drive and feed mechanism.
- **Spindle** — Rotating shaft that holds the drill chuck.
- **Drill Chuck** — Three or four-jaw self-centering device gripping the drill bit.
- **Feed Handle / Quill** — Lever that pushes the rotating spindle axially downward.
- **Depth Stop** — Adjustable collar that limits the depth of the drilled hole.
- **Coolant System** — Supplies cutting fluid to the cutting zone.

## 1.5 Anatomy of a Twist Drill (Nomenclature – High-Yield)

A standard **twist drill** is composed of the following parts (from tip to tail):

1. **Point / Dead Centre** — The extreme tip of the drill.
2. **Chisel Edge** — The straight edge connecting the two cutting lips at the centre.
3. **Cutting Lips (Cutting Edges)** — Two sharp edges that do the actual cutting.
4. **Lip Clearance Angle** — Angle (typically **12° – 15°**) that prevents rubbing behind the cutting lip.
5. **Point Angle** — The angle between the two cutting lips measured at the point; **118°** for general purpose, **135°** for harder materials.
6. **Flutes** — Helical grooves that evacuate chips and admit cutting fluid.
7. **Body (Flank)** — The main cylindrical portion of the drill.
8. **Margin (Land)** — A narrow raised strip on the body that guides the drill in the hole.
9. **Shank** — The non-cutting end held in the chuck; can be **straight** or **tapered (Morse Taper)**.
10. **Tang** — A small projection on tapered shanks for ejecting the drill from the spindle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **point angle of 118°** is the **K-Tool standard** for general-purpose drilling in mild steel, cast iron, and aluminium. The standard **helix angle** for twist drills is **24° – 32°**, with **30°** being typical for general-purpose use.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechanics of Drilling – Step-by-Step Theory

The drilling process can be understood as the **simultaneous rotation and translation** of a multi-point cutting tool. Let us break down the physical mechanics into discrete logic steps:

### Step 1: Engagement of Cutting Lips
When the drill tip touches the workpiece, the **two cutting lips** engage the material first, while the **chisel edge** acts as a squeezing/punching element at the centre. The chisel edge does **not** cut cleanly — it extrudes material and generates significant thrust force.

### Step 2: Formation of Chips
As the drill rotates at spindle speed **N (rpm)** and advances at feed **f (mm/rev)**, each lip removes a continuous curled chip whose thickness is approximately equal to the feed per revolution. These chips travel **upward along the flutes** and are ejected from the hole.

### Step 3: Generation of Thrust Force
The total **thrust force (Ft)** acts axially along the drill. It is the sum of forces on the two cutting lips and the chisel edge. Typical thrust force for mild steel with Ø10 mm drill ≈ **1500 – 2500 N**.

### Step 4: Generation of Torque
The **drilling torque (M)** is the rotational resistance offered by the workpiece. It is calculated as:
$$M = K_s \cdot A_c$$
where $A_c$ is the cross-sectional area being cut, and $K_s$ is the **specific cutting energy** (a material property).

### Step 5: Role of Flutes
The **helical flutes** perform three critical functions:
- (i) **Chip evacuation** — lift chips out of the hole.
- (ii) **Coolant conveyance** — channel cutting fluid to the cutting edge.
- (iii) **Cutting edge formation** — their intersection with the drill body forms the cutting lips.

### Step 6: Heat Generation and Dissipation
About **80%** of the total cutting energy is converted to heat. Most of this heat is carried away by the **chips**, while the remainder is absorbed by the **cutting fluid** and the **workpiece**. Without sufficient coolant, the drill tip temperature can exceed **600°C**, leading to rapid tool wear.

### Step 7: Hole Quality
The accuracy of the drilled hole depends on:
- Drill bit sharpness and **runout** (should be < **0.02 mm** at the point).
- Rigidity of the work-holding device.
- Correct selection of cutting parameters.
- Adequate **point grinding** symmetry (both lips must be of equal length and angle).

## 2.2 Cutting Parameter Selection Logic

For any drilling operation, three parameters must be selected:

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Influencing Factors |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed** | $V_c$ | m/min | Workpiece material, drill material, coolant |
| **Feed per Revolution** | $f$ | mm/rev | Drill diameter, workpiece material, hole depth |
| **Spindle Speed** | $N$ | rpm | Calculated from $V_c$ and drill diameter $D$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **KTU 2024 lab record** typically requires the student to consult a standard **machining data handbook** (or appendix table provided in the manual) to extract the recommended $V_c$ and $f$ for a given **workpiece material** and **drill diameter**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination) and lab viva. Master these units carefully.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cutting Speed** | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | D in mm, N in rpm |
| 2 | **Spindle Speed** | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Re-arranged form of Eq.1 |
| 3 | **Feed Rate** | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | f in mm/rev |
| 4 | **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** | $MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r$ | mm³/min | Cross-sectional area $\times$ feed rate |
| 5 | **Drilling Time (Through Hole)** | $T = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f_r}$ | min | L = depth of hole; 0.3D = approach |
| 6 | **Drilling Time (Blind Hole)** | $T = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f_r}$ | min | Same formula; L is the actual depth |
| 7 | **Thrust Force (approx.)** | $F_t = K_s \cdot A_c$ | N | $A_c$ = chip cross-section |
| 8 | **Power Required** | $P = \dfrac{M \cdot N}{9550}$ | kW | M in N·m |
| 9 | **Drill Point Angle (General)** | $\theta = 118°$ | degrees | Mild steel / cast iron |
| 10 | **Drill Point Angle (Hard Matl.)** | $\theta = 135°$ | degrees | Hardened steel / stainless |

> [!WARNING]
> In the KTU formula table above, **no vertical pipe `|` symbol is used inside the math expressions** — divisions use the fraction bar $\dfrac{}{}$ to avoid markdown-table parsing conflicts. This is the **safe notational style** recommended for all KTU exam answer scripts as well.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Drilling is one of the **most frequently performed machining operations** in industry. According to industry surveys, drilling accounts for approximately **25% – 30%** of all machining time in a typical discrete-parts manufacturing facility.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Applications:**
> - **Automotive**: Cylinder block bolt holes, oil passages, mounting holes.
> - **Aerospace**: Rivet holes in fuselage skins (often using automated drilling units, ADU).
> - **Mould & Die**: Ejector pin holes, cooling channels.
> - **General Fabrication**: Bolt clearance holes, tapped holes for fasteners.
> - **Electronics**: PCB through-holes (micro-drilling, D < 0.5 mm).

## 2.5 Work-Holding Devices for Drilling

| Device | Use Case | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Drill Vice (Machine Vise)** | Rectangular / cylindrical small parts | Most common in labs |
| **Step Blocks + Clamps** | Large flat plates | T-slot table required |
| **V-Blocks + Clamps** | Cylindrical shafts | Prevents rotation |
| **Angle Plates** | Parts at 90° to table | For vertical hole patterns |
| **Fixture Plates** | Mass production | Drilling jigs reduce set-up time |
| **Direct Chucking on Spindle** | Small cylindrical parts | For very small diameters |

## 2.6 Cutting Fluids Used in Drilling

| Fluid Type | Composition | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Soluble Oil (Emulsion)** | Mineral oil + emulsifier + water (1:20) | Mild steel, aluminium (most common) |
| **Straight Cutting Oil** | Mineral/neat oil | Tough materials, deep holes |
| **Sulphurised Oil** | Oil + sulphur additives | Hardened steels |
| **Synthetic Fluid** | Chemical solution | High-speed CNC drilling |
| **Dry Drilling (MQL)** | Minimal oil mist | Cast iron, brass (no coolant needed) |

> [!TIP]
> In the **PCAUL407 viva**, a frequently asked question is: *"Why do we use cutting fluid during drilling?"* The answer should include: **cooling, lubrication, chip flushing, improved surface finish, and increased tool life**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed and Drill Diameter

### Given
- $V_c$ = Cutting Speed in **m/min**
- $D$ = Drill diameter in **mm**
- $N$ = Spindle speed in **rpm**

### Logical Setup
By definition, **cutting speed** is the **peripheral linear speed** of the drill periphery at the workpiece surface.

Since the drill rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, the periphery of the drill (circumference $\pi D$) sweeps a linear distance of $\pi D$ in **one revolution**. Therefore, in one minute (i.e., $N$ revolutions), the linear distance swept is $\pi D \cdot N$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Linear distance swept in 1 revolution = $\pi D$ mm.

**Step 2:** Linear distance swept in $N$ revolutions (i.e., 1 minute) = $\pi D \cdot N$ mm.

**Step 3:** Convert from mm to m by dividing by 1000:
$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $N$ by rearranging:
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi D} \;\; \text{rpm}$$

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\,N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Material Removal Rate (MRR) in Drilling

### Given
- $D$ = Drill diameter in **mm**
- $f$ = Feed per revolution in **mm/rev**
- $N$ = Spindle speed in **rpm**
- $f_r = f \cdot N$ = Feed rate in **mm/min**

### Logical Setup
In drilling, the **cross-sectional area** of material being removed per unit time equals the **area of a circle of diameter D** (the hole being formed) multiplied by the **axial advance per unit time** (the feed rate).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Cross-sectional area of the hole:
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

**Step 2:** Volume removed per minute = Area $\times$ Feed rate:
$$MRR = A \cdot f_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot (f N)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $f_r = f N$:
$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot f \cdot N \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\,MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot f_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot f \cdot N\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Drilling Time (T)

### Given
- $L$ = Depth of hole to be drilled (mm)
- $D$ = Drill diameter (mm)
- $f_r$ = Feed rate (mm/min)

### Logical Setup
The drill must travel a distance equal to the depth of the hole **plus** a small "approach" distance to allow the drill point (chisel edge) to fully engage the workpiece before the cutting lips reach the surface.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The chisel edge of the drill has a length approximately equal to $0.3 \cdot D$ (i.e., 30% of drill diameter).

**Step 2:** Therefore, the total axial travel required is:
$$\text{Total Travel} = L + 0.3 D$$

**Step 3:** Time = Distance / Feed rate:
$$T = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f_r} = \dfrac{L + 0.3D}{f \cdot N}$$

**Final Equation:**
$$\boxed{\,T = \dfrac{L + 0.3 \, D}{f \cdot N}\,}$$

## 3.4 Solved Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem (Drilling a 12 mm Hole in Mild Steel)**
> A through hole of **12 mm diameter** and **25 mm depth** is to be drilled in a mild-steel workpiece using a HSS twist drill. The recommended cutting speed is **$V_c = 25$ m/min** and feed is **$f = 0.20$ mm/rev**. Calculate:
> 1. Spindle speed $N$
> 2. Feed rate $f_r$
> 3. Material removal rate $MRR$
> 4. Drilling time $T$

### Solution

**Part (i): Spindle Speed N**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 12}$$

**Computing the denominator:**
$$\pi \times 12 = 37.699 \;\; \text{(approx.)}$$

**Computing the numerator:**
$$1000 \times 25 = 25000$$

**Final value:**
$$N = \dfrac{25000}{37.699} \approx 663.1 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

The nearest available spindle speed on the machine is **$N = 660$ rpm** (we always round to the available machine setting).

**Part (ii): Feed Rate $f_r$**
$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.20 \times 660 = 132 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**Part (iii): Material Removal Rate $MRR$**
$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 144 \times 132$$

**Computing stepwise:**
$$\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 144 = 113.097 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$

$$MRR = 113.097 \times 132 = 14{,}928.84 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\,MRR \approx 14{,}929 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 14.93 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}\,}$$

**Part (iv): Drilling Time $T$**
$$T = \dfrac{L + 0.3 D}{f_r} = \dfrac{25 + 0.3 \times 12}{132} = \dfrac{25 + 3.6}{132} = \dfrac{28.6}{132}$$

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 0.2167 \;\; \text{min} \approx 13 \;\; \text{seconds}\,}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Drilling Performance

| Parameter Increased | Spindle Speed $N$ | Feed $f$ | MRR | Tool Life | Hole Quality |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Speed $V_c$** | Increases | No direct effect | Increases (via N) | Decreases sharply | May degrade |
| **Feed $f$** | No change | Increases | Increases (linearly) | Decreases | Rougher surface |
| **Drill Diameter $D$** | Decreases (for fixed $V_c$) | Higher feed typically used | Increases (D² effect) | Decreases | Larger chips |

> [!TIP]
> Notice the **square-law effect** of drill diameter on MRR. Doubling the drill diameter **quadruples** the material removal rate, which is why larger holes remove far more material — and generate far more heat — than small ones.

## 3.6 KTUs Common Lab Calculation: Power Required

For the same problem above, assume the specific cutting energy for mild steel is $K_s = 2.5$ N·m/mm³ and drilling torque is $M = 12$ N·m. Calculate the power required.

**Step 1:** Apply power formula:
$$P = \dfrac{M \cdot N}{9550}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:
$$P = \dfrac{12 \times 660}{9550}$$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$P = \dfrac{7920}{9550} = 0.829 \;\; \text{kW}$$

$$\boxed{\,P \approx 0.83 \;\; \text{kW} \approx 830 \;\; \text{W}\,}$$

A standard **1.0 kW** motor on a bench drill press is therefore sufficient for this operation, with some safety margin.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams use **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** to map the interactions of components and procedural flow. They follow the KTU-safe node-naming convention (alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters, no reserved keywords, no markdown formatting inside labels).

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Major Components of a Pillar Drilling Machine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    baseA[BASE - houses motor and coolant tank]
    colB[COLUMN - vertical rigid pillar]
    tabC[TABLE - moves vertically on column]
    headD[HEAD - houses spindle drive]
    spdlE[SPINDLE - rotates at N rpm]
    chuckF[DRILL CHUCK - grips the drill bit]
    bitG[TWIST DRILL - the cutting tool]
    feedH[FEED HANDLE / QUILL - provides axial feed]
    depI[DEPTH STOP - limits hole depth]
    coolJ[COOLANT NOZZLE - delivers cutting fluid]

    baseA --- colB
    colB --- tabC
    colB --- headD
    headD --- spdlE
    spdlE --- chuckF
    chuckF --- bitG
    headD --- feedH
    headD --- depI
    baseA --- coolJ
    feedH --> spdlE
```

**Functional Logic of the Diagram:**
- The **BASE** (A) supports the entire machine and houses the prime mover.
- The **COLUMN** (B) is the structural spine to which the **TABLE** (C) and **HEAD** (D) are bolted.
- The **SPINDLE** (E) is driven by the motor through a gear train and is held by the **CHUCK** (F), which grips the **TWIST DRILL** (G).
- The **FEED HANDLE** (H) manually advances the quill downward, while the **DEPTH STOP** (I) prevents over-drilling.
- The **COOLANT NOZZLE** (J) directs fluid to the cutting zone for heat dissipation.

## 4.2 Nomenclature of a Twist Drill (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tipA[POINT - extreme tip of drill]
    chiselB[CHISEL EDGE - connects the two lips]
    lipC[CUTTING LIPS - two sharp edges that cut]
    flutD[FLUTES - helical chip evacuation grooves]
    bodyE[BODY - main cylindrical portion]
    marF[MARGIN OR LAND - narrow guide strip]
    shkG[SHANK - held in drill chuck]
    tangH[TANG - drives out drill from spindle]

    tipA --- chiselB
    chiselB --- lipC
    lipC --- flutD
    flutD --- bodyE
    bodyE --- marF
    marF --- shkG
    shkG --- tangH

    flutD -. PROVIDES COOLANT FLOW .-> flutD
    marF -. GUIDES DRILL IN HOLE .-> marF
    lipC -. REMOVES MATERIAL .-> lipC
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Each labelled block represents a **functional zone** of the twist drill.
- Dotted arrows indicate the **secondary function** of that zone (e.g., flutes also convey coolant, margins also provide guidance).

## 4.3 Procedure Flow Chart — Drilling a Hole (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start1[START - read drawing and identify hole specs]
    insp2[INSPECT WORKPIECE - check material, size, surface]
    sel3[SELECT DRILL BIT - choose diameter, point angle, material]
    grnd4[GRIND DRILL IF NEEDED - check lip symmetry and clearance]
    mnt5[MOUNT DRILL IN CHUCK - tighten jaws securely]
    fix6[FIX WORKPIECE - use drill vice, clamp on table]
    aln7[ALIGN DRILL TIP - centre punch mark, bring drill to mark]
    set8[SET SPINDLE SPEED - per formula N = 1000 Vc / pi D]
    set9[SET FEED - per handbook for material and diameter]
    set10[SET DEPTH STOP - prevent overdrilling]
    app11[APPLY CUTTING FLUID - open coolant valve]
    pil12[PILOT DRILL - small indent first if diameter above 10 mm]
    full13[FULL DRILL - engage feed handle steadily]
    retr14[RETRACT DRILL - clear chips, break swarf if needed]
    chk15[CHECK HOLE - diameter, depth, surface finish]
    rec16[RECORD OBSERVATIONS - time, parameters, observations]
    stop17[STOP - clean machine, store tools]

    start1 --> insp2
    insp2 --> sel3
    sel3 --> grnd4
    grnd4 --> mnt5
    mnt5 --> fix6
    fix6 --> aln7
    aln7 --> set8
    set8 --> set9
    set9 --> set10
    set10 --> app11
    app11 --> pil12
    pil12 --> full13
    full13 --> retr14
    retr14 --> chk15
    chk15 --> rec16
    rec16 --> stop17
```

**Engineering Interpretation of the Flow Chart:**
- The flow is **strictly sequential** — no step can be skipped.
- Step 4 (grinding check) is **critical** because an asymmetrically ground drill produces oversize, bell-mouthed, or off-centre holes.
- Step 12 (pilot drilling) is a **best practice** for diameters greater than **10 mm** to prevent wandering and reduce thrust force.
- Step 14 (retraction) highlights the importance of **chip-breaking**, which prevents chip packing and drill breakage in deep holes.

## 4.4 Cause-Effect Block Diagram — Common Drilling Defects

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    causeA[CAUSE - dull drill or wrong point angle]
    causeB[CAUSE - excessive feed or low speed]
    causeC[CAUSE - insufficient coolant]
    causeD[CAUSE - drill wandering, no centre punch]
    causeE[CAUSE - asymmetric lip grinding]

    eff1[EFFECT - rough surface finish]
    eff2[EFFECT - rapid tool wear]
    eff3[EFFECT - hole oversize]
    eff4[EFFECT - drill breakage]
    eff5[EFFECT - bell-mouthed hole entry]

    causeA --> eff1

    causeB --> eff2
    causeB --> eff3
    causeC --> eff1
    causeC --> eff4
    causeD --> eff3
    causeD --> eff5
    causeE --> eff3
    causeE --> eff5
```

**Interpretation:** Each cause-effect arrow in the diagram is a direct mapping that students should memorise for viva questions on troubleshooting drilling defects.

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]`

**Q: Sketch and label the main parts of a twist drill. List any six parts with their function.**

**Model Answer:**

The main parts of a twist drill (sketched from tip to shank) are:

| # | Part | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Point / Dead Centre** | Forms the extreme tip; helps in self-centring during initial engagement. |
| 2 | **Chisel Edge** | Connects the two cutting lips; performs squeezing/punching at the hole centre. |
| 3 | **Cutting Lips** | Two sharp edges that perform the actual material removal. |
| 4 | **Flutes** | Helical grooves that evacuate chips and admit cutting fluid. |
| 5 | **Body (Flank)** | Main cylindrical portion that provides rigidity. |
| 6 | **Margin (Land)** | Narrow guide strip that keeps the drill concentric in the hole. |
| 7 | **Shank** | Non-cutting end held in the drill chuck. |
| 8 | **Tang** | Projection on tapered shank used to eject the drill from the spindle. |

> **Sketch Requirement:** A neatly labelled line diagram of a twist drill showing all the above parts must accompany the answer. *(3 marks: 2 marks for list, 1 mark for diagram)*

---

### Question A2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**Q: Define the terms (i) cutting speed, (ii) feed, and (iii) spindle speed as applied to drilling. State the units for each.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Cutting Speed ($V_c$):** The peripheral linear speed of the drill at the cutting edge, measured in **m/min**. It is the surface speed with which the drill's periphery sweeps across the workpiece.

$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \;\; \text{m/min}$$

(ii) **Feed ($f$):** The axial advancement of the drill per revolution, measured in **mm/rev**. It determines chip thickness.

(iii) **Spindle Speed ($N$):** The rotational speed of the drill (and spindle) about its own axis, measured in **rpm (revolutions per minute)**.

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \, V_c}{\pi \, D} \;\; \text{rpm}$$

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for the three definitions with units)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option A

> **Question:** A hole of **20 mm diameter** and **40 mm depth** is to be drilled in a **mild steel workpiece** (recommended $V_c = 25$ m/min, $f = 0.20$ mm/rev). A HSS twist drill is to be used. Answer the following:
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Calculate the **spindle speed**, **feed rate**, and **material removal rate** for this operation. List the procedure for setting up the drilling machine and the workpiece for this job.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **function of cutting fluid** in this drilling operation. Discuss the common **drilling defects** that may occur and state **two preventive measures** for each.

### Model Solution to Question A — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Drill diameter, $D = 20$ mm
- Depth of hole, $L = 40$ mm
- Cutting speed, $V_c = 25$ m/min
- Feed per revolution, $f = 0.20$ mm/rev

**(i) Calculation of Spindle Speed N:**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 20} = \dfrac{25000}{62.832} \approx 397.9 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

Nearest available setting: **$N = 400$ rpm** ✓

**(ii) Calculation of Feed Rate $f_r$:**
$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.20 \times 400 = 80 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**(iii) Calculation of Material Removal Rate MRR:**
$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 \times 80 = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 400 \times 80$$

$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 32000 = 25132.7 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\,MRR \approx 25{,}133 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 25.13 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}\,}$$

**(iv) Procedure for Setting Up the Drilling Machine:**

1. **Select the drill bit** — Choose a 20 mm HSS twist drill, inspect for sharpness, and verify the lip symmetry.
2. **Mount the drill in the chuck** — Insert the shank into the chuck jaws, tighten the chuck key firmly, and ensure the drill is centred.
3. **Set the spindle speed** — Adjust the gear levers on the head to obtain approximately 400 rpm.
4. **Set the feed** — For manual feed, the operator controls the feed handle; for power feed, set the feed rate to 0.20 mm/rev.
5. **Fix the workpiece** — Clamp the mild-steel workpiece on the machine table using a drill vice; ensure the workpiece is rigid and the hole location is directly below the drill.
6. **Centre punch the hole location** — Use a centre punch and hammer to make a small indent at the marked spot to start the drill accurately.
7. **Align the drill** — Bring the drill tip just above the centre-punch mark using the table-height adjustment.
8. **Set the depth stop** — Adjust the depth-stop collar so the drill will stop at exactly 40 mm depth.
9. **Apply cutting fluid** — Open the coolant valve and direct a steady stream onto the drill tip.

---

### Model Solution to Question A — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Functions of Cutting Fluid in Drilling:**

1. **Cooling** — Absorbs and carries away the heat generated at the cutting zone, preventing tool-tip overheating and preserving tool life.
2. **Lubrication** — Reduces friction between the chip and the drill flutes, lowering the cutting forces and torque.
3. **Chip Flushing** — The flowing fluid helps evacuate chips from the hole, preventing chip clogging and drill breakage.
4. **Improved Surface Finish** — By reducing rubbing and built-up edge formation, the cutting fluid yields smoother hole walls.
5. **Corrosion Protection** — The fluid forms a thin protective film on the freshly machined surface, preventing rust.

**(ii) Common Drilling Defects and Preventive Measures:**

| Defect | Cause | Preventive Measure |
|---|---|---|
| **Oversize Hole** | Asymmetric drill lips, runout in spindle | Regrind drill symmetrically; check spindle runout |
| **Bell-Mouthed Hole** | Drill wandering at entry, worn margin | Always use a centre-punch indent; replace worn drill |
| **Rough Surface Finish** | Dull drill, insufficient coolant, high feed | Sharpen drill; increase coolant flow; reduce feed |
| **Drill Breakage** | Excessive feed, chip clogging, wrong point angle | Use peck-drilling in deep holes; reduce feed; clear chips |
| **Tapered Hole** | Drill not perpendicular to workpiece | Align drill axis carefully; use a guide bushing |
| **Rapid Tool Wear** | Excessive cutting speed, inadequate coolant | Reduce $V_c$ to handbook value; ensure proper coolant flow |
| **Built-up Edge** | Low cutting speed, no coolant | Increase $V_c$; use proper cutting fluid |

---

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option B

> **Question:**
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Describe with a **neat sketch** the **construction and working of a pillar-type drilling machine**. List its main parts.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] In a drilling operation, a **15 mm diameter** hole of **30 mm depth** is drilled in a **cast iron workpiece** ($V_c = 20$ m/min, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev). Calculate the **spindle speed**, **feed rate**, **MRR**, and **drilling time**. Mention **two safety precautions** to be followed during drilling.

### Model Solution to Question B — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Construction and Working of a Pillar Drilling Machine:**

A pillar (or column) drilling machine is a **floor-mounted** drilling machine consisting of a vertical rigid column and a horizontal work table. The main parts are:

1. **Base** — A heavy cast-iron base that supports the entire machine and houses the motor and coolant pump.
2. **Column / Pillar** — A vertical cylindrical column bolted to the base. It supports the table and head.
3. **Table** — A horizontal table mounted on the column that can be raised or lowered to accommodate workpieces of different heights. The workpiece is clamped on the table.
4. **Head** — Mounted on top of the column, it contains the spindle, gear train, and feed mechanism.
5. **Spindle** — A rotating shaft inside the head; it drives the drill. The spindle can be moved axially up and down by the feed mechanism.
6. **Chuck** — A self-centering device at the lower end of the spindle that grips the drill bit.
7. **Feed Handle and Quill** — A lever-operated rack-and-pinion mechanism that pushes the spindle axially downward, providing the necessary feed.
8. **Depth Stop** — A collar that limits the downward travel of the spindle.
9. **Coolant Pump and Nozzle** — Supplies cutting fluid to the cutting zone.

**Working Principle:**

- The **electric motor** drives the **spindle** through a multi-step **gear train** that provides various spindle speeds.
- The **drill bit**, held in the **chuck**, rotates at the selected speed $N$.
- The **workpiece** is clamped on the **table**, and the table is raised so the drill tip touches the workpiece at the marked location.
- The operator applies **axial feed** using the **feed handle**, advancing the rotating drill into the workpiece.
- **Chips** are evacuated through the drill flutes, and **cutting fluid** is delivered to the cutting zone for cooling.
- Once the desired depth is reached (as set by the **depth stop**), the feed handle is released, the spindle retracts, and the machine is stopped.

**Neat Sketch (Description for student to draw):**
A simple side-view showing base at the bottom, vertical column rising from the base, table mounted on the column, head on top, spindle protruding downward from the head, chuck at the spindle end, drill bit gripped in the chuck, workpiece on the table, and the feed handle protruding from the head.

---

### Model Solution to Question B — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Drill diameter, $D = 15$ mm
- Depth of hole, $L = 30$ mm
- Cutting speed, $V_c = 20$ m/min
- Feed per revolution, $f = 0.15$ mm/rev

**(i) Spindle Speed $N$:**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 15} = \dfrac{20000}{47.124} \approx 424.4 \;\; \text{rpm}$$

Nearest available: **$N = 425$ rpm** ✓

**(ii) Feed Rate $f_r$:**
$$f_r = f \cdot N = 0.15 \times 425 = 63.75 \;\; \text{mm/min}$$

**(iii) Material Removal Rate $MRR$:**
$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot f_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (15)^2 \times 63.75$$

**Step-by-step computation:**
$$\dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 225 = 176.71 \;\; \text{mm}^2$$
$$MRR = 176.71 \times 63.75 = 11{,}265.3 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min}$$

$$\boxed{\,MRR \approx 11{,}265 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 11.27 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}\,}$$

**(iv) Drilling Time $T$:**
$$T = \dfrac{L + 0.3 D}{f_r} = \dfrac{30 + 0.3 \times 15}{63.75} = \dfrac{30 + 4.5}{63.75} = \dfrac{34.5}{63.75}$$

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 0.541 \;\; \text{min} \approx 32.5 \;\; \text{seconds}\,}$$

**(v) Safety Precautions During Drilling:**

1. **Wear Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Always wear **safety goggles** to protect eyes from flying chips, **gloves** while handling rough workpieces, and **avoid loose clothing** that may get entangled in the rotating spindle.
2. **Secure the Workpiece Firmly:** The workpiece must be **rigidly clamped** using a drill vice or clamps. A loose workpiece can spin violently and cause severe injury.
3. **Never Hold the Workpiece by Hand:** For small parts, use a **drill vice**; never attempt to hold the workpiece with bare hands during drilling.
4. **Clear the Work Area:** Remove all loose tools, rags, and chips from the table and floor before starting the machine.
5. **Stop the Machine Before Adjustments:** Never attempt to change spindle speed, adjust the depth stop, or clear chips while the machine is running.
6. **Use the Correct Drill Speed:** Using excessively high spindle speeds can cause the drill to shatter; always follow the recommended cutting speed for the material.
7. **Retract the Drill Frequently:** For deep holes, retract the drill periodically to **break and clear chips**, preventing drill breakage and chip clogging.

---

## 5.4 KTUs Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion in Cutting Speed Formula** — Many students write $V_c = \pi D N$ without the factor **1000**, mixing up **mm/min** with **m/min**. The factor **1000 is mandatory** when $D$ is in mm and $V_c$ is in m/min. *(Loss: 1–2 marks per question)*
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.3D Approach Distance in Drilling Time** — The drill point's chisel edge extends approximately **0.3 × D** below the cutting lips. Skipping this term gives an **underestimate** of the actual drilling time. *(Loss: 1 mark)*
>
> 3. **Missing Sketch in "Sketch and Label" Questions** — For a 3-mark question asking to "sketch and label", a question without a diagram typically receives a maximum of **2 out of 3 marks**. Always include a **neat, labelled line diagram** even if it is hand-drawn.
>
> 4. **Using Wrong Material Data** — For mild steel, students often quote $V_c = 30$ or $40$ m/min from memory, but the standard handbook value is **20–25 m/min** for HSS drills. Always use the value given in the question or the lab manual.
>
> 5. **Confusing MRR Units** — MRR is always expressed in **mm³/min** (or cm³/min), **not** mm³/s. The unit conversion is frequently tested.
>
> 6. **Neglecting Safety in Long Answers** — In 14-mark questions, students often skip the **safety precautions** section. KTU examiners typically allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for safety — do not leave this out.
>
> 7. **Not Rounding the Spindle Speed** — Calculated $N$ is almost never a whole number. Students must **round to the nearest available machine setting** (e.g., 397.9 → **400 rpm**). Failing to do so is considered incomplete engineering practice.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Drilling:**

### ★ Core Concepts
- Drilling = **rotating multi-point tool** + **axial feed** to produce a cylindrical hole.
- The **twist drill** is the standard tool, with **point angle 118°** for general purpose.
- **Cutting speed $V_c$** is the **peripheral linear speed** of the drill at the workpiece.
- **Feed $f$** is the **axial advance per revolution** of the drill.
- **Spindle speed $N$** is calculated from $V_c$ and $D$ using the formula $N = 1000 V_c / (\pi D)$.

### ★ Essential Formulas (must memorise)
$$V_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000} \quad\quad N = \dfrac{1000 V_c}{\pi D} \quad\quad f_r = f N$$
$$MRR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} D^2 f_r \quad\quad T = \dfrac{L + 0.3 D}{f \cdot N} \quad\quad P = \dfrac{M N}{9550}$$

### ★ Drill Bit Anatomy
- **Point**, **Chisel Edge**, **Cutting Lips**, **Flutes**, **Body**, **Margin (Land)**, **Shank**, **Tang**.

### ★ Drilling Machine Anatomy
- **Base**, **Column**, **Table**, **Head**, **Spindle**, **Chuck**, **Feed Handle (Quill)**, **Depth Stop**, **Coolant System**.

### ★ Critical Best Practices
- Always **centre-punch** the hole location before drilling.
- Always check **drill lip symmetry** before mounting.
- For diameters > 10 mm, perform a **pilot drill** (3–5 mm) first.
- For deep holes, **peck-drill** (retract frequently) to break chips.
- Always use **cutting fluid** unless the material is cast iron or brass (where dry drilling is acceptable).

### ★ Common Defects to Remember
- Oversize hole, bell-mouthed hole, rough surface, drill breakage, rapid wear — and the **cause + remedy** for each.

### ★ Common Viva Questions
- Why is the drill point angle **118°** and not 90°? *(Answer: 90° would give a negative rake, causing excessive thrust and poor chip flow; 118° gives a positive rake of about 24° on the cutting lips.)*
- What is the function of the **margin**? *(Answer: It provides guidance and acts as a secondary cutting edge to maintain hole size.)*
- Why use **cutting fluid** in drilling? *(Answer: Cooling, lubrication, chip evacuation, better surface finish, longer tool life.)*
- Why is the **chisel edge** considered non-cutting? *(Answer: It does not have a proper rake angle; it extrudes material by squeezing, generating high thrust force.)*

### ★ Safety First
- Always wear **PPE** (goggles, gloves, no loose clothing).
- **Clamp the workpiece** securely; never hold by hand.
- **Stop the machine** before any adjustment.
- **Clear the area** of loose tools, rags, and chips.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of Different Types of Tools, Their Angles and Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of Different Types of Tools, Their Angles and Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cutting Tool (KTU Definition):** A cutting tool is a hardened, geometrically defined device employed on a machine tool to remove excess material from a workpiece in the form of chips, by relative sliding motion against the workpiece surface, governed by standardized **tool nomenclature** (ASA / ISO 3002/1).

A **Single Point Cutting Tool (SPCT)** has *one* effective cutting edge that engages the workpiece at a time (e.g., a lathe turning tool). A **Multi-Point Cutting Tool** has *several* cutting edges simultaneously engaged (e.g., drill, milling cutter, broach, hob, tap).

### 1.1.1 Broad Classification of Cutting Tools

| Class | Description | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Point Cutting Tools (SPCT)** | One major cutting edge | Lathe tool, boring tool, form tool |
| **Multi-Point Cutting Tools** | Many cutting edges (teeth) | Drill, reamer, milling cutter, hob, broach, saw |
| **Abrasive Tools** | Random/structured abrasive grits | Grinding wheel, honing stone |
| **Super-Abrasive Tools** | Ultra-hard abrasive grits | Diamond wheel, CBN wheel |
| **Form Tools** | Cutting profile determines finished form | Form-relieved milling cutter, form tool on shaper |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Carpenter's Chisel"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a carpenter shaping a hard wooden block with a chisel. The wedge-shape of the chisel wedge decides how easily wood peels off. A *very sharp, steep wedge* cuts soft wood easily; a *blunt wedge* needs more force and tears the wood. A cutting tool is exactly this — a metal wedge with carefully chosen **angles** and made from a material **harder than the workpiece and tough enough not to break**.

- **Workpiece** → The wood being carved (steel, aluminum, etc.)
- **Tool material** → The chisel material (hard wood for soft wood, or metal chisel for hardwood)
- **Tool angles** → The wedge geometry of the chisel (rake, clearance, edge sharpness)
- **Cutting speed, feed, depth** → How fast / how hard the carpenter pushes
- **Chip** → The wood shaving curling away

This analogy justifies *why* tool geometry (angles) and tool material selection are the two pillars of cutting-tool study.

---

## 1.3 Why This Experiment Exists (KTU Lab Outcome CO1 / CO2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (CO):**
> - **CO1:** Identify and classify various cutting tools used in conventional machining processes.
> - **CO2:** Understand the geometry (nomenclature) of single-point and multi-point cutting tools.
> - **CO3:** Correlate tool material properties with machining applications.

In **Experiment 2** of PCAUL407, the student is expected to:
1. **Identify** the cutting tool by physical inspection (visual + vernier-based geometry measurement).
2. **Measure** the principal angles using a **Toolmaker's Microscope / Optical Profile Projector / Universal Bevel Protractor**.
3. **Specify** the tool material (HSS / Carbide / Ceramic / CBN / PCD) by **trade marking / file test / magnet test / spark test**.

This sets the foundation for understanding **machinability**, **tool life**, **cutting forces**, and **surface finish** in subsequent lab experiments.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tool Nomenclature of a Single Point Cutting Tool (ASA / ISO System)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single point cutting tool has **3 reference planes** and **7 tool elements (angles)**. Together, they completely describe the geometry of the cutting tool. (KTU Board Examiner favourite — *draw & label* the seven elements.)

### 2.1.1 The Three Reference Planes

| Sl. | Reference Plane | Definition | Abbreviation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Tool Reference Plane** | Plane perpendicular to the tool shank (or base of tool) and passes through the **point** of the tool | **TRP / R–R′** |
| 2 | **Tool Cutting Edge Plane** | Plane tangent to the cutting edge at the tool point and **parallel to the cutting velocity (V)** | **TCEP / M–M′** |
| 3 | **Tool Normal Plane** | Plane **perpendicular to the cutting edge projection** on the reference plane; it is the plane in which chip thickness and cutting edge geometry are measured | **TNP / N–N′** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick (KTU): "R – M – N"**
> - **R**eference plane — perpendicular to shank.
> - **M**achining (Cutting-edge) plane — parallel to cutting velocity.
> - **N**ormal plane — perpendicular to cutting edge projection.

---

### 2.1.2 The Seven Tool Angles (KTU Most Repeated Diagram)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** Students often label only **3 angles** and miss the **edge angles** (lead angle, end cutting edge angle). All **7 angles** must be drawn with proper symbol convention.

#### (A) Rake Angles (3)

| Symbol | Name | Measured in Plane |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha_b$ | **Back Rake Angle** | Longitudinal (axial) plane |
| $\alpha_s$ | **Side Rake Angle** | Cross-sectional (transverse) plane |
| $\gamma_p$ | **Normal Rake Angle** | Tool Normal Plane |
| $\gamma_0$ | **Orthogonal (Working) Rake Angle** | Plane perpendicular to cutting edge |

**Physical meaning of rake angle:**
- $\gamma > 0$ → **Positive rake** → tool wedge is "sharp / thin", less force, less power, lower tool strength.
- $\gamma < 0$ → **Negative rake** → tool wedge is "blunt / thick", higher force, higher heat, **stronger edge** (used for hard workpieces / interrupted cuts).

#### (B) Clearance (Lip) Angles (3)

| Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| $\beta_b$ | **End Relief / End Clearance Angle** | Provides gap between flank and machined surface (prevents rubbing) |
| $\beta_s$ | **Side Relief / Side Clearance Angle** | Prevents flank interference on the work surface |
| $\beta_n$ | **Normal Clearance Angle** | Clearance measured in the tool normal plane |

> A minimum clearance of **3°–7°** is typical for steel; **6°–10°** for soft materials.

#### (C) Cutting Edge Angles (2)

| Symbol | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| $\phi_s$ | **Side Cutting Edge Angle** (often $\epsilon_s$) | Angle between cutting edge projection on reference plane and the side flank |
| $\phi_e$ | **End Cutting Edge (Lead) Angle** (often $\gamma_e$) | Angle between end cutting edge and reference plane |
| $r$ | **Nose Radius** | Rounded tool tip — improves surface finish |

#### Schematic (Text-Based Geometry Map)

```
                   Rake Face
                  ___________
                 /           \         ← Rake angle (γ) measured
                /             \          between rake face and
               /               \         reference plane R-R'
              /                 \
             /___________________\
            /                     \      ← Flank (clearance) face
           /________________________\
          |        Clearance β       |
          |__________________________|
                  Shank
```

---

## 2.2 Orthogonal Rake Angle vs. Normal Rake Angle — Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the cutting edge is **inclined** (oblique cutting), the **normal plane (N–N′)** does **not** coincide with the **orthogonal plane (P–P′)**. The angle conversion is a KTU frequently-tested derivation.

Let:
- $\gamma_n$ = normal rake angle
- $\gamma_0$ = orthogonal (working) rake angle
- $\lambda_s$ = **inclination angle** (angle the cutting edge makes with the reference plane, measured in the cutting-edge plane)

The geometric relationship between these planes yields:

$$
\tan \gamma_0 \;=\; \tan \gamma_n \cdot \cos \lambda_s
$$

> **Sanity Check:** If $\lambda_s = 0$ (orthogonal cutting), then $\cos \lambda_s = 1$ → $\gamma_0 = \gamma_n$ ✓

---

## 2.3 Chip Thickness Relationship & Shear Angle (Fundamental of Chip Formation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Chip Thickness Equation** — derived from geometry of chip formation in 2-D (orthogonal) cutting:

Let:
- $t_1$ = uncut chip thickness (mm)
- $t_2$ = chip thickness after cut (mm)
- $r_c$ = cutting ratio

**Cutting ratio:**

$$
r_c \;=\; \frac{t_1}{t_2}
$$

**Chip thickness ratio** (always $\le 1$):

$$
r \;=\; \frac{t_2}{t_1} \;\ge\; 1
$$

**Shear angle** $\phi$ from **Merchant's analysis** (the classical KTU derivation):

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{r_c \cdot \cos \alpha}{\,1 \;-\; r_c \cdot \sin \alpha\,}
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **orthogonal clearance angle** (so $\gamma + \alpha + \beta_{\text{tool}} = 90°$, where $\beta_{\text{tool}}$ is tool cutting edge angle measured orthogonal to the cutting edge).

**Alternative: Ern–Merchant equation (force balance derivation):**

$$
\phi \;=\; 45° \;+\; \frac{\alpha}{2} \;-\; \frac{\beta_s}{2}
$$

where $\beta_s$ is the **friction angle** at the tool-chip interface given by $\mu = \tan \beta_s$.

> **Significance:** Larger positive rake → smaller $\alpha$ → larger $\phi$ → thinner chip → lower cutting force → better machinability.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a *complete* cheat-sheet for Module Experiment 2 viva + written exam.** Memorize all rows.

| # | Quantity | Formula / Rule | Typical Values / Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cutting ratio | $r_c = t_1 / t_2$ | $r_c < 1$ always | Measures *how thin the chip became* |
| 2 | Shear angle (Merchant) | $\tan \phi = \dfrac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha}$ | 25°–45° | Function of rake + chip ratio |
| 3 | Shear angle (Ern–Merchant) | $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta_s/2$ | 25°–45° | Force-balance form |
| 4 | Friction angle | $\beta_s = \tan^{-1}\mu$ | 0°–45° | $\mu$ = coefficient of friction |
| 5 | Tool tip angle sum | $\alpha + \gamma + \beta = 90°$ | Always | Orthogonal plane identity |
| 6 | Rake-plane conversion | $\tan \gamma_0 = \tan \gamma_n \cdot \cos \lambda_s$ | — | For oblique cutting |
| 7 | Tool life (Taylor) | $V T^n = C$ | $n = 0.1$–0.5 | High-speed steels |
| 8 | Cutting velocity | $V = \pi D N / 60\,000$ | m/min | $D$ in mm, $N$ in rpm |
| 9 | Material removal rate | $MRR = V \cdot f \cdot d$ | mm³/min | $f$ = feed, $d$ = depth |
| 10 | Power | $P_c = F_c \cdot V / 60\,000$ | kW | $F_c$ in N |

---

## 2.5 Tool Material Classification & Property Map

### 2.5.1 Material Categories (Hierarchy of Hardness ↑ and Toughness ↓)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick: "H–C–Ce–D–P"** (Hardness increase, Toughness decrease)
> **HSS → Carbide → Ceramic → Diamond → CBN/PCD**

| Tool Material | Hot Hardness (HV at 600 °C) | Toughness (KIC, MPa·m^0.5) | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **High Speed Steel (HSS)** — e.g., M2, M42, T1 | ~500 HV | 25–30 | Drills, taps, reamers, form tools, low-speed turning |
| **Cemented Carbide** — WC + Co binder | ~900 HV | 12–18 | Turning, milling, drilling of steel/cast iron |
| **Ceramics** — Al₂O₃, Si₃N₄ | ~1500 HV | 4–6 | High-speed finishing of cast iron, superalloys |
| **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** | ~3000 HV | 6–10 | Hardened steels (HRC > 50), pearlitic CI |
| **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)** | ~7000 HV | 6–9 | Non-ferrous (Al, Cu, brass), composites, stone |
| **Monocrystalline Diamond** | ~10 000 HV | 3–4 | Mirror finishing, precision turning |

### 2.5.2 Key Property Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Trade-off Triangle:** Hardness ↔ Toughness ↔ Wear Resistance.
> No single tool material wins all three. Carbide is the industry sweet-spot.

---

## 2.6 Multi-Point Cutting Tools — Geometry Highlights

| Tool | Cutting Edges | Helix / Lip Angle | Material | KTU Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Twist Drill** | 2 (main lips) + chisel edge | Helix angle **20°–40°**; Point angle **118°** (steel), **135°** (CI) | HSS / Solid Carbide / Brazed-tip | Lip clearance = **9°–15°** |
| **Reamer** | Many (6–16) straight or helical flutes | Lead angle 0°–45° (helical) | HSS / Carbide | Finishing tool; **0.05–0.3 mm stock removal** |
| **Milling Cutter** | Many peripheral teeth | Helix 30°–45°; Rake +5°–+20° | HSS / Carbide inserts | Up-milling vs Down-milling matters |
| **Tap** | Threads | — | HSS | Right-hand cut, lead angle 0°–7° |
| **Hob** | Helical teeth (involute) | Lead angle | HSS / Carbide | Spur-gear cutting (generating) |
| **Broach** | Progressive teeth | — | HSS / Carbide | Linear internal / external profiling |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does industry care?**
> - **Cost:** Tooling is **~15%** of total machining cost; wrong tool = 5× cost overrun.
> - **Quality:** Wrong rake angle → poor surface finish, high residual stress.
> - **Safety:** Wrong clearance → tool rubs, work heats up, may cause **catastrophic tool failure**.
> - **Productivity:** Correct carbide grade + proper geometry can **double** tool life on CNC lines.

This is why KTU insists on **physical identification** and **measurement** of tool angles in the lab — a skilled mechanical engineer must *read* a tool like a doctor reads an X-ray.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 2 Derivation Bank.** Each derivation is fully worked out. No step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation — Orthogonal to Normal Rake Angle Conversion

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider an oblique cutting tool. The cutting edge $S-S'$ makes an **inclination angle** $\lambda_s$ with the **reference plane** $R-R'$. The tool normal plane $N-N'$ is perpendicular to the cutting-edge *projection* on $R-R'$.

### 3.1.2 Statement to Prove

$$
\tan \gamma_0 \;=\; \tan \gamma_n \cdot \cos \lambda_s
$$

### 3.1.3 Geometric Derivation (line-by-line)

**Step 1 — Define orthogonal and normal rake angles in their own planes.**

In the **orthogonal plane** (perpendicular to cutting edge $S-S'$), the rake angle $\gamma_0$ is the angle between the **rake face** and the **reference plane** measured in that plane.

In the **normal plane** (perpendicular to the cutting-edge projection), the rake angle $\gamma_n$ is similarly defined.

**Step 2 — Express the elevation of the rake face above the reference plane as a single line.**

Let a unit vector along the **rake-face up-direction** in the orthogonal plane be $\vec{u}_0$. Its components relative to the reference plane and the cutting-edge direction are:

- Component normal to reference plane: $\sin \gamma_0$
- Component along cutting edge: $\cos \gamma_0$

**Step 3 — Rotate the coordinate system by $\lambda_s$ to align with the normal plane.**

The normal plane is obtained by rotating the orthogonal plane about the **cutting-velocity axis** by angle $\lambda_s$. The component along the reference plane that lies *perpendicular to the cutting-edge projection* is scaled by $\cos \lambda_s$:

$$
\sin \gamma_n \;=\; \sin \gamma_0 \cdot \cos \lambda_s \;+\; 0 \cdot \sin \lambda_s
$$

(The cross-term vanishes because we rotate *about* the velocity axis.)

**Step 4 — Rewrite using tangent.**

$$
\tan \gamma_n \;=\; \frac{\sin \gamma_n}{\cos \gamma_n}
$$

For small-to-moderate angles, $\cos \gamma_n \approx \cos \gamma_0$, hence:

$$
\tan \gamma_n \;\approx\; \tan \gamma_0 \cdot \cos \lambda_s
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\gamma_0$.**

$$
\boxed{\;\tan \gamma_0 \;=\; \tan \gamma_n \cdot \cos \lambda_s\;}
$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Example

Given: $\gamma_n = 12°$, $\lambda_s = 20°$. Compute $\gamma_0$.

- $\tan \gamma_n = \tan 12° = 0.2126$
- $\cos \lambda_s = \cos 20° = 0.9397$
- $\tan \gamma_0 = 0.2126 \times 0.9397 = 0.1998$
- $\gamma_0 = \tan^{-1}(0.1998) = 11.30°$

**Interpretation:** Because the cutting edge is inclined by 20°, the *effective* working rake (orthogonal) is slightly *smaller* than the normal rake.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Merchant's Shear Angle Equation

### 3.2.1 Setup

In orthogonal cutting, the chip slides up the rake face. A thin **shear plane** (zone) is inclined at shear angle $\phi$ to the cutting velocity. Known quantities:

- Uncut chip thickness $t_1$
- Chip thickness $t_2$
- Rake angle $\alpha$ (use this for clearance; use $\gamma$ for rake. Some texts use $\alpha$ for clearance; KTU uses $\gamma$ for rake; we follow Merchant's notation: $\alpha$ = clearance angle.)

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Draw the geometry.** From chip-flow triangle:

$$
\sin \phi \;=\; \frac{t_1 \cos \alpha}{l_s}
$$

where $l_s$ = length of shear plane.

**Step 2 — Chip-thickness relation.**

$$
\cos(\phi - \alpha) \;=\; \frac{l_s \cos \alpha}{t_2}
$$

Dividing Step 1 by Step 2:

$$
\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} \;=\; \frac{t_1}{t_2 \cos \alpha} \;=\; \frac{r_c}{\cos \alpha}
$$

**Step 3 — Use $\cos(\phi - \alpha) = \cos \phi \cos \alpha + \sin \phi \sin \alpha$.**

$$
\frac{\sin \phi}{\cos \phi \cos \alpha + \sin \phi \sin \alpha} \;=\; \frac{r_c}{\cos \alpha}
$$

**Step 4 — Cross-multiply.**

$$
\sin \phi \cdot \cos \alpha \;=\; r_c \cdot \bigl(\cos \phi \cos \alpha + \sin \phi \sin \alpha\bigr)
$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $\cos \phi \cdot \cos \alpha$.**

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{r_c}{1 \;-\; r_c \tan \alpha}
$$

Or, using $\sin \alpha = \tan \alpha / \sqrt{1 + \tan^2 \alpha}$ and simplifying (multiply numerator and denominator by $\cos \alpha$):

$$
\boxed{\;\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha}\;}
$$

### 3.2.3 Numerical Worked Example

Given: $t_1 = 0.30$ mm, $t_2 = 0.60$ mm, rake angle $\gamma = 10°$, clearance $\alpha = 6°$.

**Step 1 — Cutting ratio.**
$$
r_c = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.30}{0.60} = 0.5
$$

**Step 2 — Plug into Merchant.**
$$
\tan \phi = \frac{0.5 \times \cos 6°}{1 - 0.5 \times \sin 6°} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.9945}{1 - 0.5 \times 0.1045} = \frac{0.4973}{0.9478} = 0.5248
$$

**Step 3 — Shear angle.**
$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.5248) \approx 27.7°
$$

**Interpretation:** With a moderate positive rake and chip-thickness ratio 0.5, the shear plane is shallow — chip slides easily, cutting force is low.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Tool Tip Geometry (Sum of Angles = 90°)

In the **orthogonal plane** of an SPCT, three angles meet at the cutting tip:

- Orthogonal rake angle $\gamma_0$
- Orthogonal clearance angle $\alpha_0$ (sometimes called $\beta_0$)
- Tool cutting-edge angle $\beta_0$ (between cutting edge and horizontal in some texts)

The **identity** in the orthogonal plane:

$$
\gamma_0 \;+\; \alpha_0 \;+\; \beta_{\text{tip}} \;=\; 90°
$$

**Proof (trigonometric):** Project the tool wedge onto the orthogonal plane. The three angles around the wedge tip at the reference plane cover one **right angle** (since the tool tip angle = $90°$ in this plane). Hence the algebraic sum is $90°$.

> **KTU Quick-Test:** If $\gamma_0 = 14°$, $\alpha_0 = 6°$, then $\beta_{\text{tip}} = 70°$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Taylor's Tool-Life Equation (supporting theory)

$$
V \cdot T^{n} \;=\; C
$$

- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $T$ = tool life (min)
- $n$ = tool-life exponent (depends on tool–work pair)
- $C$ = constant (depends on tool–work, depth, feed, hardness)

### 3.4.1 Linearization (used to find $n$ and $C$ experimentally)

Take logarithms:

$$
\log V \;=\; \log C \;-\; n \log T
$$

Plotting $\log V$ vs. $\log T$ gives a straight line with slope $-n$ and intercept $\log C$.

### 3.4.2 Typical Values (KTU Reference Table)

| Tool Material | $n$ | $C$ (m/min) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Carbon Steel | 0.10 | 70 | Old, low-speed |
| HSS | 0.125 | 200 | Drills, taps |
| Carbide | 0.25 | 500 | Production turning |
| Ceramic | 0.40 | 900 | High-speed finishing |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — "Tool Selection" Decision Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This matrix is a *KTU viva-favourite*.** A student is asked: "I want to machine EN8 steel at 200 m/min with HRC 55. Which tool would you choose?"

| Workpiece Condition | Recommended Tool Material | Recommended Geometry |
|---|---|---|
| Soft aluminum (HB 30) | HSS or PCD | High positive rake (+15° to +25°), large clearance |
| Mild steel (HB 150) | HSS / Carbide P20 | Positive rake (+5° to +15°), 7° clearance |
| Hardened steel (HRC 55) | Carbide (K-grade) / CBN | Negative rake (-5° to -10°), small clearance |
| Cast iron (HB 200) | Carbide K10 / Ceramic | 0° to +5° rake, dry cut |
| Stainless steel | Carbide with TiN coating | +5° to +10° rake, +ve chip-breaker |
| High-temp superalloy (Inconel) | CBN / Ceramic | Negative rake, low cutting speed, heavy feed |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — "Tool Failure Diagnosis"

| Symptom Observed on Tool | Probable Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Flank wear** (uniform band on clearance face) | Insufficient clearance angle, low hardness, abrasive workpiece | Increase $\alpha$, use harder tool, reduce $V$ |
| **Crater wear** (concave hollow on rake face) | Excessive rake → diffusion + abrasion at high T | Reduce rake, use coated carbide, lower $V$ |
| **Built-up edge (BUE)** | Cold welding of work material on tool tip — low T, sticky work | Increase $V$, use coolant, use positive rake |
| **Chipping of edge** | Interrupted cuts, low toughness, negative rake with weak tool | Use tougher grade (HSS, M-class carbide) |
| **Thermal cracking** | Severe thermal cycling without coolant | Use flood coolant, lower $V$ |
| **Plastic deformation** | Excessive heat, tool too soft | Use ceramic / CBN, reduce $V$ |
