# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** Define the following terms with a neat sketch:
(i) **Side Rake Angle** (ii) **End Clearance Angle** (iii) **Nose Radius**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

(i) **Side Rake Angle ($\alpha_s$):** The angle between the **rake face** of the tool and a plane containing the **cutting velocity vector** and **perpendicular to the reference plane**, measured on the side of the tool. It controls chip flow direction.

(ii) **End Clearance Angle ($\beta_b$):** The angle between the **end flank face** and a line drawn from the cutting tip parallel to the reference plane; provides physical relief to prevent rubbing of the flank on the machined surface.

(iii) **Nose Radius ($r$):** The radius of the rounded tip joining the major and end cutting edges. Larger nose radius → better surface finish and stronger tool tip, but higher cutting force and tendency to chatter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Marks deducted if (a) sketch is missing, (b) symbols are wrong ($\alpha_s$ vs $\alpha_b$ confusion), (c) clearance is not measured from reference plane. Always *label* the reference plane $R\text{-}R'$ in your diagram.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** List any **four tool materials** with **one typical application** each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Tool Material | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **High Speed Steel (HSS)** | Twist drills, taps, milling cutters (low-to-medium speed) |
| 2 | **Cemented Carbide** (WC + Co) | Turning inserts on steel and cast iron (medium-to-high speed) |
| 3 | **Ceramics** (Al₂O₃/Si₃N₄) | High-speed finishing of cast iron and superalloys |
| 4 | **Cubic Boron Nitride (CBN)** | Hard turning of hardened steels (HRC 50–65) |
| 5 | **Polycrystalline Diamond (PCD)** | High-speed machining of aluminum and copper alloys |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Writing only "carbide" without specifying *grade* (P, K, M) loses 1 mark. Likewise, write "CBN" not "boron nitride."

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Module 2]`

> **Q-A (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, explain the **ASA tool nomenclature** of a single-point cutting tool. List the **three reference planes** and the **seven tool angles**.

> **Q-A (b) [7 Marks]:** A turning operation is performed with the following data:
> - Uncut chip thickness $t_1 = 0.25$ mm
> - Chip thickness after cut $t_2 = 0.45$ mm
> - Rake angle $\gamma = +12°$
> - Clearance angle $\alpha = 6°$
>
> **Compute (i) cutting ratio $r_c$, (ii) shear angle $\phi$ using Merchant's equation.**

---

#### Model Answer — Q-A (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** *Tool nomenclature* is the standardized system (ASA / ISO 3002/1) used to describe the geometry of a single-point cutting tool through **3 reference planes** and **7 tool angles**.

**Three Reference Planes (3 marks — 1 each):**

| Plane | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Tool Reference Plane** | $R\text{-}R'$ | Perpendicular to tool shank; passes through tool point |
| **Tool Cutting-Edge Plane** | $M\text{-}M'$ | Parallel to cutting velocity; tangent to cutting edge at point |
| **Tool Normal Plane** | $N\text{-}N'$ | Perpendicular to cutting-edge projection on reference plane |

**Seven Tool Angles (3 marks — list all):**

1. **Back Rake Angle** ($\alpha_b$)
2. **Side Rake Angle** ($\alpha_s$)
3. **End Relief / Clearance Angle** ($\beta_b$)
4. **Side Clearance Angle** ($\beta_s$)
5. **Side Cutting Edge Angle** ($\phi_s$)
6. **End Cutting Edge Angle** ($\phi_e$)
7. **Nose Radius** ($r$)

*(Plus the orthogonal rake $\gamma_0$ and normal rake $\gamma_n$ are derivatives of the back and side rake.)*

**Sketch (Free-hand, must show):** Tool tip, reference plane $R\text{-}R'$, rake face, flank face, side cutting edge, end cutting edge, nose, and labels for $\alpha_s$, $\alpha_b$, $\beta_s$, $\beta_b$, $\phi_s$, $\phi_e$.

---

#### Model Answer — Q-A (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $t_1 = 0.25$ mm, $t_2 = 0.45$ mm
- $\gamma = 12°$, $\alpha = 6°$

**Step 1 — Cutting ratio (2 marks):**

$$
r_c \;=\; \frac{t_1}{t_2} \;=\; \frac{0.25}{0.45} \;=\; 0.5556
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Merchant's shear-angle formula (3 marks):**

$$
\tan \phi \;=\; \frac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha}
$$

Compute each term:
- $r_c \cos \alpha = 0.5556 \times \cos 6° = 0.5556 \times 0.9945 = 0.5526$
- $r_c \sin \alpha = 0.5556 \times \sin 6° = 0.5556 \times 0.1045 = 0.0581$
- Denominator $= 1 - 0.0581 = 0.9419$

$$
\tan \phi = \frac{0.5526}{0.9419} = 0.5868
$$

**Step 3 — Shear angle (2 marks):**

$$
\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.5868) \approx 30.4°
$$

**Result:** $\boxed{r_c = 0.556, \quad \phi \approx 30.4°}$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students write $r = t_2 / t_1 = 1.8$ by mistake — that is **chip thickness ratio**, not **cutting ratio**. Cutting ratio is *always* $t_1/t_2$.
> - Forget to convert degrees to radians in $\sin/\cos$ — this gives wrong answer.
> - Merchant uses **clearance angle $\alpha$**, not rake angle $\gamma$, in the denominator. Wrong variable used → 4 marks lost.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q-B (a) [7 Marks]:** Compare the **properties and applications** of the following tool materials: (i) High Speed Steel, (ii) Cemented Carbide, (iii) Ceramics, (iv) CBN.

> **Q-B (b) [7 Marks]:** With a neat sketch, describe the **geometry of a twist drill**, listing **point angle, helix angle, lip clearance angle**, and the **function of chisel edge**.

---

#### Model Answer — Q-B (a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (4 marks for table + 3 marks for application discussion):**

| Property | HSS | Carbide (WC-Co) | Ceramics (Al₂O₃) | CBN |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Hot Hardness (HV @ 600°C) | 500 | 900 | 1500 | 3000 |
| Toughness (K_IC) | High | Medium | Low | Medium |
| Compositions | Fe + W + Cr + V + Co | WC grains + Co binder | Al₂O₃ / Si₃N₄ | Poly-CBN + binder |
| Cutting Speed (m/min, on steel) | 30–60 | 100–250 | 300–800 | 150–300 |
| Cost | Low | Medium-High | High | Very High |
| Main Application | Drills, taps, form tools, reamers | Turning inserts, milling cutters | High-speed finishing of CI, superalloys | Hard turning of HRC > 50 steel |
| Can cut steel? | Yes | Yes | No (reacts with Fe) | Yes (best for hardened) |
| Coated versions? | TiN-coated HSS available | Common (TiAlN, AlCrN) | Rare | TiN, TiAlN |

**Discussion (3 marks):**

- **HSS** is the *workhorse* of low-to-medium-speed operations and complex geometries (drills, taps); it can be re-sharpened many times.
- **Carbide** offers 4× higher productivity than HSS at 3× the cost — used as **throw-away inserts** on CNC.
- **Ceramics** are *brittle* and chemically react with iron → used only on **cast iron, Ni-based superalloys, and hardened steels with care**.
- **CBN** is *second only to diamond* in hardness, stable with iron — used for **hard turning replacing grinding**, especially on bearing steels (AISI 52100).

---

#### Model Answer — Q-B (b) [7 marks]

**Sketch (3 marks)** — A twist drill has:
- **Point** with two **lips** (main cutting edges)
- **Chisel edge** connecting the two lips
- **Helical flutes** for chip evacuation
- **Body** with **margin** (bearing surface) and **lands**
- **Shank** (tapered Morse or straight cylindrical)

**Geometrical Parameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Typical Value | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Point Angle** | 118° (steel), 135° (CI), 140° (Al) | Determines lip angle, cutting aggression |
| **Helix Angle** | 20°–40° (HSS), 25°–35° (carbide) | Determines chip flow direction (up vs sideways) |
| **Lip Clearance** | 9°–15° | Relief behind cutting edge to prevent rubbing |
| **Chisel Edge Length** | ~0.18 × D | Acts as a *ploughing* edge — slow, generates heat |

**Chisel Edge Function (1 mark):** The chisel edge is the **web-thickened ridge** between the two lips at the drill point. It is a *negative rake* cutting edge that *ploughs* (pushes) the metal rather than shears it, generating significant **thrust force** (~50% of total feed force) and heat. To reduce its effect, *split-point* drills are used which grind a notch into the chisel edge.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Students forget to label **point angle** distinctly from **helix angle** — examiner deducts 1 mark.
> - Some students state *chisel edge is the cutting edge* — wrong; it *ploughs*, it does not shear.
> - Do **not** write point angle = 90°; it must be **118°** for steel, else lip clearance becomes negative.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Consolidated Pitfall List (Read Before Every KTU Module-2 Paper):**
>
> 1. **Cutting ratio vs. chip thickness ratio** — these are reciprocals; do not interchange.
> 2. **Rake angle sign convention** — always state *positive*, *negative*, or *zero*; a number alone is incomplete.
> 3. **Tool angles require a reference plane** — always mention $R\text{-}R'$ when defining an angle.
> 4. **Ceramics cannot machine steel** — chemical reaction with Fe at high T. Examiner marks down "Ceramics used for steel turning."
> 5. **CBN ≠ Diamond** — Diamond reacts with Fe; CBN does not. Reversing this loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Taylor's $n$ value is material-dependent** — quoting $n=0.25$ for HSS (which is closer to 0.125) is wrong.
> 7. **Helix angle ≠ Point angle** on a drill — common confusion in sketches.
> 8. **Sketch must be neat** with *labelled planes, labelled angles, and a title* — examiners reward labelled diagrams.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 2 Final Revision Checklist — Cover every bullet before the exam.**

### Tool Nomenclature
- [ ] 3 reference planes: **R–R′ (reference), M–M′ (cutting-edge), N–N′ (normal)**.
- [ ] 7 tool angles: **back rake, side rake, normal rake, end clearance, side clearance, normal clearance, side & end cutting-edge angles** + nose radius.
- [ ] Tool tip identity: **$\gamma_0 + \alpha_0 + \beta_{\text{tip}} = 90°$** in the orthogonal plane.
- [ ] Oblique conversion: **$\tan \gamma_0 = \tan \gamma_n \cdot \cos \lambda_s$**.

### Cutting Mechanics
- [ ] Cutting ratio **$r_c = t_1/t_2$** (always $<1$).
- [ ] Merchant: **$\tan \phi = \dfrac{r_c \cos \alpha}{1 - r_c \sin \alpha}$**.
- [ ] Ern–Merchant: **$\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta_s/2$**.
- [ ] Positive rake ↑ → $\phi$ ↑ → cutting force ↓ but tool strength ↓.
- [ ] Friction angle: **$\beta_s = \tan^{-1}\mu$**.

### Tool Materials
- [ ] Hierarchy by hot-hardness: **HSS → Carbide → Cermet → Ceramic → CBN → PCD → Diamond**.
- [ ] Trade-off: hardness ↑ ⟷ toughness ↓.
- [ ] HSS: drills, taps, reamers, form tools; speeds 30–60 m/min.
- [ ] Carbide: turning, milling; speeds 100–250 m/min; throw-away inserts.
- [ ] Ceramics: CI, superalloys; **cannot machine steel** (Fe reaction).
- [ ] CBN: hardened steel (HRC > 50); replaces grinding in some applications.
- [ ] PCD: aluminum, copper, composites; **cannot machine steel or titanium**.
- [ ] Coatings: **TiN (gold)**, **TiAlN (purple-gray)**, **AlCrN (dark gray)**, **DLC (black)**.

### Multi-Point Tools
- [ ] Twist drill: **point angle 118°** (steel), **helix 30°**, **lip clearance 9°–15°**.
- [ ] Reamer: finishing, 0.05–0.3 mm stock removal, 6–16 flutes.
- [ ] Milling cutter: peripheral teeth; up vs down milling matters.
- [ ] Hob: gear-cutting generating tool; helical teeth.
- [ ] Broach: linear internal/external profiling.

### Failure Modes
- [ ] **Flank wear** → increase clearance, reduce speed.
- [ ] **Crater wear** → reduce rake, use coated carbide.
- [ ] **Built-up edge** → increase speed, use coolant.
- [ ] **Chipping** → use tougher grade, positive rake, reduce interruptions.

### Viva Quick-Fire
- [ ] What is the difference between **single point and multi-point** cutting tool?
- [ ] Why is **negative rake used for hard materials**?
- [ ] Why is **carbide not used at very low cutting speeds**?
- [ ] What is a **chip breaker**? Why is it needed in SPCT?
- [ ] State **Taylor's tool-life equation** and the meaning of $n$ and $C$.
- [ ] Name the **three reference planes** in 15 seconds.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Tool Wear and Tool Life
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Tool Wear and Tool Life

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Wear** is defined as the gradual loss of material from the cutting tool due to physical and chemical interactions between the tool, workpiece, and chip during the machining process. It is a progressive phenomenon and is measured as the change in tool geometry over time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tool Life** is the actual cutting time (in minutes) that elapses between the moment a new tool is first used for cutting and the moment when the tool is declared "worn out" (failure criterion reached), or the volume of material removed before the tool is replaced.

The standard end-of-life criterion adopted by **ISO 3685** for a single-point turning tool is a **Flank Wear Width (VB) = 0.3 mm** (for uniform wear) or a maximum **VBmax = 0.5 mm** (at any point). The end-of-life criterion for **Crater Wear (KT) = 0.06 + 0.3f**, where *f* is the feed in mm/rev.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **sharpening a pencil with a knife** continuously. Each stroke removes wood and graphite. After a while:
- The knife blade edge becomes **rounded and dull** (similar to **flank wear**).
- A small crater forms on the top face of the blade where it rubs the wood (similar to **crater wear**).

Now imagine you have 5 different knives, and you sharpen the same pencil 100 times with each at different speeds. The faster you sharpen, the faster the knife becomes dull — the knife "lives" for a shorter time. This time is the **Tool Life**, and the dulling is the **Tool Wear**.

In production engineering, we replace "knife" with a **cutting tool insert** (HSS, Carbide, Ceramic, CBN, PCD), the "pencil" with the **workpiece** (mild steel, aluminum, etc.), and the "sharpening strokes" with the **passes of the tool** under controlled **cutting speed, feed, and depth of cut**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Flank Wear Land Width (VB)** measured in **mm** — primary wear criterion.
- **Crater Wear Depth (KT)** measured in **mm**.
- **Cutting Speed (V)** measured in **m/min**.
- **Feed (f)** measured in **mm/rev**.
- **Depth of Cut (d)** measured in **mm**.
- **Tool Life (T)** measured in **minutes** (preferred) or **number of pieces**.
- **Material Removal Rate (MRR)** measured in **mm³/s** or **cm³/min**.
- **Average Tool Life Taylor Exponent 'n'** is typically **0.20 to 0.35** for HSS tools and **0.25 to 0.45** for carbide tools.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU Lab examinations, students are expected to record flank wear width (VB) at regular time intervals using a **Tool Maker's Microscope (TMM)**, plot a wear curve (VB vs. cutting time), and determine the **Taylor's tool life equation** *VT^n = C* experimentally.

## 1.4 Significance in Manufacturing

Tool wear directly affects:
- **Surface finish (Ra)** of the machined surface.
- **Dimensional accuracy** of the workpiece.
- **Cutting forces and power consumption**.
- **Chip formation** and **chip-breaker action**.
- **Economics of machining** (tool cost + machine cost per component).
</br>

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanisms of Tool Wear

Tool wear in metal cutting is caused by **five fundamental mechanisms** acting individually or in combination:

1. **Abrasive Wear** — Hard micro-constituents (carbides, oxides) in the workpiece mechanically plough through the softer tool surface. Most prominent at **low cutting speeds (V < 60 m/min)**.
2. **Adhesive Wear** — Micro-welding junctions formed between tool and chip fracture at the weaker side, causing fragments of the tool to be torn away. Dominant in **medium cutting speeds (60 – 120 m/min)**.
3. **Diffusion Wear** — At high temperatures (>800 °C), atoms of tool material (e.g., W, Co, Ti) diffuse into the freshly formed chip, weakening the tool surface. Dominant at **high cutting speeds with carbides**.
4. **Oxidative / Chemical Wear** — Atmospheric oxygen reacts with tool material to form oxides (e.g., Fe₃O₄ on HSS) that are sheared away by the moving chip. Common in **dry machining of steels**.
5. **Thermo-Mechanical Fatigue** — Repeated heating and cooling cycles cause micro-cracks on the rake face, leading to chipping and flaking.

## 2.2 Types / Regions of Tool Wear (Single Point Turning Tool)

- **A — Flank Wear (VB)**: Wear on the relief (flank) face due to rubbing against the machined surface. Characterized by **VB (average)** and **VBmax**.
- **B — Crater Wear (KT)**: A depression on the rake face caused by chip flow. Characterized by depth **KT** and width **KM**.
- **C — Built-Up Edge (BUE)**: A wedge of workpiece material welded to the tool tip at low-to-medium speeds, which periodically breaks off, taking a tiny piece of tool material.
- **D — Notch Wear**: Localized wear at the depth-of-cut line, due to work-hardened layer of workpiece.
- **E — Nose Wear**: Combination of flank and crater wear concentrated at the nose radius.
- **F — Chipping / Fracture**: Mechanical failure of the cutting edge (catastrophic).

## 2.3 Three Stages of Wear Curve (KTU High-Yield)

When **Flank Wear Width (VB)** is plotted against **Cutting Time (t)**, a classic **three-stage curve** is obtained:

- **Stage I — Initial Wear (Run-in)**: Rapid wear due to small initial contact area, high contact stresses, and bedding-in of tool geometry.
- **Stage II — Steady-State Wear (Normal Wear)**: Linear region with constant wear rate. This stage constitutes **>90%** of tool life and is used for productive cutting.
- **Stage III — Rapid Wear (Failure)**: Wear rate accelerates due to loss of thermal equilibrium, excessive heat generation, and tool geometry collapse. Continued use leads to **catastrophic tool failure**.

## 2.4 Taylor's Tool Life Equation — The Backbone of the Experiment

**Frederick Winslow Taylor (1907)** proposed the foundational empirical relation:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

Where:
- **V** = Cutting speed in m/min
- **T** = Tool life in minutes
- **n** = Taylor exponent (tool-life-slope index) — dimensionless
- **C** = Taylor constant (intercept) — depends on tool, workpiece, feed, depth of cut, and cutting environment.

The **Extended Taylor Equation** (with multiple variables) is:

$$V \cdot T^{n} \cdot f^{a} \cdot d^{b} = K$$

Where:
- **f** = feed (mm/rev), **a** = feed exponent
- **d** = depth of cut (mm), **b** = depth-of-cut exponent
- **K** = combined constant

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **high-yield formula table** students must memorize for the lab record and viva.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Variable | Standard Unit | Typical Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V \cdot T^{n} = C$ | Taylor's Tool Life Equation | V: m/min, T: min | n = 0.2 – 0.45 |
| 2 | $T = \left(\dfrac{C}{V}\right)^{1/n}$ | Tool life from Taylor's eqn | minutes | — |
| 3 | $V_{T_2} = V_{T_1} \left(\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^{n}$ | Cutting speed for a desired tool life | m/min | — |
| 4 | $\log V = \log C - n \log T$ | Linearized form for plotting | — | — |
| 5 | $n = \dfrac{\log V_1 - \log V_2}{\log T_2 - \log T_1}$ | Exponent from two speed-life pairs | dimensionless | 0.2 – 0.45 |
| 6 | $C = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n}$ | Taylor constant | depends on V | — |
| 7 | $C_T = \dfrac{60 \cdot C_O}{T + C_T \cdot T_h}$ | Total machining cost per component | ₹/piece | — |
| 8 | $MRR = 1000 \cdot V \cdot f \cdot d$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min | — |
| 9 | $VB_{limit} = 0.3 \text{ mm}$ | ISO flank-wear end-of-life criterion | mm | Uniform wear |
| 10 | $KT_{limit} = 0.06 + 0.3f$ | ISO crater-wear criterion | mm | function of f |
| 11 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V / 60$ | Cutting power | Watts | — |
| 12 | $U = \dfrac{C_T + C_O \cdot T}{T}$ | Optimum cost condition derivative | ₹/min | — |

> **Engineering Utility:** Taylor's equation is the basis of every CNC **Machining Database** (e.g., Sandvik Coromant, Kennametal). The exponent **n** quantifies **how sensitively tool life changes with cutting speed** — a low *n* means small speed changes cause large life changes, demanding strict speed control.

## 2.6 Real-World Application

In a CNC turning cell producing **5,000 batches of a pin shaft per shift**, the process planner must:
1. Use Taylor's equation to fix **Vc** for the chosen insert grade and workpiece.
2. Set the **tool-change alarm** in the CNC controller based on calculated T.
3. Forecast **insert consumption per month** for cost control and procurement.
4. Decide between **dry, wet (flood), or MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication)** conditions.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Linearized Form of Taylor's Equation

Taylor's empirical relation in its original form is:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C \quad \text{--- (1)}$$

To plot the relation on standard graph paper and to determine the constants *n* and *C* from experimental data, we take the **logarithm (base 10)** of both sides.

**Step 1 — Apply log₁₀ to both sides of (1):**

$$\log_{10}(V \cdot T^{n}) = \log_{10} C$$

**Step 2 — Use the logarithm product rule (log(a·b) = log a + log b):**

$$\log_{10} V + \log_{10}(T^{n}) = \log_{10} C$$

**Step 3 — Use the logarithm power rule (log(a^b) = b · log a):**

$$\log_{10} V + n \cdot \log_{10} T = \log_{10} C$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange to the standard "y = mx + c" straight-line format:**

$$\log_{10} V = -\,n \cdot \log_{10} T + \log_{10} C$$

**Step 5 — Identification of slope and intercept:**

- **Slope** of the line (when log V is plotted on y-axis and log T on x-axis) = **−n**
- **Intercept** on the y-axis = **log C**

Hence, *n* is obtained from the slope, and *C* is obtained from the anti-log of the intercept. This is the **experimental method** used in the KTU lab to verify Taylor's equation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Exponent 'n' from Two Experimental Data Points

Suppose the experiment is run at two cutting speeds **V₁** and **V₂**, producing tool lives **T₁** and **T₂** respectively. Taylor's equation applied to both:

$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = C \quad \text{--- (2)}$$
$$V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = C \quad \text{--- (3)}$$

**Step 1 — Equate (2) and (3) (since both equal C):**

$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n}$$

**Step 2 — Divide both sides by V₂ · T₁ⁿ:**

$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{T_2^{n}}{T_1^{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the powers on the right-hand side:**

$$\dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Take log₁₀ of both sides:**

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right) = n \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for n:**

$$n = \dfrac{\log_{10}(V_1) - \log_{10}(V_2)}{\log_{10}(T_2) - \log_{10}(T_1)}$$

This is the **exact form** of the KTU lab formula. The constant *C* is then computed as:

$$C = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Cutting Speed for a Desired Tool Life

In industrial practice, the question is often: *“At what cutting speed should the operator run the machine to obtain a tool life of T₂ minutes, if the catalogue gives V₁ at T₁ minutes?”*

Starting from equation (2) and (3) equating to C:

$$V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = V_2 \cdot T_2^{n}$$

**Step 1 — Solve for V₂:**

$$V_2 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_1}{T_2}\right)^{n}$$

**Interpretation:**
- If **T₂ < T₁** (we want shorter life for higher productivity), then **V₂ > V₁** (we must increase cutting speed).
- If **T₂ > T₁** (we want longer life for tool-cost reduction), then **V₂ < V₁** (we must reduce cutting speed).

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for the Lab Record / End-Semester**

**Given:**
- A turning operation on **mild steel (EN8)** with a **carbide insert (P20 grade)** at depth of cut *d* = 1.5 mm and feed *f* = 0.2 mm/rev.
- Experimentally determined data:
  - At **V₁ = 120 m/min**, tool life **T₁ = 60 min**.
  - At **V₂ = 180 m/min**, tool life **T₂ = 15 min**.

**Step 1 — Calculate the Taylor exponent n:**

$$n = \dfrac{\log(120) - \log(180)}{\log(15) - \log(60)}$$

$$n = \dfrac{2.0792 - 2.2553}{1.1761 - 1.7782}$$

$$n = \dfrac{-0.1761}{-0.6021} = 0.2925$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Taylor constant C:**

$$C = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = 120 \cdot 60^{0.2925}$$

$$60^{0.2925} = e^{0.2925 \cdot \ln 60} = e^{0.2925 \cdot 4.0943} = e^{1.1976} = 3.312$$

$$C = 120 \cdot 3.312 = 397.4$$

**Step 3 — Write the Taylor equation for this system:**

$$V \cdot T^{0.2925} = 397.4$$

**Step 4 — Find the cutting speed for a desired tool life of 30 min:**

$$V_3 = C \cdot T_3^{-n} = 397.4 \cdot 30^{-0.2925}$$

$$30^{-0.2925} = e^{-0.2925 \cdot \ln 30} = e^{-0.2925 \cdot 3.4012} = e^{-0.9949} = 0.3698$$

$$V_3 = 397.4 \cdot 0.3698 = 146.95 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the result using the alternative formula:**

$$V_3 = V_1 \cdot \left(\dfrac{T_1}{T_3}\right)^{n} = 120 \cdot \left(\dfrac{60}{30}\right)^{0.2925} = 120 \cdot (2)^{0.2925}$$

$$(2)^{0.2925} = e^{0.2925 \cdot 0.6931} = e^{0.2027} = 1.2246$$

$$V_3 = 120 \cdot 1.2246 = 146.95 \text{ m/min} \quad \checkmark$$

> **Conclusion:** A cutting speed of approximately **147 m/min** will yield a tool life of **30 min** for the given tool–workpiece combination. This matches both methods, confirming the validity of the experimental Taylor equation.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — HSS vs Carbide vs Ceramic

| Property | HSS Tool | Carbide Tool | Ceramic Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical cutting speed V | 25 – 70 m/min | 100 – 250 m/min | 300 – 800 m/min |
| Taylor exponent n | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.30 – 0.45 | 0.40 – 0.70 |
| Dominant wear mechanism | Abrasive / Adhesive | Diffusion / Adhesive | Diffusion / Chemical |
| Recommended coolant | Flood / Soluble oil | Dry / MQL (steel) | Dry (always) |
| Cost per cutting edge | Low | Medium | High |
| Sensitivity to speed change | Moderate | High | Very high |

## 3.6 Effect of Cutting Parameters on Tool Life (KTU Analysis)

- **Cutting Speed (V)**: Has the **largest effect** on tool life. Doubling *V* typically reduces T by **70 – 85%**.
- **Feed (f)**: Doubling *f* reduces T by **30 – 50%**. In Taylor's extended form, *f* has an exponent of **~0.5 to 0.7**.
- **Depth of Cut (d)**: Has the **smallest effect** on tool life among the three. Doubling *d* reduces T by **only 10 – 20%**.
- **Coolant Application**: Flood coolant can extend tool life by **50 – 200%** in continuous cutting of steels.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tool Wear Regions on a Single-Point Turning Tool

A single-point cutting tool has standardized wear zones referenced to **ISO 3685**. The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps these regions with a functional architecture approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Single Point Cutting Tool]:::tool
    A --> B[Rake Face Top]:::face
    A --> C[Flank Face Bottom]:::face
    A --> D[Cutting Edge]:::face
    A --> E[Nose Radius]:::face

    B --> B1["CRATER WEAR KT  depth KT width KM"]:::crater
    B --> B2["BUILT UP EDGE BUE  dynamic attach"]:::bue
    B --> B3["THERMAL CRACKS rake face fatigue"]:::crack

    C --> C1["FLANK WEAR VB average width"]:::flank
    C --> C2["NOTCH WEAR at DOC line work hardened"]:::notch
    C --> C3["FLANK CHIPPING mechanical fracture"]:::chip

    D --> D1["DEPTH OF CUT LINE contact line"]:::edge
    E --> E1["NOSE WEAR combined VB and KT"]:::nose

    classDef tool fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef face fill:#3a5f8a,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef crater fill:#8a3a3a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef bue fill:#8a6a3a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef crack fill:#5a3a5a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff

    classDef notch fill:#3a8a8a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef chip fill:#8a3a8a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef edge fill:#6a6a3a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef nose fill:#3a3a3a,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Tool Wear Curve — Three Stages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Time t equals 0 Fresh tool]:::s0 --> S1[STAGE I Initial run in 0 to 3 min]:::s1
    S1 --> S2[STAGE II Steady state linear 3 to 25 min]:::s2
    S2 --> S3[STAGE III Rapid failure 25 min onward]:::s3
    S3 --> S4[TOOL LIFE T at VB equals 0.3 mm]:::s4

    classDef s0 fill:#264653,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef s1 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef s2 fill:#e9c46a,stroke:#1a1a1a,color:#1a1a1a
    classDef s3 fill:#f4a261,stroke:#1a1a1a,color:#1a1a1a

```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Experimental Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Step 1 Prepare workpiece and tool insert]:::p --> P2[Step 2 Measure initial VB using Tool Makers Microscope]:::p
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Mount on lathe set V f d]:::p
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Start turning record cutting time t]:::p
    P4 --> P5{Step 5 Reach tool life criterion VB equals 0.3 mm}:::decide
    P5 -->|No| P6[Step 6 Stop lathe at intervals measure VB with TMM]:::p
    P6 --> P4
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Step 7 Record final tool life T in minutes]:::p
    P7 --> P8[Step 8 Repeat for 2 to 3 different cutting speeds]:::p
    P8 --> P9[Step 9 Plot log V versus log T graph]:::p
    P9 --> P10[Step 10 Find slope equals negative n and intercept log C]:::p
    P10 --> P11[Step 11 Write final Taylor equation V T power n equals C]:::p

    classDef p fill:#1d3557,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef decide fill:#e63946,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture of Wear-Mechanism vs Cutting-Speed Influence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[Cutting Speed V m per min]:::speed --> L[LOW V below 60]:::regime
    S --> M[MEDIUM V 60 to 120]:::regime
    S --> H[HIGH V 120 to 250]:::regime
    S --> VH[VERY HIGH V above 250]:::regime

    L --> LW[Abrasive wear dominant]:::wear
    M --> MW[Adhesive and BUE]:::wear
    H --> HW[Diffusion and Oxidation]:::wear
    VH --> VHW[Plastic deformation and chemical wear]:::wear

    classDef speed fill:#0a2342,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
    classDef regime fill:#2ca58d,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef wear fill:#8338ec,stroke:#ffd700,color:#ffffff
```
</br>

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Define the term "Flank Wear" in a single-point cutting tool. State the ISO criterion for tool-life end.**

**Model Answer:**
Flank wear is the gradual loss of material from the **flank face (relief face)** of a single-point cutting tool due to rubbing between the tool and the newly machined workpiece surface. It is quantified as the **flank wear land width VB**, measured perpendicular to the cutting edge.

According to **ISO 3685**, the tool is considered to have reached end-of-life when:
- The **average flank wear VB = 0.3 mm** (uniform wear criterion), **OR**
- The **maximum flank wear VBmax = 0.5 mm** (localized wear criterion), **OR**
- The **crater wear depth KT = 0.06 + 0.3 f**, where *f* is the feed in mm/rev.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State Taylor's tool-life equation. What do the terms 'n' and 'C' signify?**

**Model Answer:**
Taylor's tool-life equation (1907) is:

$$V \cdot T^{n} = C$$

Where:
- **V** = cutting speed in m/min.
- **T** = tool life in minutes.
- **n** = **Taylor's exponent**, a dimensionless constant that represents the sensitivity of tool life to cutting speed. Higher *n* implies a more wear-resistant tool.
- **C** = **Taylor's constant**, a constant determined by the tool–workpiece pair, feed, depth of cut, and cutting environment. Numerically equal to the cutting speed that would give a tool life of 1 minute.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** Explain the **three stages of the tool wear curve** with a neat sketch. Discuss the dominant wear mechanism in each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a turning experiment on mild steel using a carbide tool (feed = 0.2 mm/rev, depth of cut = 1.5 mm), the following data were recorded. Verify Taylor's tool-life equation and determine the **cutting speed for a tool life of 45 minutes**. **(7 marks)**

| Experiment | Cutting Speed V (m/min) | Tool Life T (min) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 90 | 90 |
| 2 | 120 | 30 |
| 3 | 150 | 12 |

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Stage I — Initial Run-in Wear (0 to ~5% of T):**
The freshly ground tool has a small contact area between the flank and the workpiece, producing **high contact pressure**. Wear rate is rapid. The tool tip "beds in" to its working geometry. **Dominant mechanism:** Adhesive micro-welding and abrasion at micro-peaks.

**Stage II — Steady-State (Normal) Wear (5% to ~95% of T):**
The wear rate becomes approximately **constant and linear** with cutting time. The tool maintains a stable contact area. This is the productive working region. **Dominant mechanism:** Steady abrasion and mild diffusion. Tool life calculations are based on this linear rate.

**Stage III — Rapid Failure Wear (95% to 100% of T):**
Heat generation rises steeply because the worn tool geometry (large VB) increases rubbing. The cutting temperature escalates, accelerating diffusion and oxidation. Failure is sudden and may be **catastrophic (chipping or fracture)**. **Dominant mechanism:** Severe diffusion, oxidation, and plastic deformation.

*Sketch — Refer to the three-stage wear curve in Section 4.2.*

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Compute log V and log T for each experiment:**

| Experiment | V (m/min) | T (min) | log V | log T |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 90 | 90 | 1.9542 | 1.9542 |
| 2 | 120 | 30 | 2.0792 | 1.4771 |
| 3 | 150 | 12 | 2.1761 | 1.0792 |

**Step 2 — Compute n between Experiments 1 and 2:**

$$n = \dfrac{\log V_1 - \log V_2}{\log T_2 - \log T_1} = \dfrac{1.9542 - 2.0792}{1.4771 - 1.9542} = \dfrac{-0.1250}{-0.4771} = 0.2620$$

**Step 3 — Compute n between Experiments 2 and 3:**

$$n = \dfrac{2.0792 - 2.1761}{1.0792 - 1.4771} = \dfrac{-0.0969}{-0.3979} = 0.2435$$

**Step 4 — Average exponent n:**

$$n_{avg} = \dfrac{0.2620 + 0.2435}{2} = 0.2528 \approx 0.253$$

**Step 5 — Compute C using Experiment 1 data:**

$$C = V_1 \cdot T_1^{n} = 90 \cdot 90^{0.253}$$

$$90^{0.253} = e^{0.253 \cdot \ln 90} = e^{0.253 \cdot 4.4998} = e^{1.1384} = 3.121$$

$$C = 90 \cdot 3.121 = 280.9 \approx 281$$

**Step 6 — Verify with Experiment 2:**

$$V_2 \cdot T_2^{n} = 120 \cdot 30^{0.253}$$

$$30^{0.253} = e^{0.253 \cdot 3.4012} = e^{0.8605} = 2.364$$

$$120 \cdot 2.364 = 283.7 \approx 284 \;\; (\text{close to 281, small experimental error}) \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Verify with Experiment 3:**

$$150 \cdot 12^{0.253}$$

$$12^{0.253} = e^{0.253 \cdot 2.4849} = e^{0.6287} = 1.875$$

$$150 \cdot 1.875 = 281.3 \approx 281 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Write the verified Taylor equation:**

$$V \cdot T^{0.253} = 281$$

**Step 9 — Find the cutting speed for a tool life of 45 minutes:**

$$V_{45} = \dfrac{281}{T^{n}} = \dfrac{281}{45^{0.253}}$$

$$45^{0.253} = e^{0.253 \cdot 3.8067} = e^{0.9631} = 2.620$$

$$V_{45} = \dfrac{281}{2.620} = 107.25 \text{ m/min}$$

**Conclusion:** The Taylor equation is **verified** and the required cutting speed for a 45-min tool life is **≈ 107 m/min**.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Q2]**

**(a)** With neat sketches, describe the **different types of tool wear** observed on a single-point cutting tool. Mention the operating condition that promotes each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During an orthogonal turning experiment on **EN8 mild steel with HSS tool**, the following data were obtained for three cutting speeds at constant *f* = 0.25 mm/rev and *d* = 2 mm:

| V (m/min) | 25 | 35 | 50 |
|---|---|---|---|
| T (min) | 90 | 30 | 8 |

Calculate: **(i)** Taylor's exponent *n*, **(ii)** constant *C*, and **(iii)** the cutting speed for a tool life of 20 minutes. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The single-point cutting tool exhibits **six principal wear types**:

1. **Flank Wear (VB)** — Wear on the **clearance (flank) face** due to rubbing against the freshly machined surface. Caused mainly by **abrasive and adhesive wear**. Promoted at **moderate cutting speeds**. (Sketch: rectangular wear land of width VB.)

2. **Crater Wear (KT)** — A crescent-shaped depression on the **rake face** caused by hot chip flow. Caused mainly by **diffusion and chemical wear**. Promoted at **high cutting speeds (>120 m/min) with carbide tools**. (Sketch: concave crater with depth KT and width KM.)

3. **Built-Up Edge (BUE)** — A wedge of **workpiece material welded** to the tool tip that periodically detaches. Caused by **adhesion at low-to-medium cutting temperatures (200 – 600 °C)**. Common when machining **ductile materials like low-carbon steel and aluminum** at speeds < 60 m/min. (Sketch: triangular projection at the cutting edge.)

4. **Notch Wear** — A **local groove at the depth-of-cut line** on the flank face. Caused by **work-hardening of the workpiece surface layer** and oxidation. Common in **machining of stainless steel and heat-resistant alloys**. (Sketch: V-groove at the nose on the flank.)

5. **Nose Wear** — Combined flank and crater wear concentrated at the **nose radius**. Caused by **highest stress and temperature** at the nose. Promoted in **finishing operations with small nose radii**.

6. **Chipping / Catastrophic Fracture** — Mechanical breakage of the cutting edge due to **excessive mechanical or thermal shock**. Promoted by **interrupted cutting, improper insert seating, or sudden overloads**. (Sketch: irregular chip-out on the edge.)

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Tabulate log values:**

| V (m/min) | T (min) | log V | log T |
|---|---|---|---|
| 25 | 90 | 1.3979 | 1.9542 |
| 35 | 30 | 1.5441 | 1.4771 |
| 50 | 8 | 1.6990 | 0.9031 |

**Step 2 — Compute n using Experiments 1 and 2:**

$$n = \dfrac{1.3979 - 1.5441}{1.4771 - 1.9542} = \dfrac{-0.1462}{-0.4771} = 0.3065$$

**Step 3 — Compute n using Experiments 2 and 3:**

$$n = \dfrac{1.5441 - 1.6990}{0.9031 - 1.4771} = \dfrac{-0.1549}{-0.5740} = 0.2699$$

**Step 4 — Average n:**

$$n = \dfrac{0.3065 + 0.2699}{2} = 0.2882 \approx 0.288$$

**Step 5 — Compute C using Experiment 1:**

$$C = 25 \cdot 90^{0.288}$$

$$90^{0.288} = e^{0.288 \cdot 4.4998} = e^{1.2959} = 3.653$$

$$C = 25 \cdot 3.653 = 91.3$$

**Step 6 — Verify with Experiment 2:**

$$35 \cdot 30^{0.288} = 35 \cdot e^{0.288 \cdot 3.4012} = 35 \cdot e^{0.9795} = 35 \cdot 2.664 = 93.2 \;\; (\text{within 2% of 91.3}) \checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Verify with Experiment 3:**

$$50 \cdot 8^{0.288} = 50 \cdot e^{0.288 \cdot 2.0794} = 50 \cdot e^{0.5989} = 50 \cdot 1.820 = 91.0 \;\; \checkmark$$

**Step 8 — Calculate V for T = 20 min:**

$$V_{20} = \dfrac{91.3}{20^{0.288}} = \dfrac{91.3}{e^{0.288 \cdot 2.9957}} = \dfrac{91.3}{e^{0.8628}} = \dfrac{91.3}{2.370} = 38.5 \text{ m/min}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) **n ≈ 0.288**
- (ii) **C ≈ 91.3**
- (iii) **V (for T = 20 min) ≈ 38.5 m/min**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Where Students Lose Marks)**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of C**: Always state units of C explicitly (e.g., *m/min · minⁿ* or simply state that C is a constant whose value is the cutting speed for T = 1 min).
> 2. **Averaging n incorrectly**: Some students take the arithmetic mean of T and V first, then compute n. **Always compute n from each pair separately, then average** for higher accuracy.
> 3. **Mixing up log V and log T in the slope formula**: Remember that the slope of the *log V vs. log T* plot is **−n**, not *+n*. A common sign error.
> 4. **Ignoring the wear criterion**: When plotting experimental data, students forget to specify the wear criterion (e.g., VB = 0.3 mm). The examiner expects a statement of the criterion at the end of the tool-life determination.
> 5. **Skipping the tool-maker's microscope mention**: For the experiment, always include measurement of VB using a **Tool Maker's Microscope (TMM)** with a typical **magnification 30×**, and resolution **0.01 mm**.
> 6. **No repeat trial / no spread of speeds**: Examiners expect at least **3 cutting speeds** with **wide enough range** (e.g., 60 m/min, 90 m/min, 120 m/min) to obtain a reliable log–log plot.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Tool Wear** is the **progressive loss of tool material**; **Tool Life** is the **operating time until failure**.
- **ISO 3685 end-of-life criterion** → **VB = 0.3 mm** (flank), **KT = 0.06 + 0.3f** (crater).
- **Five wear mechanisms**: Abrasive, Adhesive, Diffusion, Oxidative, Thermo-mechanical fatigue.
- **Six wear types**: Flank, Crater, BUE, Notch, Nose, Chipping.
- **Three stages of wear curve**: Initial run-in (steep), Steady-state (linear, used for production), Rapid failure (accelerated).
- **Taylor's Equation**: $V \cdot T^{n} = C$.
- **Linearized form**: $\log V = -n \log T + \log C$ — used for graphical determination of *n* and *C*.
- **Typical n values**: HSS → 0.20 – 0.30; Carbide → 0.30 – 0.45; Ceramic → 0.40 – 0.70.
- **Sensitivity order of cutting parameters on tool life**: **Cutting Speed > Feed > Depth of Cut**.
- **Measurement of VB** is done with a **Tool Maker's Microscope (TMM)** at standard magnification 30×.
- **BUE occurs at low cutting speeds** for ductile materials — increases surface roughness, can be minimized by **increasing V**, using **coated tools**, or **applying coolant**.
- **Crater wear is most prominent on the rake face at high speeds** — it is a **diffusion-controlled** phenomenon.
- **To extend tool life**: Reduce V (most effective), use coated inserts, use proper coolant, optimize tool geometry (positive rake for low strength workpieces, negative rake for hard materials).
- **Tool life equation is the foundation of**: CNC machining parameter selection, tool inventory management, and **cost-per-component calculation** ($C_T = C_O \cdot T_h + C_T \cdot T$).
- **Always state the wear criterion adopted** when presenting experimental results.
- **Always specify units**: V in m/min, T in min, f in mm/rev, d in mm.
- **Typical lab procedure**: Mount workpiece → set V, f, d → turn for a fixed time → stop and measure VB at multiple locations using TMM → repeat → plot log V vs. log T → find *n* and *C*.
</br>

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Experiment on Jigs/Fixtures and Their Uses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Experiment on Jigs/Fixtures and Their Uses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Production Engineering Lab syllabus, **Jigs** and **Fixtures** are classified under the broader domain of **Work-Holding Devices (WHDs)** used in mass production and modern manufacturing systems. They are special-purpose tools designed to hold, support, and locate the workpiece in a fixed, repeatable position during a manufacturing operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Definition (Board Examiner Wording):**
> A **Jig** is a work-holding device that **holds, supports, locates**, and most importantly **GUIDES the cutting tool** to perform an operation (typically drilling, reaming, tapping, boring). The tool is guided by **bushes** built into the jig body.
> A **Fixture** is a work-holding device that **holds, supports, and locates** the workpiece but **does NOT guide the cutting tool**. The tool path is controlled by the machine tool itself (e.g., milling cutter, grinding wheel).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you are getting a haircut at a barber shop:
- Your **head is the workpiece**.
- The **barber's chair with the headrest** acts as a **fixture** – it holds you firmly in a fixed position.
- The **barber's hand guiding the scissors** is like a **JIG** because the scissors (tool) are being guided along a controlled path to give a precise cut.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:**
> **"JIG = TOOL GUIDED"** → "JIG works for JOINING/DRILLING operations (where the tool needs help to be accurate)."
> **"FIXTURE = WORK HELD FIRMLY"** → "FIXTURE works for finishing/machining operations (where the machine, not the tool, decides the path)."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard **6 Degrees of Freedom (6 DOF)** must be fully constrained for a rigid body to be in a fixed position.
- The **3-2-1 Principle** uses only 6 contact points to fully constrain all 6 DOF – this is the **golden rule** of jig/fixture design.
- Clamping force is typically measured in **Newton (N)** or **Kilogram-force (kgf)** where 1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N.
- Standard drill bushes are hardened to **HRC 60–65** to withstand tool wear.
- Locating tolerances in KTU lab experiments are generally **±0.05 mm to ±0.1 mm**.

## 1.4 Essential Elements of a Jig/Fixture (KTU Board Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> Every jig/fixture has the following six essential elements. Examiners often ask this as a 3-mark direct question:
> 1. **Base/Body** – Main frame (often made of Cast Iron or Mild Steel)
> 2. **Locating Elements** – Pins, V-blocks, rest pads (define position)
> 3. **Clamping Elements** – Screws, levers, straps (resist cutting forces)
> 4. **Tool Guide Elements** – Drill bushes (only in JIGS)
> 5. **Setting/Supporting Elements** – Jack screws, support pads
> 6. **Indexing/Positioning Elements** – Used in indexing jigs (optional)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3-2-1 Principle of Location (The Heart of Jig/Fixture Design)

The **3-2-1 Principle** is a fundamental KTU syllabus concept for the location of workpieces. A free body in space has **6 DOF**: 3 translational (along X, Y, Z) and 3 rotational (about X, Y, Z). To fix the body, all 6 DOF must be eliminated using minimum 6 contact points.

### Logic Steps:

**Step 1 — Primary Locating Surface (3 Points):**
The first surface chosen should be the **largest, most accurate, and easily accessible** face of the workpiece. Place **3 support pads** (or pins) on it. This eliminates:
- 1 translational DOF (perpendicular movement)
- 2 rotational DOF (tilting and rocking)

**Step 2 — Secondary Locating Surface (2 Points):**
Choose a surface **perpendicular to the primary surface** and the **longest accessible face**. Place **2 locating pins/posts**. This eliminates:
- 2 translational DOF

**Step 3 — Tertiary Locating Surface (1 Point):**
Choose a surface **perpendicular to both primary and secondary** surfaces. Place **1 locating pin or button**. This eliminates the final 1 translational DOF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The 3 points must be placed on a **non-collinear** surface to prevent rocking. A 4th point on the primary surface is a **REDUNDANT LOCATOR** and leads to over-location, causing inaccuracies.

## 2.2 Force Analysis (The "Why" Behind Clamping)

When a cutting tool engages the workpiece, three kinds of forces try to disturb the workpiece's position:
- **Tangential Cutting Force (Ft)** – acts in the direction of tool rotation
- **Radial Force (Fr)** – acts perpendicular to the tool axis
- **Axial/Thrust Force (Fa)** – acts along the tool axis

The **clamping force (Fc)** must be **greater than the resultant of these cutting forces** multiplied by a **Safety Factor (n = 2 to 3)** to prevent slippage.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------|-------|
| 1 | DOF Constraint | $\sum \text{Contact Points} \geq 6$ | dimensionless | Minimum for full constraint |
| 2 | Tangential Cutting Force (Turning) | $F_t = C_s \cdot f^{xF} \cdot d^{yF} \cdot K_c$ | N | $C_s$ = specific cutting energy coeff. |
| 3 | Thrust Force in Drilling | $F_a = 0.5 \cdot F_t$ (approx) | N | Empirical KTU relation |
| 4 | Clamping Force | $F_c = n \cdot F_t$ | N | $n$ = safety factor (2–3) |
| 5 | Spindle Power | $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000}$ | kW | $V_c$ = cutting speed (m/min) |
| 6 | Torque on Drill | $T = F_t \cdot \dfrac{D}{2}$ | N·m | $D$ = drill diameter |
| 7 | Locator Error (3-2-1) | $e \leq 0.05 \, D$ | mm | General tolerance check |
| 8 | Chip Load (Drilling) | $f_z = \dfrac{V_f}{N}$ | mm/rev | $V_f$ = feed rate, $N$ = RPM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** In all formulas, use $\vert F_t \vert$ instead of $\vert F_t \vert$ in written form. When writing in tables, always use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Used in mass production of engine blocks, gearboxes, and chassis components where hundreds of identical parts are made daily.
- **Aerospace Industry:** Critical for high-precision drilling of aircraft wings and fuselage panels (positional accuracy $\pm 0.01$ mm).
- **Defense Manufacturing:** Production of ammunition components, where interchangeability is key.
- **General Manufacturing:** Reduces **set-up time by 60–80%** and **increases production rate by 2–4 times** compared to marking-out methods.
- **Interchangeability:** Enables parts to be made in different batches and assembled later without re-fitting, which is the **foundation of modern mass production**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clamping Force for a Milling Operation (KTU Model Solution Pattern)

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):**
A mild steel workpiece is being milled on a horizontal milling machine. The cutter diameter is $D = 80$ mm, depth of cut is $d = 4$ mm, feed per tooth is $f_z = 0.15$ mm/tooth, and the cutter has $Z = 8$ teeth. Cutting speed $V_c = 30$ m/min. Specific cutting force $k_s = 2000$ N/mm². Calculate the **required clamping force** with a safety factor of $n = 2.5$.

### Step 1: Compute the Spindle Speed (N)

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 30}{\pi \cdot 80}$$

$$N = \dfrac{30000}{251.327}$$

$$N \approx 119.37 \, \text{RPM}$$

The spindle rotates at approximately 119.37 revolutions per minute.

### Step 2: Compute the Feed Rate (Vf)

$$V_f = f_z \cdot Z \cdot N$$

$$V_f = 0.15 \cdot 8 \cdot 119.37$$

$$V_f = 0.15 \cdot 955.0$$

$$V_f = 143.25 \, \text{mm/min}$$

The feed rate is the total linear movement of the table per minute.

### Step 3: Compute the Average Uncut Chip Thickness (h)

$$h = \dfrac{2 \cdot V_f}{N \cdot Z \cdot \sqrt{D \cdot d}}$$

For a simplified KTU derivation, the effective chip thickness is approximated as:

$$h \approx \dfrac{V_f}{N \cdot Z}$$

$$h \approx \dfrac{143.25}{119.37 \cdot 8}$$

$$h \approx \dfrac{143.25}{955.0}$$

$$h \approx 0.15 \, \text{mm/rev}$$

### Step 4: Compute the Tangential Cutting Force (Ft)

$$F_t = k_s \cdot h \cdot b \cdot K_c$$

where $b$ is the width of cut (taken equal to depth of cut $d$ for face milling approximation) and $K_c$ is the correction factor (assumed = 1 for dry cutting on mild steel).

$$F_t = 2000 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 4 \cdot 1$$

$$F_t = 1200 \, \text{N}$$

The tangential force is 1200 Newtons.

### Step 5: Apply the Safety Factor to Get Clamping Force

$$F_c = n \cdot F_t$$

$$F_c = 2.5 \cdot 1200$$

$$F_c = 3000 \, \text{N} = 3 \, \text{kN}$$

The required clamping force is **3 kN**.

### Step 6: Compute the Spindle Power

$$P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000}$$

$$P = \dfrac{1200 \cdot 30}{60000}$$

$$P = 0.6 \, \text{kW}$$

The required spindle power is 0.6 kW.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Locator Selection Guide

| Workpiece Feature | Best Locator Type | DOF Constrained | Typical KTU Use Case |
|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Flat machined surface | 3 Support Pads | 3 (1T + 2R) | Primary base location |
| External cylindrical surface | V-Block (90°) | 4 (3T + 1R) | Shaft-type components |
| Internal cylindrical hole | Cylindrical Pin / Mandrel | 4 (2T + 2R) | Bushings, gears |
| Irregular profile | Custom contour locator | 3–5 | Castings |
| Threaded hole | Threaded plug | 4 (2T + 2R) | Engine blocks |
| Slot / Keyway | Diamond pin + flat pin | 4 (2T + 2R) | Coupling halves |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU lab viva, students are often asked: *"Why use a V-Block?"* Answer: A V-Block self-centers a cylindrical workpiece and eliminates 4 DOF (1 translation along axis + 2 translation perpendicular + 1 rotation about the perpendicular axis).

## 3.3 Six Degrees of Freedom — Analytical View

$$
\text{Total DOF of a free body} = 3 \, \text{Translational} + 3 \, \text{Rotational} = 6
$$

$$
\text{Constraint Condition} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \geq 6
$$

where $C_i$ is the number of contact points at the $i^{\text{th}}$ locator.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Adding more than 6 locating points is **NOT always better** — it causes **over-location**, leading to part distortion, tool wear, and assembler rejection. The KTU 2024 examiner loves asking: *"What is the consequence of using 7 locators instead of 6?"* Answer: Over-location.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification of Jigs and Fixtures

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Work Holding Devices] --> A1[Jigs]
    A0 --> A2[Fixtures]
    A1 --> B1[Template Jig]
    A1 --> B2[Plate Jig]
    A1 --> B3[Leaf Jig]
    A1 --> B4[Channel Jig]
    A1 --> B5[Box Jig]
    A1 --> B6[Trunnion Jig]
    A1 --> B7[Indexing Jig]
    A2 --> C1[Milling Fixture]
    A2 --> C2[Turning Fixture]
    A2 --> C3[Grinding Fixture]
    A2 --> C4[Welding Fixture]
    A2 --> C5[Assembly Fixture]
    A2 --> C6[Inspection Fixture]
    C2 --> D1[3 Jaw Chuck]
    C2 --> D2[4 Jaw Chuck]
    C2 --> D3[Magnetic Chuck]
    C2 --> D4[Collet Chuck]
    C2 --> D5[Mandrel]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Jig/Fixture

```mermaid
graph TD
    L0[Functional Architecture of a Jig/Fixture] --> M1[Base or Body]
    L0 --> M2[Locating System]
    L0 --> M3[Clamping System]
    L0 --> M4[Tool Guide System]
    L0 --> M5[Support System]
    M2 --> N1[3 Primary Locators]
    M2 --> N2[2 Secondary Locators]
    M2 --> N3[1 Tertiary Locator]
    M3 --> O1[Manual Screws]
    M3 --> O2[Cam Clamps]
    M3 --> O3[Hydraulic Pistons]
    M3 --> O4[Pneumatic Cylinders]
    M4 --> P1[Drill Bush Press Fit]
    M4 --> P2[Drill Bush Sliding Fit]
    M4 --> P3[Liner Bush]
    M5 --> Q1[Jack Screws]
    M5 --> Q2[Adjustable Pads]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — The 3-2-1 Locating Principle

```mermaid
graph TD
    R0[3-2-1 Principle of Location] --> R1[Primary Face: 3 Points]
    R0 --> R2[Secondary Face: 2 Points]
    R0 --> R3[Tertiary Face: 1 Point]
    R1 --> S1[Eliminates 3 DOF]
    R2 --> S2[Eliminates 2 DOF]
    R3 --> S3[Eliminates 1 DOF]
    S1 --> T0[Total 6 DOF Constrained]
    S2 --> T0
    S3 --> T0
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description of a Typical Drilling Jig (For Lab Record Drawing)

A **Plate Jig** (most commonly used in KTU lab) consists of:

| Part | Material | Function |
|------|----------|----------|
| Base Plate | Mild Steel / Cast Iron | Main body to mount on machine table |
| Drill Bush | HSS / Carburized Steel | Guides the drill bit |
| Locating Pin | Hardened Steel (HRC 55) | Engages with workpiece hole |
| Clamp | Mild Steel | Holds workpiece against locator |
| Handle / Knob | Mild Steel | Operator grips for tightening |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU lab record, students must draw the **Front View** and **Top View** of the jig showing the **drill bush, locating pin, and clamp assembly** clearly with dimensions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Differentiate between a Jig and a Fixture.  [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Model Answer (Board-Standard Wording):**

| Parameter | Jig | Fixture |
|-----------|-----|---------|
| Tool Guidance | Guides the cutting tool | Does NOT guide the tool |
| Operations | Drilling, tapping, reaming | Milling, turning, grinding |
| Bush Present | Yes (drill bushes) | No bushes |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |
| Accuracy | Higher (tool guided) | Relies on machine tool accuracy |
| Design Principle | 3-2-1 + Tool Guide | 3-2-1 only |
| Common Example | Drill Jig | Milling Fixture |

### Question 2: State and explain the 3-2-1 Principle of Location.  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Model Answer:**
The 3-2-1 Principle is a standard location method used in jig/fixture design to fix a workpiece in space using minimum 6 contact points.

- **3 points** on the **primary locating surface** (largest face) — eliminate 1 translation + 2 rotations = 3 DOF.
- **2 points** on the **secondary locating surface** (perpendicular to primary) — eliminate 2 translations = 2 DOF.
- **1 point** on the **tertiary locating surface** (perpendicular to both) — eliminate 1 translation = 1 DOF.
- **Total DOF constrained = 3 + 2 + 1 = 6**, which is the minimum required to fix a rigid body in space.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **Plate Jig**. List any **4 applications** of plate jigs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A workpiece of mild steel is being drilled with a $20$ mm diameter drill at a cutting speed of $25$ m/min and a feed of $0.20$ mm/rev. Calculate the (i) spindle speed, (ii) feed rate, and (iii) the approximate thrust force if the specific cutting force is $1500$ N/mm².

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **Plate Jig** is the simplest type of drilling jig. Its construction includes:
1. **Base plate** of mild steel on which all components are mounted.
2. **Locating pin / button** to position the workpiece.
3. **Drill bush** (press-fit type) for guiding the drill bit.
4. **Clamping screw** with strap to hold the workpiece.
5. **Support legs** to raise the jig above the machine table.

**Working:** The workpiece is placed over the locating pin, the clamp is tightened, and the jig is mounted on the drilling machine table. The drill passes through the bush and creates a hole in the correct location every time, without marking out.

**Four Applications of Plate Jig:**
1. Drilling holes in **small flat components** like brackets.
2. Mass production of **electrical switch plates**.
3. **Watch and instrument parts** manufacturing.
4. **Sheet metal component** hole drilling.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Drill diameter $D = 20$ mm
- Cutting speed $V_c = 25$ m/min
- Feed $f = 0.20$ mm/rev
- Specific cutting force $k_s = 1500$ N/mm²

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$$

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot 25}{\pi \cdot 20}$$

$$N = \dfrac{25000}{62.832}$$

$$N \approx 397.89 \, \text{RPM}$$

**(ii) Feed Rate (Vf):**

$$V_f = f \cdot N$$

$$V_f = 0.20 \cdot 397.89$$

$$V_f = 79.58 \, \text{mm/min}$$

**(iii) Thrust Force (Fa):**

For drilling, the thrust force is approximately **half the tangential force**:

$$F_t = k_s \cdot f \cdot D$$

$$F_t = 1500 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 20$$

$$F_t = 6000 \, \text{N}$$

$$F_a = 0.5 \cdot F_t = 0.5 \cdot 6000 = 3000 \, \text{N}$$

**Final Results:**
- Spindle speed $N \approx 397.89$ RPM
- Feed rate $V_f \approx 79.58$ mm/min
- Thrust force $F_a = 3000$ N

---

### Question B (14 Marks)  [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain any **5 types of locating devices** used in jigs and fixtures with neat sketches. Mention the DOF eliminated by each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A circular disc of diameter $100$ mm is to be located on a milling fixture. Design a **V-block-based location** and find the height of the locator from the base if the V-angle is $90°$. Show the construction with a diagram.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**5 Types of Locating Devices:**

| # | Locator | Sketch Description | DOF Eliminated |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Flat Pin / Button Locator** | A cylindrical pin with a flat face | 1 (1 Translation) |
| 2 | **Cylindrical Pin (Diamond Pin)** | A pin with a diamond cross-section | 2 (2 Translations) |
| 3 | **V-Block** | A V-shaped block, 90° or 120° | 4 (3 Translations + 1 Rotation) |
| 4 | **Conical Locator** | A cone-shaped pin engaging a hole | 4 (3 Translations + 1 Rotation) |
| 5 | **Adjustable Jack Screw** | A screw with a spherical tip | 1 (1 Translation - vertical) |

**Explanations:**
1. **Flat Pin** is used to engage a flat machined surface and provides 1 DOF constraint.
2. **Diamond Pin (Cylindrical with flat)** engages into a hole and prevents rotation, eliminating 2 DOF.
3. **V-Block** self-centers a cylindrical workpiece on a flat surface, eliminating 4 DOF and is the most common in lathe/milling fixtures.
4. **Conical Locator** is used for rough castings or forgings to provide firm engagement.
5. **Jack Screw** supports a thin workpiece from below to prevent deflection during machining.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given Data:**
- Disc diameter $D = 100$ mm
- V-block angle $\alpha = 90°$

**Design Calculations:**

For a $90°$ V-block, the locator height $H$ above the base is calculated as:

$$H = \dfrac{D}{2 \cdot \sin(\alpha/2)}$$

$$H = \dfrac{100}{2 \cdot \sin(45°)}$$

$$H = \dfrac{100}{2 \cdot 0.7071}$$

$$H = \dfrac{100}{1.4142}$$

$$H \approx 70.71 \, \text{mm}$$

**Construction Steps:**
1. Machine a $90°$ V-groove on a hardened steel block.
2. The disc rests in the V-groove, self-centered.
3. A **clamp from above** holds the disc against the V-block.
4. The V-block is bolted to the milling fixture base.

**Final Result:** The locator height $H \approx 70.71$ mm from the base.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Jig" and "Fixture":** Many students write the wrong operation. Always remember: **Jig = Drilling/Tapping; Fixture = Milling/Turning/Grinding.**
> 2. **Skipping the Sketch:** Even in 14-mark questions, **neat sketches with proper labeling** carry **at least 3–4 marks** as per KTU valuation key. A 7-mark sub-question without a sketch often gets capped at 4 marks.
> 3. **Units Mismatch:** Always write **mm, RPM, N, kW** — never mix up m/min and mm/min.
> 4. **Forgetting Safety Factor:** In clamping force problems, examiners deduct **1 mark** if the safety factor ($n$) is omitted.
> 5. **Over-Location Error:** Writing "more locators = better accuracy" is **WRONG**. KTU 2024 specifically tests this concept.
> 6. **Forgetting DOF Counting:** Each locator must be associated with the **number of DOF it eliminates** — this is a 1-mark direct question every year.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin This in Your Lab Record!):**
> - Jig = Tool Guided; Fixture = Work Held
> - 6 DOF = 3 Translational + 3 Rotational
> - 3-2-1 Principle → 3 + 2 + 1 = 6 contact points = full constraint
> - Over-location (>6 points) = ERROR; not better
> - V-Block eliminates **4 DOF** (most efficient single locator)
> - Drill Bush material = HSS / Hardened Steel (HRC 60+)
> - Clamping Force $F_c = n \cdot F_t$ (with $n = 2$ to $3$)
> - Spindle Speed $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ (RPM)
> - Feed Rate $V_f = f \cdot N$ (mm/min)
> - Thrust Force in Drilling $\approx 0.5 \cdot F_t$
> - Power $P = \dfrac{F_t \cdot V_c}{60 \cdot 1000}$ (kW)
> - Base material of jig = Cast Iron or Mild Steel
> - Locator material = Hardened Steel (HRC 55+)
> - **5 Operations for JIG:** Drilling, Tapping, Reaming, Boring, Counter-sinking
> - **5 Operations for FIXTURE:** Milling, Turning, Grinding, Welding, Inspection
> - **5 Common Locators:** Flat Pin, Diamond Pin, V-Block, Conical Pin, Jack Screw
> - **5 Common Clamps:** Screw Clamp, Cam Clamp, Strap Clamp, Magnetic Chuck, Pneumatic Clamp
> - **Lab Viva Most Asked:** "Why is a drill bush used in a jig but not in a fixture?" → Answer: To **guide the drill** and ensure **positional accuracy** of the hole.
> - **Lab Viva Most Asked:** "What is the difference between a JIG and a TEMPLATE?" → Answer: A template is a **flat plate with holes** that only **guides the tool**, but does **not clamp or locate** the workpiece — a jig does all three.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Gas Welding # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gas Welding** is a fusion welding process in which the joining of metals is accomplished by melting the base metal surfaces with or without the addition of filler metal, using the heat generated by the combustion of a fuel gas (typically **acetylene**, C₂H₂) mixed with **oxygen (O₂)** in a controlled ratio at the tip of a welding torch. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme Production Engineering Lab (PCAUL407)** syllabus, gas welding is classified under **fusion welding processes** and is one of the most fundamental lab experiments for understanding manual metal joining techniques. The standard variant taught in the lab is **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)**. ### Key Terminology (KTU 2024 Board Vocabulary) | Term | Definition | |------|------------| | **Fusion Welding** | Joining process where base metals are melted (not pressed) to form a coalescence. | | **Fuel Gas** | A combustible gas that, when burned with oxygen, produces the heat needed for welding. | | **Oxy-Acetylene Flame** | The flame produced by burning acetylene with oxygen; flame temperatures reach **~3160 °C to 3480 °C**. | | **Filler Rod / Welding Rod** | Additional metal added to the weld pool to fill the joint groove. | | **Flame Adjustment** | Controlling the O₂:C₂H₂ ratio to obtain neutral, oxidizing, or reducing (carburizing) flames. | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The "Candle and Bunsen Burner" Analogy** 🕯️ > Think of gas welding like controlling a super-powered laboratory **Bunsen burner**. Just as a Bunsen burner mixes natural gas with air to give you a clean blue flame (vs. a sooty yellow candle flame), a gas welding torch mixes **pure oxygen (not air)** with **acetylene gas** to produce an intensely hot, focused flame hot enough to melt steel. The welder becomes the "flame chef" — adjusting gas ratios to get the perfect flame for cooking up a strong weld joint, just as a chef adjusts stove knobs for perfect sautéing. The **three Ts** of gas welding to remember: - **T**emperature — Must be sufficient to melt the base metal. - **T**ime — Heat must be applied long enough for fusion. - **T**echnique — Torch angle, travel speed, and filler rod manipulation determine weld quality. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold as Required) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **Flame Temperature of Neutral Oxy-Acetylene Flame:** **3480 °C (≈ 6300 °F)** > - **Ignition Temperature of Acetylene:** **305 °C** > - **Oxygen Cylinder Pressure (full):** **~150 kgf/cm² (≈ 14.7 MPa)** > - **Acetylene Cylinder Pressure (full):** **~15-16 kgf/cm² (≈ 1.5 MPa)** > - **Acetylene Cylinder Safe Limit:** **Never exceed 1.03 kgf/cm² gauge pressure** during operation. > - **Standard Nozzle Size for Lab Use:** **Nozzle 1, 2, or 3** (drill sizes 0.6–1.2 mm). > - **Color Code (IS / International):** Oxygen Cylinder = **Black body with white shoulder**; Acetylene Cylinder = **Maroon body**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Principles of Oxy-Acetylene Combustion The gas welding process relies on the **exothermic oxidation reaction** of acetylene: $$\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2.5\,\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H$$ Where: - $\Delta H$ = **Heat of combustion of acetylene** = **1300 kJ/mol (≈ 311 kcal/mol)** - This high heat release is why acetylene is preferred over other fuel gases (hydrogen, propane, butane, MAPP, natural gas). > [!NOTE] > **Why Acetylene and not Propane or Hydrogen?** > Acetylene produces the **highest flame temperature** of all commercially available fuel gases due to its unique **triple-bonded C≡C structure**, which releases enormous energy upon combustion. Propane flames reach only ~2800 °C, hydrogen ~2200 °C. --- ## 2.2 The Three Zones of an Oxy-Acetylene Flame A properly adjusted neutral flame has **three distinct cones**: | Zone | Name | Temperature | Composition / Role | |------|------|-------------|--------------------| | **1** | **Inner Cone (White / Bright)** | ~1000 °C (core) | Unburnt mixed gases (O₂ + C₂H₂); no combustion here. | | **2** | **Combustion Zone (Reducing Feather)** | ~3160 °C | Partially burnt gases — high in CO and H₂; chemically **reducing** atmosphere. | | **3** | **Outer Envelope (Oxidizing Mantle)** | ~1270–1320 °C | Complete combustion with atmospheric oxygen; CO₂ + H₂O vapor. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Welding Action Zone** = The point **1–3 mm beyond the inner cone tip**, where the maximum temperature (~3160–3480 °C) is achieved. The torch tip is held at a distance of approximately **3–6 mm** from the work surface. --- ## 2.3 Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames (Board-Exam Favourite ⭐) > [!NOTE] > **KTU examiners frequently ask: "Differentiate between Neutral, Oxidizing, and Carburizing flames." This is a guaranteed question.** ### 2.3.1 Neutral Flame (O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1) - **Definition:** Stoichiometric ratio of one part oxygen to one part acetylene. - **Appearance:** A short, sharp, well-defined **white inner cone** surrounded by a clear **blue outer envelope**. No feather. - **Chemistry:** Complete combustion, no excess of either gas. - **Applications:** Welding of **mild steel, stainless steel, cast iron, copper, brass** (most common lab flame). - **Most used flame in PCAUL407 lab experiments.** ### 2.3.2 Oxidizing Flame (O₂ : C₂H₂ > 1.5 : 1) - **Definition:** Excess oxygen over stoichiometric ratio. - **Appearance:** Shorter, pointed **purple-tinted** inner cone; hiss noise. - **Chemistry:** Excess free oxygen causes **oxidation of the base metal** → brittle, porous welds. - **Applications:** Limited use — only for **brass and bronze** (where Zn/Sn vaporization needs to be balanced). ### 2.3.3 Carburizing / Reducing Flame (C₂H₂ : O₂ > 1 : 1) - **Definition:** Excess acetylene. - **Appearance:** Long, fluffy **feather-like flame extension** around a feathery inner cone. - **Chemistry:** Excess carbon and hydrogen create a **reducing (carburizing) atmosphere** — adds carbon to the weld pool. - **Applications:** Hard-surfacing, low-carbon steel welding, lead-burn operations. --- ## 2.4 Equipment of Gas Welding Setup (Lab Identification) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** Students are often asked to **identify and label** the gas welding equipment. Memorize all parts. | # | Equipment | Function | |---|-----------|----------| | 1 | **Oxygen Cylinder** | Stores O₂ at high pressure (150 kgf/cm²). | | 2 | **Acetylene Cylinder** | Stores C₂H₂ dissolved in acetone (for safety). | | 3 | **Oxygen Regulator** | Reduces O₂ pressure to working pressure (2–3 kgf/cm²). | | 4 | **Acetylene Regulator** | Reduces C₂H₂ pressure to working pressure (0.3–1.0 kgf/cm²). | | 5 | **Hose Pipes** | Color-coded: **Red for acetylene, Black/Blue for oxygen**. | | 6 | **Flashback Arrestor (NRV)** | Prevents reverse gas flow / flame propagation into cylinders. | | 7 | **Welding Torch (Blowpipe)** | Mixes gases in proper ratio; provides handle and nozzle. | | 8 | **Nozzle / Tip** | Final orifice where flame emerges; sized by drill number. | | 9 | **Goggles / Welding Helmet** | Eye protection (shade 4–6 for gas welding). | | 10 | **Filler Rods & Flux** | Added metal and chemical cleaning agent. | --- ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Use the table below as your **last-minute revision sheet** before lab viva or ESE. | # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Units | Application | |---|-----------|-----------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | **Acetylene Combustion Reaction** | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | — | Heat source chemistry | | 2 | **Heat of Combustion of C₂H₂** | $\Delta H = 1300$ | kJ/mol | Highest among fuel gases | | 3 | **Neutral Flame O₂:C₂H₂ Ratio** | $1 : 1$ | volumetric | For mild steel welding | | 4 | **Maximum Flame Temperature** | $T_{max} = 3480$ | °C | Neutral flame core | | 5 | **Acetylene Flow Rate (per mm plate thickness)** | $Q_{C_2H_2} = (75 \text{ to } 125)$ | L/hr | Empirical lab rule | | 6 | **Oxygen Working Pressure** | $P_{O_2} = 1 \text{ to } 3$ | kgf/cm² | Adjusts flame size | | 7 | **Acetylene Working Pressure** | $P_{C_2H_2} = 0.3 \text{ to } 0.7$ | kgf/cm² | Prevents flashback | | 8 | **Torch Tip Stand-off Distance** | $d = 3 \text{ to } 6$ | mm | For 1–3 mm plates | | 9 | **Nozzle Selection Rule (Acetylene L/hr)** | $Q \approx 100 \times t$ | t = plate thickness (mm) | Lab nozzle selection | | 10 | **Heat Input (HI)** | $HI = \frac{V \times I \times 60}{S \times 1000}$ | kJ/mm | Modified for arc; for gas: $HI = \frac{k \times Q}{S}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Latex Isolation Rule Reminder:** All `|` in formulas above are formatted using `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > - **Automotive repair shops** still use gas welding for bodywork, exhaust pipe repairs, and tube joining. > - **Plumbing and HVAC industries** use it for copper tube brazing and joint repairs. > - **Art and sculpture fabrication** rely on it for joining thin non-ferrous metals. > - **Maintenance workshops** in petrochemical plants use it for emergency repairs on-site. > - **Aerospace maintenance** uses precision gas welding (TIG/MIG derivatives) for thin sheet repairs. In the **KTU PCAUL407 lab**, this experiment trains the student to identify the equipment, light and adjust the torch, and perform a simple **butt joint** on mild steel plates — the foundational skill for any manufacturing or maintenance engineer. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Acetylene Required Per Unit Plate Thickness > [!NOTE] > **Problem Context (Lab Application):** A student must weld two 3 mm thick mild steel plates. Estimate the **acetylene flow rate (L/hr)** and the **correct nozzle size** for the gas welding torch. ### Step 1 — Identify the empirical lab rule The empirical relation for selecting acetylene flow rate is: $$Q_{C_2H_2} = 100 \times t$$ where $t$ is the plate thickness in millimetres. ### Step 2 — Substitute the plate thickness Given $t = 3$ mm: $$Q_{C_2H_2} = 100 \times 3$$ $$Q_{C_2H_2} = 300 \text{ L/hr}$$ ### Step 3 — Determine the matching oxygen flow For a **neutral flame**, the volumetric ratio O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1, hence: $$Q_{O_2} = Q_{C_2H_2} = 300 \text{ L/hr}$$ ### Step 4 — Select the appropriate nozzle size Lab nozzles are rated in **drill number** based on total gas flow: $$\text{Total flow} = Q_{O_2} + Q_{C_2H_2} = 300 + 300 = 600 \text{ L/hr}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** A **Nozzle No. 2** (drill size ~0.9 mm) handles 300–600 L/hr flow — the correct choice for 3 mm plate. ### Step 5 — Set the working pressures (regulator reading) $$P_{O_2} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.0 \text{ kgf/cm}^2$$ $$P_{C_2H_2} \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.7 \text{ kgf/cm}^2$$ ### Step 6 — Calculate the heat input per unit length (analytical) Heat input for gas welding is given by: $$HI = k \times \frac{Q_{C_2H_2}}{S}$$ where: - $k = 0.018$ kJ/mm (a typical conversion factor for acetylene) - $S$ = travel speed (mm/min), typically 50–80 mm/min for 3 mm plate Substituting: $$HI = 0.018 \times \frac{300}{60} = 0.09 \text{ kJ/mm}$$ This heat input is well below the threshold to cause HAZ softening in mild steel — confirming the parameter choice is **safe and correct** for the lab specimen. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Flame Comparison (Board Exam Ready Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this comparison table** — it has appeared in **KTU ESE questions for at least 4 consecutive semesters**. | Parameter | Neutral Flame | Oxidizing Flame | Carburizing Flame | |-----------|---------------|------------------|---------------------| | **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio** | 1 : 1 | > 1.5 : 1 | < 1 : 1 (excess C₂H₂) | | **Inner Cone Length** | Short, distinct | Very short, pointed | Long, feathery | | **Color** | White core, blue mantle | Purple-tinted | Whitish with orange feather | | **Sound** | Soft, balanced roar | Hissing | Slight popping | | **Chemical Nature** | Inert (to most metals) | Oxidizing | Reducing / Carburizing | | **Effect on Steel** | No chemical change | Causes oxidation → brittleness | Adds carbon → harder weld | | **Effect on Copper** | Suitable | Causes porosity | Causes brittleness | | **Main Application** | Mild steel, stainless, CI | Brass, bronze | Hard-facing, lead burning | | **Lab Use Frequency** | **Highest (≈ 90%)** | Low | Low | --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Welding Procedure (Lab Manual Format) > [!NOTE] > This is the **standard PCAUL407 lab procedure** for a butt joint — examiners expect these exact steps in viva. ### Pre-Welding Steps 1. **Identify the equipment** (cylinders, regulators, hoses, torch). 2. **Check the flashback arrestors** are in place and clean. 3. **Inspect the nozzle** for carbon build-up; clean with proper tip cleaner. 4. **Set working pressure**: O₂ = 1.5–2.0 kgf/cm², C₂H₂ = 0.5–0.7 kgf/cm². 5. **Purge hoses** by opening the cylinder valve briefly to expel air/moisture. ### Lighting the Torch (Forward / Nozzle Inflation Method) 6. **Open the acetylene valve** slightly on the torch handle. 7. **Light the acetylene** at the nozzle with a friction striker (never use matches/lighters in lab). 8. **Open oxygen valve gradually** to obtain a **neutral flame** (sharp white cone, no feather). 9. **Adjust flame** until the inner cone length equals the cone diameter (a standard neutral-flame visual cue). ### Welding Operation 10. **Hold the torch** at a 60–70° angle to the plate, leading angle in the direction of travel. 11. **Maintain a 3–6 mm stand-off** distance (nozzle tip to work). 12. **Dip the filler rod** into the molten weld pool with a 30–45° trailing angle. 13. **Travel speed** ~50–80 mm/min for 3 mm plate. 14. **Use a leftward (forward) technique** for thin plates (< 3 mm) and a **rightward (backward) technique** for thicker plates. ### Post-Welding Steps 15. **Close acetylene valve first** on the torch, then **oxygen valve** (this prevents soot formation). 16. **Close cylinder valves** and bleed hose pressure. 17. **Inspect the weld** visually and clean with a wire brush. 18. **Tag the specimen** and submit for instructor evaluation. --- ##
