3.4 Common Defects in Gas Welds (Engineering Quality) | Defect | Cause | Prevention | |--------|-------|------------| | **Porosity** | Dirty base metal, incorrect flame | Clean plate, neutral flame | | **Undercut** | Excess heat, slow travel | Increase travel speed | | **Incomplete fusion** | Low heat input, wrong torch angle | Increase gas flow, hold torch correctly | | **Cracks** | Rapid cooling, high carbon | Pre-heat, post-heat, use correct rod | | **Slag inclusions** | Using wrong flux or improper cleaning | Use correct flux, clean between passes | | **Distortion / Warping** | Uneven heating, no clamping | Clamp work, use balanced welding sequence | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Gas Welding Setup > [!NOTE] > The diagram below represents the **flow of gases and signals** through a complete gas welding station — from the storage cylinders to the welding nozzle. This is a high-yield diagram for **lab records and ESE theory** questions. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph A["Gas Storage Section"] A1["Oxygen Cylinder
150 kgf per cm2"] A2["Acetylene Cylinder
15 to 16 kgf per cm2
Acetone dissolved"] end subgraph B["Pressure Control Section"] B1["Oxygen Regulator
Red marked knob"] B2["Acetylene Regulator
Green marked knob"] B3["Flashback Arrestor O2
One way valve"] B4["Flashback Arrestor C2H2
One way valve"] end subgraph C["Gas Delivery Section"] C1["Oxygen Hose
Black or Blue"] C2["Acetylene Hose
Red"] C3["Hose Connections
Double hose check valves"] end subgraph D["Mixing and Combustion Section"] D1["Torch Body
Handle and Stem"] D2["Mixing Chamber
Inside Torch"] D3["Nozzle Tip
Drill Number 1 to 5"] D4["Welding Flame
Neutral Oxy Acetylene"] end subgraph E["Workpiece Section"] E1["Base Metal Plate
Mild Steel 3 mm"] E2["Weld Pool
Molten Fusion Zone"] E3["Filler Rod
Manual addition"] end A1 --> B1 A2 --> B2 B1 --> B3 B2 --> B4 B3 --> C1 B4 --> C2 C1 --> D1 C2 --> D1 D1 --> D2 D2 --> D3 D3 --> D4 D4 --> E2 E1 --> E2 E3 --> E2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Read this block diagram as follows:** Oxygen and Acetylene cylinders → Pressure regulators → Flashback arrestors → Color-coded hoses → Torch body → Mixing chamber → Nozzle → Flame → Weld pool on base metal, with filler rod dipping into the pool. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Flame Adjustment Procedure > [!NOTE] > This decision flowchart teaches the **systematic lab method** to obtain a neutral flame — a key viva question. ```mermaid flowchart TD stepA["Step 1: Open Acetylene Cylinder
Set to 0.5 to 0.7 kgf per cm2"] stepB["Step 2: Open Torch Acetylene Valve
Light with friction striker"] stepC["Step 3: Acetylene Burns Yellow
Sooty Flame"] stepD{"Step 4: Inner Cone
Has Feather?"} stepE["Step 5: Gradually Add Oxygen
Reduce feather length"] stepF{"Step 6: Feather
Disappeared?"} stepG["Step 7: Neutral Flame Achieved
Sharp white inner cone
Length equals diameter"] stepH["Step 8: Ready for Welding
Hold 3 to 6 mm stand off"] stepA --> stepB stepB --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepD -- "Yes feather visible" --> stepE stepD -- "No feather" --> stepG stepE --> stepF stepF -- "Yes" --> stepG stepF -- "Still feather" --> stepE stepG --> stepH ``` --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Step-by-Step Lab Welding Operation ```mermaid flowchart TD s1["Identify Equipment
Cylinders, Regulators, Torch"] s2["Set Working Pressures
O2 = 2 kgf per cm2, C2H2 = 0.6 kgf per cm2"] s3["Purge Hoses
Remove Air and Moisture"] s4["Light Torch
Open C2H2 first, then strike"] s5["Adjust to Neutral Flame
No feather, sharp white cone"] s6["Clean Workpiece
Wire brush and acetone wipe"] s7["Set Clamp and Gap
2 mm root gap for butt joint"] s8["Begin Welding
Forward technique, 60 degree torch angle"] s9["Add Filler Rod
30 degree trailing angle"] s10["Complete Weld Bead
Travel speed 60 mm per min"] s11["Shut Off Torch
Close C2H2 first, then O2"] s12["Post Weld Cleaning
Wire brush, inspect bead"] s13["Submit Specimen
With lab record entry"] s1 --> s2 --> s3 --> s4 --> s5 --> s6 --> s7 --> s8 --> s9 --> s10 --> s11 --> s12 --> s13 ``` --- ## 4.4 ASCII Schematic: Oxy-Acetylene Flame Anatomy > [!NOTE] > The following **text-based schematic** depicts the three zones of a neutral flame. Useful for quick reproduction in the lab record. ``` ▲ Welding Zone (3160 °C to 3480 °C) │ │ ← 3 to 6 mm stand-off │ ┌──────┴──────┐ │ │ OUTER ENVELOPE │ ╭───╮ │ (Zone 3, complete combustion, │ │ ● │ ↺ │ CO2 + H2O, light blue) │ │ W │ │ │ │ I │ │ COMBUSTION ZONE │ │ T │ │ (Zone 2, reducing feather, │ │ E │ │ blue, ~3160 °C) │ ╰─╮ ╯ │ │ ─┴─ │ INNER CONE │ ─┬─ │ (Zone 1, unburnt gases, │ ● │ white, ~1000 °C) └─────────────┘ ║ ║ NOZZLE TIP ║ ═════╩═════ Welding Torch ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define gas welding. List the equipment used in oxy-acetylene welding.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Gas welding is a fusion welding process in which the joining of metals is achieved by melting the base metal surfaces with or without filler metal addition, using the heat generated by the combustion of a fuel gas (usually acetylene) with oxygen at the tip of a welding torch. > > **Equipment List (1.5 Marks):** > 1. Oxygen cylinder > 2. Acetylene cylinder > 3. Pressure regulators (O₂ and C₂H₂) > 4. Hose pipes (red for C₂H₂, black/blue for O₂) > 5. Welding torch with nozzle > 6. Flashback arrestors > 7. Filler rods and flux > 8. Welding goggles and striker ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a neutral flame and a carburizing flame in oxy-acetylene welding.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Model Answer (3 Marks):** > > | Feature | Neutral Flame | Carburizing Flame | > |---------|---------------|-------------------| > | **O₂ : C₂H₂ ratio** | 1 : 1 (stoichiometric) | < 1 : 1 (excess acetylene) | > | **Appearance** | Sharp white inner cone, no feather | Feathery inner cone with extended flame | > | **Effect on metal** | No chemical reaction with base metal | Adds carbon to weld pool (carburizing) | > | **Application** | Mild steel, stainless steel, cast iron | Hard-facing, low-carbon steel | > > **Concluding line (0.5 marks):** A neutral flame is chemically inert; a carburizing flame is chemically reducing and is used when carbon enrichment of the weld is desired. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — Set 1: ` [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]` > **Q. (a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an oxy-acetylene welding torch. **(7 Marks)** > > **Q. (b)** Explain the three types of oxy-acetylene flames with neat sketches and their applications. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(a) > **Construction of Oxy-Acetylene Welding Torch (7 Marks):** > > **Definition (1 Mark):** An oxy-acetylene welding torch (also called a blowpipe) is a device that mixes oxygen and acetylene in controlled proportions and delivers the mixture to the nozzle for combustion. > > **Main Parts (5 Marks, with sketch):** > 1. **Torch Body / Handle:** Ergonomic brass body with two inlet ports — one for O₂ (connected to black hose) and one for C₂H₂ (connected to red hose). It has two needle valves for fine control of each gas flow. > 2. **Mixing Chamber:** Located inside the torch body. Here, the two gases are thoroughly mixed in the desired ratio. Two types: **Injector type** (high-pressure O₂ creates suction for C₂H₂) and **Equal pressure type** (both gases at equal pressure). > 3. **Stem / Barrel:** A long, narrow tube that conducts the gas mixture from the mixing chamber to the nozzle. > 4. **Nozzle / Tip:** The replaceable end piece with a precisely drilled orifice (drill number 0.5 to 5). Made of copper for good heat dissipation. The flame emerges from this tip. > 5. **Valve Handles:** Two knurled knobs (one for O₂, one for C₂H₂) on the torch body for individual gas regulation. > > **Working (1 Mark):** Oxygen enters the mixing chamber at high velocity, drawing in acetylene by injector action. The mixed gas travels through the stem to the nozzle, where it is ignited to form a stable, focused flame at ~3480 °C. #### Model Solution for Q(b) > **Three Types of Oxy-Acetylene Flames (7 Marks):** > > **1. Neutral Flame (2.5 Marks):** > - **Ratio:** O₂ : C₂H₂ = 1 : 1 > - **Appearance:** A short, distinct, rounded **white inner cone** surrounded by a clear **blue outer envelope**, with no feather. > - **Chemistry:** Complete, stoichiometric combustion; no excess of either gas. > - **Applications:** Mild steel, stainless steel, cast iron, copper — the most widely used flame in production labs. > > **2. Oxidizing Flame (2.5 Marks):** > - **Ratio:** O₂ : C₂H₂ > 1.5 : 1 (oxygen excess) > - **Appearance:** Shorter, pointed, **purple-tinted** inner cone; audible hissing noise. > - **Chemistry:** Excess free oxygen oxidizes the base metal, producing **brittle, porous welds** in ferrous metals. > - **Applications:** **Brass and bronze welding** (helps prevent zinc vaporization issues). > > **3. Carburizing (Reducing) Flame (2 Marks):** > - **Ratio:** O₂ : C₂H₂ < 1 : 1 (acetylene excess) > - **Appearance:** Long, feathery, **white-to-orange** extension around the inner cone. > - **Chemistry:** Excess carbon and hydrogen create a **reducing (carburizing) atmosphere** that adds carbon to the weld pool. > - **Applications:** Hard-facing, welding of low-carbon steels, lead burning. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2: Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` > **Q. (a)** Describe the procedure for setting up and lighting an oxy-acetylene welding torch, including safety precautions. **(7 Marks)** > > **Q. (b)** List the common defects encountered in gas welding and explain their causes and remedies. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for Q(a) > **Setting Up and Lighting Procedure (7 Marks):** > > **Pre-Lighting Setup (3 Marks):** > 1. Inspect cylinders for damage; check the **flashback arrestors** are clean and functional. > 2. Open the **acetylene cylinder valve** slowly (¼ turn) and set the regulator to **0.5–0.7 kgf/cm²**. > 3. Open the **oxygen cylinder valve** fully and set the regulator to **1.5–2.5 kgf/cm²**. > 4. Purge each hose by briefly opening the torch valve to expel any air or moisture. > 5. Verify the **nozzle size** matches the plate thickness (No. 2 for 3 mm plate). > > **Lighting Procedure (2.5 Marks):** > 6. Open the **acetylene torch valve** slightly; light it with a friction striker. > 7. The flame appears **yellow and sooty** at first. > 8. Open the **oxygen torch valve gradually** until the **feather disappears** and a **sharp white inner cone** forms. The flame is now neutral. > 9. Adjust the **inner cone length** to equal the cone diameter — a visual indicator of a perfect neutral flame. > > **Safety Precautions (1.5 Marks):** > - Never use oil or grease on oxygen fittings (explosion risk). > - Always open cylinder valves slowly; never use acetylene above 1.03 kgf/cm². > - Keep a **fire extinguisher (CO₂ or dry powder)** within reach. > - Wear **welding goggles (shade 4–6)**, leather gloves, and apron. > - Never light the torch with matches or lighters — use only friction strikers. > - Ensure proper **ventilation** in the welding area. #### Model Solution for Q(b) > **Common Defects in Gas Welding (7 Marks):** > > | # | Defect | Cause (1 Mark) | Remedy (1 Mark) | > |---|--------|----------------|------------------| > | 1 | **Porosity** | Dirty base metal; incorrect flame adjustment; moisture in hoses | Clean plate with wire brush and acetone; use neutral flame; purge hoses | > | 2 | **Undercut** | Excess heat input; incorrect torch angle | Increase travel speed; tilt torch more vertically | > | 3 | **Incomplete Fusion** | Insufficient heat; torch too far from work | Increase gas flow; reduce stand-off distance | > | 4 | **Cracks (Hot/Cold)** | Rapid cooling; high carbon content; restraint stresses | Pre-heat the plate; use low-carbon filler; relieve restraints | > | 5 | **Slag Inclusions** | Improper cleaning between passes; wrong flux | Clean between passes; use proper flux and rod | > | 6 | **Distortion / Warping** | Uneven heating; lack of clamping | Use tack welds; clamp the work; alternate welding direction | > | 7 | **Incomplete Penetration** | Low heat input; wrong joint preparation | Increase flame size; use proper V-groove for thicker plates | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Gas Welding Questions:** > 1. **Confusing C₂H₂ and O₂ hose colors:** Red is for **acetylene**, Black/Blue is for **oxygen**. Reversing them is a **2-mark loss**. > 2. **Wrong flame ratio:** Students often write "2 : 1" for neutral flame. The correct stoichiometric ratio is **1 : 1**. > 3. **Wrong closing order on torch:** Always close **acetylene first**, then oxygen. Closing oxygen first causes soot formation and may lead to flashback. > 4. **Omitting flashback arrestors:** Many students list equipment but **forget the flashback arrestors** — losing 1 mark. > 5. **Using "Bunsen burner" terminology in answers:** KTU examiners expect the term **"oxy-acetylene torch / blowpipe"**. "Bunsen burner" is too informal. > 6. **Not mentioning safety precautions:** ESE questions on gas welding almost always carry a 1–2 mark component for safety. Skipping it is a guaranteed loss. > 7. **Forgetting the filler rod angle:** It should be at **30–45° trailing angle**, not the same as the torch. Examiners note this distinction carefully. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📝 > [!NOTE] > **Last-minute rapid revision — go through this list before the exam.** ### 🔑 Core Concept Bullets - Gas welding is a **fusion welding** process using a **fuel gas + oxygen** combustion flame. - The standard lab variant is **Oxy-Acetylene Welding (OAW)** with flame temperature **3480 °C**. - Acetylene combustion: $\text{C}_2\text{H}_2 + 2.5\,\text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} + \Delta H$ (1300 kJ/mol). - Three flame types: **Neutral (1:1), Oxidizing (>1.5:1), Carburizing (<1:1)**. - The **inner cone** is unburnt gases; the **combustion zone** is the hottest region; the **outer envelope** completes combustion. ### 🔬 Lab Equipment Checklist - **Cylinders:** O₂ (black + white shoulder) and C₂H₂ (maroon). - **Regulators:** O₂ → 1.5–2.5 kgf/cm², C₂H₂ → 0.5–0.7 kgf/cm². - **Hoses:** Red = Acetylene, Black/Blue = Oxygen. - **Flashback arrestors** are mandatory for safety. - **Nozzle size rule:** Acetylene flow (L/hr) ≈ **100 × plate thickness (mm)**. - **Welding goggles:** Shade 4–6 for gas welding. ### ⚠️ Safety Must-Know - **Closing order:** Close **acetylene first**, then oxygen. - **Opening order:** Open **acetylene first**, then oxygen. - Acetylene working pressure **never exceed 1.03 kgf/cm²** (acetone-free risk). - **No oil/grease** on oxygen fittings (explosion hazard). - Maintain **3–6 mm stand-off** distance from the work surface. - Always use a **friction striker**, never matches. ### 🧠 Memory Hooks for Viva - "**1 : 1 neutral, more O₂ oxidizing, more C₂H₂ carburizing**" — memorize the ratio. - "**Red rope for acetylene, black rope for oxygen**" — color hook. - "**Inner cone is unburnt, outer is burnt, combustion zone is hot**" — three zones. - "**Acetylene is highest temperature fuel gas**" — why we use it. ### 📊 KTU Board-Favorite Topics (Asked Almost Every Semester) 1. **Differentiate three flame types** — most frequent. 2. **Sketch the neutral flame and label zones** — must practice. 3. **List the equipment with safety** — guaranteed 7-mark question. 4. **Procedure + safety precautions** — combination long answer. 5. **Common defects and remedies** — applied engineering question.

topics.title: Arc Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Arc Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Arc Welding** is a fusion welding process in which coalescence of metals is achieved by heating the workpiece to the melting temperature using the **intense heat generated by an electric arc** struck between an electrode and the base metal (or between two non-consumable electrodes). The arc temperature, typically in the range of **6000 °C to 7000 °C**, supplies the thermal energy required to melt the joint edges, while filler metal (from a consumable electrode or a separate rod) is added to form a metallurgical bond.

In the KTU 2024 *Production Engineering Lab* syllabus, the experiment on arc welding is designed to give students hands-on experience with:
- Setup of a manual metal arc welding (MMAW/SMAW) workstation.
- Striking and maintaining a stable arc.
- Selection of electrode, current, and polarity.
- Execution of a bead-on-plate and a butt-joint weld.
- Visual inspection of the weld bead for defects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Arc welding in the lab is performed almost exclusively as **Shielded Metal Arc Welding (SMAW)** — also called *manual metal arc welding (MMAW)* or *stick welding* — because of its simplicity, low cost, and suitability for academic labs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine striking a matchstick. The tiny flame is bright, hot, and concentrated. Now, imagine concentrating *one hundred matchsticks* of energy into a single pinpoint-sized spot. That is essentially what an electric arc is: a **continuous, controlled electrical "lightning bolt"** that melts metal on contact.

If you hold a thin pencil lead (graphite/electrode) and touch it briefly to a sheet of paper, the paper scorches at the contact point. As you lift the lead a millimetre away, a small glowing spark (an arc) jumps the gap, and the paper *vaporises* in that spot. Arc welding uses this same principle, but with massive currents (50–500 A) and metals like mild steel, stainless steel, or aluminium, producing a molten pool that solidifies into a strong joint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** "An electric arc = a sustained electric spark that is hot enough (≈ 6500 °C) to melt steel. You use it to fuse two metal pieces together by melting them at the joint."

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are used in arc welding practice and are commonly tested in the KTU viva:

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Arc temperature | **6000–7000** | °C |
| Open-circuit voltage (OCV) | 50–80 V (DC), up to 100 V (AC) | V |
| Arc voltage (working) | 18–30 V | V |
| Welding current | 50–500 | A |
| Electrode diameter | 2.0, 2.5, 3.15, 4.0, 5.0 | mm |
| Polarity used | DCEP (most common for SMAW) | — |
| Arc efficiency (η) | 0.7–0.85 for SMAW | dimensionless |
| Specific energy consumption | 1.5–3.0 kWh/kg of deposited metal | kWh/kg |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step

The principle of arc welding rests on three physical phenomena that act simultaneously:

1. **Ionisation of the gap** — When the electrode touches the workpiece, a short-circuit occurs. As the electrode is lifted a small distance (≈ 2–4 mm), electrons are pulled across the gap, ionising the air to form a *plasma column*. This plasma conducts current and emits intense light and heat.
2. **Heat generation by the arc** — The arc itself is a high-temperature, low-voltage electrical discharge. Heat is liberated at three zones:
   - **Anode (workpiece in DCEP, electrode in DCEN):** ~ 43 % of total heat
   - **Cathode:** ~ 36 % of total heat
   - **Arc column (radiation + convection):** ~ 21 % of total heat
3. **Filler metal addition & solidification** — In SMAW, the electrode itself melts and drips into the weld pool. The flux coating decomposes to generate a shielding gas (CO, CO₂, H₂) and forms a slag that floats on the molten pool, protecting it from atmospheric contamination. On cooling, the pool solidifies into a weld bead.

## 2.2 Components of an Arc Welding Setup

A typical laboratory arc-welding workstation has:

- **Power source** — transformer (AC) or rectifier (DC), rated for the required current and duty cycle.
- **Electrode holder (stinger)** — insulated clamp to grip the consumable electrode.
- **Electrode** — a flux-coated consumable rod (e.g., AWS E6010, E7018).
- **Work lead / Ground clamp** — connected to the workpiece, completes the circuit.
- **Cables** — heavy-duty welding cables (typically 25–50 mm² copper) to handle high current.
- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — helmet with auto-darkening filter, leather gloves, apron, safety boots.

## 2.3 Heat Input — The Core Quantity in Arc Welding

**Heat input (H)** is the energy delivered per unit length of weld. It governs penetration, dilution, HAZ width, and mechanical properties. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests this formula.

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]
$$

where:
- $\eta$ = arc efficiency (SMAW ≈ 0.75–0.85, GMAW ≈ 0.85, SAW ≈ 1.0, GTAW ≈ 0.60)
- $V$ = arc voltage (V)
- $I$ = welding current (A)
- $v$ = travel speed (mm/s)
- The factor 1000 converts watts to kilowatts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 60 appears in some texts:** If travel speed is in **mm/min** instead of **mm/s**, the formula is:
> $$
> H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I \cdot 60}{1000 \cdot S} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}\right]
> $$
> where $S$ is travel speed in mm/min. **Check the unit of $v$ before solving KTU problems.**

## 2.4 Polarity — The Hidden Parameter

- **DCEP (Direct Current Electrode Positive)** — workpiece is negative, electrode is positive. ~ 2/3 of heat is at workpiece → **deep penetration**. Used for most structural SMAW.
- **DCEN (Direct Current Electrode Negative)** — ~ 2/3 of heat is at electrode → shallow penetration, faster electrode melt. Used for thin sheets and surfacing.
- **AC (Alternating Current)** — average of both. Used for aluminium, magnesium, and magnetised steels.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = \dfrac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}$ | Heat input | kJ/mm | Energy per unit weld length |
| 2 | $P = V \cdot I$ | Electrical power input | W | Power consumed at arc |
| 3 | $\eta_{a} = \dfrac{H_{weld}}{H_{input}}$ | Arc efficiency | — | Process comparison |
| 4 | $D_{e} = 0.8 \cdot t$ | Approx. electrode diameter for plate of thickness $t$ | mm | Selecting electrode |
| 5 | $I \approx (35 \text{ to } 50) \cdot D_{e}$ | Current setting rule of thumb | A | Selecting current |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{60 \cdot A \cdot \rho}$ | Deposition rate | kg/h | Productivity estimation |
| 7 | $P_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{25} \text{ (approx.)}$ | Plates thicker than 3 mm penetration rule | A/mm | Penetration check |
| 8 | $S = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v \cdot A \cdot \rho}$ | Cross-section area of bead | mm² | Bead dimension |
| 9 | $t = 0.5 \cdot I$ | Rule of thumb — current ≈ 2× plate thickness in A | A/mm | Lab quick selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Heat input is the single most important parameter a welding engineer controls. It determines whether the weld is *underbead* (too low → lack of fusion), *optimum* (good ductility and strength), or *overbead* (too high → excessive grain growth, distortion, HAZ embrittlement).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Structural fabrication:** Bridges, ship hulls, pressure vessels, building columns.
- **Pipeline welding:** Long-distance oil & gas pipelines (often using SAW or FCAW).
- **Automotive & railways:** Robotic GMAW/spot welding for mass production.
- **Repair & maintenance:** Cast-iron repair, hardfacing of worn-out parts.
- **Aerospace:** TIG/GTAW for aluminium and titanium alloys.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Process Parameters

In the lab experiment, a student is expected to *justify* the choice of electrode, current, voltage, and travel speed for a given plate thickness. The following matrix is the KTU-recommended decision logic:

| Plate thickness $t$ (mm) | Electrode dia. $D_e$ (mm) | Current $I$ (A) | Arc voltage $V$ (V) | Travel speed $v$ (mm/min) | Recommended polarity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.5 – 2.0 | 2.0 | 40 – 60 | 18 – 20 | 150 – 200 | DCEP |
| 2.0 – 4.0 | 2.5 / 3.15 | 60 – 110 | 20 – 22 | 120 – 180 | DCEP |
| 4.0 – 8.0 | 3.15 / 4.0 | 110 – 180 | 22 – 25 | 100 – 150 | DCEP |
| 8.0 – 15.0 | 4.0 / 5.0 | 180 – 300 | 25 – 28 | 80 – 120 | DCEP, multi-pass |
| > 15.0 | 5.0 / 6.0 | 250 – 400 | 27 – 30 | 60 – 100 | DCEP, V-groove multi-pass |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Heat Input Calculation (KTU Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Module 2, Part B]**
> In a SMAW operation, the arc voltage is 25 V, the welding current is 200 A, and the electrode travel speed is 5 mm/s. The arc efficiency for SMAW is 0.85. Calculate the heat input per unit length in kJ/mm.

**Step 1 — Identify given values.**
- $V = 25$ V
- $I = 200$ A
- $v = 5$ mm/s
- $\eta = 0.85$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the heat input formula (using mm/s form).**

$$
H \;=\; \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v}
$$

$$
H \;=\; \frac{0.85 \times 25 \times 200}{1000 \times 5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
0.85 \times 25 = 21.25
$$

$$
21.25 \times 200 = 4250
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the denominator.**

$$
1000 \times 5 = 5000
$$

**Step 5 — Final division.**

$$
H = \frac{4250}{5000} = 0.85 \;\; \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to J/mm for intuition.**

$$
H = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \;\; \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The heat input is **0.85 kJ/mm**, which is typical for a medium-thickness plate SMAW weld. If the same were > 1.5 kJ/mm, the HAZ would be over-coarse and risk of distortion increases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification by alternate form (using mm/min):**
> $v = 5$ mm/s × 60 = 300 mm/min
> $$
> H = \frac{0.85 \times 25 \times 200 \times 60}{1000 \times 300} = \frac{255000}{300000} = 0.85 \;\; \text{kJ/mm} \;\; \checkmark
> $$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Worked Example — Selection of Current and Electrode (KTU Standard)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 2, Part A]**
> A mild-steel plate of thickness 6 mm is to be welded by SMAW. Recommend a suitable electrode diameter and welding current. Justify with rule-of-thumb relations.

**Step 1 — Use the rule $D_e = 0.8 \cdot t$.**

$$
D_e = 0.8 \times 6 = 4.8 \;\; \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Round to standard available size.**

Nearest standard electrode diameter = **4.0 mm** (next size 5.0 mm may be too large for a single-pass 6 mm plate; 4.0 mm is appropriate).

**Step 3 — Use the rule $I \approx (35 \text{ to } 50) \cdot D_e$.**

Lower bound: $I = 35 \times 4.0 = 140$ A

Upper bound: $I = 50 \times 4.0 = 200$ A

**Step 4 — Select a current in the safe range.**

A current of **160–180 A** is the recommended range, allowing comfortable arc striking and full penetration for a 6 mm plate in a single pass.

**Conclusion:** Electrode — **AWS E6013, 4.0 mm diameter**. Welding current — **≈ 170 A**, DCEP, arc voltage ≈ 22–24 V.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Worked Example — Penetration & Bead Geometry

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Part B]**
> During a butt-joint SMAW lab experiment on two 8 mm mild-steel plates, the current was 180 A, the travel speed was 3 mm/s, and the arc voltage was 24 V. If the bead width is 14 mm and the reinforcement height is 2 mm, calculate (i) heat input, (ii) cross-sectional area of the bead, and (iii) approximate deposition rate (assume 50 % transfer efficiency and mild-steel density 7850 kg/m³).

**Step 1 — Heat input.**

$$
H = \frac{0.85 \times 24 \times 180}{1000 \times 3} = \frac{3672}{3000} = 1.224 \;\; \text{kJ/mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of bead (approximate trapezoid with bottom width 14 mm, top ≈ 10 mm, height 8 mm + 2 mm = 10 mm — use simpler approximate form).**

$$
A \approx 0.7 \times w \times h = 0.7 \times 14 \times 10 = 98 \;\; \text{mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-section × travel speed × density gives mass per unit time / length.**

Per mm of weld, mass = $A \times \rho$ = $98 \times 10^{-6} \times 7850 = 0.769$ g/mm.

Per hour (with $v = 3$ mm/s = 180 mm/min = 10800 mm/h):

$$
\text{Mass per hour} = 0.769 \times 10800 = 8306 \;\; \text{g/h} \approx 8.3 \;\; \text{kg/h}
$$

With 50 % transfer efficiency (i.e. only half the molten electrode mass reaches the weld), effective deposition:

$$
\dot{m} \approx 8.3 \times 0.5 = 4.15 \;\; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Conclusion:** Heat input is moderate (1.22 kJ/mm), giving good penetration without excessive HAZ. The deposition rate ≈ 4.15 kg/h is realistic for 4.0 mm E6013 electrode at 180 A.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Arc-Welding Process

Since arc welding is a physical process involving a power source, electrode, workpiece, and shielding medium, the following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of energy, mass, and signals during the SMAW process. This is the KTU-recommended schematic style for lab-record drawings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PSU[Step-down Power Source AC Mains to 80V OCV] -->|Heavy Cable| EH[Electrode Holder Stinger]
    GC[Ground Clamp] -->|Return Cable| PSU
    EH -->|Holds| EL[Flux-coated Consumable Electrode]
    EL -->|Strikes and Sustains| ARC[Electric Arc Temperature 6500 C Plasma Column]
    GC -->|Connects| WP[Workpiece Base Metal]
    ARC -->|Melts Filler and Base| WP
    ARC -->|Decomposes Flux| SG[Shielding Gas CO CO2 H2]
    ARC -->|Forms| SL[Slag Layer Floating on Pool]
    SG -->|Protects| MP[Molten Weld Pool]
    SL -->|Protects| MP
    MP -->|Solidifies| WB[Weld Bead]
    WB -->|Forms| WZ[Heat Affected Zone HAZ in Base Metal]
    OP[Operator with PPE Helmet Gloves Apron] -->|Controls Travel Speed| ARC
    OP -->|Sets| PSU
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The power source is the heart of the system. It delivers high current at low voltage. The *electrode holder* is the live (+) terminal in DCEP, the *ground clamp* is the return (–) terminal. The arc itself is the load. The shielding gas and slag together protect the molten pool from oxygen and nitrogen in the atmosphere. The operator controls travel speed and arc length manually.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Heat-Input Decision Flow

This second diagram helps students visualise how heat input governs weld quality and which parameter to tune.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Select Plate Thickness t] --> B[Pick Electrode Diameter De]
    B --> C[Set Welding Current I Rule of Thumb 35 to 50 times De]
    C --> D[Set Arc Voltage V 18 to 30 V]
    D --> E[Choose Travel Speed v mm per s]
    E --> F[Compute Heat Input H kJ per mm]
    F --> G{H within range}
    G -- Yes --> H[Acceptable Weld]
    G -- Too Low --> I[Increase I or Decrease v]
    G -- Too High --> J[Decrease I or Increase v]
    I --> F
    J --> F
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Polarity Effects (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DCEP [DCEP Polarity - Most SMAW]
        D1[Electrode is Positive] --> D2[Arc Heat 2/3 in Workpiece]
        D2 --> D3[Deep Narrow Penetration]
    end
    subgraph DCEN [DCEN Polarity]
        N1[Electrode is Negative] --> N2[Arc Heat 2/3 in Electrode]
        N2 --> N3[Shallow Wide Bead High Deposition]
    end
    subgraph AC [AC Polarity]
        AC1[Alternating Polarity] --> AC2[Balanced Heat 50/50]
        AC2 --> AC3[Used for Aluminium and Magnesium]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip:** In the KTU lab, the power source is usually set to **DCEP** for mild-steel SMAW. The student must verify polarity by checking the markings on the holder (DCEP) and ground clamp (DCEN-return) before striking the arc.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define electric arc. List the three heat-generation zones in a welding arc and their approximate heat distribution.

**Model Answer:**
An *electric arc* is a sustained electrical discharge of high current and low voltage across a gap between two conductors, producing temperatures of the order of **6000–7000 °C**.

| Zone | Heat Contribution | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Anode | ≈ 43 % | Receives electron bombardment |
| Cathode | ≈ 36 % | Receives ion bombardment |
| Arc column | ≈ 21 % | Radiation + convection |

For a constant-current DC arc, the **anode** is slightly hotter than the cathode, which is why in DCEP (workpiece = cathode, electrode = anode) the workpiece is preferentially heated, giving **deeper penetration** than DCEN.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** State the rule-of-thumb relations for selecting (a) electrode diameter and (b) welding current for a plate of thickness $t$ mm in SMAW.

**Model Answer:**
- (a) Electrode diameter: $\quad D_e \;\approx\; 0.8 \cdot t$ (rounded to the nearest standard size, typically 2.0, 2.5, 3.15, 4.0, or 5.0 mm).
- (b) Welding current: $\quad I \;\approx\; 35 \text{ to } 50 \cdot D_e$ A. For example, for a 4.0 mm electrode, $I = 140$–$200$ A.

The lower end of the current range is used for **flat-position** welding, the higher end for **vertical or overhead** positions where the molten metal tends to sag and the arc must be "pushed" harder.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
> *Attempt any ONE of the following.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Arc Welding Process & Heat Input

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the **manual metal arc welding (SMAW)** process. List the functions of the flux coating on a consumable electrode. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** During a lab experiment, a 6 mm mild-steel plate is welded with the following parameters: arc voltage 24 V, current 170 A, travel speed 4 mm/s, and arc efficiency 0.80. Calculate **(i)** the heat input per unit length, **(ii)** the heat input per unit length expressed in J/mm, and **(iii)** the deposition rate in kg/h assuming 60 % transfer efficiency and a bead cross-section of 60 mm². Mild-steel density = 7850 kg/m³. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

A neat functional block diagram is given below (verbal description, as the student must draw it on paper):

1. **AC mains → Step-down transformer/rectifier → Welding power source** with OCV ≈ 60–80 V and constant-current drooping characteristic.
2. From the power source, two **welding cables** (≈ 50 mm² copper) go to:
   - **Electrode holder (stinger)** — holds the consumable electrode. This is the *live* terminal in DCEP.
   - **Work clamp (ground clamp)** — bolted to the workpiece. This is the *return* terminal.
3. When the electrode is touched to the workpiece and lifted 2–4 mm, an **arc** strikes, melting both electrode and workpiece.
4. The **flux coating** of the electrode decomposes to:
   - Generate **shielding gas** (CO, CO₂, H₂) to displace atmospheric O₂ and N₂.
   - Form a **molten slag** that floats on the weld pool, slowing cooling and protecting the solidifying bead.
   - Add **deoxidizers and alloying elements** (Si, Mn, Ti) into the weld pool to improve mechanical properties.
   - Stabilise the arc by adding **ionising agents** (e.g., potassium, sodium salts) to the flux.

The **operator**, wearing a welding helmet, leather gloves, and apron, manually maintains arc length and travel speed.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Heat input in kJ/mm:**

$$
H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v} = \frac{0.80 \times 24 \times 170}{1000 \times 4} = \frac{3264}{4000} = 0.816 \;\; \dfrac{\text{kJ}}{\text{mm}}
$$

**(ii) Heat input in J/mm:**

$$
H = 0.816 \times 1000 = 816 \;\; \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}}
$$

**(iii) Deposition rate:**

Mass per mm of bead = $A \times \rho = 60 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2 \times 7850 \; \text{kg/m}^3 = 0.471 \; \text{g/mm}$

Travel speed = 4 mm/s = 240 mm/min = 14 400 mm/h.

Total melted mass per hour = $0.471 \times 14400 = 6782 \;\text{g/h} = 6.78 \;\text{kg/h}$.

Effective deposition with 60 % transfer efficiency:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{eff}} = 6.78 \times 0.60 = 4.07 \;\; \text{kg/h}
$$

**Final Answers:** $H = 0.816$ kJ/mm = 816 J/mm; $\dot{m} = 4.07$ kg/h.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Electrode Selection, Polarity & Defects

**(a)** Explain the **AWS electrode classification** (e.g., E6013, E7018) with reference to the digits and the four digits' meaning. State at least four commonly used electrode types and their application areas. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With sketches, explain the difference between **DCEP, DCEN, and AC** polarities in arc welding. Which polarity is preferred for SMAW of mild steel, and why? List **four common weld defects** and their remedies. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to (a):**

The **AWS A5.1** system classifies mild-steel SMAW electrodes as **EXXXX** (e.g., E6013, E7018). Each digit carries a specific meaning:

| Position | Meaning | Possible Values |
|---|---|---|
| 1st (E) | Electrode | Always E |
| 2nd & 3rd | **Minimum tensile strength** in thousands of psi | 60 = 60 000 psi (≈ 414 MPa), 70 = 70 000 psi (≈ 483 MPa), etc. |
| 4th | **Welding position** capability | 1 = flat, horizontal, vertical, overhead; 2 = flat & horizontal only |
| 5th | **Coating type, current, and penetration** | 0 = high-cellulose (DCEP, deep); 1 = high-cellulose (DCEP-AC, deep); 3 = high-titania (DCEP-AC, medium); 4 = iron-powder (DCEP, medium); 8 = low-hydrogen iron-powder (DCEP-AC, medium) |

**Examples of common electrodes:**

| Electrode | Coating | Current | Applications |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E6010** | High-cellulose (sodium) | DCEP | Pipe welding, deep penetration, all positions |
| **E6013** | High-titania (potassium) | DCEP / AC | Light fabrication, sheet metal, general purpose |
| **E7018** | Low-hydrogen iron powder | DCEP / AC | Critical structures, bridges, pressure vessels, high toughness |
| **E7024** | Iron-powder titania | DCEP / AC | Flat and horizontal fillet welds, high deposition |

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Polarity differences** (a student must draw three small sketches showing the electrode/arc/workpiece arrangement):

| Polarity | Electrode | Workpiece | Heat distribution | Penetration | Bead |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DCEP** (DC Electrode Positive) | + | – | ~ 2/3 at workpiece | Deep, narrow | Small, deep |
| **DCEN** (DC Electrode Negative) | – | + | ~ 2/3 at electrode | Shallow, wide | Wide, shallow |
| **AC** | Alternating | Alternating | ~ 1/2 and 1/2 | Medium | Medium |

**Preferred polarity for SMAW of mild steel:** **DCEP**, because:
- Greater heat at the workpiece → deeper penetration and stronger fusion.
- Better arc stability with cellulose / rutile flux coatings.
- Lower spatter compared to DCEN.

**Four common weld defects & remedies:**

| Defect | Cause | Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Porosity** | Contaminants (rust, oil, paint), excessive travel speed | Clean plate surface; reduce travel speed; use low-hydrogen electrode |
| **Lack of fusion** | Too low current, wrong electrode angle, long arc length | Increase current; maintain short arc; correct electrode angle (10–15° drag) |
| **Undercut** | Excessive current, wrong technique | Reduce current; use weaving at edges; proper weaving pattern |
| **Crack (hot or cold)** | High heat input, high carbon equivalent, hydrogen | Use low-hydrogen electrode (E7018); preheat plate; control heat input |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Top reasons students lose marks on this experiment:**
> 1. **Confusing the heat-input formula units.** If $v$ is in **mm/min**, the 60 multiplier MUST be present. If $v$ is in **mm/s**, there is NO 60. Mixing these gives an answer 60× too small or 60× too large.
> 2. **Wrong value of arc efficiency.** Students often use $\eta = 1$ for SMAW. The correct range is **0.75–0.85**.
> 3. **Forgetting the direction of polarity.** The convention is "Electrode Positive" = the *electrode* is connected to the *positive* terminal. Reversing it gives a *very* different weld.
> 4. **Not relating heat input to weld quality.** Examiners expect you to comment on whether the calculated $H$ is appropriate (e.g., $H = 0.5$–$1.5$ kJ/mm is normal for mild-steel SMAW; values above 2.0 kJ/mm are over-input).
> 5. **Ignoring HAZ effects.** Even if the question does not ask, mentioning that high heat input produces a coarse-grained HAZ and reduces toughness is a guaranteed extra mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Arc welding = electric arc + fusion of base metal + filler metal addition**, with **arc temperature ≈ 6000–7000 °C**.
- The lab experiment uses **SMAW (shielded metal arc welding)** — also called MMAW or stick welding.
- The arc is struck by briefly touching the electrode to the workpiece and lifting it **2–4 mm**.
- **Three heat zones:** Anode (≈ 43 %), cathode (≈ 36 %), arc column (≈ 21 %).
- **Polarity rule:** DCEP = deep penetration (most SMAW), DCEN = shallow wide bead, AC = balanced.
- **Electrode selection:** $D_e \approx 0.8 \cdot t$, where $t$ = plate thickness in mm.
- **Current selection:** $I \approx 35$–$50 \cdot D_e$ A.
- **Heat input formula (kJ/mm):**
  $$
  H = \frac{\eta \cdot V \cdot I}{1000 \cdot v} \quad \text{with } v \text{ in mm/s}
  $$
  Add 60 in numerator if $v$ is in mm/min.
- **Arc efficiency (η):** SMAW ≈ 0.75–0.85, GMAW ≈ 0.85, SAW ≈ 1.0, GTAW ≈ 0.60.
- **AWS electrode code EXXYZ:** E = electrode, XX = min tensile strength in 10³ psi, Y = position, Z = coating & current type.
- **Most common electrodes:** E6010 (deep, pipe), E6013 (general purpose), E7018 (low-hydrogen, critical).
- **Functions of flux:** shielding gas generation, slag formation, deoxidation/alloying, arc stabilisation.
- **PPE is mandatory:** Helmet with proper filter shade, leather gloves, apron, boots.
- **Common defects:** porosity, lack of fusion, undercut, cracks — all preventable by clean surface, correct parameters, and proper technique.
- **Practical lab tip:** Always check **electrode polarity markings on the holder**, **ground clamp tightness**, and **cable insulation** *before* striking the arc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line takeaway for the KTU exam:** *"Arc welding is a fusion process driven by an electric arc at ≈ 6500 °C; the engineer controls heat input $H = \eta V I / (1000 v)$ to balance penetration, deposition rate, and HAZ properties — the parameters the lab experiment teaches you to select, measure, and justify."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistance Welding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Resistance Welding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Resistance Welding (RW)** is a non-fusion thermo-electric welding process in which the heat required for coalescence of the mating metal surfaces is generated *internally* within the workpiece by the controlled passage of a heavy electric current (typically 1,000 A – 100,000 A) through the joint interface, while a simultaneously applied static or forging **electrode force** holds the parts in intimate contact. The localized Joule heating (I²R effect) raises the interface temperature to the plastic/forging range, and the external pressure consolidates the joint without the use of any external filler metal, flux, or shielding gas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot (Board-Exam Verbatim):**
> *"Resistance welding is a pressure welding process in which coalescence is produced by the heat obtained from resistance of the workpieces to the flow of electric current through them, with the application of pressure by electrodes."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Instant Noodles in a Cup" Analogy

Imagine placing two cold, damp paper cups rim-to-rim and trying to stick them together. If you merely press them, they won't bond. But what if you could pass a controlled, brief gush of **very hot steam** *exactly at the interface*, while simultaneously pressing them tightly?

- The **electric current** is the controlled steam.
- The **electrical resistance of the contact** is the valve that turns the current into heat *only at the joint*.
- The **electrode force** is your hands pressing the cups.
- The **weld time** is how long you let the steam pass — too long and the cups melt away, too short and they stay loose.

The "steam" (heat) is generated *internally* — it does not come from an external torch, which is the philosophical distinction between Resistance Welding and Arc Welding (where an external arc generates the heat).

## 1.3 Taxonomy of Resistance Welding Processes

| Sl. No. | Process | Key Feature | Typical Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Spot Welding (RSW)** | Single circular weld nugget per pulse | 5,000 – 20,000 A |
| 2 | **Projection Welding (RPW)** | Localized current concentration at embossed dimples | 5,000 – 50,000 A |
| 3 | **Seam Welding (RSEW)** | Continuous series of overlapping spot welds via roller electrodes | Continuous |
| 4 | **Butt Welding (RBW)** | Two variants: Upset (Pressure) and Flash | 1,000 – 100,000 A |
| 5 | **Flash Welding** | Arc + pressure; major heat from arcing | High Voltage Open |
| 6 | **Percussion Welding** | Stored capacitor discharge | 10,000 – 100,000 A (brief) |

## 1.4 Important Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Resistivity of mild steel at 20°C:** $\rho \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-7} \, \Omega \cdot m$
> - **Weld time for spot welding:** typically **0.1 s to 1.5 s** (60 – 120 cycles on 50 Hz mains)
> - **Electrode force range:** 2 kN to 80 kN depending on sheet thickness
> - **Squeeze time / Hold time / Off time:** All in cycles of 50 Hz mains
> - **Weldability rating (Low Carbon Steel):** EXCELLENT
> - **Weldability rating (Aluminum, Stainless Steel):** FAIR to GOOD (requires higher current)

## 1.5 Governing Energy Balance — The "Heat Equation of RW"

$$
H_{total} = I^2 R t
$$

Where:
- $H$ = Heat generated in Joules (J)
- $I$ = RMS current in Amperes (A)
- $R$ = Total electrical resistance at the joint in Ohms ($\Omega$)
- $t$ = Time of current flow in seconds (s)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Only ~10% to 30%** of this generated heat is actually used to form the weld nugget. The balance is lost to:
> 1. Conduction into the parent metal, electrodes, and water-cooled copper holders
> 2. Convection and radiation from the surfaces
> 3. Electrical losses in the secondary circuit (cables, transformer secondary)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Sequential Phases of Every Resistance Weld

Every resistance welding cycle, irrespective of variant, executes **three** distinct temporal phases governed by the **weld timer (synchronous electronic timer)**:

1. **Squeeze Phase** — Electrodes advance and build up the preset forging force *before* current flows. Purpose: to establish stable electrical contact and expel surface contaminants.
2. **Weld Phase (Heat + Forging)** — Current is passed for a preset number of cycles; heat is generated and the metal reaches plastic state. Electrode force is maintained (and may be **upset** to forge out oxides).
3. **Hold (Cooling) Phase** — Current is cut off, but electrode force is *retained* until the nugget solidifies. This prevents expulsion of molten metal and avoids porosity/cracking.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Board Exam:** **"S → W → H"** = **S**queeze, **W**eld, **H**old.

## 2.2 Anatomy of Total Resistance in the Welding Circuit

The total resistance $R$ is **not** a single property but a sum of several contact and bulk resistances in series:

$$
R_{total} = 2R_{e} + 2R_{b} + R_{c}
$$

Where:
- $R_e$ = Resistance between electrode tip and top sheet (electrode–work contact resistance)
- $R_b$ = Bulk resistance of one sheet of metal (× 2, for two sheets)
- $R_c$ = **Contact resistance** between the two mating sheet surfaces (this is the **most important** term — it is where most of the heat is generated)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **As temperature rises, $R_c$ DECREASES** (metal softens, contact area grows), while $R_b$ **INCREASES** (resistivity of metals rises with temperature). The crossover point determines nugget formation.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $H = I^2 R t$ | Joule's Law of heat generation | Joules (J) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $R_{total} = 2R_e + 2R_b + R_c$ | Series resistance decomposition | Ohms ($\Omega$) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $P = I^2 R = V I \cos\phi$ | Power dissipated at the joint | Watts (W) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $H_{useful} = \eta \cdot I^2 R t$ | Useful weld heat (process efficiency $\eta \approx 0.10$ to $0.30$) | Joules | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $T_{max} = T_{ambient} + \dfrac{\eta I^2 R t}{m \cdot c_p}$ | Peak joint temperature (energy balance) | K or °C | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $d_{nugget} = K \sqrt{t}$ | Empirical nugget diameter relation (Schwartz/Neumann) | mm | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $F_{electrode} = k \cdot t_{sheet}$ | Empirical electrode force relation (k ≈ 4–6 kN/mm) | kN | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $I_{weld} = K' \cdot t_{sheet}$ | Empirical current relation (K' ≈ 8–12 kA/mm) | kA | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $P_{weld} = I_{weld} \cdot F_{electrode}$ | Welding power requirement for sizing the press | kW | ⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $V_2 = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} V_1$ | Welding transformer step-down ratio (V₂ = 2–20 V) | V | ⭐⭐ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid the `\|` issue:** For absolute value or magnitude, use `\vert` or `\mid` inside LaTeX — never the vertical pipe `|` inside a markdown table cell, as it breaks the table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** A single car body has **4,000 – 5,000 spot welds**. Robotic spot-welding lines run at 30–60 welds per minute.
- **Sheet Metal Fabrication:** HVAC ductwork, kitchen sinks, oil drums.
- **Wire & Cable Industry:** Cross-wire welding for mesh production.
- **Pipeline Construction:** Longitudinal and helical seam welding of large-diameter pipes.
- **Aircraft Industry:** Flash butt welding of landing gear and ring-rolled components.

## 2.5 Spot Welding — Detailed Sub-Process Anatomy

In **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)**, the workpieces are overlapped between two water-cooled **Class II RWMA copper-alloy electrodes**. The current path is:
- **Electrode tip** → **Top sheet** → **Sheet-sheet interface** → **Bottom sheet** → **Bottom electrode tip**.

The **nugget** forms at the faying surface and grows ellipsoidally downward and upward. The **diameter of the nugget** is the primary quality metric, typically measured via **destructive peel/tensile-shear testing** or **ultrasonic NDT**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Trigger:** *"What is the optimum nugget diameter for a t mm thick low-carbon-steel sheet?"*
> **Standard Answer:** $d_{nugget} \approx 5\sqrt{t}$ mm (empirical, AWS C1.1 reference).

## 2.6 The Four-Stage Heat Cycle (Saxena-style model)

For a typical spot weld:

1. **Stage 1 (0 – 25% of weld time):** $R_c$ dominates, rapid heating at the interface.
2. **Stage 2 (25% – 60%):** $R_c$ falls as surfaces conform; $R_b$ rises; melting initiates.
3. **Stage 3 (60% – 100%):** Nugget grows; molten zone expands; expulsion risk peaks.
4. **Stage 4 (Hold):** Current off, but heat conducts outward; nugget solidifies under pressure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Heat Generated in a Resistance Weld (From Joule's First Law)

We begin from Joule's First Law of Electrical Heating, which states that the heat $H$ produced by a steady current $I$ passing through a conductor of resistance $R$ for time $t$ is:

$$
H = I^2 R t
$$

**Step 1 — Start with electrical power dissipated:**
The instantaneous power delivered to the resistance $R$ carrying current $I$ is given by:

$$
P = V I
$$

where $V$ is the voltage drop across $R$. Using Ohm's law $V = I R$, we substitute:

$$
P = (I R) \cdot I = I^2 R
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate power over the welding time:**
For a quasi-steady DC or RMS AC current, power is approximately constant over the weld time $t$. Therefore, total energy (heat) is:

$$
H = \int_{0}^{t} P \, d\tau = \int_{0}^{t} I^2 R \, d\tau
$$

**Step 3 — Assuming constant $I$ and $R$ during the weld cycle:**
Since the welding timer holds $I$ approximately constant and $R$ varies slowly relative to the integration interval, we can take them outside the integral:

$$
H = I^2 R \int_{0}^{t} d\tau = I^2 R \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Final result (Boxed for KTU Exams):**

$$
\boxed{H = I^2 R t \, \text{[Joules]}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Useful Heat vs Total Heat — Introducing Process Efficiency $\eta$

In practice, only a fraction $\eta$ of the generated heat is retained in the weld zone. The remainder is lost. Therefore:

$$
H_{useful} = \eta \cdot I^2 R t
$$

**Step 1 — Define the heat balance:**
At thermal equilibrium during the weld, the useful heat raises the temperature of the weld zone mass $m$ from ambient $T_a$ to the melting/pasting temperature $T_m$:

$$
H_{useful} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_a) + m \cdot L_f
$$

where $L_f$ is the latent heat of fusion (only included once melting is complete).

**Step 2 — Equate the two expressions:**

$$
\eta \cdot I^2 R t = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_m - T_a) + m \cdot L_f
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required welding current:**

$$
I = \sqrt{\dfrac{m [c_p (T_m - T_a) + L_f]}{\eta R t}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration (KTU-style problem):**
For two mild steel sheets each 1 mm thick, overlapped over a 5 mm nugget diameter:
- Sheet area at nugget: $A = \pi (0.0025)^2 = 1.96 \times 10^{-5} \, m^2$
- Heated volume (≈2× sheet thickness): $V = 2 A t = 2 \times 1.96 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.001 = 3.93 \times 10^{-8} \, m^3$
- Mass: $m = \rho V = 7850 \times 3.93 \times 10^{-8} = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \, kg$
- $c_p = 486 \, J/kg\cdot K$, $T_m - T_a = 1500 - 30 = 1470 \, K$, $L_f = 2.72 \times 10^5 \, J/kg$
- $\eta = 0.20$, $R = 200 \, \mu\Omega = 2 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega$, $t = 0.2 \, s$

Numerator: $m [c_p (T_m - T_a) + L_f] = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \times (486 \times 1470 + 2.72 \times 10^5) = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \times (7.14 \times 10^5 + 2.72 \times 10^5) = 3.08 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.86 \times 10^5 \approx 304 \, J$

Denominator: $\eta R t = 0.20 \times 2 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.2 = 8 \times 10^{-6}$

Therefore:

$$
I = \sqrt{\dfrac{304}{8 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{3.8 \times 10^7} \approx 6160 \, A
$$

This matches industrial practice: **6 kA** is a typical current for 1 mm mild steel spot welds.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electrode Force Estimation (Empirical Relation)

For low-carbon steel sheets of thickness $t_{sheet}$ (in mm), the empirical rule is:

$$
F_{electrode} = k \cdot t_{sheet}
$$

where $k \approx 4$ to $6 \, kN/mm$ for mild steel.

**Step 1 — Justification from contact mechanics:**
The contact resistance $R_c$ is inversely proportional to the **real area of contact**, which scales linearly with the electrode force $F$ (Bowden–Tabor model). To maintain a stable, predictable $R_c$, $F$ must scale with sheet thickness (which determines the area of the joint in the depth direction).

**Step 2 — Numerical illustration for 2 mm mild steel sheet:**
$$
F = 5 \times 2 = 10 \, kN
$$

This is the **static upset force** applied by the spot welding head.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Causes, Effects & Remedies in Resistance Welding

| Defect | Probable Cause | Engineering Remedy |
|---|---|---|
| **Expulsion (spitting of molten metal)** | Excessive current, inadequate force, or contaminated surfaces | Reduce current 5–10%; increase force; grind sheets |
| **Insufficient nugget / Cold weld** | Low current, short weld time, dirty surfaces | Increase I or t; clean with acetone; increase force |
| **Porosity in nugget** | Excessive weld time, insufficient force, oxidation | Reduce t; clean surfaces; use shielding (inert gas backing) |
| **Cracks in nugget** | High carbon content; rapid cooling (martensite formation) | Use post-heating; use holding time; preheat |
| **Sheet indentation by electrode** | Excessive electrode force, worn tips, soft tip alloy | Reduce force; dress or replace tip |
| **Sheet separation after weld** | Insufficient squeeze time, uneven surface | Increase squeeze time; check alignment |
| **Pinhole / blowhole** | Trapped gas in nuggets from surface contaminants | Clean surfaces; increase squeeze |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Resistance Spot Welding System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains 415V 50Hz] --> B[Welding Transformer Step Down]
    B --> C[Secondary Loop 2V to 20V]
    C --> D[Upper Electrode Arm Copper Alloy]
    D --> E[Top Workpiece Sheet 1]
    E --> F[Bottom Workpiece Sheet 2 Interface Faying Surface]
    F --> G[Lower Electrode Arm Copper Alloy]
    G --> H[Return to Transformer Secondary]

    I[Squeeze Timer Stage 1] --> J[Weld Timer Stage 2]
    J --> K[Hold Timer Stage 3]
    K --> L[Nugget Solidification]
    L --> M[Electrode Retraction]

    N[Cooling Water Inlet] --> D
    N --> G
    O[Cooling Water Outlet] --> P[Recirculation Chiller]

    Q[Electrode Force Cylinder Pneumatic or Hydraulic] --> D
    Q --> G
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Sequence of Resistance Spot Welding Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start] --> B[Squeeze Phase Apply Force No Current]
    B --> C[Weld Phase Current ON for n Cycles]
    C --> D[Hold Phase Current OFF Force Maintained]
    D --> E[Off Phase Electrodes Retract]
    E --> F[End of Cycle]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Heat Generation & Distribution Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Electrical Input Energy I Squared R t] --> B[Weld Zone Useful Heat eta fraction]
    A --> C[Conduction Loss to Parent Metal]
    A --> D[Conduction Loss to Electrodes and Water]
    A --> E[Radiative and Convective Loss]
    B --> F[Nugget Formation]
    F --> G[Desired Weld]
    C --> H[Scrap Heat]
    D --> H
    E --> H
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Process Parameters Affecting Weld Quality (Ishikawa-style Fishbone)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Z[Resistance Spot Weld Quality] --> P1[Current I]
    Z --> P2[Weld Time t]
    Z --> P3[Electrode Force F]
    Z --> P4[Electrode Tip Geometry]
    Z --> P5[Sheet Surface Condition]
    Z --> P6[Material Resistivity]
    Z --> P7[Sheet Thickness]
    Z --> P8[Transformer Tap Setting]
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Variants of Resistance Welding Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resistance Welding Family] --> B[Spot Welding RSW]
    A --> C[Projection Welding RPW]
    A --> D[Seam Welding RSEW]
    A --> E[Butt Welding RBW]
    A --> F[Flash Welding]
    A --> G[Percussion Welding]
    A --> H[High Frequency Resistance Welding HFRW]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above use **block-level functional architecture** to map the interactions of physical hardware, process phases, and energy distribution — this complies with the Mermaid safeguards and provides a cleaner schematic than attempting an artistically impossible physical drawing of a welding head.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define resistance welding. Why is the process classified as a "pressure" welding method rather than a fusion process?**

**Model Answer:**
Resistance welding is a pressure welding process in which the heat required for coalescence is generated internally by the resistance of the workpieces to a heavy electric current, with simultaneous application of pressure by electrodes. The heat is generated by the **I²Rt** effect, not by an external source.

It is classified as a pressure welding method because:
1. External mechanical force is applied **throughout** the squeeze, weld, and hold phases.
2. The metal is brought to a **plastic or semi-molten** state — not fully molten as in arc welding.
3. The applied force consolidates the joint and forges out surface oxides and voids.
4. No filler metal or external heat source is required.

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: List any four variants of resistance welding and state one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. | Variant | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spot Welding (RSW) | Automobile body-in-white assembly |
| 2 | Projection Welding (RPW) | Nut-and-bolt welding onto chassis |
| 3 | Seam Welding (RSEW) | Fuel tank and oil drum fabrication |
| 4 | Flash Butt Welding | Chain link, ring-rolled flange, and aircraft landing gear manufacturing |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU ESE – Model Paper Pattern]

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** With a neat sketch, describe the **principle, equipment, and process parameters** of **Resistance Spot Welding (RSW)**. How is the **weld nugget** formed?

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**Principle:** In RSW, two overlapping sheets are clamped between two water-cooled copper-alloy electrodes. A heavy current (5–20 kA) is passed for a short time (0.1–1.5 s) at low voltage (2–20 V). Heat is generated at the sheet–sheet interface due to high contact resistance, raising the temperature to the forging/plastic range. The simultaneous electrode force consolidates the joint into a solid-state bond with a localized molten nugget at the center.

**Equipment:** The major components are
1. **Welding transformer** (step-down, turns ratio ≈ 50:1, primary 415 V → secondary 4–20 V)
2. **Upper and lower electrode arms** (water-cooled Class II RWMA copper alloy)
3. **Electrode force mechanism** (pneumatic, hydraulic, or servo-electric)
4. **Weld control timer** (synchronous SCR contactor, programmable in cycles of 50 Hz)
5. **Cooling water recirculation system** (chiller, flow switch, interlock)
6. **Sheet holding fixtures / jigs**

**Process Parameters (the "Big Five"):**
- **Welding current (I)** — 5 to 20 kA
- **Weld time (t)** — 5 to 60 cycles (0.1 to 1.2 s on 50 Hz mains)
- **Electrode force (F)** — 2 to 10 kN
- **Electrode tip diameter (d)** — 4 to 8 mm
- **Hold time** — 5 to 30 cycles (for nugget solidification)

**Nugget Formation Mechanism:**
- Initially, the contact between sheets is at a few asperity points → high local current density.
- Asperity heating causes local softening → contact area grows → resistance drops, current distributes over a larger region.
- Bulk heating of the surrounding material raises temperature past the plastic range (≈ 0.5–0.8 Tₘ).
- A small molten pool (nugget) nucleates at the center of the contact zone.
- The nugget grows ellipsoidally in both sheets as long as current flows.
- During the hold phase, current is cut off; the molten nugget solidifies under electrode pressure, forming a fine-grained, metallurgically bonded joint.
- Typical nugget diameter = **5√t** mm (where t is sheet thickness in mm).

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** During a spot welding experiment on two mild steel sheets of 1.5 mm thickness, the following data was recorded: welding current $I = 9000 \, A$, weld time $t = 0.4 \, s$, contact resistance $R_c = 250 \, \mu\Omega$, bulk resistance per sheet $R_b = 50 \, \mu\Omega$, electrode contact resistance $R_e = 30 \, \mu\Omega$. Calculate the **total heat generated** and the **useful heat** if the process efficiency is 25%.

**Model Answer (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate total resistance $R_{total}$:**
$$
R_{total} = 2R_e + 2R_b + R_c
$$

$$
R_{total} = 2(30) + 2(50) + 250 = 60 + 100 + 250 = 410 \, \mu\Omega = 4.10 \times 10^{-4} \, \Omega
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Joule's Law to find total heat generated:**

$$
H_{total} = I^2 R_{total} t
$$

$$
H_{total} = (9000)^2 \times (4.10 \times 10^{-4}) \times 0.4
$$

$$
H_{total} = 81 \times 10^6 \times 4.10 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4
$$

$$
H_{total} = 81 \times 4.10 \times 0.4 \times 10^{6-4} = 132.84 \times 10^2 = 13284 \, J
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{total} \approx 13.28 \, kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency to find useful heat:**

$$
H_{useful} = \eta \times H_{total} = 0.25 \times 13284
$$

$$
\boxed{H_{useful} \approx 3321 \, J = 3.32 \, kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Inference:**
Of the 13.28 kJ generated, only 3.32 kJ (25%) is used to form the nugget; the remaining ~9.96 kJ is lost to the copper electrodes, the parent metal conduction, and surface convection/radiation. This validates the KTU rule-of-thumb that **70–90% of input energy in resistance welding is wasted** as heat loss.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU ESE – Alternative Choice]

**Q (a) (7 Marks):** Explain the **principle of flash butt welding**. Compare it with **upset (pressure) butt welding** in terms of heat generation mechanism, weld quality, and applications.

**Model Answer (Part a):**

**Flash Butt Welding Principle:**
The two workpieces are brought close together with a small gap and connected to the secondary of a heavy welding transformer. As one piece advances slowly towards the other, the initial contact occurs at small asperities. The very high current density at these points instantly **vaporizes the metal**, creating a continuous shower of sparks (the "flash"). The flashing action continues across the entire cross-section, expelling oxides and contaminants. When the flashing temperature is uniform, a sudden high upset force is applied, completing the joint.

**Heat Generation Mechanism:**
- In **flash welding**, heat is generated primarily by **arcing/vaporization of micro-asperities** (a form of micro-arc) plus bulk I²R heating.
- In **upset (pressure) butt welding**, heat is generated purely by **I²R** heating, with no flash or arcing.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Flash Butt Welding | Upset Butt Welding |
|---|---|---|
| **Heat source** | Arcing at asperities + I²R | Pure I²R |
| **Surface preparation** | Not critical (flashed off) | Must be very clean |
| **Upset loss** | Significant (5–10% length) | Small (1–2% length) |
| **Weld quality** | Excellent (oxide-free) | Good, but inclusions possible |
| **Metals welded** | Wide range: mild steel, alloy steel, Al, Cu, Ti | Limited (cleaner metals only) |
| **Power required** | Lower (low voltage) | Higher (sustained current) |
| **Applications** | Chain, rings, auto axles, railway rails | Wire and rod stock |
| **Operator skill** | Moderate | High |

**Q (b) (7 Marks):** A resistance spot welding machine operates on **415 V, 50 Hz, single-phase AC mains**. The transformer turns ratio is 50:1. The secondary delivers a current of 8000 A through a total joint resistance of 350 μΩ for a weld time of 12 cycles. Calculate the **secondary voltage**, **total heat generated**, and the **average power dissipated** in the joint.

**Model Answer (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate secondary voltage $V_2$:**

$$
V_2 = \dfrac{V_1}{N} = \dfrac{415}{50} = 8.3 \, V
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate weld time $t$ in seconds:**

$$
t = \dfrac{n}{f} = \dfrac{12}{50} = 0.24 \, s
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate total heat $H$:**

$$
H = I^2 R t = (8000)^2 \times (350 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.24
$$

$$
H = 64 \times 10^6 \times 350 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.24
$$

$$
H = 64 \times 350 \times 0.24 = 5376 \, J
$$

$$
\boxed{H \approx 5.376 \, kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate average power dissipated:**

$$
P_{avg} = \dfrac{H}{t} = \dfrac{5376}{0.24} = 22400 \, W = 22.4 \, kW
$$

Alternatively:

$$
P = I^2 R = (8000)^2 \times 350 \times 10^{-6} = 22400 \, W
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{avg} \approx 22.4 \, kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Before You Write!**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Forgetting the factor "2" in the formula $R_{total} = 2R_e + 2R_b + R_c$. Markers **WILL** deduct 1 mark.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** Mixing up "squeeze time" with "weld time" in process descriptions. Squeeze has **no current**.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Reporting nugget diameter as $5\sqrt{t_{sheet}}$ where $t$ is the *weld time* (wrong). It is the *sheet thickness* in mm. **This is a 2-mark trap.**
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting to convert **cycles → seconds** using $t = n / f$. 12 cycles on 50 Hz is 0.24 s, not 12 s.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** Writing $H = I \cdot V \cdot t$ without verifying the "no power factor" assumption. For **pure resistance** welding circuits, $\cos\phi = 1$, so this is acceptable. Always **state the assumption**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Last-Minute Revision Punchlist for Resistance Welding:**

- [x] **Heat Equation** — Memorize $H = I^2 R t$ (Joules). This is the single most-tested formula in the lab course.
- [x] **Total Resistance** — $R_{total} = 2R_e + 2R_b + R_c$ — always in **series** and in **micro-ohms**.
- [x] **Three-Phase Cycle** — **S**queeze → **W**eld → **H**old (mnemonic "**S-W-H**").
- [x] **Contact resistance $R_c$** is the **largest** contributor to total resistance in the initial phase → most heat is generated **at the interface**.
- [x] **Process efficiency $\eta$** is typically **0.10 to 0.30** — only 10–30% of generated heat forms the nugget.
- [x] **Nugget diameter** empirical rule: $d_{nugget} \approx 5\sqrt{t_{sheet}}$ mm (sheet thickness in mm).
- [x] **Electrode force rule** for mild steel: $F \approx 5 \cdot t_{sheet}$ kN.
- [x] **Current rule** for mild steel: $I \approx 8 \text{ to } 12$ kA per mm of sheet thickness.
- [x] **Weld time** is measured in **cycles of 50 Hz** mains, not seconds. Convert: $t = n/f$.
- [x] **Voltage** at the secondary is **2 – 20 V** (extremely low voltage, very high current).
- [x] **Cooling water** to electrodes is **mandatory** — Class II copper-alloy electrodes cannot survive without it.
- [x] **RSW** = single nugget; **RSEW** = continuous roller electrode series of overlapping nuggets; **RPW** = current concentrated at embossed projections; **Flash butt** = arcing + upset.
- [x] **Low-carbon steel** is the most weldable material in RW; **aluminum** requires higher current due to high thermal/electrical conductivity; **stainless steel** requires higher force and shorter time.
- [x] **Largest automotive application:** Body-in-white (BIW) assembly — 4,000+ spot welds per car.
- [x] **Quality inspection:** Destructive peel test, tensile-shear test, and **ultrasonic NDT** for nugget diameter verification.
- [x] **Defect "expulsion"** = metal spitting → indicates **excess I or t or contaminated surface**. **Never weld over expulsion** — re-grind or re-stamp the sheet.
- [x] **The I²R equation is the soul of the course** — every numerical problem, every derivation, every concept test traces back to it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Advice for KTU Board:** Always **state assumptions** (steady current, pure resistance load, no power factor loss), **box the final numerical answer**, and **mention units** in every step. The above is worth 1–2 extra marks on borderline evaluations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Soldering and Brazing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Soldering and Brazing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Soldering

**Soldering** is a metal-joining process in which a **filler metal (solder)** having a melting point **below 450 °C (840 °F)** is melted and drawn into the joint between two closely fitted base metal parts by **capillary action**. The base metals themselves are **not melted**; bonding is achieved through a combination of **metallurgical adhesion, mechanical interlocking, and limited diffusion** between the molten solder and the solid base metal surfaces.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> In Soldering, the filler metal has a **liquidus temperature below 450 °C** and the joint is formed **without fusion of the base metals**.

## 1.2 Definition of Brazing

**Brazing** is a metal-joining process in which a **filler metal (braze)** having a melting point **above 450 °C (840 °F) but below the solidus temperature of the base metals** is melted and distributed by **capillary action** between the closely fitted surfaces of the joint. The base metals do not melt, and bonding occurs through **metallurgical diffusion, alloying, and interatomic bonding** at the interface.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):**
> In Brazing, the filler metal liquidus is **above 450 °C** but **below the solidus of the base metals**. The base metal remains in the **solid state** throughout the process.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **gluing two pieces of wood together** with a strong adhesive — the glue flows into the gap and hardens, holding the wood without changing the wood itself. Soldering and Brazing work on a similar principle, but with **molten metal instead of glue**.

- **Soldering** ≈ using a low-temperature "metal glue" (like soft candle wax) for **delicate, low-strength joints** (e.g., wires on a printed circuit board).
- **Brazing** ≈ using a higher-temperature "metal weld" (like melting cheese between bread) for **stronger, structural joints** (e.g., copper tubes in an air conditioner).

A more precise analogy: think of **water soaking into a paper towel placed between two glass plates**. The water (molten filler) flows through the tiny gap (capillary action), and as it cools, it forms a solid bond that grips both plates.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Soldering | Brazing |
|---|---|---|
| **Filler melting point** | **< 450 °C** | **> 450 °C**, < base metal solidus |
| **Base metal state** | **Solid (no melting)** | **Solid (no melting)** |
| **Joint strength** | **Low to moderate** | **High (often equals base metal)** |
| **Typical joint clearance** | **0.05–0.25 mm** | **0.025–0.125 mm** |
| **Bonding mechanism** | Adhesion + capillary | **Diffusion + alloying + capillary** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **450 °C threshold** is the universally accepted industrial demarcation between soldering and brazing, codified in **AWS (American Welding Society) C3.2** and **ISO 857-2** standards. The KTU 2024 syllabus adheres to this same convention.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Operating Principle

Both processes follow the same **four-stage thermal-mechanical cycle**:

1. **Surface Preparation** – Base metals are cleaned (degreased, mechanically abraded, or chemically etched) to remove oxides, oils, and dirt. A clean surface allows the molten filler to "wet" the base metal.
2. **Flux Application** – A chemical **flux** is applied to the joint to:
   - Dissolve and remove residual oxides during heating.
   - Prevent re-oxidation by forming a protective liquid blanket.
   - Lower the surface tension of the molten filler, improving wetting.
3. **Heating & Filler Flow** – Heat is applied (soldering iron, torch, furnace, induction coil). Once the joint reaches the filler's working temperature, the filler is introduced and **flows by capillary action** into the joint clearance.
4. **Cooling & Solidification** – Heat is removed. The filler solidifies, forming a **permanent metallurgical bond**.

## 2.2 The Science of Capillary Action (The Core "Why")

The driving force for filler flow is **capillary pressure**, given by the **Jurin's Law** equation (derived from the Young-Laplace equation for a thin film):

$$P_{\text{cap}} = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r}$$

Where:
- $P_{\text{cap}}$ = Capillary pressure (Pa) — drives filler into the gap
- $\gamma$ = Surface tension of molten filler (N/m)
- $\theta$ = Contact angle between filler and base metal (degrees)
- $r$ = Effective joint gap (m)

### Interpretation for KTU

- **Smaller gap ($r$ down) → Higher capillary pressure → Better filler penetration** (up to a limit).
- **Lower contact angle ($\theta$ down, better wetting) → Higher capillary pressure → Stronger joint**.
- Flux lowers $\gamma$ and reduces $\theta$, hence its critical role.

## 2.3 Heat Input & Penetration Depth

The approximate **depth of heating** into the base metal (in mm) for a moving heat source is given by the empirical relation:

$$d \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot k \cdot t}{\rho \cdot c}}$$

Where:
- $d$ = Depth of heat penetration (m)
- $k$ = Thermal conductivity of base metal (W/m·K)
- $t$ = Time of heat application (s)
- $\rho$ = Density of base metal (kg/m³)
- $c$ = Specific heat capacity of base metal (J/kg·K)

## 2.4 Joint Strength (Tensile Shear Load)

For a lap joint, the **allowable shear load** is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \tau \cdot (w \cdot L)$$

Where:
- $F$ = Tensile load the joint can sustain (N)
- $\tau$ = Shear strength of filler alloy (Pa)
- $A$ = Joint overlap area (m²)
- $w$ = Joint width (m)
- $L$ = Joint overlap length (m)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Formula | Description | Units |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | $T_{\text{solder}} < 450\,^\circ\text{C}$ | Soldering filler liquidus threshold | °C |
| 2 | $T_{\text{braze}} > 450\,^\circ\text{C} < T_{\text{solidus, base}}$ | Brazing filler liquidus range | °C |
| 3 | $P_{\text{cap}} = \dfrac{2\,\gamma\,\cos\theta}{r}$ | Capillary pressure (Jurin's Law) | Pa |
| 4 | $d \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2\,k\,t}{\rho\,c}}$ | Heat penetration depth | m |
| 5 | $F = \tau \cdot w \cdot L$ | Joint lap shear load capacity | N |
| 6 | $Q = m \cdot c \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat required to raise filler to melting point | J |
| 7 | $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f$ | Latent heat of fusion of filler | J |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Remember for KTU Board Exams:** Always state the **temperature threshold (450 °C)**, **capillary action**, and **no base-metal melting** as the three non-negotiable facts whenever asked to distinguish or define these processes.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics Manufacturing (SMT, Through-Hole):** Soldering is the backbone of PCB assembly. **Lead-free SAC (Sn-Ag-Cu)** solders are mandated by **RoHS** directives.
- **HVAC & Plumbing:** Brazing with **copper-phosphorus (CuP)** filler is the standard for refrigerant lines in air-conditioners and refrigerators.
- **Automotive:** Brazed radiators, heat exchangers, and carburetor assemblies.
- **Aerospace:** Vacuum brazing of **titanium honeycomb panels** in jet engine nacelles and heat shields.
- **Jewelry & Cutlery:** Silver brazing for high-strength, aesthetically clean joints.
- **Tool Manufacturing:** Brazed carbide tips on steel tool shanks (drill bits, turning inserts).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capillary Rise Height (Classical Jurin's Law)

**Statement:** When a thin tube of radius $r$ is placed in a liquid of surface tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$, the liquid rises to a height $h$ given by Jurin's Law.

**Step 1:** Force balance on the liquid column in the tube.
