The **upward force** is the vertical component of surface tension acting along the tube's circumference.

$$F_{\text{up}} = \gamma \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 2:** The **downward force** is the weight of the liquid column of height $h$.

$$F_{\text{down}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot (\pi r^{2} h)$$

**Step 3:** At equilibrium, set $F_{\text{up}} = F_{\text{down}}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma \cdot 2\pi r \cdot \cos\theta &= \rho \cdot g \cdot \pi r^{2} h \\
h &= \frac{2\,\gamma\,\cos\theta}{\rho\,g\,r}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to capillary **pressure** form. Since pressure = force/area:

$$P_{\text{cap}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h = \frac{2\,\gamma\,\cos\theta}{r}$$

This is the driving pressure that pulls molten filler into the joint gap.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Heat Required to Melt Filler Metal

**Step 1:** Heat to raise filler from room temperature to its melting point (sensible heat).

$$Q_{1} = m \cdot c \cdot (T_{\text{melt}} - T_{\text{room}})$$

**Step 2:** Heat to change state from solid to liquid (latent heat).

$$Q_{2} = m \cdot L_{f}$$

**Step 3:** Heat to superheat liquid filler slightly above melting point for good flow.

$$Q_{3} = m \cdot c_{\text{liq}} \cdot (T_{\text{working}} - T_{\text{melt}})$$

**Step 4:** Total heat required.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{total}} &= Q_{1} + Q_{2} + Q_{3} \\
&= m \cdot \left[ c\,(T_{\text{melt}} - T_{\text{room}}) + L_{f} + c_{\text{liq}}\,(T_{\text{working}} - T_{\text{melt}}) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Favourite Table)

| Parameter | Soldering | Brazing | Welding (for contrast) |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Filler Melting Point** | **< 450 °C** | **> 450 °C, < Base Solidus** | **= or > Base Melting** |
| **Base Metal Melting** | **No** | **No** | **Yes (fusion)** |
| **Joint Strength** | Low–Moderate | **High (≈ base metal)** | **Highest (continuous)** |
| **Filler Material Examples** | 60/40 Sn-Pb, SAC 305 | Cu, Brass, Ag-alloys | Parent / matching metal |
| **Heat Source** | Iron, hot plate | Torch, furnace, induction | Arc, gas flame, laser |
| **Flux Used** | Rosin, organic | Borax, fluoride-based | Not always required |
| **Joint Clearance** | 0.05–0.25 mm | 0.025–0.125 mm | Direct contact / 0 mm |
| **Typical Applications** | PCBs, wires, plumbing | HVAC, auto, aerospace | Structural, shipbuilding |
| **Distortion of Base Metal** | Negligible | Very low | Possible (heat-affected zone) |
| **Skill Level** | Low–Moderate | Moderate–High | High |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A soldered lap joint is 12 mm wide with a 20 mm overlap. The filler alloy (60/40 Sn-Pb) has a shear strength of **38 MPa**. Calculate the maximum tensile load the joint can sustain. If the joint is to carry a load of **8 kN**, is the design safe? If not, what overlap length is required?

**Step 1:** Calculate joint area.

$$A = w \cdot L = 12\,\text{mm} \times 20\,\text{mm} = 240\,\text{mm}^{2} = 240 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate maximum load.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{\max} &= \tau \cdot A \\
&= 38 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Pa} \times 240 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{2} \\
&= 9120\,\text{N} = 9.12\,\text{kN}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compare with applied load.

$$F_{\text{applied}} = 8\,\text{kN} < F_{\max} = 9.12\,\text{kN} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{Design is SAFE (Factor of Safety} \approx 1.14\textbf{)}$$

**Step 4 (alternative):** Required overlap for 8 kN load.

$$
L_{\text{required}} = \frac{F}{\tau \cdot w} = \frac{8000}{38 \times 10^{6} \times 12 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0175\,\text{m} = 17.5\,\text{mm}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow Architecture (Soldering vs Brazing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Base Metal Preparation] --> B[Cleaning: Degrease, Abrade, Etch]
    B --> C[Apply Flux to Joint Area]
    C --> D{Process Selection}
    D -->|Temp < 450 C| E[Soldering Process]
    D -->|Temp > 450 C| F[Brazing Process]
    E --> E1[Heat Source: Soldering Iron / Hot Plate]
    F --> F1[Heat Source: Torch / Furnace / Induction]
    E1 --> G[Introduce Filler: Sn-Pb / SAC]
    F1 --> H[Introduce Filler: Cu / Brass / Ag-alloy]
    G --> I[Capillary Flow into Joint]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Cooling and Solidification]
    J --> K[Flux Residue Removal and Cleaning]
    K --> L[Visual and Dimensional Inspection]
    L --> M[End: Completed Brazed or Soldered Joint]
```

## 4.2 Joint Configuration and Clearance Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Base Metal Part A] --- B[Joint Clearance: 0.025 to 0.25 mm]
    B --- C[Base Metal Part B]
    D[Molten Filler Inlet] --> B
    E[Capillary Force] -.-> B
    F[Flux Layer on Surface] -.-> A
    F -.-> C
    G[Heat Source: External] -.-> A
    G -.-> C
```

## 4.3 Equipment and Tool Block Diagram (Lab Setup)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph H[Heat Source Options]
        H1[Soldering Iron 25 to 100 W]
        H2[Gas Torch Oxy Acetylene]
        H3[Induction Coil]
        H4[Muffle Furnace]
    end
    subgraph F[Filler Metals]
        F1[Soft Solder: Sn-Pb 60-40]
        F2[Lead Free: SAC 305]
        F3[Brass Filler: Cu-Zn]
        F4[Silver Brazing: BAg Series]
    end
    subgraph X[Fluxes]
        X1[Rosin based for Electronics]
        X2[Zinc Chloride for Plumbing]
        X3[Borax Boric Acid for Brazing]
    end
    subgraph T[Tools]
        T1[Wire Cutters and Strippers]
        T2[Files and Abrasive Cloth]
        T3[Soldering Iron Stand]
        T4[Tongs and Heat Resistant Gloves]
        T5[Safety Goggles]
    end
    H --> J[Lab Joint Assembly Station]
    F --> J
    X --> J
    T --> J
    J --> OUT[Finished Joint for Inspection]
```

## 4.4 Inspection Methods Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Completed Joint] --> B{Inspection Type}
    B --> C[Visual Inspection: Color, Shape, Smoothness]
    B --> D[Dimensional Check: Clearance, Fillet Size]
    B --> E[Mechanical Test: Tensile or Shear]
    B --> F[Non Destructive Test: Dye Penetrant Test]
    B --> G[Thermal Test: Thermal Cycling for Electronics]
    C --> H[Accept or Reject Decision]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define soldering. State any two applications of soldering.**

**Model Answer:**
Soldering is a metal-joining process in which a filler metal (solder) with a melting point **below 450 °C** is melted and drawn into the joint between two close-fitting base metal parts by capillary action, without melting the base metals.

**Applications (any two):**
1. Joining of electronic components on **printed circuit boards (PCBs)**.
2. **Plumbing** and pipe-fitting for water supply lines using lead-tin solder.
3. **Sheet metal joints** in tin cans, automobile bodies, and roofing.
4. **Jewelry making** and **stained-glass artwork**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**List the functions of flux in soldering and brazing.**

**Model Answer:**
The functions of flux are:
1. **Dissolves and removes** existing oxide layers from the base metal surface during heating.
2. **Prevents re-oxidation** by forming a protective liquid blanket over the joint.
3. **Reduces surface tension** of the molten filler, improving **wetting** and capillary flow.
4. **Improves the strength and quality** of the joint by ensuring intimate metal-to-metal contact.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **soldering process**. Discuss the **types of solders**, **fluxes used**, and **step-by-step procedure** for soldering a joint. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Soldering is a joining process in which a filler metal (solder) having a melting point **below 450 °C** is melted and drawn into the joint gap by capillary action, joining the base metals without melting them.

**Types of Solders:**
1. **Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb) Solders** – Most common, e.g., **60/40 (60% Sn, 40% Pb)** melts at 188 °C, used in general electronics.
2. **Lead-Free Solders** – e.g., **SAC 305 (Sn 96.5%, Ag 3%, Cu 0.5%)** melts at 217 °C, mandated by **RoHS** for eco-friendly electronics.
3. **Silver-bearing Solders** – Used where higher strength is needed.
4. **Bismuth and Indium Solders** – Used for low-temperature applications.

**Types of Fluxes:**
- **Rosin flux** – Non-corrosive, used in electronics.
- **Organic (water-soluble) flux** – Mild activity, easy to clean.
- **Inorganic (acid) flux** – Highly active, used in plumbing; must be cleaned after.

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. **Clean** the base metal surfaces (degrease with solvent, abrade with emery).
2. **Apply flux** to the joint area.
3. **Heat** the joint with a soldering iron or hot plate.
4. **Apply solder** to the joint (not the iron tip) — let it melt and flow by capillary.
5. **Remove heat** and allow the joint to **cool undisturbed**.
6. **Clean the joint** to remove flux residue and inspect.

---

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the **brazing process**. List **three methods of brazing** and state **two advantages** of brazing over soldering. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Brazing is a joining process in which a filler metal (braze) with a melting point **above 450 °C but below the solidus of the base metals** is melted and drawn into the joint by capillary action.

**Sketch (Capillary Brazed Joint):**

```
   Base Metal A        Base Metal B
   |=================|=================|
   |    (Joint Clearance 0.05 mm)      |
   |====[==Filler Metal Filet==]=======|
   |=================|=================|
        (Filler flows from outside in by capillary)
```

**Three Methods of Brazing:**
1. **Torch Brazing** – Heat applied manually using an oxy-acetylene or air-acetylene torch; flexible and common.
2. **Furnace Brazing** – Entire assembly heated in a controlled-atmosphere furnace; suitable for mass production.
3. **Induction Brazing** – High-frequency AC induces eddy-current heating in the base metal; very fast, localized.

**Two Advantages of Brazing over Soldering:**
1. **Higher joint strength** – Often approaches the strength of the base metal, suitable for structural and load-bearing applications.
2. **Better heat resistance** – Brazed joints withstand service temperatures well above 450 °C, while soldered joints soften.
3. **Joining dissimilar metals** – Brazing can join widely different metals (e.g., copper to steel) that cannot be easily welded.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice

**(a)** Compare **soldering and brazing** based on **filler metal, temperature, joint strength, flux used, and applications** in a tabular form. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Soldering | Brazing |
|---|---|---|
| **Filler Metal** | Sn-Pb, SAC 305 (soft) | Cu, Brass, Ag-alloys (hard) |
| **Temperature** | **< 450 °C** | **> 450 °C, < Base Solidus** |
| **Joint Strength** | Low to moderate | **High (≈ base metal)** |
| **Bonding** | Adhesion + capillary | **Diffusion + alloying** |
| **Flux** | Rosin, organic | **Borax, fluoride** |
| **Applications** | PCBs, plumbing, sheet metal | HVAC, auto, aerospace |
| **Heat Source** | Soldering iron, hot plate | **Torch, furnace, induction** |

---

**(b)** Describe the **step-by-step procedure** for performing a **brazing experiment in the lab** with **safety precautions** and **inspection of the joint**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure:**
1. **Prepare specimens** – Cut two mild steel / copper plates (say 50 mm × 25 mm).
2. **Clean surfaces** – File and abrade the joint surfaces; degrease with acetone.
3. **Set clearance** – Maintain a uniform gap of **0.05–0.1 mm** using spacers.
4. **Apply flux** – Coat both surfaces with **borax-based brazing flux**.
5. **Assemble joint** – Clamp the plates in a lap or butt-brazing fixture.
6. **Heat** – Use an **oxy-acetylene torch** with a reducing flame; heat uniformly until flux melts and flows.
7. **Add filler** – Touch the brass / CuP filler rod to the joint; it will melt and flow by capillary action.
8. **Cool** – Allow the joint to cool naturally (do not quench).
9. **Clean** – Remove flux residue with warm water and a wire brush.
10. **Inspect** – Check for uniform fillet, no porosity, no cracks.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Wear **safety goggles**, **heat-resistant gloves**, and **leather apron**.
- Work in a **well-ventilated area** to avoid inhaling flux fumes.
- Keep a **fire extinguisher (CO₂ type)** nearby.
- Never heat near flammable materials; use a firebrick backing.

**Inspection Methods:**
- **Visual** – Uniform fillet, smooth, shiny surface.
- **Dye Penetrant Test (DPT)** – Reveals surface cracks.
- **Tap Test** – Sharp metallic ring indicates a sound joint; dull sound indicates defects.
- **Dimensional check** – Verify joint dimensions with vernier calipers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Wrong temperature threshold** – Many students write "soft soldering < 200 °C" — this is **incorrect**. The KTU/ISO/AWS standard demarcation is **< 450 °C for soldering** and **> 450 °C for brazing**.
> 2. **Confusing brazing with welding** – Brazing does **NOT** melt the base metal. Writing "filler and base metal both melt" will fetch **zero marks**.
> 3. **Skipping the term "capillary action"** – The driving force is **capillary action**, not "just heating". A definition without it is incomplete.
> 4. **Omitting flux in the procedure** – The procedure steps in a 7-mark question **must include flux application**; examiners specifically look for it.
> 5. **No sketch in 7-mark procedure questions** – A **neat labeled diagram** is worth **2–3 marks by itself**; skipping it is a guaranteed loss.
> 6. **Mixing up lead-free solder composition** – SAC 305 means **Sn 96.5%, Ag 3.0%, Cu 0.5%** — get the numbers right.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Rapid Revision – Soldering and Brazing**

- **Soldering** = Filler melting point **< 450 °C**.
- **Brazing** = Filler melting point **> 450 °C, < base metal solidus**.
- **Base metal is NOT melted** in either process.
- **Bonding driver** = **Capillary action** (Jurin's Law: $P_{\text{cap}} = 2\gamma\cos\theta / r$).
- **Flux** = dissolves oxides, prevents re-oxidation, lowers surface tension, improves wetting.
- **60/40 Sn-Pb** melts at **188 °C**; **SAC 305** (lead-free) melts at **217 °C**.
- **Joint clearance** = Soldering: 0.05–0.25 mm; Brazing: 0.025–0.125 mm.
- **Brazing is stronger** and **more heat-resistant** than soldering.
- **Common brazing methods** = Torch, Furnace, Induction, Dip, Resistance, Vacuum.
- **Common soldering methods** = Iron, hot plate, wave, reflow (SMT).
- **Lab procedure must include** = Cleaning → Fluxing → Heating → Filler addition → Capillary flow → Cooling → Inspection.
- **Safety** = Goggles, gloves, ventilation, fire extinguisher, no quenching.
- **Inspection** = Visual, Dye Penetrant, Tap test, Shear/tensile test.
- **Capillary pressure formula** and **lap joint load formula** are the two most-tested equations in the 14-mark module questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC Programming Simulation Using Any Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** CNC Programming Simulation Using Any Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC Programming & Simulation** is the process of authoring a sequence of alphanumeric instructions (called a *part program*) using standardized ISO/EIA G-codes and M-codes, followed by virtually executing those instructions in a software environment that emulates a CNC machine tool's kinematics, tool motion, and material removal behavior, in order to **verify dimensional accuracy, detect collisions, and validate tool paths before any physical machining takes place.**

In the context of **KTU PCAUL407 (Production Engineering Lab)**, this experiment falls under the umbrella of *Computer-Aided Manufacturing (CAM)* and constitutes one of the foundational lab cycles where students demonstrate competency in:

- Drafting a manual part program (hand-coded NC code)
- Selecting an appropriate simulation platform (e.g., **CNC Simulator Pro**, **Fusion 360**, **Mastercam**, **SolidWorks CAM**, **EdgeCAM**, or **CATIA**)
- Setting up the work coordinate system (WCS)
- Defining stock, fixture, and tool geometry
- Running the simulation, interpreting the rendered tool path, and confirming the final machined profile against the drawing

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme – Course Outcome Mapping:**
> This experiment primarily satisfies **CO2**: *Apply CNC programming concepts to manufacture simple components and verify the program using simulation software.* It also reinforces **CO4** (use of modern tools) and **CO5** (documentation of lab work).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are learning to drive a car in heavy Bengaluru (or Kochi) traffic. Before being allowed on the actual road, the driving school makes you practice on a **driving simulator** — a fake cockpit with screens, a steering wheel, and pedals. The simulator reacts exactly like a real car: you press the accelerator, it accelerates; you steer left, the road bends; if you crash into a virtual wall, you crash (and no real damage occurs).

**CNC Programming Simulation is exactly this — but for machining.**

- The **part program** = your driving instructions (turn, accelerate, brake).
- The **CNC simulator software** = the virtual cockpit.
- The **3D rendered workpiece** = the virtual road.
- The **tool crashing into the stock or fixture** = your virtual accident.

This means a student can **safely test dangerous operations** (high-speed pocketing, deep drilling, 5-axis simultaneous motion) without ever breaking a real cutting tool — which in industry can cost anywhere from ₹500 for a HSS drill to ₹50,000+ for a single carbide insert.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** Modern CNC shops (e.g., automotive tier-1 suppliers like Bosch, TVS, or aerospace OEMs) **never** run a raw part program on a real machine first. They simulate it offline in software like **Vericut** (the gold standard for verification) before any chip is ever cut. This single step saves an estimated **30–40% of setup time** and dramatically reduces tool breakage.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature (Bolded Constants)

The following terms are **standardized across ISO 6983 (G-code)** and must be memorized verbatim:

| Term | Definition | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| **NC** | Numerical Control | Historical term |
| **CNC** | Computer Numerical Control | **ISO 6983** |
| **G-code** | Preparatory function (geometry/motion) | **ISO 6983-1** |
| **M-code** | Miscellaneous function (on/off actions) | **ISO 6983-2** |
| **DNC** | Direct Numerical Control (networked) | MTConnect |
| **WCS** | Work Coordinate System | **G54–G59** |
| **APT** | Automatically Programmed Tools (high-level lang.) | MIT 1959 |
| **Modal code** | Stays active until cancelled | G01, G02, etc. |
| **Block** | One line of NC code terminated by `;` or LF | ISO 6983 |

---

## 1.4 Why This Experiment Matters in KTU Lab Evaluation

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Typical Question Style:** "Write a manual part program to machine the given profile and simulate it using CNC simulator software. Show the tool path and final component." — This is a **12–14 mark question** in the lab exam and an internal assessment component.

The lab record typically expects:
1. **Aim & Apparatus** block
2. **Workpiece drawing** (with dimensions)
3. **Process sheet** (tool selection, cutting parameters)
4. **Hand-written part program**
5. **Simulation screenshots** (tool path + final profile)
6. **Result / Inference** paragraph
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a CNC Part Program Block

A standard NC block (line of code) follows this rigid structure:

$$
\boxed{\text{N\_} \;\; \text{G\_} \;\; \text{X\_} \;\; \text{Y\_} \;\; \text{Z\_} \;\; \text{F\_} \;\; \text{S\_} \;\; \text{T\_} \;\; \text{M\_}}
$$

Where:
- **N** = Sequence/line number (optional in modern controls)
- **G** = Preparatory function (motion type)
- **X, Y, Z** = Coordinate positions (in **mm** for metric, **inch** for imperial)
- **F** = Feed rate (**mm/min** or **mm/rev**)
- **S** = Spindle speed (**RPM**)
- **T** = Tool number (e.g., T01)
- **M** = Miscellaneous function (coolant, spindle stop, etc.)

> [!NOTE]
> A **block** = one line. Multiple commands per line are allowed in most controllers (e.g., Fanuc, Siemens, Haas), but readability is best with one logical command per line.

---

## 2.2 Essential G-Codes (KTU High-Yield List — Memorize!)

### 2.2.1 Motion G-Codes (Modal)

| G-Code | Function | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|
| **G00** | Rapid traverse (no cutting) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (straight cut) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G02** | Circular interpolation – **clockwise (CW)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G03** | Circular interpolation – **counter-clockwise (CCW)** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G04** | Dwell (pause) — `G04 X2.0` = pause 2 seconds | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G17 / G18 / G19** | Plane selection — **XY / XZ / YZ** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G20 / G21** | Inch / Metric units | ⭐⭐ |
| **G28** | Return to home position via reference point | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G40 / G41 / G42** | Cutter compensation — **OFF / LEFT / RIGHT** | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G43** | Tool length compensation (+ve direction) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G54–G59** | Work coordinate system selection (fixture offsets) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G90 / G91** | Absolute / Incremental programming | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **G94 / G95** | Feed per minute / Feed per revolution | ⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.2.2 Essential M-Codes

| M-Code | Function |
|---|---|
| **M00** | Program stop (mandatory) |
| **M01** | Optional stop (machine-dependent) |
| **M02** | Program end |
| **M03** | Spindle ON — **CW** |
| **M04** | Spindle ON — **CCW** |
| **M05** | Spindle stop |
| **M06** | Automatic tool change (ATC) |
| **M07 / M08** | Coolant ON (mist / flood) |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF |
| **M30** | Program end + rewind to start |

---

## 2.3 Cutting Parameter Formulas (The KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three formulas account for **70% of numerical calculation marks** in any CNC-related question. Commit them to memory.

### Formula 1: Spindle Speed (N)

$$
\boxed{N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}}
$$

Where:
- $N$ = Spindle speed (**RPM**)
- $V_c$ = Cutting speed (**m/min**)
- $D$ = Tool diameter (**mm**)
- $\pi \approx 3.14159$

### Formula 2: Feed Rate (F)

$$
\boxed{F = N \cdot f_r \cdot z}
$$

Where:
- $F$ = Feed rate (**mm/min**)
- $N$ = Spindle speed (**RPM**)
- $f_r$ = Feed per tooth (**mm/tooth**)
- $z$ = Number of effective teeth/flutes

### Formula 3: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F}
$$

Where:
- $a_p$ = Axial depth of cut (**mm**)
- $a_e$ = Radial depth of cut (**mm**)
- $F$ = Feed rate (**mm/min**)
- Units: **mm³/min**

### Formula 4: Machining Time (for a straight pass)

$$
\boxed{T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{F}}
$$

Where:
- $T_m$ = Machining time (**min**)
- $L$ = Length of cut (**mm**)
- $L_a$ = Tool approach length (**mm**, typically $\approx D$ for end mill)
- $F$ = Feed rate (**mm/min**)

---

## 2.4 Reference Cutting Parameter Table (For Mild Steel, HSS/Carbide Tools)

> [!TIP]
> These are **typical engineering values** used in simulation. Real industry values depend on tool manufacturer catalogues (Sandvik Coromant, Kennametal, Iscar).

| Operation | Tool | $V_c$ (m/min) | $f_r$ (mm/tooth) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Face milling | Carbide insert | 150–250 | 0.10–0.25 |
| End milling (steel) | HSS | 25–35 | 0.05–0.10 |
| End milling (steel) | Carbide | 80–150 | 0.05–0.15 |
| Drilling (mild steel) | HSS twist drill | 25–30 | 0.05–0.15 |
| Turning (mild steel) | Carbide | 150–250 | 0.15–0.40 |
| Aluminium milling | Carbide | 300–600 | 0.05–0.15 |

---

## 2.5 Circular Interpolation — The I, J, K vs R Method

For **G02/G03**, there are two ways to specify the arc center:

**Method 1: I, J, K (incremental from arc start)**
$$
I = X_{center} - X_{start}, \quad J = Y_{center} - Y_{start}, \quad K = Z_{center} - Z_{start}
$$

**Method 2: R (radius of the arc)**
$$
\text{G02 X\_ Y\_ R\_}
$$
- $R > 0$ → arc $\leq 180°$
- $R < 0$ → arc $> 180°$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When the arc is exactly **180°**, using R is ambiguous (the controller may reject it). **Always use I, J, K** for 180° arcs. Many students lose 2–3 marks here.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of CNC Simulation

| Industry | Use Case | Software Used |
|---|---|---|
| Aerospace | Turbine blade 5-axis machining | Vericut, CATIA, NX CAM |
| Automotive | Engine block, gearbox housings | Mastercam, Delcam |
| Die & Mold | Injection mold cores/cavities | PowerMill, WorkNC |
| Medical | Orthopedic implants | Fusion 360, GibbsCAM |
| Job-shops (Kerala MSMEs) | General turning/milling | CNC Simulator Pro (free!) |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU lab purposes, **CNC Simulator Pro** (by G-Code Tutor / CNC Training Centre) is the **most common choice** because it is free for educational use, lightweight, and runs on Windows without a GPU. **Fusion 360** is also widely used and is free for students with an Autodesk education license.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical: Cutting Parameter Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-style):**
> A mild steel workpiece is to be face-milled with a Ø50 mm 4-flute carbide insert cutter. The recommended cutting speed is $V_c = 180$ m/min and feed per tooth is $f_r = 0.15$ mm/tooth. The depth of cut is $a_p = 3$ mm and radial engagement is $a_e = 40$ mm. The length of the cut is $L = 120$ mm. Calculate:
> 1. Spindle speed $N$
> 2. Feed rate $F$
> 3. Material removal rate (MRR)
> 4. Approximate machining time (assume $L_a \approx D$)

---

### Step 1: Spindle Speed Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 50} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{180000}{157.0796} \\[8pt]
&= 1145.91 \;\; \text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering rounding:** Most CNC controllers accept integer RPM. We round **down** (never up) for safety →

$$
\boxed{N \approx 1145 \;\; \text{RPM}}
$$

> **Logic:** Rounding up can overshoot the recommended cutting speed, causing premature tool wear or breakage.

---

### Step 2: Feed Rate Calculation

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= N \cdot f_r \cdot z \\[8pt]
&= 1145 \times 0.15 \times 4 \\[8pt]
&= 687.0 \;\; \text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{F = 687 \;\; \text{mm/min}}
$$

---

### Step 3: Material Removal Rate (MRR)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F \\[8pt]
&= 3 \times 40 \times 687 \\[8pt]
&= 82440 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \\[8pt]
&= 82.44 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} = 82.44 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}}
$$

---

### Step 4: Machining Time

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_a &\approx D = 50 \;\; \text{mm} \\[8pt]
T_m &= \frac{L + L_a}{F} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{120 + 50}{687} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{170}{687} \\[8pt]
&= 0.2474 \;\; \text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_m \approx 14.85 \;\; \text{seconds}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical: Circular Interpolation Arc Center (I, J)

> **Problem:** Mill a clockwise circular arc from point **A(20, 30)** to point **B(50, 50)** in the XY plane (G17), with the arc center at **(50, 30)**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

- Start point: $A = (20, 30)$
- End point: $B = (50, 50)$
- Center: $C = (50, 30)$

Since we are in the **XY plane** and the arc is **clockwise (as viewed from +Z looking down)**:

1. Direction = **CW** → use **G02**
2. Plane = **XY** → use **G17** (default on most mills)
3. Compute incremental offsets:
   - $I = X_C - X_A = 50 - 20 = 30$
   - $J = Y_C - Y_A = 30 - 30 = 0$

**Resulting code block:**

```gcode
N40 G17 G02 X50.0 Y50.0 I30.0 J0.0 F100
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention sanity check:** Since the center is to the **right** of the start point along +X, and the arc curves **clockwise** toward +Y, this represents a quarter-circle arc of radius 30 mm. ✓

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Code-to-Action Mapping

| G-Code Written | Tool Motion (X, Y, Z) | Visual Effect on Simulator |
|---|---|---|
| `G00 X10 Y10` | Rapid to (10, 10) at max feed (e.g., 5000 mm/min) | Blue dashed line, no chip |
| `G01 X50 Y10 F150` | Linear cut at 150 mm/min | Solid yellow line, chip forms |
| `G02 X80 Y40 R20` | CW arc, radius 20 mm | Curved yellow arc, chip forms |
| `G03 X80 Y40 R20` | CCW arc, radius 20 mm | Curved yellow arc (opposite side) |
| `M08` | Flood coolant ON | Blue fluid animation |
| `M05` | Spindle stops rotating | Spindle icon greyed out |
| `G28 Z0` | Retract Z to machine zero | Tool moves straight up |

---

## 3.4 Complete Part Program: Pocket Milling in a 100 × 80 × 10 mm Plate

> **Setup:** Aluminum plate, Ø10 mm 2-flute end mill, $V_c$ = 300 m/min, $f_r$ = 0.05 mm/tooth, depth of pocket = 3 mm, pocket size = 60 × 40 mm.

**Cutting parameter pre-calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \times 300}{\pi \times 10} = 9549.3 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 9500 \;\; \text{RPM} \\[8pt]
F &= 9500 \times 0.05 \times 2 = 950 \;\; \text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Full hand-written part program:**

```gcode
%
O0001 (RECTANGULAR POCKET - KTU LAB EXPT 2)
N10 G21 G90 G94          (Metric, Absolute, Feed/min)
N20 G17 G54              (XY plane, Work Coordinate System 1)
N30 G00 X-5 Y-5 Z50      (Rapid above stock, slightly offset)
N40 T01 M06              (Tool 1 = Ø10 end mill, ATC)
N50 M03 S9500            (Spindle CW at 9500 RPM)
N60 M08                  (Flood coolant ON)
N70 G00 X0 Y0            (Rapid to corner of pocket)
N80 G00 Z2               (Rapid to safe Z above stock top)
N90 G01 Z-3 F300         (Plunge into pocket at reduced feed)
N100 G01 X60 Y0 F950     (Cut along +X)
N110 G01 X60 Y40         (Cut along +Y)
N120 G01 X0  Y40         (Cut along -X)
N130 G01 X0  Y0          (Cut back to start, closing rectangle)
N140 G00 Z50             (Retract clear)
N150 G28 Z0              (Return to machine Z home)
N160 M05                 (Spindle stop)
N170 M09                 (Coolant OFF)
N180 M30                 (Program end + rewind)
%
```

**Expected simulation outcome:**
- Tool traces a 60 × 40 mm rectangle at Z = -3 mm
- Stock material within the rectangle is removed (shown in green/different color)
- No collisions with fixture
- Final profile matches the engineering drawing

---

## 3.5 Verification Checklist (Use This Before Submitting Your Lab Record)

| # | Check | Status |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Program starts with safe Z above stock | ✅ |
| 2 | Spindle (M03) and coolant (M08) start BEFORE cutting | ✅ |
| 3 | All cutting moves use G01 with explicit F | ✅ |
| 4 | Retract moves use G00 (rapid) | ✅ |
| 5 | Spindle and coolant stop AFTER final retract | ✅ |
| 6 | M30 at the very end | ✅ |
| 7 | G90 absolute OR consistent G91 incremental — never mix | ✅ |
| 8 | Tool diameter in simulation matches T-code tool | ✅ |
| 9 | WCS (G54) origin matches the workpiece datum | ✅ |
| 10 | No syntax errors flagged by the simulator parser | ✅ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CNC Simulation Workflow — Block-Level Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engineering Drawing of Workpiece] --> B[Process Planning Sheet]
    B --> C[Select Tools, Speeds, Feeds]
    C --> D[Hand-Write Part Program G-Code]
    D --> E[Load Program into Simulator]
    E --> F{Simulator Parser Check}
    F -->|Syntax Error| G[Debug Edit Program]
    G --> D
    F -->|Syntax OK| H[Define Stock Geometry in Software]
    H --> I[Set Work Coordinate System G54]
    I --> J[Mount Virtual Tool T01]
    J --> K[Run Simulation Play Mode]
    K --> L{Visual Inspection of Tool Path}
    L -->|Collision or Undercut| G
    L -->|Path Looks Correct| M[Compare Final Profile vs Drawing]
    M --> N{Dimensional Match within Tolerance}
    N -->|No| G
    N -->|Yes| O[Save Screenshots and Tool Path Report]
    O --> P[Generate Lab Record with Inference]
```

---

## 4.2 G-Code Execution State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> RapidMove: G00
    Idle --> LinearCut: G01
    RapidMove --> LinearCut: G01
    LinearCut --> ArcCW: G02
    LinearCut --> ArcCCW: G03
    ArcCW --> ArcCCW: G03
    ArcCCW --> ArcCW: G02
    ArcCW --> Dwell: G04
    ArcCCW --> Dwell: G04
    LinearCut --> Dwell: G04
    Dwell --> LinearCut: G01
    Dwell --> RapidMove: G00
    RapidMove --> SpindleControl: M03 or M05
    LinearCut --> CoolantControl: M08 or M09
    CoolantControl --> ProgramEnd: M30
    SpindleControl --> ProgramEnd: M30
    ProgramEnd --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 Tool Path Visualization — Pocket Milling (Schematic Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start Point 0,0] -->|G01 +X| B[Point 60,0]
    B -->|G01 +Y| C[Point 60,40]
    C -->|G01 -X| D[Point 0,40]
    D -->|G01 -Y| A
    A -->|G00 Z up| E[Retract to Safe Z]
    E -->|G28| F[Machine Home Position]
    F -->|M30| G[Program End]
```

```mermaid
        P4[Point X10 Y50]
        O --- P1
        P1 --- P2

        P3 --- P4

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Dec 2024]**

### Q1. Define CNC simulation. List any two commercially / freely available CNC simulation software.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CNC Simulation** is the virtual execution of a numerical control part program in a software environment that emulates the kinematics, tool motion, and material removal of an actual CNC machine tool, in order to verify the tool path and detect any collisions or errors before real machining.
>
> **Two examples:**
> 1. **CNC Simulator Pro** (free for education, by G-Code Tutor)
> 2. **Fusion 360** (Autodesk, free for students with .edu email)
>
> *(Also acceptable: Vericut, Mastercam, SolidWorks CAM, CATIA, EdgeCAM)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between G00 and G01 with one example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | G00 (Rapid) | G01 (Linear Interpolation) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Non-cutting positioning | Cutting in a straight line |
| Feed rate | Machine maximum (e.g., 5000 mm/min) | Programmed F value |
| Block example | `G00 X50 Y25` | `G01 X50 Y25 F150` |
| Chip formation | None | Yes |

> G00 is used for **fast, safe tool positioning** above the workpiece. G01 is used for **actual material removal** along a straight path at the operator-defined feed rate.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full ESE-Style Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]**

> **Q A.** A mild steel workpiece of size **120 mm × 80 mm × 15 mm** is to be milled with a rectangular pocket of dimensions **80 mm × 50 mm** and depth **4 mm** using a **Ø12 mm, 4-flute HSS end mill**. The recommended cutting speed is $V_c$ = **30 m/min** and feed per tooth is $f_r$ = **0.08 mm/tooth**. Use **G17 plane, G54 WCS, absolute programming (G90), and metric units (G21)**.
>
> **(a)** Calculate the spindle speed, feed rate, and MRR. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a complete manual part program with safety blocks, ATC, coolant, and program end. Sketch the expected tool path. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Cutting Parameter Calculations (7 marks)

**Given data:**
- Tool diameter $D = 12$ mm
- Number of teeth $z = 4$
- $V_c = 30$ m/min, $f_r = 0.08$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 4$ mm (axial depth)
- $a_e = 12$ mm (= tool diameter for full slot, but for pocket we use the cutter width — assume climb milling, so effective $a_e = D$ for the roughing pass; if step-over is used, the engagement changes. For simplicity, take $a_e = D = 12$ mm).

**Step 1: Spindle speed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 12} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{30000}{37.699} \\[8pt]
&= 795.77 \;\; \text{RPM} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 795 \;\; \text{RPM (rounded down)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Feed rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= N \cdot f_r \cdot z \\[8pt]
&= 795 \times 0.08 \times 4 \\[8pt]
&= 254.4 \;\; \text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: MRR**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR} &= a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F \\[8pt]
&= 4 \times 12 \times 254.4 \\[8pt]
&= 12211.2 \;\; \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \\[8pt]
&= 12.21 \;\; \text{cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answers:**
- **Spindle speed $N = 795$ RPM** (1 mark)
- **Feed rate $F = 254.4$ mm/min** (3 marks with formula)
- **MRR = 12.21 cm³/min** (3 marks with formula)

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Part Program (7 marks)

```gcode
%
O0002 (RECTANGULAR POCKET - KTU ESE Q A)
(SAFETY AND SETUP BLOCKS)
N10 G21 G90 G94 G17        (Metric, Absolute, Feed/min, XY plane)
N20 G54                     (Work Coordinate System 1)
N30 G00 X-10 Y-10 Z50       (Rapid to safe position above corner)
(TOOL CHANGE)
N40 T01 M06                 (Tool 1 = Ø12 HSS end mill, 4 flutes)
N50 M03 S795                (Spindle CW at 795 RPM)
N60 M08                     (Flood coolant ON)
(POSITION ABOVE START POINT)
N70 G00 X0 Y0               (Rapid to pocket corner 0,0)
N80 G00 Z2                  (Rapid to 2 mm above stock top)
(PLUNGE)
N90 G01 Z-4 F60             (Plunge into pocket at reduced feed)
(POCKET RECTANGLE CONTOUR)
N100 G01 X80 Y0   F254.4    (Cut along +X)
N110 G01 X80 Y50            (Cut along +Y)
N120 G01 X0  Y50            (Cut along -X)
N130 G01 X0  Y0             (Close the rectangle, return to start)
(SAFE RETRACT AND PROGRAM END)
N140 G00 Z50                (Retract clear of stock)
N150 G28 Z0                 (Return to machine Z home)
N160 M05                    (Spindle stop)
N170 M09                    (Coolant OFF)
N180 M30                    (Program end and rewind)
%
```

**Expected Tool Path Sketch:**

```
   Y
   ↑
50 ┤ ┌─────────────┐
   │ │             │
   │ │   POCKET    │
   │ │  (machined │
   │ │   region)  │
   │ │             │
 0 ┤ └─────────────┘
   └─┬─────┬─────┬──→ X
     0    40    80
```

**Mark distribution for Q A(b):**
- Safety/setup blocks (G21, G90, G94, G17, G54): **1 mark**
- Tool change and spindle ON (T01, M06, M03, S___): **1 mark**
- Coolant ON and positioning: **1 mark**
- Plunge (G01 Z-4): **0.5 mark**
- Four linear cuts closing the rectangle: **2 marks**
- Retract, spindle off, coolant off, M30: **1 mark**
- Tool path sketch with dimensions: **0.5 mark**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Dec 2023]**

> **Q B.** A circular boss of **Ø40 mm** is to be machined on a 100 × 100 × 10 mm aluminum plate using a **Ø10 mm, 2-flute carbide end mill**. The boss center is at **(50, 50)** in the WCS. The cutting speed is $V_c = 250$ m/min and feed per tooth is $f_r = 0.06$ mm/tooth. Depth of cut is 3 mm.
>
> **(a)** Calculate spindle speed, feed rate, and machining time for one full circular path (assume approach length = 10 mm). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write a complete CNC part program using **G02** (CW circular interpolation) to machine the boss periphery, with full safety blocks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 10$ mm, $z = 2$, $V_c = 250$ m/min, $f_r = 0.06$ mm/tooth, boss radius $R = 20$ mm.

**Step 1: Spindle speed**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{1000 \times 250}{\pi \times 10} \\[8pt]
&= \frac{250000}{31.4159} \\[8pt]
&= 7957.7 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; 7950 \;\; \text{RPM}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Feed rate**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 7950 \times 0.06 \times 2 \\[8pt]
&= 954 \;\; \text{mm/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Circular path length and machining time**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{arc} &= 2 \pi R = 2 \pi \times 20 = 125.66 \;\; \text{mm} \\[8pt]
L_{total} &= L_{arc} + L_a = 125.66 + 10 = 135.66 \;\; \text{mm} \\[8pt]
T_m &= \frac{L_{total}}{F} = \frac{135.66}{954} = 0.1422 \;\; \text{min} \\[8pt]
&= 8.53 \;\; \text{seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final answers (a):** $N = 7950$ RPM, $F = 954$ mm/min, $T_m \approx 8.53$ s.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) (7 marks)

> **Geometric setup:**
> - Approach the boss from the left side (outside, at X = 20, Y = 50).
> - Plunge to Z = -3.
> - Mill a full circle around the boss at radius 20 mm using **G02**.
> - Start point: (20, 50); End point: (20, 50) — same as start (full loop).
> - Center offset: $I = +20$ (from X=20 to center X=50), $J = 0$.

```gcode
%
O0003 (CIRCULAR BOSS PERIPHERY - KTU ESE Q B)
N10 G21 G90 G94 G17
N20 G54
N30 G00 X-10 Y-10 Z50
N40 T02 M06                 (Tool 2 = Ø10 carbide end mill)
N50 M03 S7950
N60 M08
N70 G00 X20 Y50             (Approach entry point on boss periphery)
N80 G00 Z2
N90 G01 Z-3 F60             (Plunge to depth)
N100 G02 X20 Y50 I20 J0 F954 (Full CW circle, back to start)
N110 G00 Z50                (Retract clear)
N120 G28 Z0
N130 M05
N140 M09
N150 M30
%
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical trick for full-circle G02:** The end point MUST equal the start point (or differ by a full revolution), and the I, J values give the **center relative to the start point**. Here: $I = 50 - 20 = +20$, $J = 50 - 50 = 0$. ✓

**Mark distribution for Q B(b):**
- Setup blocks + WCS: **1 mark**
- Tool change + spindle: **1 mark**
- Coolant + approach: **0.5 mark**
- Plunge move: **0.5 mark**
- Correct G02 syntax with I, J offsets: **2.5 marks**
- Retract + M05 + M09 + M30: **1 mark**
- Logical flow (modal consistency): **0.5 mark**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Read Carefully!)**
>
> 1. **Mixing G90 and G91** in the same program — this creates ambiguous coordinates and the simulator will throw a "modal conflict" error. **Pick one mode and stick to it.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting to plunge (Z move) before X/Y cutting moves** — the tool will try to cut sideways at Z = 50 mm (above the stock), so no chip is removed. Always do `G01 Z-__ F__` before the contour.
>
> 3. **Wrong I, J sign convention** — I and J are **incremental from the START point of the arc**, not absolute from origin. Many students compute them from the origin and lose 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Plunge feed too high** — using the full cutting F for plunge causes HSS tools to snap. Use a reduced F (typically 1/3 to 1/5 of cutting F) for plunge moves.
>
> 5. **Missing M05/M09 before M30** — abrupt coolant stop and spindle stop at end-of-program is unprofessional and triggers alarms on most controllers.
>
> 6. **Rounding RPM up instead of down** — rounding $N = 795.77$ to 800 instead of 795 violates the safe cutting speed limit and examiners often deduct 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 7. **Not including `M30` or using `M02` when M30 is expected** — M30 also rewinds the program to the beginning, M02 does not. For lab records, always use **M30**.
>
> 8. **Skipping the safety Z move** — every program must start with the tool at a safe Z (e.g., +50 mm) before any rapid in X or Y.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CNC Simulation** = offline virtual execution of a G-code program before real machining.
- [x] A **part program** is made of **blocks** (lines), each ending with `;` or a newline.
- [x] Each block follows: `N__ G__ X__ Y__ Z__ F__ S__ T__ M__`.
- [x] **G00 = rapid**, **G01 = linear cut**, **G02 = CW arc**, **G03 = CCW arc**.
- [x] **G17/G18/G19** select XY/XZ/YZ plane.
- [x] **G54–G59** select work coordinate systems (fixture offsets).
- [x] **G90 = absolute**, **G91 = incremental** — never mix.
- [x] **G94 = mm/min**, **G95 = mm/rev**.
- [x] **G40/G41/G42** = cutter compensation off/left/right.
- [x] **M03/M04/M05** = spindle ON CW / ON CCW / OFF.
- [x] **M06** = automatic tool change.
- [x] **M08/M09** = coolant ON/OFF.
- [x] **M30** = program end and rewind (use this, not M02).
- [x] **Spindle speed formula:** $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$
- [x] **Feed rate formula:** $F = N \cdot f_r \cdot z$
- [x] **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F$
- [x] **Machining time:** $T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{F}$
- [x] **I, J, K are incremental offsets from arc start to arc center** — not from origin.
- [x] For a 180° arc, **always use I, J, K** (R is ambiguous).
- [x] Common simulator software: **CNC Simulator Pro, Fusion 360, Mastercam, Vericut, CATIA**.
- [x] Always **round RPM down**, never up.
- [x] Always use a **reduced plunge feed** before full cutting feed.
- [x] Always end with **safe Z retract → M05 → M09 → M30**.
- [x] KTU lab record must contain: aim, drawing, process sheet, program, **simulation screenshots**, result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC Machining in CNC Lathe
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** CNC Machining in CNC Lathe

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CNC Machining in CNC Lathe

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Machining** is a subtractive manufacturing process in which pre-programmed computer software dictates the movement of factory tools and machinery. In a **CNC Lathe**, the workpiece rotates about a fixed axis while a cutting tool is traversed along programmable paths to produce cylindrical, conical, threaded, or contoured surfaces with high dimensional accuracy.

**CNC Lathe** is a 2-axis turning machine tool controlled by a microprocessor-based numerical control system using alphanumeric coded instructions (G-codes and M-codes as per **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267** standards). The two principal linear axes are conventionally designated as:

- **X-axis** → Radial (cross-slide) movement, perpendicular to spindle axis.
- **Z-axis** → Longitudinal (carriage) movement, parallel to spindle axis.

The spindle itself rotates about the C-axis (optional on modern machines for live tooling).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Robotic Chef":**
> Imagine you ask a chef to bake a cake. Instead of you standing and cooking every step, you write the entire recipe on paper — preheat oven to 180 °C, mix flour for 5 minutes, bake for 45 minutes — and hand it to a robotic chef. The robot follows the recipe **exactly**, without getting tired or distracted.
>
> In CNC Machining on a Lathe:
> - **The robotic chef** = CNC controller (Fanuc, Siemens, Mitsubishi).
> - **The recipe** = Part Program (G-code & M-code text file).
> - **The ingredients** = Workpiece blank (mild steel rod, aluminum billet, etc.).
> - **The oven/mixer** = Spindle (rotates the workpiece) + Tool turret (does the cutting).
> - **The baked cake** = Finished turned component (shaft, bush, threaded stud).

The key advantage is **repeatability**: once a perfect part is made, the same program can produce 10, 10,000 or 1,00,000 identical parts within micrometer tolerances.

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU Highlight Box)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key CNC Lathe Parameters (Industry Standards):**
> - **Spindle Speed (N):** Measured in **rpm**, typically **50 – 4000 rpm**.
> - **Cutting Speed (V):** Measured in **m/min**, typically **30 – 300 m/min** for mild steel.
> - **Feed Rate (f):** Measured in **mm/rev** (per revolution), typically **0.05 – 0.5 mm/rev**.
> - **Depth of Cut (d):** Measured in **mm**, typically **0.5 – 3.0 mm** (roughing) and **0.2 – 0.5 mm** (finishing).
> - **Positioning Accuracy:** ± **0.005 mm** (5 microns) for precision CNC lathes.
> - **Repeatability:** ± **0.002 mm** (2 microns).
> - **Least Count / Resolution:** **0.001 mm** (1 micron) on standard machines.

## 1.4 Typical Components Manufactured on CNC Lathes

- Stepped shafts and axles
- Threaded studs and bolts (external & internal)
- Bushings and sleeves
- Knurled handles and grips
- Hydraulic fittings and adapters
- Splined shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a CNC Lathe System (Structured Logic Steps)

A CNC Lathe is composed of **six integrated subsystems**:

1. **Input Device** — Keyboard, USB port, RS-232 interface for part-program entry.
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** — Microprocessor-based brain; decodes G/M codes and interpolates tool paths.
3. **Servo Drive System** — Closed-loop DC/AC servo motors driving ballscrews along X and Z axes.
4. **Feedback System** — Linear encoders (Heidenhain, Renishaw) on slides; spindle encoder on chuck axis.
5. **Machine Tool (Mechanical Body)** — Bed, headstock, carriage, cross-slide, tool turret, tailstock, chip conveyor.
6. **Coolant & Lubrication System** — Flood coolant (water-soluble emulsion) for heat dissipation; automatic lubrication for slideways.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for the KTU exam:** When asked "Explain the working principle of a CNC lathe," examiners expect you to mention **MCU → Servo Drive → Ballscrew → Slide → Tool → Feedback → MCU** as a closed-loop flow. This single chain is worth 4–5 marks on its own.

## 2.2 The "Why & How" of CNC Programming

| Step | What Happens | Why It Matters |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| 1. Part Drawing Study | Identify features, tolerances, datums | Defines machining strategy |
| 2. Process Planning | Select tools, speeds, feeds | Determines cycle time & cost |
| 3. Tool Selection | Insert type (CNMG, DNMG), holder | Affects surface finish |
| 4. Work Coordinate System (WCS) | Define X0, Z0 on part datum | Establishes program origin |
| 5. Writing G-Code | Use G00, G01, G02, G03, G90, G94 | Directs tool path |
| 6. Dry Run / Simulation | Graphical verification | Prevents crashes |
| 7. First-Cut Trial | Machining with offsets | Validates accuracy |
| 8. Production Run | Full batch manufacturing | Final output |

## 2.3 Essential G-Codes and M-Codes (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following codes form the **core vocabulary** of any CNC Lathe program. Memorizing them is mandatory for KTU practical exams and viva voce.

| Code | Function | Use Case |
|------|----------|----------|
| **G00** | Rapid traverse (non-cutting, max feed) | Tool positioning between cuts |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation at programmed feed | Straight turning, facing |
| **G02** | Circular interpolation CW | External radius, arc cutting |
| **G03** | Circular interpolation CCW | Internal radius, arc cutting |
| **G28** | Return to reference / home position | Program end, tool change |
| **G90** | Absolute programming (modal) | Coordinates relative to origin |
| **G91** | Incremental programming (modal) | Coordinates relative to current point |
| **G94** | Feed per minute (mm/min) | Milling mode |
| **G95** | Feed per revolution (mm/rev) | **Standard for turning** |
| **G96** | Constant surface speed (CSS) | Variable spindle speed for diameter changes |
| **G97** | Constant spindle speed (rpm) | Spindle speed override off |
| **M03** | Spindle ON clockwise | Forward rotation |
| **M04** | Spindle ON counter-clockwise | Reverse rotation |
| **M05** | Spindle stop | End of cut |
| **M06** | Automatic tool change (ATC) | Lathe with turret |
| **M08** | Coolant ON | Flood coolant activated |
| **M09** | Coolant OFF | End of coolant requirement |
| **M30** | Program end & rewind | Final line of program |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (All Key Equations)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas use SI units unless specified. Replace all `|` symbols with `\vert` in equations below.**

### 2.4.1 Cutting Speed, Spindle Speed and Feed Relations

| Parameter | Formula | Variables & Units |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|
| Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | $V$ = m/min, $D$ = workpiece diameter (mm), $N$ = rpm |
| Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | $N$ = rpm |
| Feed Rate (per minute) | $f_m = f \cdot N$ | $f_m$ = mm/min, $f$ = mm/rev |
| Material Removal Rate (Turning) | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V$ | $MRR$ = mm³/min, $d$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| Machining Time (Turning) | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | $T_m$ = min, $L$ = length of cut (mm) |
| Machining Time (with approach + overtravel) | $T_m = \dfrac{L_a + L}{f \cdot N}$ | $L_a$ = approach length, $L_o$ = overtravel |
| Power Required | $P_c = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | $P_c$ = kW, $F_c$ = cutting force (N), $\eta$ = efficiency (0.7–0.85) |
| Specific Cutting Energy | $K_c = \dfrac{F_c}{A_c}$ | $K_c$ = N/mm², $A_c = f \cdot d$ = mm² |
| Approximate Cutting Force | $F_c = K_c \cdot f \cdot d$ | Empirical formula for estimating forces |
| Taylor's Tool Life Equation | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | $T$ = tool life (min), $C, n$ = tool material constants |
| Average Surface Roughness (Turning) | $R_a \approx \dfrac{f^2}{8r}$ | $r$ = nose radius of tool (mm) |

### 2.4.2 Coordinate System & Geometry (Turning-Specific)

| Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Taper Ratio | $\tan(\alpha) = \dfrac{D - d}{2L}$ | $\alpha$ = half taper angle |
| Thread Lead (single-start) | $L_t = P$ (pitch) | Multi-start: $L_t = n \cdot P$ |
| Pitch Diameter (Metric) | $D_p = D - 0.6495 \cdot P$ | For external threads |

### 2.4.3 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace industry** uses CNC turning for turbine shafts where **±0.01 mm** tolerance is mandatory.
- **Automotive crankshafts** are rough-turned on CNC lathes at **MRR = 15,000 mm³/min** to reduce floor-to-floor time.
- **Medical implants (titanium bone screws)** rely on **Ra ≤ 0.4 µm** finish achieved through G96 constant surface speed turning.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed (N) from Cutting Speed (V)

**Given:** Workpiece diameter $D = 50$ mm, recommended cutting speed for mild steel $V = 120$ m/min.

**Step 1 — Start from the geometric definition of cutting speed:**

$$V = \pi \cdot D \cdot N_{\text{(per minute)}}$$

In SI units, since $D$ is in mm and $V$ in m/min, multiply by $10^{-3}$:

$$V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange algebraically to solve for $N$:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 120}{\pi \times 50}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate step-by-step:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{120{,}000}{50 \cdot \pi} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{120{,}000}{157.0796} \\[4pt]
  &\approx 763.94 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Round to a practical value programmed on CNC:**

$$N \approx 764 \text{ rpm (or programmed as } S764\text{ in G96 mode)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** In KTU viva voce, 70% of students forget the conversion factor $\dfrac{\pi}{1000}$. Always write the units explicitly in the substitution step to avoid losing marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Machining Time for a Plain Turning Operation

**Given:** Length of cut $L = 80$ mm, approach length $L_a = 5$ mm, overtravel $L_o = 2$ mm, feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, spindle speed $N = 1000$ rpm.

**Step 1 — Recall the basic formula for turning time (without approach/over):**

$$T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$$

**Step 2 — Account for tool approach (tool must accelerate to reach full feed before engaging workpiece):**

The tool travels an additional distance $L_a$ at the programmed feed. So total length traveled while cutting is $L + L_a$. The overtravel $L_o$ occurs **after** the cut, so it does **not** add to machining time when feed is active, but it matters for tool-clearance.

$$T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{f \cdot N}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$T_m = \dfrac{80 + 5}{0.2 \times 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \dfrac{85}{200} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.425 \text{ min} \\[4pt]
    &= 25.5 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Verify dimensional consistency:**

$$\frac{\text{mm}}{\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{rev}} \times \frac{\text{rev}}{\text{min}}} = \frac{\text{mm}}{\text{mm/min}} = \text{min} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Material Removal Rate (MRR)

**Given:** Depth of cut $d = 2$ mm, feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, cutting speed $V = 150$ m/min.

**Step 1 — Write the basic geometric definition of MRR in turning:**

$$MRR = \text{Area of cross-section} \times \text{Cutting velocity} = (f \cdot d) \cdot V$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values (remember to convert $V$ from m/min to mm/min):**

$$V = 150 \text{ m/min} = 150{,}000 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$MRR = (0.25 \times 2) \times 150{,}000$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 0.5 \times 150{,}000 \\[4pt]
    &= 75{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to cm³/min (often used in KTU labs):**

$$MRR = \dfrac{75{,}000}{1000} = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Cutting Parameters (Mild Steel on HSS Tool)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix is a **ready-reckoner** for KTU viva and lab record. Tool: HSS single-point turning tool, Workpiece: EN8 mild steel.

| Operation Type | Cutting Speed $V$ (m/min) | Feed $f$ (mm/rev) | Depth of Cut $d$ (mm) | Surface Finish $R_a$ (µm) |
|----------------|---------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Roughing | 60 – 90 | 0.3 – 0.5 | 2.0 – 5.0 | 3.2 – 12.5 |
| Semi-Finishing | 90 – 120 | 0.15 – 0.25 | 0.5 – 1.5 | 1.6 – 3.2 |
| Finishing | 120 – 180 | 0.05 – 0.12 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.4 – 1.6 |
| Threading | 20 – 40 | = Pitch (e.g., 1.5) | Radial infeed 0.3 | 1.6 (typical) |
| Parting Off | 40 – 60 | 0.05 – 0.1 | Full blade width | — |
| Knurling | 30 – 50 | 0.5 – 1.0 (mm/rev) | Knurl profile | 6.3 typical |

For **carbide inserts (CNMG 120408)**, multiply cutting speeds above by 2–3×.

---

## 3.5 Sample Numerical — Multi-Diameter Stepped Shaft Program Calculation

**Given:** A stepped shaft is to be turned from $\phi 40$ mm to $\phi 30$ mm over a length of 60 mm, followed by a chamfer. Tool: CNMG insert. Recommended cutting parameters: $V = 180$ m/min, $f = 0.2$ mm/rev.

**Step 1 — Calculate spindle speed at the larger diameter ($D = 40$ mm):**

$$N_1 = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 40} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{125.66} \approx 1432 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate spindle speed at the smaller diameter ($D = 30$ mm):**

$$N_2 = \dfrac{1000 \times 180}{\pi \times 30} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{94.25} \approx 1910 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Note that with G96 (constant surface speed), the controller will automatically vary N from 1432 to 1910 rpm as the tool advances from $\phi 40$ to $\phi 30$.** This preserves surface quality across varying diameters.

**Step 4 — Calculate feed rate in mm/min:**

$$f_m = f \cdot N_1 = 0.2 \times 1432 = 286.4 \text{ mm/min} \approx 286 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate machining time:**

$$T_m = \dfrac{60}{0.2 \times 1432} = \dfrac{60}{286.4} \approx 0.21 \text{ min} \approx 12.6 \text{ s}$$

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CNC Lathe Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Part Program G-Code File] --> B[Machine Control Unit MCU]
    B --> C[Servo Drive X Axis]
    B --> D[Servo Drive Z Axis]
    B --> E[Spindle Drive Inverter]
    C --> F[X Axis Ballscrew]
    D --> G[Z Axis Ballscrew]
    F --> H[Cross Slide Tool Turret]
    G --> I[Carriage Saddle]
    H --> J[Cutting Tool Insert]
    I --> J
    E --> K[Spindle Motor Chuck]
    K --> L[Workpiece Blank]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Finished Component]
    H --> N[X Axis Encoder Feedback]
    I --> O[Z Axis Encoder Feedback]
    K --> P[Spindle Encoder Feedback]
    N --> B
    O --> B
    P --> B
```

**Reading the Diagram (KTU Explanation Tip):**

- The **MCU** is the central node receiving instructions from the G-code file.
- It issues commands to the **X-axis servo**, **Z-axis servo**, and **spindle inverter**.
- These drives actuate the **ballscrews, slides, and chuck** respectively.
- The **cutting tool** removes material from the **workpiece**, producing the finished component.
- **Encoder feedback** closes the loop back to the MCU, ensuring position accuracy.
- This is a classic **closed-loop position control system** as per KTU Mechatronics / CNC syllabus.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Define CNC Machining. List any four advantages of CNC Lathes over conventional lathes.**

**Model Answer:**
CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Machining is a manufacturing process where pre-programmed computer software controls the movement of machine tools. In a CNC lathe, the spindle rotates the workpiece while a computer-controlled cutting tool removes material to produce the desired geometry.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **High Precision:** Positioning accuracy of ±0.005 mm and repeatability of ±0.002 mm.
2. **Repeatability:** Identical parts can be produced in large batches with zero variation.
3. **Reduced Human Error:** Operator intervention minimized, lowering defect rate.
4. **Complex Geometry:** Tapers, threads, radii, and contours are easily programmed.
5. **Reduced Lead Time:** Once programmed, changeover between parts is rapid.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Explain the function of the following G-codes: G00, G01, G02, G90.**

**Model Answer:**

- **G00 — Rapid Traverse:** Commands the tool to move at the machine's maximum feed rate (typically 5000–10000 mm/min) to a new position. Used **only for non-cutting** movements such as tool positioning between cuts. Path is not controlled (machine decides straight or dogleg).
- **G01 — Linear Interpolation:** Moves the tool in a straight line at the **programmed feed rate (F)**. Used for actual cutting passes such as turning, facing, and chamfering.
- **G02 — Circular Interpolation (Clockwise):** Produces a **clockwise circular arc** as seen from the positive Y-axis looking toward the workpiece. Used for external radii (convex surfaces).
- **G90 — Absolute Programming:** All coordinates in the program are referenced to the **programmed zero point (work origin)**. Modal and remains active until canceled by G91.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students confuse G90 (absolute) with G91 (incremental) — answer carries 2 marks; getting it wrong costs you directly.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to specify the **modal nature** of G-codes (i.e., they remain active until overridden). Examiners explicitly deduct 1 mark for this.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In machining time calculations, students use only the cut length and forget to add **approach length (La)** and **overtravel (Lo)**. You lose 1 mark per missing term.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Writing G02/G03 without specifying the **R (radius) or I, K (center offsets)** — incomplete syntax; you lose 1 mark.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Failing to mention **closed-loop feedback** when explaining CNC lathe working — examiners explicitly look for the word "encoder" or "feedback."

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a CNC Lathe. Discuss the role of the Machine Control Unit (MCU), servo drives, and feedback encoders.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter 60 mm is to be reduced to 50 mm by a turning operation over a length of 100 mm. Using cutting speed V = 100 m/min and feed f = 0.25 mm/rev, calculate: (i) Spindle speed N, (ii) Feed rate in mm/min, (iii) Machining time including an approach of 4 mm and overtravel of 2 mm, (iv) Material removal rate in cm³/min.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Working Principle of CNC Lathe [7 Marks]

The CNC Lathe operates on the principle of **closed-loop numerical control**, where every motion is commanded by software but verified by feedback.

**Block Diagram Description (3 Marks):**

```
Part Program → MCU → Servo Drives → Ballscrews → Slides/Tool → Workpiece
                  ↑                                            ↓
                  └────────── Encoders (Feedback) ──────────────┘
```

1. **Part Program Entry:** The operator loads a G-code file via USB, RS-232, or DNC network.
2. **MCU (Machine Control Unit):** The microprocessor-based controller (Fanuc Series 0i, Siemens Sinumerik 828D) decodes each block of G-code. It performs **interpolation** (linear or circular path generation) and **acceleration/deceleration** calculations. The MCU also monitors **limit switches, emergency stop, and coolant level**. [1.5 Marks]
3. **Servo Drives:** Receive pulse trains from MCU (one pulse = 0.001 mm typically). AC servo motors rotate the X and Z axis ballscrews. Closed-loop current control ensures precise torque. [1 Mark]
4. **Ballscrews & Slides:** Convert rotary motion of servo motors into linear motion of cross-slide (X) and carriage (Z). Preloaded nuts eliminate backlash. [0.5 Mark]
5. **Feedback Encoders:** Linear glass/steel scales mounted on slides report actual position back to MCU every 1–4 milliseconds. Any deviation between commanded and actual position generates a **following error**, which the servo drive corrects within the same loop cycle. [1 Mark]

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution [7 Marks]

**Given:**
- Initial diameter $D_0 = 60$ mm, Final diameter $D_f = 50$ mm
- Length of cut $L = 100$ mm, Approach $L_a = 4$ mm, Overtravel $L_o = 2$ mm
- Cutting speed $V = 100$ m/min, Feed $f = 0.25$ mm/rev

**Step 1 — Calculate spindle speed N (1.5 Marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$$

The diameter changes during turning; use **average diameter** for simplicity or use **G96 mode**. Let us use initial diameter:

$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 100}{\pi \times 60} = \dfrac{100{,}000}{188.496} \approx 530.5 \text{ rpm}$$

$$\boxed{N \approx 530 \text{ rpm}}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate feed rate in mm/min (1 Mark):**

$$f_m = f \cdot N = 0.25 \times 530 = 132.5 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\boxed{f_m \approx 133 \text{ mm/min}}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate machining time (1.5 Marks):**

$$T_m = \dfrac{L + L_a}{f \cdot N} = \dfrac{100 + 4}{0.25 \times 530}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_m &= \dfrac{104}{132.5} \\[4pt]
    &= 0.785 \text{ min} \\[4pt]
    &\approx 47.1 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T_m \approx 0.785 \text{ min } (47.1 \text{ s})}$$

> Note: Overtravel is excluded since feed remains active; tool retracts after cut ends.

**Step 4 — Calculate MRR (2 Marks):**

Depth of cut: $d = \dfrac{D_0 - D_f}{2} = \dfrac{60 - 50}{2} = 5$ mm

$$MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V = 0.25 \times 5 \times 100{,}000 \text{ mm/min}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
MRR &= 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\[4pt]
    &= \dfrac{125{,}000}{1000} \text{ cm}^3/\text{min} \\[4pt]
    &= 125 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{MRR = 125 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer Summary (1 Mark):**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Spindle speed N | 530 rpm |
| Feed rate $f_m$ | 133 mm/min |
| Machining time $T_m$ | 0.785 min (47.1 s) |
| Material removal rate | 125 cm³/min |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the various **axes of a CNC Lathe** and explain how the **work coordinate system (WCS)** is established with reference to the part zero. Sketch a labeled diagram showing the X, Z, and optional C axis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a **complete part program** (using standard G-codes) to perform the following turning operation on a CNC Lathe:
- Raw workpiece: $\phi 50$ mm mild steel, length 80 mm.
- Operation 1: Face the front face.
- Operation 2: Turn down to $\phi 40$ mm over 50 mm length.
- Operation 3: Cut a 1 mm × 45° chamfer at the corner.
- Operation 4: Cut a metric external thread M30 × 1.5 over 20 mm length.
- Use cutting speed V = 120 m/min, feed = 0.2 mm/rev (turning), and depth of cut = 1 mm per pass.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Axes of CNC Lathe and WCS [7 Marks]

**Two Linear Axes (3 Marks):**
- **Z-axis:** Lies along the **spindle rotation axis**. Positive Z is conventionally toward the **tailstock** (away from chuck). The carriage moves in Z to vary the axial position of the tool.
- **X-axis:** Lies in the plane **perpendicular to the spindle axis**. Positive X moves the tool **away from the spindle centerline**, increasing the workpiece diameter. On modern CNC lathes, X is always specified as a **diameter value** (not radius), so X20 means the tool is at $\phi 20$ mm.

**One Rotary Axis (1 Mark):**
- **C-axis:** Coincides with the spindle axis. On lathes equipped with live tooling and a servo-driven spindle, C can be precisely positioned (e.g., C90° = spindle index at 90°). Used for **driven-tool milling, cross-drilling, and polygon turning**.

**Work Coordinate System (WCS) — 3 Marks:**
The **program zero (part zero)** is set at the **rightmost finished face** of the workpiece, at the **centerline of rotation**.

- The operator touches off (probes) the X-axis by bringing a known tool to the **finished outer diameter** and sets that value (e.g., X40) as the offset.
- The operator touches off the Z-axis by bringing the tool to the **front face** of the part and sets Z0.
- All subsequent program coordinates are referenced to this origin.
- The WCS is stored in the controller's **G54–G59** work-offset registers.

**Labeled Diagram (text representation, 0 Mark — sketch on paper):**

```
                              (Tailstock)
                                   |
        ──────────────────────────► Z+
        |  ◯ Chuck ◯  |  Part   |  Live Center
        |     X-      |  Body   |
        |     ↓       |         |
        |  Cross-Slide|         |
        |  (X-axis)   |         |
        ──────────────────────────
                    Spindle axis
```

#### Part (b) — Complete Part Program [7 Marks]

**Program Structure with Annotations:**

```gcode
O1001;                                       (Program number)
N10 G21 G97 G40;                             (Metric, constant rpm, cancel cutter comp)
N20 G28 U0 W0;                               (Return to home position)
N30 T0101;                                   (Tool 1: CNMG 120408 rough turning)
N40 M03 S1500;                               (Spindle ON CW at 1500 rpm)
N50 G00 X52.0 Z2.0;                          (Rapid to start position)
N60 G96 S120;                                (Constant surface speed 120 m/min)
N70 G01 Z0 F0.2;                             (Face to center, feed 0.2 mm/rev)
N80 G00 X52.0 Z2.0;                          (Retract)
N90 G90 G01 X50.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;               (Turn to φ50 over 50 mm — rough)
N100 G00 X52.0;                              (Retract in X)
N110 Z2.0;                                   (Return in Z)
N120 G01 X48.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;                  (Pass 2)
N130 G00 X52.0;
N140 Z2.0;
N150 G01 X46.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;                  (Pass 3)
N160 G00 X52.0;
N170 Z2.0;
N180 G01 X44.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;                  (Pass 4)
N190 G00 X52.0;
N200 Z2.0;
N210 G01 X42.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;                  (Pass 5)
N220 G00 X52.0;
N230 Z2.0;
N240 G01 X40.0 Z-50.0 F0.2;                  (Final pass to φ40)
N250 G00 X45.0 Z-50.0;                       (Position for chamfer)
N260 G01 X38.0 Z-51.0 F0.1;                  (1 mm × 45° chamfer)
N270 G00 X100.0 Z100.0;                      (Retract to safe position)
N280 T0202;                                  (Tool 2: Threading insert 60°)
N290 G00 X31.0 Z2.0;                         (Position for threading)
N300 G76 P010060 Q0.1 R0.05;                 (Threading cycle, 1 spring pass, 0.1 mm chamfer)
N310 G76 X28.05 Z-20.0 P0.974 F1.5;          (Thread depth 0.974, pitch 1.5 mm)
N320 G00 X100.0 Z100.0;                      (Retract)
N330 M05;                                    (Spindle stop)
N340 M09;                                    (Coolant OFF)
N350 M30;                                    (Program end and rewind)
```

**Annotation Table (for answer script):**

| Block | Function | Marks |
|-------|----------|-------|
| O1001 / N10–N20 | Program number, units, return home | 0.5 |
| N30 T0101 | Tool selection (rough turning) | 0.5 |
| N40 M03 S1500 | Spindle ON, initial speed | 0.5 |
| N70 G01 Z0 | Facing operation | 1.0 |
| N90 + repeated passes | Multi-pass turning to φ40 | 1.5 |
| N260 G01 X38 Z-51 | Chamfer 1×45° | 0.5 |
| N280–N310 G76 | Threading cycle for M30×1.5 | 2.0 |
| N330–N350 M05/M30 | Safe program termination | 0.5 |

**Why this program is correct:**
- The depth of cut per pass is 2 mm (50 → 48 → 46 → 44 → 42 → 40 = 5 passes × 2 mm = 10 mm total radial = 2 × depth). Each pass maintains finish quality.
- G76 is the standard **multi-pass threading cycle** on Fanuc controllers. The thread depth 0.974 mm = $0.6495 \times P = 0.6495 \times 1.5$.
- The minor diameter for M30 × 1.5 is $30 - 1.0825 \times 1.5 \approx 28.376$ mm; X28.05 leaves 0.326 mm for finishing spring passes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Revision Points for KTU PCAUL407 Module 2 Viva & Exam:**

- [ ] **CNC** = Computer Numerical Control; replaces manual operation of lathes.
- [ ] Standard axes: **X (radial, diameter-programmed)**, **Z (longitudinal)**, optional **C (rotary/spindle index)**.
- [ ] **WCS (Work Coordinate System)** is set at the front face centerline of the finished part.
- [ ] **G00** = rapid (no cutting); **G01** = linear cutting; **G02/G03** = CW/CCW arc; **G90/G91** = absolute/incremental; **G96/G97** = constant surface speed / constant rpm.
- [ ] **M03/M04/M05** = spindle CW/CCW/Stop; **M08/M09** = coolant ON/OFF; **M30** = program end.
- [ ] **Cutting speed** $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ — units m/min, mm, rpm.
- [ ] **Feed rate (mm/min)** = feed per rev × spindle speed = $f \cdot N$.
- [ ] **MRR** = $f \cdot d \cdot V$ (mm³/min) — measure of productivity.
- [ ] **Machining time** = $\dfrac{L + L_a}{f \cdot N}$ — always include approach length.
- [ ] **Surface finish** improves (lower Ra) with lower feed and larger nose radius.
- [ ] **G96 constant surface speed** automatically adjusts spindle RPM as diameter changes; G50 is used to set maximum RPM clamp.
- [ ] **Tool offset** registers (T0101) carry **tool geometry** (first 01) and **wear compensation** (second 01).
- [ ] **Feedback encoders** (linear & rotary) close the position loop; without them, accuracy would not be guaranteed.
- [ ] **CNC lathe advantages:** repeatability, complex geometry, low scrap rate, reduced operator fatigue.
- [ ] **CNC lathe disadvantages:** high initial cost, requires skilled programmer, not economical for small batches.
- [ ] Common carbide insert for mild steel roughing: **CNMG 120408** (Coated, 80° rhombic).
- [ ] For thread cutting: use **G32 (single pass), G76 (multi-pass cycle), or G92 (simple cycle)**.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — CNC Machining in CNC Lathe**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CNC Milling in a VMC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** CNC Milling in a VMC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**CNC Milling in a VMC (Vertical Machining Center)** is a computer-controlled material removal process where a rotating multi-point cutting tool removes material from a workpiece mounted on a vertically oriented spindle machine. The VMC integrates a numerical control (NC) unit with a vertical-axis spindle, automatic tool changer (ATC), worktable, and servo-driven linear axes, enabling the production of complex 3D prismatic components with high repeatability and minimal human intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment tests the student's ability to **set up a workpiece, select tools, write a part program, simulate, and execute the program on a VMC** to produce a defined geometry (commonly a rectangular pocket, slot, or profile).

## 1.2 Key Terminology

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **VMC** | Vertical Machining Center — a CNC milling machine where the spindle axis is vertical (Z-axis) |
| **ATC** | Automatic Tool Changer — mechanism that swaps tools based on program command |
| **DRO** | Digital Read-Out — electronic display of axis position |
| **Work Origin (W)** | Program zero point set on the workpiece |
| **Machine Origin (M)** | Permanent reference point of the machine tool |
| **G-Code** | Preparatory functions defining tool path geometry (e.g., G00, G01) |
| **M-Code** | Miscellaneous functions controlling machine actions (e.g., M03, M08) |
| **DNC** | Distributed Numerical Control — direct transfer of program from computer to CNC |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Robotic Carpenter"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a VMC as a highly skilled robotic carpenter that never gets tired.**

Imagine a craftsman standing at a workbench. He holds a chisel (the **cutting tool**) in his hand, and a piece of wood (the **workpiece**) is clamped to the table. To carve a square pocket, he must:
1. Know exactly **where** to start → *G-code coordinates*
2. Move the chisel **how fast** → *Feed rate (F)*
3. Spin the chisel at the **right speed** → *Spindle speed (S)*
4. Cut **how deep** in one pass → *Depth of cut (d)*
5. Remember to **change chisels** when needed → *Tool change (M06, T#)*

In a VMC, the **CNC controller** is the brain that remembers all these instructions and executes them with micrometer accuracy — 24/7, without coffee breaks.

## 1.4 Classification of VMC

```
VMC
├── 3-Axis VMC (X, Y, Z)            → Most common in KTU labs
├── 4-Axis VMC (+ Rotary A-axis)    → Indexing/turning operations
└── 5-Axis VMC (+ B-axis)           → Aerospace, die-mould making
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants/Standards Used:**
> - Standard NC tape format: **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267-A**
> - Coordinate system follows the **Right-Hand Rule**
> - Tool diameter compensation: **G41 (Left) / G42 (Right)**
> - Absolute programming: **G90**, Incremental: **G91**
> - **SI Units** are mandatory — mm for length, mm/min for feed, rpm for spindle speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Components of a VMC

The VMC integrates several critical sub-systems. Understanding each is essential for both programming and the lab viva.

### 2.1.1 Mechanical Subsystem
- **Base & Column:** Provides rigidity, houses coolant tank and electrical cabinets.
- **Spindle:** A high-precision, variable-speed motor (typically 6,000 – 12,000 rpm) that holds the cutting tool via a **BT/CAT/HSK taper**.
- **Worktable:** Mounted on X (longitudinal) and Y (cross) slides; workpiece is clamped to it.
- **ATC (Automatic Tool Changer):** A carousel/magazine storing 16–24 tools; swaps tools in **< 8 seconds**.

### 2.1.2 Control Subsystem
