- **CNC Controller:** The brain (Fanuc, Siemens, Mitsubishi, Haas). Interprets G-code and commands servo motors.
- **Servo Motors with Ball Screws:** Convert rotary motion to linear motion with **positioning accuracy of ±0.005 mm** typical.
- **Feedback System:** Linear scales / rotary encoders provide closed-loop position feedback.

### 2.1.3 Auxiliary Subsystem
- **Coolant System:** Flood coolant or through-spindle coolant (TSC) at **50–80 L/min**.
- **Chip Conveyor:** Removes metal chips; auger or hinge-belt type.
- **Enclosure:** Safety guard with interlocked doors.

## 2.2 Axes Designation (Right-Hand Rule)

| Axis | Motion | Driven By |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **X** | Longest table travel (left-right) | Servo motor + ball screw |
| **Y** | Cross travel (in-out) | Servo motor + ball screw |
| **Z** | Vertical (spindle up-down) | Servo motor + ball screw |
| **A** (optional) | Rotational about X | 4th-axis table |
| **B** (optional) | Rotational about Y | 5th-axis head |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Right-Hand Rule:** Point the right thumb along +X (to the right), index finger along +Y (away from operator), and middle finger along +Z (upward). All CNC codes follow this convention.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Unit | Typical Range (Mild Steel) |
|-----------|--------|---------|------|----------------------------|
| Spindle Speed | $N$ | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$ | **rpm** | 800 – 3000 |
| Cutting Velocity | $V_c$ | $V_c = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | **m/min** | 20 – 60 (HSS); 80 – 250 (Carbide) |
| Feed Rate | $V_f$ | $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ | **mm/min** | 100 – 800 |
| Feed per Tooth | $f_z$ | Given by tool chart | **mm/tooth** | 0.05 – 0.30 |
| Number of Teeth | $z$ | Tool specification | — | 2 – 6 |
| Material Removal Rate | $MRR$ | $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$ | **mm³/min** | — |
| Depth of Cut | $a_p$ | Programmer-defined | **mm** | 0.5 – 6.0 |
| Width of Cut (Radial) | $a_e$ | Programmer-defined | **mm** | ≤ 0.6 × D |
| Machining Time | $T_m$ | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$ | **min** | — |
| Power Required | $P_c$ | $P_c = \dfrac{k_c \cdot a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f}{60 \cdot 10^6 \cdot \eta}$ | **kW** | — |

> [!NOTE]
> - $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²), typically **1500–2500 N/mm²** for mild steel.
> - $\eta$ = machine efficiency, typically **0.70 – 0.85**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **feed per tooth ($f_z$)** with **feed per revolution ($f$)**. For milling, the standard formula is $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ — students frequently write $V_f = f \cdot N$ (turning formula) and lose marks.

## 2.4 Work Holding Devices

| Device | Use Case | KTU Lab Note |
|--------|----------|--------------|
| **Machine Vise** | Most common for rectangular workpieces | Always indicate clamping length |
| **T-Bolts & Step Blocks** | Irregular / large workpieces | Use parallel blocks under workpiece |
| **Vacuum Chuck** | Thin plates (non-ferrous) | Requires smooth surface |
| **3-Jaw Chuck** | Cylindrical work | Mostly on lathes |
| **4th-Axis Rotary Table** | Indexing features | Advanced experiments |

## 2.5 Standard G-Code Reference (Fanuc / Haas-style)

| Code | Function | Modal |
|------|----------|-------|
| **G00** | Rapid traverse (positioning) | Yes |
| **G01** | Linear interpolation (cutting) | Yes |
| **G02** | Circular CW interpolation | Yes |
| **G03** | Circular CCW interpolation | Yes |
| **G17 / G18 / G19** | Select XY / XZ / YZ plane | Yes |
| **G20 / G21** | Inch / Metric input | Yes |
| **G28** | Return to reference point | No |
| **G40 / G41 / G42** | Cutter comp. off / left / right | Yes |
| **G54–G59** | Work coordinate systems (1–6) | Yes |
| **G80–G89** | Canned drilling cycles | Yes |
| **G90 / G91** | Absolute / Incremental | Yes |
| **G94 / G95** | Feed per min / per rev | Yes |
| **M03 / M04** | Spindle CW / CCW | Yes |
| **M05** | Spindle stop | Yes |
| **M06** | Tool change | No |
| **M08 / M09** | Coolant on / off | Yes |
| **M30** | Program end & rewind | No |

## 2.6 Why CNC Milling Matters in Industry

- **Repeatability:** ±0.01 mm across 10,000 parts.
- **Complexity:** Free-form surfaces, pockets, slots drilled automatically.
- **Productivity:** 5–20× faster than manual milling for batch production.
- **Industries:** Aerospace (wing ribs), automotive (engine blocks), die & mould (cavities), medical (implants), electronics (heat sinks).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Experimental Procedure (Lab Workflow)

The following is the **standard KTU-aligned procedure** for executing an experiment on CNC milling in a VMC.

### Step 1: Study the Drawing & Identify Operations
Read the engineering drawing. Identify:
- Workpiece material (e.g., **Al 6061 / Mild Steel / Brass**).
- Stock size (e.g., 100 × 80 × 25 mm).
- Final dimensions and tolerances.
- Required operations (facing, pocketing, drilling, profiling, slotting).
- Suitable tools (face mill, end mill, drill, chamfer tool).

### Step 2: Select Cutting Tools & Parameters
For each tool, choose:
- **Tool material:** HSS (low cost) or Carbide (high productivity).
- **Diameter $D$** and **number of teeth $z$**.
- **Cutting speed $V_c$** from machinability data card.
- **Feed per tooth $f_z$** from tool chart.
- Compute $N$ and $V_f$ using the formula sheet.

### Step 3: Work Coordinate System (WCS) Planning
- Decide the **work origin (W)**. Convention: **top-left corner of the workpiece top surface** (Z=0 at top).
- This is referenced as **G54** (or G55–G59 if multiple fixtures are used).
- Define **safe Z height** (e.g., +5 mm above workpiece) for rapid moves.

### Step 4: Write the Part Program
Structure of a typical VMC program:

```
O0001 (PROGRAM NAME: RECT_POCKET)
G21 G90 G17 G94 G54         (Setup: Metric, Abs, XY-plane, Feed/min, WCS-1)
G00 Z25.0                   (Safe Z)
M06 T01                     (Tool change: Tool 1 = Face Mill Ø50)
M03 S1200                   (Spindle CW, 1200 rpm)
G00 X-5.0 Y-5.0             (Position outside stock)
G00 Z2.0                    (Plunge to clearance)
G01 Z0.0 F100               (Touch top surface — set Z=0)
... (Tool paths) ...
M05                         (Spindle stop)
M09                         (Coolant off)
G91 G28 Z0                  (Return to Z ref. in Incremental mode)
G28 X0 Y0                   (Return to XY reference)
M30                         (End & rewind)
%
```

### Step 5: Simulation & Dry Run
- Use **CAM software (Mastercam / Fusion 360 / NX)** or the controller's built-in **GRAPHICS mode**.
- Verify the tool path avoids clamps, fixture, and machine limits.
- Check for **gouges, collisions, and undercuts**.
- Perform a **dry run** on the machine with the tool **50 mm above** the workpiece (Z offset raised). This confirms motion without cutting.

### Step 6: Mount Workpiece
- Clean the vise jaws and workpiece bottom with a brush.
- Place the workpiece on the vise with **parallel blocks** (if needed).
- Tighten the vise firmly; check with **0.01 mm dial indicator** for parallelism.
- Indicate the workpiece face for **perpendicularity to spindle axis** (within **0.02 mm/100 mm**).

### Step 7: Set Work Origin (G54)
- Methods:
  1. **Mechanical edge-finder** (most accurate, ±0.01 mm).
  2. **Dial test indicator** (DTI).
  3. **Probing system** (advanced VMCs).
- Touch off **X-edge**, **Y-edge**, and **Z-top**; enter values into the **G54 offset page** on the controller.

### Step 8: Load Program & Execute
- Transfer the program via **USB / DNC / Ethernet** to the controller.
- Place controller in **SINGLE BLOCK** mode first; step through safely.
- Switch to **AUTO** or **MEMORY** mode and press **CYCLE START**.
- Keep the **FEED HOLD** and **EMERGENCY STOP** buttons within reach.

### Step 9: Measurement & Verification
- After machining, blow off chips with compressed air.
- Measure critical dimensions with **vernier caliper (±0.02 mm)** and **micrometer (±0.01 mm)**.
- Check surface roughness if required using a **profilometer (Ra in µm)**.
- Compare with drawing tolerances; record observations in the lab manual.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style)

**Problem:** A 4-flute carbide end mill of diameter $D = 10$ mm is to machine mild steel (recommended $V_c = 100$ m/min, $f_z = 0.08$ mm/tooth) at a depth of cut $a_p = 2$ mm and width $a_e = 5$ mm, with feed rate $V_f$. Calculate the spindle speed $N$ and the feed rate $V_f$.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Spindle Speed:**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}
$$

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot 100}{\pi \cdot 10} = \frac{100000}{31.4159}
$$

$$
N \approx 3183 \text{ rpm}
$$

We round to a standard value: **$N = 3200$ rpm** (or the nearest programmable value on the controller, e.g., 3000 or 3200).

**Step 2 — Feed Rate:**

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N
$$

$$
V_f = 0.08 \cdot 4 \cdot 3200
$$

$$
V_f = 1024 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 3 — Actual Cutting Speed (verification):**

$$
V_c = \frac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000} = \frac{\pi \cdot 10 \cdot 3200}{1000} \approx 100.5 \text{ m/min}
$$

This is within the recommended range ✅.

**Step 4 — Material Removal Rate:**

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f
$$

$$
MRR = 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 1024 = 10240 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**Step 5 — Machining Time for a 200 mm linear tool path:**

$$
T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{200}{1024} \approx 0.195 \text{ min} \approx 11.7 \text{ s}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common VMC Operations

| Operation | Tool | Typical Code Snippet | Notes |
|-----------|------|----------------------|-------|
| **Facing** | Ø50 face mill | G01 X100.0 Y0 F200 | Levels the top surface; depth = 0.5 mm |
| **Pocketing** | Ø10 end mill (with step-over 40% D) | G01 ... with multiple Z levels | Use trochoidal / zigzag strategy |
| **Slotting** | Ø6 end mill = slot width | G01 Y80.0 with X-multiple passes | Cutter diameter = slot width |
| **Drilling** | Ø5 twist drill | G81 R2.0 Z-10.0 F80 | Canned cycle G81 |
| **Counter-boring** | Ø8 step drill | G82 ... dwell P500 | Pause for clean hole bottom |
| **Tapping** | M6 tap | G84 (RH) or G74 (LH) | Use rigid tapping if available |
| **Profiling** | Ø10 end mill with G41/G42 | G42 D01 ... | Cutter compensation enabled |

## 3.4 Cutter Compensation — Worked Logic

When the programmed path is the **part profile**, the **tool centre** must be offset by the **tool radius $R$** so the cutter does not cut into the part.

**Logic:**
- **G41 (Left compensation):** Tool is to the **left** of the part in the direction of travel. Used for **external profiles (outside)** when climbing.
- **G42 (Right compensation):** Tool is to the **right** of the part. Used for **internal pockets**.

**Offset activation block:**
```
G41 D01 X0 Y0      (Activate left comp., D01 stores R = 5.0)
G01 X50.0 Y0 F150  (Cutter centre path starts)
... (cutter shifts inward by R) ...
G40 X100.0 Y100.0  (Cancel compensation)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often write `G41` for both pocket and external profile. The rule is: **Walk along the part profile in the programmed direction. If the tool is on your LEFT, it's G41.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 VMC Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef hw fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef sw fill:#059669,stroke:#059669,color:#ffffff
    classDef proc fill:#d97706,stroke:#d97706,color:#ffffff
    classDef io fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#7c3aed,color:#ffffff

    A[Operator HMI Panel]:::io --> B[CNC Controller Unit]:::sw
    B --> C[Servo Amplifier X axis]:::sw
    B --> D[Servo Amplifier Y axis]:::sw
    B --> E[Servo Amplifier Z axis]:::sw
    C --> F[X axis Ball Screw and Motor]:::hw
    D --> G[Y axis Ball Screw and Motor]:::hw
    E --> H[Z axis Spindle Head]:::hw

    H --> I[Automatic Tool Changer ATC]:::hw
    I --> J[Tool Magazine 16 to 24 tools]:::hw
    H --> K[Spindle Motor 6k to 12k rpm]:::hw
    K --> L[Cutting Tool Holder BT40 CAT40 HSK]:::hw

    F --> M[Worktable]:::hw
    G --> M
    M --> N[Workpiece clamped in Vise]:::hw

    O[Coolant Pump 50 to 80 L per min]:::proc --> H
    P[Chip Conveyor]:::proc --> M
    Q[Chip Auger]:::proc --> M

    R[Feedback Encoders and Linear Scales]:::io --> B

    style B fill:#065f46
    style K fill:#b45309
    style N fill:#991b1b
```

## 4.2 VMC Axis Layout (Functional Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef axis fill:#0ea5e9,stroke:#0369a1,color:#ffffff
    classDef base fill:#475569,stroke:#1e293b,color:#ffffff
    classDef tool fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff

    BASE[Machine Base Casting]:::base
    Z[Z axis Vertical Spindle Head]:::axis
    X[X axis Saddle]:::axis
    Y[Y axis Worktable]:::axis
    TBL[Workpiece]:::tool
    SP[Spindle with Taper]:::tool
    T[Tool]:::tool

    Z --> SP
    SP --> T
    BASE --> X
    X --> Y
    Y --> TBL

    direction Z --> X-axis direction
    direction X --> Y-axis direction
```

## 4.3 CNC Programming Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef step fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    classDef check fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef stop fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff

    S1[Step 1: Read Drawing and Identify Features]:::step
    S2[Step 2: Select Tools and Cutting Parameters]:::step
    S3[Step 3: Choose Work Origin G54 to G59]:::step
    S4[Step 4: Write G code and M code Program]:::step
    S5[Step 5: Simulate in CAM or Controller]:::step
    S6[Step 6: Mount Workpiece and Set Tools]:::step
    S7[Step 7: Set Work Origin using Edge Finder]:::step
    S8[Step 8: Dry Run with Tool Above Stock]:::step
    S9[Step 9: Execute in Single Block]:::step
    S10[Step 10: Measure and Verify]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5
    S5 --> C1{Simulation OK?}:::check
    C1 -- No --> S4
    C1 -- Yes --> S6
    S6 --> S7 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10

    S9 --> E1{Emergency?}:::stop
    E1 -- Yes --> STOP[Halt and Reset]
    E1 -- No --> S10
```

## 4.4 Coordinate System — Right-Hand Rule Visual

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef pos fill:#16a34a,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    classDef neg fill:#dc2626,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
    classDef ctr fill:#0f172a,stroke:#020617,color:#ffffff

    OX[Plus X Right]:::pos
    OY[Plus Y Away from Operator]:::pos
    OZ[Plus Z Up Spindle Up]:::pos
    NX[Minus X Left]:::neg
    NY[Minus Y Toward Operator]:::neg
    NZ[Minus Z Down Spindle Down]:::neg
    O[Machine Origin M]:::ctr

    O --- OX
    O --- NX
    O --- OY
    O --- NY
    O --- OZ
    O --- NZ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick:** **X = Right (X marks the spot to the Right)**, **Y = Away (Why are you going away?)**, **Z = Up (Zealously going up).**

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Define a VMC. List any four major components of a VMC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Vertical Machining Center (VMC)** is a CNC machine tool in which the spindle is oriented vertically, the worktable moves in the X and Y axes, and the spindle head moves in the Z axis. It integrates automatic tool changing and computer numerical control for the production of prismatic and 3D contoured parts.

**Four major components:**
1. **Spindle assembly** with vertical orientation and tool taper (BT/CAT).
2. **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC)** with tool magazine.
3. **Worktable** with X–Y axis travels and T-slots for clamping.
4. **CNC controller unit** (Fanuc/Siemens) with HMI and servo amplifiers.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing four components with one-line description)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between absolute (G90) and incremental (G91) programming with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | G90 (Absolute) | G91 (Incremental) |
|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Reference** | All coordinates measured from **work origin (W)** | Coordinates measured from **current tool position** |
| **Use** | Most common; easier to read and edit | Used in subprograms, drilling patterns, and for relative moves (e.g., `G91 G28 Z0`) |
| **Example** | `G90 G01 X50.0 Y30.0` → tool moves to absolute point (50, 30) | `G91 G01 X30.0 Y20.0` → tool moves +30 in X and +20 in Y from current position |

*Example using same endpoint from (10,10):*
- Absolute: `G90 G01 X40.0 Y30.0`
- Incremental: `G91 G01 X30.0 Y20.0`

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / June 2024 – Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a Vertical Machining Center (VMC). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Ø12 mm, 4-flute carbide end mill is used to machine an aluminium block (cutting speed $V_c = 200$ m/min, feed per tooth $f_z = 0.06$ mm/tooth). The depth of cut is 3 mm, width of cut is 6 mm. Calculate:
1. Spindle speed $N$ (rpm)
2. Feed rate $V_f$ (mm/min)
3. Material removal rate (mm³/min)
4. Time to cut a 250 mm long straight pass.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — VMC Subsystems (7 marks)**

A VMC consists of **four major subsystems**:

1. **Mechanical / Structural Subsystem (2 marks)**
   - **Base & Column:** Provides rigidity and houses the spindle head.
   - **Worktable:** Mounted on linear guides, moves in X and Y.
   - **Spindle Head:** Travels vertically (Z axis), holds the cutting tool via a taper.

2. **Tooling & ATC Subsystem (2 marks)**
   - **Tool Magazine:** Holds 16–24 tools.
   - **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC):** Two-arm swing-arm type; exchanges tools in < 8 s.
   - **Tool Holder:** BT40 / CAT40 / HSK type with pull-stud.

3. **Control & Drive Subsystem (2 marks)**
   - **CNC Controller:** Fanuc / Siemens / Haas. Interprets G-code.
   - **Servo Motors + Ball Screws:** Drive X, Y, Z axes.
   - **Feedback System:** Encoders and linear scales for closed-loop positioning.

4. **Auxiliary Subsystem (1 mark)**
   - **Coolant system** (flood or TSC).
   - **Chip conveyor** (auger / hinge-belt).
   - **Enclosure** with safety interlocks.

*Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for full visual.*

#### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 12$ mm, $z = 4$, $V_c = 200$ m/min, $f_z = 0.06$ mm/tooth
- $a_p = 3$ mm, $a_e = 6$ mm, $L = 250$ mm

**1. Spindle Speed (2 marks):**

$$
N = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D} = \frac{1000 \cdot 200}{\pi \cdot 12} = \frac{200000}{37.699} \approx 5305 \text{ rpm}
$$

Round to **$N = 5300$ rpm** (or the nearest programmable value).

**2. Feed Rate (2 marks):**

$$
V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.06 \cdot 4 \cdot 5305 = 1273.2 \text{ mm/min}
$$

Round to **$V_f = 1270$ mm/min**.

**3. Material Removal Rate (1.5 marks):**

$$
MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f = 3 \cdot 6 \cdot 1273.2 = 22917.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 22918 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

**4. Machining Time (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_m = \frac{L}{V_f} = \frac{250}{1273.2} \approx 0.1964 \text{ min} = 11.78 \text{ s}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Using $V_f = f \cdot N$ (turning formula) instead of $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$. **−2 marks.**
> - **Common Error 2:** Forgetting the factor 1000 in the $N$ formula. The diameter is in mm, so $V_c$ in m/min requires the 1000 conversion. **−1 mark.**
> - **Common Error 3:** Not including units in the final answer or writing $V_c$ and $V_f$ in wrong units. **−0.5 mark each.**
> - **Common Error 4:** Not rounding to a realistic programmable value (e.g., 5305 is fine mathematically but 5300 is the practical answer). **−0.5 mark.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Module 2 Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the procedure for setting the **work coordinate system (G54)** on a VMC using a **mechanical edge-finder**. List the safety precautions to be observed during CNC milling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a part program (in standard G-code, Fanuc-compatible) to machine a **rectangular pocket of size 60 mm × 40 mm, depth 3 mm**, on a workpiece of size 100 × 80 × 20 mm (mild steel). The pocket's top-left corner is at X=20, Y=20 (work origin is at the top-left corner of the workpiece, top surface Z=0). Use:
- Tool 1: Ø10 mm, 2-flute HSS end mill, $N = 1500$ rpm, $V_f = 300$ mm/min.
- Cutter compensation NOT required.
- Assume climb milling, with step-over 50% of tool diameter (i.e., 5 mm). Use 3 passes (Z = −1, −2, −3 mm). The pocket is open on top.

**(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — WCS Setting Using Edge-Finder (7 marks)**

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Insert the edge-finder** (typically Ø10 mm) into the spindle; set spindle to **OFF** and Z raised to a safe height.
2. **Select a spindle speed of ~500 rpm** and turn it ON; bring the edge-finder close to the **X-edge** of the workpiece manually.
3. The edge-finder will "wobble" off-centre until it touches the workpiece edge. **Slowly jog** the table so the wobble reduces and the edge-finder becomes concentric.
4. At the moment of **concentricity (no wobble)**, the spindle centre is exactly **5 mm** (radius of the edge-finder) from the workpiece edge.
5. Read the **machine coordinate (M)** of the X-axis on the controller. Calculate:
   - $G54\,X = M_X - 5.0$ mm (if edge is at −X) or $G54\,X = M_X + 5.0$ mm (if edge is at +X).
6. Repeat for the **Y-edge** to set G54 Y.
7. For **Z**, jog the edge-finder to just touch the **top face of the workpiece** (use a piece of paper shim) and set $G54\,Z = 0.0$.

**Safety Precautions (3 marks):**

1. Wear **safety goggles, gloves, and ear protection**. **Remove loose clothing, ties, rings, and avoid loose hair.**
2. Ensure the **machine enclosure door is closed** during operation.
3. **Never touch** the spindle, tool, or workpiece while the spindle is rotating.
4. Keep **EMERGENCY STOP and FEED HOLD** buttons within reach at all times.
5. Perform a **dry run** at reduced feed (e.g., 25%) before the actual cut.
6. Verify **tool length offset** and **work origin** before pressing CYCLE START.
7. Ensure **adequate coolant flow** and **proper chip evacuation**.

#### **Part (b) — Part Program (7 marks)**

**Strategy:** Climb mill the pocket in 3 depth passes (Z = −1, −2, −3 mm), with step-over = 5 mm. The pocket is 60 mm long and 40 mm wide. Entry/lead-in: 5 mm arc ramp or plunge.

```
O0002 (RECT POCKET 60 X 40 X 3 MM, TOOL 1, TURN-MILL STEEL)
(*** SETUP ***)
G21 G90 G17 G94 G54               (Metric, Abs, XY plane, Feed/min, WCS-1)
G00 Z25.0                          (Safe Z)
M06 T01                            (Tool change: T1 = Ø10 HSS end mill)
M03 S1500                          (Spindle CW, 1500 rpm)
M08                                (Coolant ON)
G00 X20.0 Y20.0                    (Approach pocket top-left corner)
G00 Z2.0                           (Plunge clearance)

(*** PASS 1: Z = -1 mm ***)
G01 Z-1.0 F30                      (Plunge to first depth)
G01 X80.0 Y20.0 F300               (Cut along X to top-right)
G01 X80.0 Y60.0                    (Cut down to bottom-right)
G01 X20.0 Y60.0                    (Cut back to bottom-left)
G01 X20.0 Y25.0                    (Step-over 5 mm, back up)
G01 X80.0 Y25.0                    (Pass 2 within layer)
G01 X80.0 Y60.0                    (Move down)
G01 X20.0 Y60.0                    (Move back)
G01 X20.0 Y30.0                    (Step-over to Y=30)
G01 X80.0 Y30.0                    (Final pass at this layer)
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0

(*** PASS 2: Z = -2 mm ***)
G01 Z-2.0 F30                      (Plunge to second depth)
G01 X80.0 Y20.0 F300
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y25.0
G01 X80.0 Y25.0
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y30.0
G01 X80.0 Y30.0
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0

(*** PASS 3: Z = -3 mm ***)
G01 Z-3.0 F30                      (Plunge to final depth)
G01 X80.0 Y20.0 F300
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y25.0
G01 X80.0 Y25.0
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y30.0
G01 X80.0 Y30.0
G01 X80.0 Y60.0
G01 X20.0 Y60.0

(*** END ***)
G00 Z25.0                          (Retract to safe Z)
M09                                (Coolant off)
M05                                (Spindle stop)
G91 G28 Z0                         (Return to Z reference, incremental)
G91 G28 X0 Y0                      (Return to XY reference)
M30                                (Program end & rewind)
%
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Error 1:** Writing a program without proper **header block** (`G21, G90, G17, G54`) and **safety retraction** (`G91 G28 Z0`). **−1.5 marks.**
> - **Common Error 2:** Confusing **plunge feed (F30)** with **cutting feed (F300)**. The plunge rate should be ~10% of cutting feed. **−1 mark.**
> - **Common Error 3:** Not stepping **out of the pocket** before retracting to safe Z — this **gouges** the workpiece or breaks the tool. **−1.5 marks.**
> - **Common Error 4:** Writing `M30` without **M05 / M09** before it (spindle keeps running, coolant stays on). **−0.5 mark.**
> - **Common Error 5:** Writing the program in **G91 (incremental)** when the question specifies absolute coordinates. **−1 mark.**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ☐ **VMC = Vertical Machining Center**; spindle axis is vertical (Z).
- ☐ Axes follow the **Right-Hand Rule**: X = right, Y = away from operator, Z = up.
- ☐ Key formulas to memorize:
  - $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{\pi \cdot D}$
  - $V_f = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$
  - $MRR = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot V_f$
  - $T_m = \dfrac{L}{V_f}$
- ☐ **G90 = Absolute**, **G91 = Incremental** (memorize the difference).
- ☐ **G41 = Tool Left**, **G42 = Tool Right** (memorize "**L**eft = **L**ooking along tool path, tool is on your **L**eft").
- ☐ Canned cycles: **G81 = Drilling**, **G84 = Tapping (RH)**, **G80 = Cancel cycle**.
- ☐ Always perform a **dry run** and **single-block** first run.
- ☐ Set **G54 work origin** with **edge-finder**; remember to **subtract the edge-finder radius** (typically 5 mm).
- ☐ Standard **safety**: goggles, gloves, enclosure closed, emergency stop accessible.
- ☐ Part program structure: **Setup line → Tool change → Approach → Plunge → Cut → Retract → Reference return → M30**.
- ☐ Units: **mm, mm/min, rpm** — never mix inch/metric; declare **G21** explicitly.
- ☐ For VMC lab, common operations: **Facing, Pocketing, Drilling, Slotting, Profiling**.
- ☐ Controller codes: **M03 = Spindle CW**, **M05 = Stop**, **M06 = Tool change**, **M08 = Coolant on**, **M30 = End**.
- ☐ Tolerance range: VMC positioning accuracy is typically **±0.005 to ±0.01 mm**.

> **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Production Engineering Lab examination!** 🚀

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Cutting Experiments (e.g., Cutting Force Measurement, Cutting Temperature Measurement)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Metal Cutting Experiments (e.g., Cutting Force Measurement, Cutting Temperature Measurement) # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Metal Cutting — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Metal Cutting (Machining):** It is a manufacturing process in which a hardened cutting tool removes excess material in the form of chips from a workpiece by plastic deformation, in order to produce a finished component of desired shape, size, and surface finish. The relative motion between the tool and workpiece, combined with the tool's geometry, produces **cutting force** and **cutting temperature** — the two most fundamental measurable outputs in any machining experiment. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme PCAUL407 lab**, two of the most important physical phenomena that students are required to measure experimentally are: 1. **Cutting Force** — measured using a **Tool Dynamometer**. 2. **Cutting Temperature** — measured using a **Tool-Work Thermocouple (T-W TC)** or a **Single Thermocouple Technique**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Potato Peeler" Imagine peeling a potato with a kitchen peeler: - Your hand pushes the peeler **forward** → this is the **Cutting Force (F_c)**, the energy that actually shears the material. - Your hand presses the peeler **downward** onto the potato → this is the **Thrust/Feed Force (F_t)**, which prevents the tool from bouncing off. - The friction as the chip slides over the peeler's face creates **heat** — your peeler becomes warm/hot. A machinist's tool does **exactly the same thing** to a metal workpiece — just at speeds a million times higher, and the heat generated can melt the tool tip if not controlled! ## 1.3 Orthogonal vs. Oblique Cutting (Foundation Concept) | Feature | Orthogonal Cutting | Oblique Cutting | |---|---|---| | Cutting Edge Orientation | Perpendicular to cutting velocity | Inclined at an angle (inclination angle i ≠ 0) | | Chip Flow | 2-Dimensional (planar) | 3-Dimensional (helical curl) | | Lab Relevance | Simpler to analyze mathematically | Real-world turning/milling | | Force Components | F_c and F_t only | F_c, F_t, F_f (radial) | > [!IMPORTANT] > In PCAUL407 lab experiments, **orthogonal cutting on a lathe** is the standard test setup because it reduces the problem to a 2-D analysis using **Merchant's Circle**, which directly maps measurable forces to shear-plane parameters. ## 1.4 Cutting Force — The Three Components In an **oblique cutting operation** (real turning, drilling, milling), the resultant cutting force **R** is resolved into **three orthogonal components**: - **F_c (Cutting Force / Tangential Force):** Acts in the direction of cutting velocity. This is the **largest** component and consumes the **majority of the cutting power**. - **F_t (Thrust Force / Axial Force):** Acts parallel to feed direction; tends to push the tool away from the workpiece. - **F_f (Feed Force / Radial Force):** Acts perpendicular to the cutting velocity in the horizontal plane; pushes the tool radially. The **Resultant Force R** is given by: $$R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2 + F_f^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard KTU convention:** In 2-D orthogonal cutting, the lab manual typically reports only **F_c and F_t**, with **F_f = 0** by definition of the orthogonal coordinate system. ## 1.5 Cutting Temperature — Definition > [!NOTE] > **Cutting Temperature (θ):** The temperature developed at the **tool–chip interface** during machining, arising from the conversion of **~98% of the mechanical cutting energy into heat** in the primary shear zone and the secondary deformation (tool–chip friction) zone. It is the single most important parameter governing **tool life, wear rate, and surface integrity.** The heat partition (according to Trigger & Chao) is approximately: - **Primary shear zone:** ~**80%** of total heat - **Secondary (tool-chip) zone:** ~**18%** - **Workpiece:** ~**2%** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Merchant's Force Circle Analysis (Orthogonal Cutting) In orthogonal cutting, the **cutting force F_c** and **thrust force F_t** measured by the dynamometer are resolved through **Merchant's Circle** to find the **shear force F_s** and **normal force F_n** acting on the shear plane. ### Step-by-Step Logic of the Force Resolution 1. **Measured quantities (from dynamometer):** F_c and F_t. 2. **Tool geometry (measured from tool):** Rake angle (α) and clearance angle. 3. **Friction angle (β):** Computed from the friction coefficient μ on the tool face. 4. **Shear angle (φ):** Computed using **Merchant's equation** (derived from force balance). 5. **Resultant force R** acts on the shear plane; its components are F_s (along shear) and F_n (normal to shear). The **fundamental relationship** between measurable and shear-plane forces: $$F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi$$ $$F_n = F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi$$ The **friction force F** and **normal force N** on the tool face: $$F = F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi$$ $$N = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi$$ The **coefficient of friction** at the tool-chip interface: $$\mu = \frac{F}{N} = \frac{F_c \sin\phi + F_t \cos\phi}{F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi}$$ The **friction angle β** is: $$\beta = \tan^{-1}(\mu)$$ ## 2.2 Shear Angle — Merchant's Equation > [!IMPORTANT] > **Merchant's Shear Angle Equation** (most-cited equation in PCAUL407 exams): $$\boxed{2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°}$$ Or equivalently: $$\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$ This equation is derived from the **principle of minimum cutting energy**. ## 2.3 Chip Thickness Ratio (r) $$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)}$$ Where: - **t₁** = Uncut chip thickness (feed, f) - **t₂** = Actual chip thickness (measured experimentally with micrometer) - **φ** = Shear angle - **α** = Rake angle ## 2.4 Specific Cutting Energy & Power **Specific cutting energy (u_s):** $$u_s = \frac{F_c}{A_c} = \frac{F_c}{d \times f}$$ Where: - **d** = Depth of cut (mm) - **f** = Feed (mm/rev) - **A_c** = Cross-sectional area of uncut chip (mm²) **Cutting Power (P_c):** $$P_c = F_c \times V_c$$ Where **V_c** is the cutting velocity in m/s. (Note: F_c must be in **Newtons** and V_c in **m/s** to get **Watts**.) ## 2.5 Tool Dynamometer — Working Principle A **Tool Dynamometer** is a force-measuring instrument mounted between the tool holder and the machine slide, designed to record the three force components during cutting. ### Common Dynamometer Types | Type | Principle | KTU Lab Use | |---|---|---| | **Mechanical (Pendulum)** | Deflection against calibrated spring | Outdated — qualitative only | | **Strain-Gauge (most common)** | Strain gauges bonded to a calibrated elastic body (octagonal ring or square plate) | **Standard PCAUL407 instrument** | | **Piezoelectric** | Quartz crystal generates charge proportional to force | High-frequency, very stiff | | **Hydraulic** | Pressure in a fluid cell measured by Bourdon gauge | Rare, low accuracy | ### Strain-Gauge Dynamometer — Operating Logic 1. The cutting force causes a **minute elastic deformation** in the tool-holding column. 2. **Strain gauges** bonded to the column change their **electrical resistance** in proportion to strain (ΔR/R ∝ ε). 3. The gauges are wired in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration; the output voltage is proportional to the applied force. 4. A **multi-channel amplifier + A/D converter + PC** records the force in real time. 5. **Calibration** is done by applying known dead-weights and recording the mV output → calibration constant (N/division). > [!NOTE] > The strain gauge equation: $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$, where **G_f ≈ 2.0** is the gauge factor (a constant of the strain gauge material). ## 2.6 Cutting Temperature Measurement Methods ### Method 1: Tool-Work Thermocouple (T-W TC) — **Standard KTU Method** 1. One wire of a thermocouple is **insulated and routed through the tool shank**, with its hot junction positioned **as close as possible to the cutting edge**. 2. The **workpiece itself** acts as the second thermo-element (e.g., if the tool is carbide and workpiece is mild steel, the junction is a **carbide-steel thermocouple**). 3. The **EMF generated** is measured using a cold-junction-compensated millivolt meter or data logger. 4. EMF is converted to temperature using standard **calibration curves** (mV vs. °C). ### Method 2: Single Thermocouple Technique (Workpiece-Tool Embedded) A small calibrated thermocouple (e.g., **Chromel-Alumel**, Type K) is embedded into a hole drilled in the tool, with the hot junction positioned at a known distance (e.g., 1–2 mm) from the cutting edge. The cold junction is held at **0 °C (ice bath)** for accurate reference. ### Method 3: Infrared / Optical Pyrometer A non-contact method where the **thermal radiation** from the chip or tool face is focused onto a detector. The intensity follows **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $$E = \sigma \cdot T^4$$ Where **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. ### Method 4: Infrared Thermal Imaging Camera Modern method — gives a **2-D thermal map** of the cutting zone, allowing visualization of the **hot zone** and chip-tool interface temperature distribution. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2 + F_f^2}$ | Resultant cutting force | N (Newtons) | | 2 | $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ | Merchant's shear angle | Degrees | | 3 | $r = t_1/t_2 = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$ | Chip thickness ratio | Dimensionless | | 4 | $u_s = F_c / (d \cdot f)$ | Specific cutting energy | N/mm² or J/mm³ | | 5 | $P_c = F_c \cdot V_c$ | Cutting power | Watts (F_c in N, V_c in m/s) | | 6 | $\mu = \tan\beta = (F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi)/(F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi)$ | Tool-chip friction coefficient | Dimensionless | | 7 | $F_s = F_c\cos\phi - F_t\sin\phi$ | Shear force on shear plane | N | | 8 | $F_n = F_c\sin\phi + F_t\cos\phi$ | Normal force on shear plane | N | | 9 | $\tau_s = F_s / A_s$ | Shear strength of workpiece | N/mm² | | 10 | $A_s = t_1 \cdot w / \sin\phi$ | Shear plane area | mm² | | 11 | $\theta_{max} = C \cdot V_c^a \cdot f^b \cdot d^c$ | Empirical cutting temperature (Shaw) | °C | | 12 | $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ | Strain gauge equation | Gauge factor G_f ≈ 2.0 | | 13 | $E = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann law for radiation | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, students MUST maintain **angle consistency** (all in degrees for trig functions in calculators, or convert to radians). A common mark-deduction is forgetting to convert (φ − α) into degrees before computing sin/cos. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cutting force** data is used to size **CNC machine spindle motors** (e.g., 15 kW spindle must deliver F_c × V_c ≤ 15,000 W with safety margin). - **Cutting temperature** data drives **tool material selection** (e.g., coated carbide for steel at 800 °C, CBN for hardened steel at 1200 °C). - **Specific cutting energy u_s** is used in **CAM software** (Mastercam, NX) to estimate machining cycle time and energy consumption. - **Dynamometer data** is critical in **Industry 4.0 smart machining** for **tool condition monitoring (TCM)** — sudden drop in F_c indicates tool breakage; gradual rise indicates wear. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Merchant's Shear Angle Equation ### Statement To derive the relationship: $2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$ ### Free Body Diagram Setup Consider the **cutting tool** as a free body. The forces acting on the tool are: - **F_c** → horizontal (cutting direction) - **F_t** → vertical (thrust direction) - **R'** → equal and opposite to **R** (force from chip on tool) - **F** → friction force on rake face - **N** → normal force on rake face ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** The resultant cutting force **R** makes an angle (φ + β − α) with the horizontal (cutting direction). This is because: - R is inclined at angle **(90° − φ − β)** with the horizontal (from the force polygon). - Resolving along horizontal: $F_c = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ - Resolving along vertical: $F_t = R \sin(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ **Step 2:** The ratio of F_t to F_c gives the slope of the resultant force vector: $$\frac{F_t}{F_c} = \tan(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$$ **Step 3:** This means: $$\phi + \beta - \alpha = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{F_t}{F_c}\right)$$ **Step 4:** The **principle of minimum energy** (Ernst & Merchant) states that the cutting process naturally adopts a shear angle that **minimizes F_c** for given tool-workpiece conditions. Differentiating F_c with respect to φ and setting dF_c/dφ = 0 yields: $$\frac{dF_c}{d\phi} = 0 \implies 2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$$ **Step 5:** Solving for φ: $$\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}$$ $$\boxed{\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Force & Shear Angle Calculation > **Problem (Typical KTU Numerical):** > In an orthogonal cutting experiment on mild steel with a tool of rake angle **α = 10°**, the following readings were recorded: > - Cutting force: **F_c = 1200 N** > - Thrust force: **F_t = 600 N** > - Uncut chip thickness: **t₁ = 0.25 mm** > - Width of cut: **w = 3.0 mm** > - Cutting velocity: **V_c = 2.0 m/s** > > Calculate: (a) Friction angle β, (b) Shear angle φ, (c) Shear force F_s, (d) Cutting Power P_c. ### Solution **Step 1: Calculate the Resultant Force R** $$R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2 + F_f^2} = \sqrt{1200^2 + 600^2 + 0^2}$$ $$R = \sqrt{1{,}440{,}000 + 360{,}000} = \sqrt{1{,}800{,}000}$$ $$R \approx 1341.64 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the Friction Angle β** First, find the friction coefficient: $$\mu = \frac{F_t}{F_c} = \frac{600}{1200} = 0.5$$ Therefore: $$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) \approx 26.57°$$ **Step 3: Calculate the Shear Angle φ using Merchant's Equation** $$\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2} = 45° + \frac{10°}{2} - \frac{26.57°}{2}$$ $$\phi = 45° + 5° - 13.28° = 36.72°$$ **Step 4: Calculate the Shear Force F_s** $$F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi$$ Converting φ to radians for computation, or using degrees directly: - $\cos(36.72°) \approx 0.8013$ - $\sin(36.72°) \approx 0.5982$ $$F_s = (1200)(0.8013) - (600)(0.5982) = 961.56 - 358.92$$ $$F_s \approx 602.64 \text{ N}$$ **Step 5: Calculate the Cutting Power P_c** $$P_c = F_c \times V_c = 1200 \text{ N} \times 2.0 \text{ m/s} = 2400 \text{ W} = 2.4 \text{ kW}$$ ### Result Summary | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Resultant Force R | 1341.64 N | | Friction Coefficient μ | 0.50 | | Friction Angle β | 26.57° | | Shear Angle φ | 36.72° | | Shear Force F_s | 602.64 N | | Cutting Power P_c | 2.4 kW | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Influence of Cutting Parameters | Parameter Increase | Effect on F_c | Effect on θ (Temperature) | Effect on Tool Life | |---|---|---|---| | Cutting speed (V_c) ↑ | ↓ slightly (then ↑ at very high V_c) | ↑↑↑ (strongest effect) | ↓↓↓ (worst) | | Feed (f) ↑ | ↑↑ (proportional) | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | | Depth of cut (d) ↑ | ↑↑↑ (proportional) | ↑ | ↓ | | Rake angle (α) ↑ | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | | Friction coefficient (μ) ↑ | ↑ | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | | Use of cutting fluid | ↓ | ↓↓↓ | ↑↑↑ | > [!NOTE] > This table is **high-yield** for both theory and viva questions. KTU examiners frequently ask "What happens to cutting force when rake angle is increased?" — the answer is **it decreases** because the shear angle increases, reducing shear work. ## 3.4 Calibration of Dynamometer — Worked Steps A dynamometer must be calibrated before each experiment. The procedure: 1. **Mount the tool holder** in the dynamometer. 2. **Apply a known dead-weight W** vertically (simulating thrust force F_t). 3. **Record the output voltage V_out** (mV) from the strain gauge bridge. 4. **Calculate calibration constant k** (N/mV): $$k = \frac{W}{V_{out}}$$ 5. **Repeat for 5–6 different weights** to verify linearity. 6. **Plot W vs. V_out** — slope of straight line = **k**. 7. **Apply known horizontal force** for F_c calibration similarly. > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU lab records, students must tabulate: Weight (N) | Output (mV) | Calibration Constant (N/mV) | Standard Deviation. **A linear regression slope with R² > 0.99** is expected for full marks. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Cutting Force Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Workpiece on Lathe] --> B1[Cutting Tool] B1 --> C1[Tool Holder] C1 --> D1[Tool Dynamometer
Strain Gauge Bridge] D1 --> E1[Amplifier Unit] E1 --> F1[ADC Converter] F1 --> G1[Data Acquisition PC] G1 --> H1[Force vs Time Plot] G1 --> H2[Force vs Cutting Speed Plot] G1 --> H3[Tabulated Force Readings] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cutting Temperature Measurement (Tool-Work Thermocouple) ```mermaid flowchart TD T1[Insulated Thermocouple Wire
Routed Through Tool Shank] --> T2[Hot Junction
Near Cutting Edge] T3[Workpiece Acts as
Second Thermoelement] --> T2 T2 --> T4[EMF Generated in mV] T4 --> T5[Cold Junction
Ice Bath 0 degC] T4 --> T6[Digital Millivolt Meter
or Data Logger] T6 --> T7[EMF to Temperature
Conversion via Calibration Curve] T7 --> T8[Temperature vs Time Graph] T7 --> T9[Temperature vs Cutting Speed Graph] ``` ## 4.3 Merchant's Force Circle — Free Body Concept ```mermaid flowchart TD M1[Measured Quantities
F_c and F_t from Dynamometer] --> M2[Force Polygon Construction] M2 --> M3[Resultant Force R] M2 --> M4[Friction Force F on Rake Face] M2 --> M5[Normal Force N on Rake Face] M3 --> M6[Shear Force F_s on Shear Plane] M3 --> M7[Normal Force F_n on Shear Plane] M6 --> M8[Compute Shear Angle phi
Merchant Equation] M7 --> M8 M4 --> M9[Compute Friction Angle beta] M5 --> M9 M8 --> M10[Final Output
phi, F_s, mu, tau_s] M9 --> M10 ``` ## 4.4 Heat Partition in Metal Cutting ```mermaid flowchart LR H1[Total Mechanical Energy
99 percent Converts to Heat] --> H2[Primary Shear Zone
80 percent Heat] H1 --> H3[Secondary Zone
Tool Chip Friction
18 percent Heat] H1 --> H4[Workpiece
2 percent Heat] H2 --> H5[Conducted into Chip] H2 --> H6[Conducted into Tool] H3 --> H5 H3 --> H6 H6 --> H7[Tool Tip Temperature
Measured by Thermocouple] ``` ## 4.5 Functional Block — Tool Dynamometer Signal Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Forces F_c and F_t
Applied on Tool] --> S2[Elastic Deformation
of Tool Holder Column] S2 --> S3[Strain Gauges
Resistance Changes] S3 --> S4[Wheatstone Bridge
Voltage Output mV] S4 --> S5[Differential Amplifier
Gain 100 to 1000] S5 --> S6[Low Pass Filter
Removes Noise] S6 --> S7[Analog to Digital Converter] S7 --> S8[Digital Display
or PC Software] ``` > [!NOTE] > The **octagonal ring dynamometer** is the most common in KTU labs. It has **4 strain gauges** mounted on an octagonal cross-section column. The 4 gauges are connected in a **full Wheatstone bridge** so that temperature-induced resistance changes are **self-cancelled** (since all 4 gauges experience the same temperature). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2, PCAUL407]** **Q: Define the following terms related to orthogonal cutting: (i) Shear angle, (ii) Rake angle, (iii) Chip thickness ratio. List the measuring instrument used for cutting force measurement.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Shear Angle (φ):** The angle between the shear plane and the cutting velocity direction. It is determined experimentally by the formula $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ (Merchant's equation). **(ii) Rake Angle (α):** The angle between the rake face of the tool and a reference plane perpendicular to the cutting velocity. A positive rake angle reduces cutting force. **(iii) Chip Thickness Ratio (r):** The ratio of uncut chip thickness (t₁) to actual chip thickness (t₂), expressed as $r = t_1/t_2 = \sin\phi / \cos(\phi - \alpha)$. It is always less than 1 for metal cutting. **Instrument:** **Tool Dynamometer** (Strain-Gauge type) is used for cutting force measurement. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2, PCAUL407]** **Q: Explain the working principle of the Tool-Work Thermocouple method for measuring cutting temperature. Mention one advantage and one limitation.** **Model Answer:** **Working Principle:** In the Tool-Work Thermocouple (T-W TC) method, an insulated thermocouple wire is routed through the tool shank, and its hot junction is positioned as close as possible to the cutting edge. The workpiece itself acts as the second thermoelement of the thermocouple. The **Seebeck EMF** generated at the hot junction (cutting zone) is measured by a millivolt meter, and is then converted to temperature using standard calibration tables. **Advantage:** Highly accurate measurement of the **interface temperature** between tool and chip, which directly affects tool life. **Limitation:** Requires a **specially modified tool holder** with insulated wiring, and the method is sensitive to **electrical noise** from the machine spindle. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2, PCAUL407]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **Merchant's circle force analysis** for an orthogonal cutting process. Derive the expression for the **shear angle** in terms of rake angle and friction angle. **(b) [7 Marks]** In an orthogonal cutting experiment, the following data were obtained: - Tool rake angle α = 15° - Uncut chip thickness t₁ = 0.3 mm - Measured chip thickness t₂ = 0.5 mm - Cutting force F_c = 1500 N - Thrust force F_t = 800 N - Width of cut w = 4 mm - Cutting velocity V_c = 1.5 m/s Calculate: (i) Friction coefficient μ, (ii) Shear angle φ, (iii) Shear force F_s, (iv) Cutting Power P_c, and (v) Specific cutting energy u_s. --- #### Model Solution for Question A **Part (a) — Merchant's Circle Analysis:** **Sketch Description (not drawable in text, but verbally):** Draw the orthogonal cutting system showing: - Workpiece with tool - Cutting force F_c (horizontal, →) - Thrust force F_t (vertical, ↓) - Rake face of tool inclined at α - Shear plane inclined at φ from cutting direction - Resultant force R acting on the tool at angle (φ + β − α) **Analysis Steps:** 1. **Forces on Tool Face (Friction Side):** The tool face experiences a **friction force F** (along rake face) and a **normal force N** (perpendicular to rake face). Their resultant is **R'** (reaction from chip). 2. **Forces on Shear Plane (Material Side):** The shear plane experiences **shear force F_s** (along the plane) and **normal force F_n** (perpendicular to the plane). Their resultant is **R** (the same as R' but opposite in direction). 3. **Equilibrium of the chip** (treated as a free body) gives the relations: - $F_c = R \cos(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ - $F_t = R \sin(\phi + \beta - \alpha)$ 4. **Derivation of Shear Angle:** Using the **principle of minimum energy** (Merchant's assumption), the shear angle φ is found by minimizing F_c w.r.t. φ: $$\frac{dF_c}{d\phi} = 0 \implies 2\phi + \beta - \alpha = 90°$$ Solving for φ: $$\boxed{\phi = 45° + \frac{\alpha}{2} - \frac{\beta}{2}}$$ This is **Merchant's shear angle equation**. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate Shear Angle from Chip Thickness Data** $$r = \frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{0.3}{0.5} = 0.6$$ Using the chip thickness ratio formula: $$r = \frac{\sin\phi}{\cos(\phi - \alpha)} = 0.6$$ We can directly use the simplified expression: $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r \cos\alpha}{1 - r\sin\alpha}$ $$\tan\phi = \frac{0.6 \times \cos(15°)}{1 - 0.6 \times \sin(15°)} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.9659}{1 - 0.6 \times 0.2588}$$ $$\tan\phi = \frac{0.5795}{1 - 0.1553} = \frac{0.5795}{0.8447} = 0.6861$$ $$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.6861) \approx 34.45°$$ **Step 2: Calculate Friction Coefficient μ** $$\mu = \frac{F_t}{F_c} = \frac{800}{1500} = 0.5333$$ **Step 3: Calculate Friction Angle β** $$\beta = \tan^{-1}(0.5333) \approx 28.07°$$ **Step 4: Verify with Merchant's Equation (Optional Check)** $$\phi_{predicted} = 45° + \frac{15°}{2} - \frac{28.07°}{2} = 45° + 7.5° - 14.04° = 38.46°$$ > [!NOTE] > The two values (34.45° from chip thickness, 38.46° from force data) differ slightly due to measurement error and the inherent idealization in Merchant's model. In KTU exams, either may be used depending on the question's instruction. **Step 5: Calculate Shear Force F_s** $$F_s = F_c \cos\phi - F_t \sin\phi$$ Using φ = 34.45°: - $\cos(34.45°) \approx 0.8246$ - $\sin(34.45°) \approx 0.5658$ $$F_s = (1500)(0.8246) - (800)(0.5658) = 1236.9 - 452.6 = 784.3 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6: Calculate Cutting Power P_c** $$P_c = F_c \times V_c = 1500 \times 1.5 = 2250 \text{ W} = 2.25 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 7: Calculate Specific Cutting Energy u_s** Cross-sectional area of uncut chip: $$A_c = t_1 \times w = 0.3 \times 4 = 1.2 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$u_s = \frac{F_c}{A_c} = \frac{1500}{1.2} = 1250 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1.25 \text{ J/mm}^3$$ #### Final Answer Tabulation (Question A) | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Shear angle φ (from chips) | 34.45° | | Friction coefficient μ | 0.5333 | | Friction angle β | 28.07° | | Shear force F_s | 784.3 N | | Cutting power P_c | 2.25 kW | | Specific cutting energy u_s | 1.25 J/mm³ | --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, Module 2, PCAUL407]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe with a block diagram the **construction and working of a strain-gauge type tool dynamometer** used for cutting force measurement in a lathe. What is its principle of operation? **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the various methods of **cutting temperature measurement** in machining. Compare the Tool-Work Thermocouple method and the Infrared Pyrometer method in terms of accuracy, cost, and ease of use. --- #### Model Solution for Question B **Part (a) — Strain-Gauge Tool Dynamometer:** **Construction (Block Diagram — see SECTION_4.5 above):** 1. **Elastic Body (Octagonal Ring):** A vertical column with an **octagonal cross-section** is mounted between the machine slide and the tool holder. It is the **deforming element** that responds to cutting forces. 2. **Strain Gauges (4 nos.):** Four **foil-type strain gauges** are bonded onto the four flat faces of the octagonal ring using special adhesive (cyanoacrylate or epoxy). They are oriented along the sensitive axis (45° to the axis for shear sensitivity). 3. **Wheatstone Bridge:** The 4 gauges are connected in a **full-bridge configuration** so that: - Output voltage is **4× that of a single gauge** - **Temperature compensation is automatic** (all 4 gauges at same temperature) - **Bending effects are isolated** from axial effects. 4. **Cable & Connectors:** Shielded cables carry the mV output to the amplifier. 5. **Amplifier + ADC + Display:** The millivolt signal is amplified (gain ~1000), filtered, digitized, and displayed on PC software. **Working Principle:** When the cutting tool experiences forces F_c, F_t (and possibly F_f), the octagonal ring **elastically deforms** by a small amount (typically 1–10 micrometers). This deformation causes the strain gauges to **change their electrical resistance** according to: $$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$$ where **G_f ≈ 2.0** (gauge factor) and **ε** is the strain. The Wheatstone bridge converts this resistance change into a **proportional voltage (mV)**, which is then amplified and recorded. The **calibration constant (N/mV)** is used to convert the mV reading to actual force in Newtons. **Part (b) — Cutting Temperature Measurement Methods:** **Method 1: Tool-Work Thermocouple (T-W TC):** - A thermocouple wire is embedded in the tool, with the workpiece as the other element. - Measures **Seebeck EMF** proportional to the temperature difference between the hot junction (cutting edge) and cold junction (ice bath at 0 °C). - Highly accurate for **average tool-chip interface temperature**. **Method 2: Embedded Single Thermocouple:** - A single Chromel-Alumel (Type K) thermocouple is embedded in a hole drilled in the carbide insert, very close (1–2 mm) to the cutting edge. - Standard EMF-to-temperature calibration curves are used. - Best for **localized temperature** near the tool tip. **Method 3: Infrared / Optical Pyrometer:** - A non-contact method; the chip's hot zone is sighted through an optical lens onto a thermopile detector. - The detector output is proportional to **T⁴** (Stefan-Boltzmann law). - Measures **chip temperature** in the visible/IR spectrum. **Method 4: Infrared Thermal Imaging Camera:** - A 2-D map of the cutting zone is captured. - Shows the spatial distribution of the heat-affected zone in real time. **Comparison Table:** | Feature | Tool-Work Thermocouple | Infrared Pyrometer | |---|---|---| | **Contact Type** | Contact (invasive) | Non-contact (non-invasive) | | **Accuracy** | High (±2 °C) | Moderate (±10–20 °C) | | **Cost** | Low to moderate | High | | **Setup Complexity** | Moderate (tool modification needed) | Low (sight and measure) | | **Best For** | Interface temperature (tool–chip) | Free chip temperature | | **Limitation** | Insulated wiring required; electrical noise | Emissivity correction needed | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert** t₁ and t₂ into the same unit (mm) before computing r. Always check units. > 2. Using **radians in trigonometric functions** without converting the angle from degrees. In KTU calculators, students must remember to **switch to DEG mode**. > 3. Confusing **F_c (cutting/tangential)** with **F_t (thrust)** in Merchant's circle — these are NOT interchangeable. > 4. Writing **F_s = F_c sin φ − F_t cos φ** (wrong sign) instead of the correct **F_s = F_c cos φ − F_t sin φ**. This single sign error costs **2–3 marks**. > 5. Failing to mention the **gauge factor (G_f ≈ 2.0)** when describing strain-gauge operation. > 6. Reporting cutting power in **N·m/s** (which is correct as Watts) but writing it incorrectly as **J/s** (this is fine) vs. the most common error — **omitting units altogether** (loses 1 mark). > 7. For Tool-Work Thermocouple, students often forget to specify the **cold junction reference (0 °C ice bath)**, which is mandatory for accuracy. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Fire Revision Points (Must memorize for KTU exams):** - [x] **Merchant's Equation:** $\phi = 45° + \alpha/2 - \beta/2$ — derived from minimum energy principle. - [x] **Resultant Force:** $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2 + F_f^2}$ (3D oblique) or $R = \sqrt{F_c^2 + F_t^2}$ (2D orthogonal). - [x] **Chip Thickness Ratio:** $r = t_1/t_2$, always **< 1** for most metals (chips are thicker than uncut material due to plastic deformation). - [x] **Specific Cutting Energy:** $u_s = F_c / (d \times f)$ — typical values: **0.5–1.5 N/mm² for aluminum, 2–4 N/mm² for steel**. - [x] **Cutting Power:** $P_c = F_c \times V_c$ in SI units. - [x] **Cutting Temperature** primarily depends on cutting speed V_c (exponent a ≈ 0.3–0.5 in Shaw's empirical equation). - [x] **Tool Dynamometer types:** Mechanical (obsolete), Strain Gauge (most common in KTU), Piezoelectric (high freq), Hydraulic (rare). - [x] **Strain gauge equation:** $\Delta R/R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ with $G_f \approx 2.0$. - [x] **Wheatstone bridge** gives 4× sensitivity + automatic temperature compensation. - [x] **Tool-Work Thermocouple** — workpiece is the second thermoelement; hot junction is at cutting edge. - [x] **Stefan-Boltzmann law:** $E = \sigma T^4$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/(m²·K⁴) — used in pyrometer-based temperature measurement. - [x] **Heat partition (Trigger & Chao):** 80% primary zone + 18% tool-chip zone + 2% workpiece. - [x] **Friction coefficient:** $\mu = F_t / F_c$ (in orthogonal cutting). - [x] **Cutting fluid** reduces **both** cutting force and cutting temperature, but its main role is **temperature reduction** (cooling). - [x] **Empirical cutting temperature formula (Shaw):** $\theta = C \cdot V_c^a \cdot f^b \cdot d^c$ where a, b, c are constants determined experimentally. - [x] **Effect of parameters:** ↑ V_c → ↑↑↑ θ, ↑ f → ↑↑ θ, ↑ d → ↑ θ; ↑ α → ↓ θ and ↓ F_c. - [x] **Dynamometer calibration** is done with known dead weights, and the **slope of W vs. mV plot** is the calibration constant. - [x] **For 7-mark sub-questions in KTU ESE**, always provide: (i) Definition, (ii) Diagram/Sketch, (iii) Working principle, (iv) Sample calculation, and (v) Practical significance — this structure guarantees full marks. > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Tip:** Before the exam, solve at least **3 numerical problems** involving Merchant's circle, F_s computation, and cutting power. KTU exams consistently test these three computations in Part B. The Part A questions almost always ask for **definitions and instrument names** — memorize them as short 2–3 sentence answers.

topics.title: 3D Printing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAUL407 - PRODUCTION ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** 3D Printing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧩 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**3D Printing**, formally known as **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, is defined by the **ISO/ASTM 52900** standard as the *"process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing and formative manufacturing methodologies."*

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme Production Engineering Lab (PCAUL407)**, 3D Printing is treated as a **rapid prototyping and rapid manufacturing** process where a physical three-dimensional object is constructed directly from a **Computer-Aided Design (CAD) model** through the successive addition of material in thin cross-sectional layers — typically ranging from **0.05 mm to 0.4 mm** in thickness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCAUL407 – Module 2):** Students are expected to gain hands-on exposure to the complete **3D Printing workflow** — from CAD model generation → STL file conversion → Slicing → Machine setup → Actual printing → Post-processing → Dimensional inspection. Theory questions frequently test the workflow, process parameters, and comparative analysis of technologies.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are making a multi-layered **chocolate cake** 🍰:

1. You first imagine the cake's shape (this is your **3D CAD model**).
2. You slice the cake mentally into thin horizontal layers (this is the **slicing software** operation).
3. You bake one thin layer, then pour the next on top, and so on — **layer-by-layer** until the cake is complete.
4. Finally, you trim the rough edges and decorate it (this is the **post-processing** stage).

3D Printing works **exactly the same way**, except instead of cake batter, you use **plastic filament, resin, or metal powder**, and instead of an oven, you use a **computer-controlled printer head or laser**.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Terminology (Must Memorize)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **STL File** | Standard Tessellation Language — triangulates the 3D surface geometry |
| **Slicer** | Software that converts STL into G-code layer instructions |
| **G-code** | Machine language that drives motors and extruders |
| **Layer Height** | Vertical thickness of each printed layer (typically **0.1 mm – 0.3 mm**) |
| **Infill** | Internal dense structure percentage (commonly **15%–30%**) |
| **Build Volume** | Maximum printable size, e.g., **220 × 220 × 250 mm** |
| **Support Material** | Temporary scaffolding for overhangs > **45°** |

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"Rapid Prototyping"** was the original name for 3D Printing in the 1980s, coined when **Chuck Hull** invented **Stereolithography (SLA)** in **1984** and filed the first patent. It was later rebranded as **Additive Manufacturing** to reflect its evolution from prototyping-only to end-part production.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete 3D Printing Workflow (Step-by-Step Logic)

The additive manufacturing pipeline consists of **six sequential stages**. Each stage is critical — a flaw in any stage propagates into the final part quality.

### Stage 1 → **3D Modeling (CAD)**
- The part is designed in software such as **SolidWorks, Fusion 360, Creo, or CATIA**.
- Output format: Native CAD file (`.sldprt`, `.step`, `.iges`).

### Stage 2 → **STL Conversion (Tessellation)**
- The smooth CAD surface is converted into a mesh of **triangular facets**.
- The **chordal deviation** or **angular tolerance** controls how closely the triangles approximate the curved surface.
- Output format: `.stl` file (ASCII or Binary).

### Stage 3 → **Slicing (G-code Generation)**
- Software like **Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D** slices the STL into horizontal layers.
- Generates toolpaths: **outer walls, inner infill, top/bottom layers, supports, prime tower**.
- Output format: `.gcode` file.

### Stage 4 → **Machine Setup & Printing**
- Filament loading, **bed leveling**, **nozzle temperature** (typically **190 °C – 250 °C** for PLA/ABS), **bed temperature** (typically **60 °C – 110 °C**), **chamber temperature** (for ABS/PC).
- The printer executes G-code line-by-line.

### Stage 5 → **Cooling & Part Removal**
- Layer-by-layer cooling allows solidification (for **thermoplastics**) or photopolymerization (for **resins**).
- The part is removed from the build platform.

### Stage 6 → **Post-Processing**
- **Support removal**, **sanding**, **acetone smoothing (ABS)**, **curing (UV resin)**, **painting**, **dimensional inspection**.

## 2.2 Classification of 3D Printing Technologies (ASTM F42 Standard)

The ASTM F42 committee classifies Additive Manufacturing into **seven process categories**. The most relevant for the PCAUL407 lab course are:

| Category | Technology | Material State | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Material Extrusion** | **FDM / FFF** | Thermoplastic filament | Prototyping, jigs |
| **Vat Photopolymerization** | **SLA / DLP** | Liquid photopolymer resin | High-detail models, jewelry, dental |
| **Powder Bed Fusion** | **SLS / SLM / DMLS** | Polymer / Metal powder | Functional parts, aerospace |
| **Material Jetting** | PolyJet, MultiJet | Photopolymer droplets | Multi-color prototypes |
| **Binder Jetting** | Binder Jetting | Powder + Liquid binder | Sand casting patterns, full-color models |
| **Directed Energy Deposition** | DED | Metal wire / powder | Large metal part repair |
| **Sheet Lamination** | LOM | Paper / Metal sheets | Low-cost prototypes |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For your exam: Memorize the following equations.** They are frequently asked in numerical and short-answer questions on 3D Printing.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Number of Layers** | $N = \dfrac{H_{total}}{h_{layer}}$ | $N$ = number of layers, $H_{total}$ = part height (mm), $h_{layer}$ = layer thickness (mm) |
| 2 | **Print Time (Approx.)** | $T_{print} = \dfrac{V_{part}}{Q_{extruder}} + N \cdot t_{travel}$ | $V_{part}$ = part volume (mm³), $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (mm³/s), $t_{travel}$ = layer travel time (s) |
| 3 | **Material Volume (with infill)** | $V_{mat} = V_{shell} + f_{infill} \cdot V_{interior}$ | $f_{infill}$ = infill fraction (0.15 for 15%), $V$ in mm³ |
| 4 | **Mass of Material** | $m = \rho \cdot V_{mat}$ | $\rho$ = material density (g/cm³), $m$ in grams |
| 5 | **Volumetric Throughput** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_{filament}^{2}}{4} \cdot v_{feed}$ | $d$ = filament diameter (mm), $v_{feed}$ = feed rate (mm/s) |
| 6 | **Theoretical Surface Roughness** | $R_{a} \approx \dfrac{h_{layer}}{2}$ (for staircase effect) | $R_{a}$ in mm |
| 7 | **Cost Estimation** | $C_{total} = C_{material} + C_{machine} + C_{labor} + C_{post}$ | All costs in ₹ / USD |
| 8 | **STL Triangle Count** | $A_{triangle} = \dfrac{1}{2} \vert \vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} \vert$ | Area using cross product, vectors in mm |

> **Critical Note on Units**: Always convert **mm³ → cm³** when using density in **g/cm³** by dividing by **1000**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (GE Aviation, Boeing)**: Fuel nozzle tips printed in Ti-6Al-4V using **DMLS** — **20 parts consolidated into 1**, 25% weight reduction.
- **Medical (Implants, Orthotics)**: Patient-specific **cranial implants** in titanium; **3D-printed hearing aids** (phonak) using SLA.
- **Automotive (Bugatti, BMW)**: **Brake calipers, jigs, fixtures** printed in metal for weight optimization.
- **Architecture**: **Scale models** built using binder jetting for full-color representations.
- **Tooling**: **Investment casting patterns** and **injection mold inserts** with conformal cooling channels printed directly in metal.

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Print Time Estimation for an FDM Part

### Problem Setup

A rectangular block of dimensions **L × W × H = 100 mm × 80 mm × 60 mm** is to be printed on an FDM machine with:
- Layer thickness, $h_{layer} = 0.2 \text{ mm}$
- Infill fraction, $f_{infill} = 0.20$ (i.e., 20%)
- Nozzle/Extruder volumetric flow rate, $Q = 5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$
- Average time for non-extrusion movements (travel, retraction, Z-hop) per layer, $t_{travel} = 4 \text{ s}$
- Wall (perimeter) shell count = 2 walls, each **0.4 mm** thick (1.2 mm outer shell total)
- Top and bottom solid layers = 3 each

### Step 1 — Calculate Total Number of Layers

$$
N = \dfrac{H_{total}}{h_{layer}}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{60 \text{ mm}}{0.2 \text{ mm}} = 300 \text{ layers}
$$

**Explanation**: Every 0.2 mm of vertical height requires one printed layer. A 60 mm tall object therefore needs 300 such layers stacked.

### Step 2 — Compute Outer Shell Volume

The outer shell is a hollow rectangular box of wall thickness $t_{wall} = 1.2 \text{ mm}$.

$$
V_{shell} = \bigl[(L \cdot W) - (L - 2t_{wall})(W - 2t_{wall})\bigr] \cdot H
$$

$$
V_{shell} = \bigl[(100 \cdot 80) - (100 - 2.4)(80 - 2.4)\bigr] \cdot 60
$$

$$
V_{shell} = \bigl[8000 - (97.6)(77.6)\bigr] \cdot 60
$$

$$
V_{shell} = \bigl[8000 - 7573.76\bigr] \cdot 60
$$

$$
V_{shell} = 426.24 \cdot 60 = 25{,}574.4 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Top and Bottom Solid Layer Volume

Top and bottom: 3 solid layers each = **6 layers total**. Each solid top/bottom layer has thickness 0.2 mm.

Total top + bottom thickness:

$$
h_{solid} = 6 \times 0.2 = 1.2 \text{ mm}
$$

Volume of top + bottom:

$$
V_{top\,bottom} = L \cdot W \cdot h_{solid} = 100 \cdot 80 \cdot 1.2 = 9{,}600 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Infill Volume

Remaining interior volume (excluding outer shell and top/bottom):

$$
V_{interior} = (L - 2t_{wall})(W - 2t_{wall}) \cdot (H - h_{solid})
$$

$$
V_{interior} = (97.6)(77.6)(60 - 1.2)
$$

$$
V_{interior} = 7573.76 \cdot 58.8 = 445{,}337.088 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Infill material volume:

$$
V_{infill} = f_{infill} \cdot V_{interior} = 0.20 \cdot 445{,}337.088 = 89{,}067.42 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 5 — Total Material Volume

$$
V_{mat} = V_{shell} + V_{top\,bottom} + V_{infill}
$$

$$
V_{mat} = 25{,}574.4 + 9{,}600 + 89{,}067.42 = 124{,}241.82 \text{ mm}^3
$$

### Step 6 — Calculate Printing Time

Pure extrusion time:

$$
t_{extrude} = \dfrac{V_{mat}}{Q} = \dfrac{124{,}241.82}{5} = 24{,}848.36 \text{ s}
$$

Non-extrusion time (travel, Z-hop, retraction):

$$
t_{travel,total} = N \cdot t_{travel} = 300 \cdot 4 = 1{,}200 \text{ s}
$$

Total print time:

$$
T_{print} = t_{extrude} + t_{travel,total} = 24{,}848.36 + 1{,}200 = 26{,}048.36 \text{ s}
$$

Convert to hours:

$$
T_{print} = \dfrac{26{,}048.36}{3600} \approx 7.24 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 7 — Material Mass (if PLA, ρ = 1.24 g/cm³)

Convert volume:

$$
V_{mat} = 124{,}241.82 \text{ mm}^3 = 124.24 \text{ cm}^3
$$

$$
m = \rho \cdot V = 1.24 \cdot 124.24 \approx 154.06 \text{ g}
$$

## 3.2 Final Analytical Matrix (Summary of Numerical Results)

| Parameter | Symbol | Calculated Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of layers | $N$ | **300 layers** |
| Outer shell volume | $V_{shell}$ | **25,574.40 mm³** |
| Top + Bottom volume | $V_{top\,bottom}$ | **9,600.00 mm³** |
| Infill volume | $V_{infill}$ | **89,067.42 mm³** |
| Total material volume | $V_{mat}$ | **124,241.82 mm³** |
| Total print time | $T_{print}$ | **≈ 7.24 hours** |
| Material mass (PLA) | $m$ | **≈ 154.06 g** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters in the lab**: When students conduct a 3D printing experiment, they are expected to **predict the print time and material usage beforehand** using the above formulae, then **validate the prediction** against the actual experimental reading. Discrepancies are analyzed in terms of slicing settings, operator skill, and machine calibration.
