# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define 3D Printing. List any four advantages of 3D Printing over conventional manufacturing.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition**: 3D Printing, or **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, is a process of creating three-dimensional solid objects from a digital CAD file by **successively adding material layer-by-layer** until the entire part is formed, as opposed to subtractive (milling, turning) or formative (casting, forging) methods.

**Four Advantages**:
1. **Design Freedom**: Complex internal geometries (lattice structures, conformal channels) that are impossible in conventional machining can be printed easily.
2. **Zero Material Waste**: Only the required amount of material is deposited; no chips/swaf generated (unlike CNC).
3. **Rapid Prototyping**: Lead time reduced from weeks (tooling) to hours/days.
4. **Customization**: Each part can be uniquely customized (e.g., patient-specific implants) without retooling.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between FDM and SLA processes.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | FDM (Fused Deposition Modeling) | SLA (Stereolithography) |
|---|---|---|
| **Material State** | Thermoplastic filament (solid) | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| **Energy Source** | Heated nozzle extrudes melted plastic | UV laser cures photosensitive resin |
| **Layer Bonding** | Thermal fusion between layers | Photopolymerization (chemical curing) |
| **Surface Finish** | Rough, visible layer lines | Smooth, very high detail |
| **Tolerance** | ±0.2 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| **Cost** | Low (₹15k–₹2L machines) | Medium–High (₹50k–₹10L machines) |
| **Support** | Required for overhangs > 45° | Required (scaffold structures) |
| **Common Use** | Functional prototypes, jigs | Dental, jewelry, master patterns |

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice Provided)

### ✏️ **Choice Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

### **Q.A(a): Explain the Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process with a neat sketch. List its process parameters and applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle**:
FDM, invented by **Scott Crump** in **1989** (Stratasys), works on the principle of **material extrusion**. A thermoplastic filament of diameter typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** is fed into a heated nozzle (heated to **190 °C – 250 °C** for PLA/ABS). The nozzle melts the plastic and extrudes it as a thin bead of diameter **0.4 mm** (default nozzle size) onto a **heated build platform**. The nozzle traces the cross-sectional profile of the part for each layer. After one layer is complete, the build platform descends by **one layer thickness (0.1–0.3 mm)**, and the next layer is deposited on top. This continues until the part is complete.

**Key Components**:
- Filament spool
- Filament feed mechanism (Bowden or Direct Drive extruder)
- Heated hot-end with nozzle
- Heated build plate
- X, Y, Z stepper motors
- Controller board (e.g., Arduino Mega + RAMPS, or SKR Mini)

**Process Parameters (KTU High-Yield)**:
1. **Layer Thickness**: 0.1 mm (fine) – 0.3 mm (standard)
2. **Nozzle Temperature**: 190 °C – 250 °C
3. **Bed Temperature**: 60 °C (PLA) – 110 °C (ABS)
4. **Print Speed**: 40 mm/s – 100 mm/s
5. **Infill Percentage**: 0% – 100%
6. **Shell/Perimeter Count**: 1 – 5 walls
7. **Support Angle Threshold**: 45° (default)
8. **Flow Rate**: 90% – 110% (multiplier)

**Applications**:
- Functional prototypes, jigs, fixtures, brackets
- Educational models in engineering labs
- Custom enclosures for electronics
- Low-volume production of end-use parts

---

### **Q.A(b): A part of dimensions 100 mm × 80 mm × 50 mm is to be printed on an FDM machine with the following parameters: Layer thickness = 0.25 mm, Infill = 20%, Outer wall thickness = 1.2 mm, Nozzle flow rate = 6 mm³/s, Travel time per layer = 3 s, Material: PLA (density = 1.24 g/cm³). Calculate: (i) Number of layers, (ii) Material volume, (iii) Material mass, (iv) Print time. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Number of Layers**:

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{h_{layer}} = \dfrac{50}{0.25} = 200 \text{ layers}
$$

**(ii) Material Volume**:

Outer shell volume (as derived earlier):

$$
V_{shell} = \bigl[(100 \cdot 80) - (97.6)(77.6)\bigr] \cdot 50
$$

$$
V_{shell} = 426.24 \cdot 50 = 21{,}312 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Top + Bottom (3 layers each, total 6 layers × 0.25 mm = 1.5 mm solid):

$$
V_{top\,bottom} = 100 \cdot 80 \cdot 1.5 = 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Interior volume:

$$
V_{interior} = (97.6)(77.6)(50 - 1.5) = 7573.76 \cdot 48.5 = 367{,}327.36 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Infill volume (20%):

$$
V_{infill} = 0.20 \cdot 367{,}327.36 = 73{,}465.47 \text{ mm}^3
$$

Total material volume:

$$
V_{mat} = 21{,}312 + 12{,}000 + 73{,}465.47 = 106{,}777.47 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**(iii) Material Mass**:

Convert to cm³: $106{,}777.47 / 1000 = 106.78 \text{ cm}^3$

$$
m = 1.24 \cdot 106.78 = 132.41 \text{ g}
$$

**(iv) Print Time**:

Extrusion time:

$$
t_{extrude} = \dfrac{106{,}777.47}{6} = 17{,}796.25 \text{ s}
$$

Travel time:

$$
t_{travel} = 200 \cdot 3 = 600 \text{ s}
$$

Total:

$$
T_{print} = 17{,}796.25 + 600 = 18{,}396.25 \text{ s} \approx 5.11 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Common Mark Losers)**:
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap**: Many students forget to convert **mm³ → cm³** when multiplying with density in g/cm³. Always divide by **1000**. Lose **1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Infill Definition Confusion**: A common error is treating infill fraction as **shell volume also**. Only the **interior region** (after shell and top/bottom) is filled with infill pattern. Lose **2 marks** for this conceptual error.
> 3. **Travel Time Inclusion**: Some students compute only extrusion time, forgetting that Z-hops and travel movements add significant time (often 10–30% extra). Mention both.

---

### ✏️ **Choice Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

### **Q.B(a): Explain the working principle of Stereolithography (SLA) with a neat sketch. Discuss its advantages, limitations, and applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle**:
Stereolithography (SLA), invented by **Chuck Hull in 1984**, is the **first commercial 3D printing technology** and belongs to the **Vat Photopolymerization** category. The process uses a vat of **liquid photopolymer resin** that hardens (cures) when exposed to **ultraviolet (UV) light**.

**Working Sequence**:
1. The build platform is positioned just **below the surface** of the liquid resin (typically 0.05–0.15 mm).
2. A **UV laser** (wavelength 355 nm for solid-state UV lasers) traces the cross-section of the part on the resin surface, **polymerizing (hardening)** the resin selectively along the laser path.
3. After one layer is cured, the **build platform lifts** by one layer thickness, allowing fresh liquid resin to flow beneath.
4. A **wiper blade or roller** re-coats the surface uniformly.
5. The laser traces the next layer, bonding it to the previous one.
6. This process repeats layer-by-layer until the part is complete.
7. The part is then **rinsed in isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** to remove uncured resin and **post-cured in a UV chamber** for full strength.

**Advantages**:
- Highest accuracy among AM processes (**±0.05 mm**)
- Excellent surface finish (Ra as low as **0.01–0.05 μm**)
- Fine features (down to **0.05 mm wall thickness**)
- Wide range of engineering-grade resins (tough, flexible, castable, biocompatible)

**Limitations**:
- Limited to **photopolymers** (UV-curable resins)
- Mechanical properties often inferior to engineering thermoplastics
- Requires **post-curing** step
- Resins are **sensitive to UV degradation** (yellowing over time)
- Higher machine and consumable cost than FDM

**Applications**:
- **Dental**: Crowns, bridges, surgical guides
- **Jewelry**: Master patterns for investment casting
- **Medical**: Anatomical models, hearing aid shells
- **Engineering**: High-detail prototypes, optical components

---

### **Q.B(b): Compare Additive Manufacturing and Subtractive Manufacturing with suitable examples. Highlight the engineering scenario where each is preferred. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Additive Manufacturing (AM) | Subtractive Manufacturing (SM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Removal** | Material is **added** layer-by-layer | Material is **removed** by cutting tools |
| **Material Wastage** | Very low (5–10%) | High (50–80%) for complex shapes |
| **Tooling** | **No tooling** required | Requires cutting tools, jigs, fixtures |
| **Lead Time** | Hours to days | Days to months (especially with tooling) |
| **Geometric Complexity** | **Very high** (internal channels, lattice) | **Limited** by tool access |
| **Batch Size** | Best for **low-volume / customized** | Best for **high-volume identical parts** |
| **Surface Finish** | Layer-line texture (FDM); smooth (SLA) | Excellent machined finish possible |
| **Material Options** | Limited to printer-compatible materials | Virtually **all engineering materials** |
| **Tolerance** | ±0.05 to ±0.3 mm | ±0.01 mm or better achievable |
| **Cost Structure** | High machine, low per-part cost | Low machine, high per-part tooling cost |
| **Examples** | 3D printed impeller, prosthetic socket | CNC milled aerospace bracket, turned shaft |

**Preferred Engineering Scenarios**:

- **Choose AM when**:
  1. Part has **complex internal geometry** (e.g., bionic spine implant, turbine blade with cooling channels).
  2. **Low volume, high customization** (e.g., one-off prototype, patient-specific surgical guide).
  3. **Short lead time** is critical (e.g., engineering validation, design iteration).
  4. Part has **lattice structures** for weight reduction (e.g., aerospace bracket, drone frame).

- **Choose SM when**:
  1. Part is required in **mass production** (e.g., 10,000+ identical gears).
  2. **Tight tolerances** (±0.01 mm) are mandatory (e.g., bearing races, hydraulic spools).
  3. **Material must be a specific alloy** not available in AM feedstock (e.g., Inconel 718 in bulk).
  4. **Excellent surface finish** is needed without post-processing (e.g., optical mirror mounts).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. Many students write "AM is always better than SM" or vice versa — this is a **conceptual error**. The correct view is **scenario-dependent selection**. Marks are deducted for one-sided answers.
> 2. **Specific Examples Required**: Don't write "AM is for prototypes" — mention a **named industrial example** (e.g., *GE LEAP fuel nozzle*, *Bugatti 3D-printed brake caliper*, *Aligntech Invisalign*).
> 3. **Mention ASTM/ISO**: Using terms like "AM (ISO/ASTM 52900-15)" shows command over standards and fetches extra credit.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **3D Printing = Additive Manufacturing = Layer-by-layer material addition** (ISO/ASTM 52900-15 definition).
- ✅ **Two most commonly tested processes**: **FDM (extrusion)** and **SLA (vat photopolymerization)**. Know their differences cold.
- ✅ **FDM invented by Scott Crump (1989)**; **SLA invented by Chuck Hull (1984)**.
- ✅ **Layer thickness formula**: $N = H/h$ — appears in nearly every numerical.
- ✅ **Mass formula**: $m = \rho \cdot V$ — remember **mm³ → cm³** conversion (divide by 1000).
- ✅ **Material densities to remember**: PLA = **1.24 g/cm³**, ABS = **1.04 g/cm³**, Resin ≈ **1.10 g/cm³**, Nylon = **1.14 g/cm³**.
- ✅ **Typical FDM temperatures**: Nozzle **190–250 °C**, Bed **60–110 °C**.
- ✅ **Common STL tolerance**: Chordal deviation **0.01 mm**; angular **15°**.
- ✅ **Slicer software names**: **Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D, IdeaMaker**.
- ✅ **Support threshold**: Overhangs **greater than 45°** require supports.
- ✅ **AM advantages**: design freedom, low waste, customization, short lead time.
- ✅ **AM limitations**: limited materials, post-processing needed, anisotropy, slow for large parts.
- ✅ **Always mention at least one industrial example** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward applied knowledge.
- ✅ **Cross product for STL triangle area**: $A = \tfrac{1}{2}\vert \vec{AB} \times \vec{AC} \vert$ — write without vertical pipes (use `\vert` or `\mid`) for clean notation.
- ✅ **Common KTU question patterns**: (a) Define + advantages, (b) Compare FDM/SLA/SLS, (c) Numerical on layer count + time, (d) Application-based discussion.
